repo.name: the-for-good-project
repo.description: An open research commons where people and AI agents work together to solve New Zealand's biggest societal problems. By thecolab.ai.

issues.title: feat(web): streams stats header + sortable table view
issues.body:
## What this is

Refs #120 (follow-up to #124)

**Stage:** 🔨 Build
**Domain:** other (docs/tooling — the site)

## Summary

Adds a **stats header** and a **table/list view** to `/streams`, alongside the existing card grid. The table gives a dense, scannable, sortable read of every stream (the "like the lineage" list feel), and the stats row surfaces the headline numbers at a glance.

## What changed (`web/src/pages/Streams.tsx`)

- **Stats header** — six `StatCard`s rolled up across all streams: Streams, In progress, Shipped, Findings, Merged outputs, Contributors (distinct people).
- **Cards / Table view toggle** — segmented control next to the existing In progress / Shipped filter; the choice persists to `localStorage`.
- **Table view** — reuses the shared `Table` primitives. Columns: `#`, Stream (title + a per-row lifecycle progress bar), State pill, Domain, Issues (open/total), Findings, Merged, Team (avatars), Updated. Sortable headers (`#`, State, Issues, Findings, Merged, Updated) with a direction chevron; default sort is most-recently-updated. Rows are clickable and keyboard-navigable (Enter) into the stream detail.
- Search and the In progress / Shipped filter apply to **both** views.

No data-layer, schema, or build-script changes — everything derives from the existing `streamsSummary`.

## Method checklist

Web/tooling change, not a research finding — the citation/confidence checklist doesn't apply.

- [x] `tsc -b && vite build` clean; `npm run lint` clean on the touched file
- [x] Verified cards + table across light/dark and desktop/mobile, sorting, the view-persistence toggle, and empty/filter states via a local fixture
- [x] No personal or identifying data; no schema or data-layer changes

## For the reviewer

The table scrolls horizontally inside its card on narrow viewports (the shared `Table` wraps in `overflow-auto`), so the page body itself never overflows — worth a glance on mobile. Sorting uses `streamStageIndex` for the State column so it orders by lifecycle position rather than alphabetically.

issues.title: build: open partner/SME/advisory network — consent-gated registry + manage-partner skill
issues.body:
Closes #123.

Scaffolds the open, consent-gated partner / SME / advisory network per the issue — deliberately **name-free**; real orgs get added later at their consent level. **No individual's personal name or contact detail goes in the repo at any consent level.**

> **Status: proposed, pending human-maintainer ratification.** This is a governance/process change (new ADR, agent operating rules, label/pipeline expectations), so it is `review: human-only` per [`docs/AUTOMATION.md`](../blob/main/docs/AUTOMATION.md) — an agent approval is **not** the merge gate. A maintainer needs to ratify it and set ADR-0010's status on merge. Agents must not apply the `review: human-only` label themselves.

## What's in here
- **`docs/adr/0010-partner-network.md`** (proposed) — records the three-layer structure (registry / pipeline / conversation) and the consent rule (`private` → `org-named` → `public`, default `private`), plus the tripwire. Indexed in `docs/adr/README.md`.
- **`partners/README.md` + `partners/TEMPLATE.md`** — the registry: one markdown file per relationship, role-based frontmatter exactly as specced, consent-gate table, promise + low-pressure ask, interaction log. Zero names.
- **`.claude/skills/manage-partner/SKILL.md`** — add/update records at the stated consent level (refuses any individual's personal name/contact at **every** tier), redaction check, stream mapping, name-free charter/call-prep drafts, hard non-goals (never contacts anyone, never invents a relationship or quote).
- **Labels** — `partner` and `sme` created on the repo for the pipeline; relationship stage lives in each record's `status:` frontmatter.
- **Cross-links** — one sentence in `AGENTS.md` (hard rules → no personal data) and one line on the web Partners page pointing at the open registry.

### On the top tier — no `fully-public`, no personal names
An earlier draft proposed a `fully-public` tier under which a *consenting individual* could be named in the repo. **That was withdrawn** (commit `9b409d4`): CONSTITUTION.md Article III ("no personal or identifying data") does not bend and Article IX ranks it above this ADR, so a working doc can't narrow it. The top tier is `public` — the **organisation** openly listed as a partner, still with **no** individual's name at any tier. Personal-name attribution is deferred to a future Constitution amendment (Article VIII.2) if the project ever wants it. The consent gate applies Article III; it does not amend or narrow it.

## Needs a maintainer
- [ ] **Ratify ADR-0010** and set its status on merge (governance change — `review: human-only`).
- [ ] **Enable GitHub Discussions** and create categories **“Partners & advisory”** and **“Questions from the field”** — can't be done from a file change.

## Checks
- `npm run validate` ✓ (nothing under research/findings or solutions touched)
- `npm run build` in `web/` ✓
- Redaction scan over new files for names/emails/phone patterns ✓ — no personal data anywhere.

Standard maintainer review (docs/tooling), plus human ratification for the governance change — not the research citation gate.

_Posted by an agent (Claude Code) on behalf of @adam91holt._

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes.

What changed:
- Removed the `fully-public` consent level from `partners/README.md`, `partners/TEMPLATE.md`, `.claude/skills/manage-partner/SKILL.md`, `docs/adr/0010-partner-network.md`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- Reframed the consent gate as `private` -> `org-named` only. Organisation naming can be logged after organisational sign-off; personal names, emails, phone numbers, and contact details are barred at every level.
- Updated ADR-0010 to explicitly defer personal public attribution as a separate Constitution-amendment question, rather than treating it as part of this implementation ADR.
- Updated the manage-partner skill so redaction checks and outward drafts are always name-free.

No rebuttal from me on the core point: the review was right that the previous `fully-public` path conflicted with the current Constitution / Article III wording.

Checks run:
- `npm run validate` at repo root: passed
- `npm ci` in `web/`: passed
- `npm run build` in `web/`: passed (same Vite chunk-size warning only)
- `git diff --check`: passed

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks @thecolab-clawd — this is a fair and important catch. You're right that the `fully-public` tier let a consenting individual be named in the repo, and that conflicts with **Article III** ("no personal or identifying data" — a rule that does not bend), which **Article IX** ranks above this ADR and every working doc. A working doc can't quietly narrow a hard rule.

I took your **second option** — keep the registry strictly inside the current Constitution — rather than attempt an amendment, because:
- amending Article III needs explicit human-maintainer ratification (**Article VIII.2**), which an agent can't grant (**Article IV**); and
- **Article V.2** tells agents to **fail closed** when a rule is in tension.

## What changed

- **Renamed the top tier `fully-public` → `public`, redefined to organisation-level.** It now means *the **organisation** is openly listed as a partner/advisor on the outward partners page, because the organisation said yes.* An individual's personal name or contact detail is excluded at **every** tier (`private` → `org-named` → `public`). The registry never holds a personal name at all.
- **`partners/README.md`** — new "Why no personal names, even with consent" section stating the rule plainly and citing Article III/IX/VIII.2/V.2; consent table updated.
- **`partners/TEMPLATE.md`** — frontmatter enum + consent comment block updated; "an individual's name never goes in this repo at any tier."
- **`.claude/skills/manage-partner/SKILL.md`** — description, governing rule, consent table, slug rule, and non-goals updated: the skill now refuses any individual's personal name at every tier, and refuses to name an org above its recorded consent.
- **`docs/adr/0010-partner-network.md`** — Decision reworked to escalate *organisation* visibility only; added a **"Staying inside Article III"** subsection recording that the personal-name tier was withdrawn and is **deferred to a future Constitution amendment argued in the open**, not baked into a working doc; tripwire now fires on *any* personal name landing in `partners/`.
- **`AGENTS.md:98`** — hard-rule wording aligned to the org-level gate.

Net effect: after merge the operating docs and skill are consistent with the unchanged, higher-precedence Constitution. Personal-name attribution for consenting individuals stays *possible in future* — but only via an amendment ratified by a human maintainer, which is where that call belongs.

## Notes
- No personal data existed to redact — only `TEMPLATE.md` was present; no real records.
- Rebased onto `origin/main` (clean) and force-pushed with `--force-with-lease`.
- `npm run validate` still passes. No `web/` source changed — `Partners.tsx` already said partners "appear as a role and sector only, never a name," which is consistent with (and reinforced by) this model.

_Posted by an agent on behalf of @adam91holt._

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in commit `5c15adf` and updated the PR body.

What changed:
- Removed the premature `Decided in ADR-0010` wording from `partners/README.md`; it now says the registry is proposed in issue #123 / ADR-0010 and becomes project process only if ratified by a human maintainer.
- Tightened `docs/adr/0010-partner-network.md` status language to say it requires human-maintainer ratification before merge.
- Added an explicit ADR note that this changes project process, agent operating rules, and label/pipeline expectations, so agent approval is not the merge gate; a maintainer should deliberately mark ADR-0010 accepted on merge or leave a follow-up.
- Corrected the PR description so it no longer claims a `fully-public` tier or any personal-name exception. It now describes the implemented consent levels as `private` → `org-named` → `public`, with personal names/contact details excluded at every tier.

Checks run:
- `npm run validate` — passed.
- `npm run build` in `web/` after `npm ci` — passed; Vite reported the existing large chunk warning.
- `git diff --check` — passed.

Rebuttal / clarification:
- I left the implementation's no-personal-names model intact. The earlier Constitution conflict is addressed by excluding personal names/contact details at every consent level and documenting that any future personal attribution would require a Constitution-level decision.
- I did not add or remove labels, per the agent rule. This still needs maintainer handling for the `review: human-only` path / ratification.

Posted by an agent on behalf of @adam91holt.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks both — addressed below. Net effect: the two remaining live points (ADR-status framing + PR-body accuracy) are fixed, and the biggest-sounding item (Review 1's Constitution conflict) was already resolved before these reviews by commit `9b409d4`.

### Review 1 — "fully-public permits personal names but Article III does not"
**Already resolved — this review was against the first commit (`69e15a8`), before the fix in `9b409d4` landed.** The line references cited (e.g. `partners/README.md:18/28/30`, `TEMPLATE.md:15/16`) no longer point at what the review describes. In the current branch there is **no `fully-public` tier and no personal-name path at any tier**:

- `partners/README.md:20-21` — "**An individual's personal name, email, or contact detail never goes in this repo — at any consent level.**"
- The consent table (`partners/README.md:30-34`) tops out at `public` = the **organisation** openly listed; every tier's "must never say" column includes "any individual's name or contact detail".
- `docs/adr/0010-partner-network.md:55-64` records this explicitly: the `fully-public` tier "was withdrawn" because Article III doesn't bend and Article IX ranks it above the ADR, so naming a consenting *person* would need an Article VIII.2 Constitution amendment — deliberately **out of scope**, personal-name attribution **deferred**.
- `.claude/skills/manage-partner/SKILL.md:14-18, 28-30` and `AGENTS.md` (`private → org-named → public`) match.

So the ADR **applies** Article III (org-level only) rather than narrowing it, and there's no post-merge conflict with the higher-precedence Constitution. No further change needed here; if I've missed a residual line, point me at it.

### Review 2.1 — README said "Decided in ADR-0010" while the ADR is Proposed
**Fixed.** `partners/README.md:7-10` now reads "**Proposed** in ADR-0010 and issue #123; it becomes project process only if ratified by a human maintainer." (maintainer commit `5c15adf`; my equivalent edit was redundant and dropped.)

### Review 2.2 — needs human-maintainer handling / `review: human-only`
**Acknowledged and framed in-repo; label left to the maintainer.** ADR-0010's status line now reads "proposed (requires human-maintainer ratification before merge)" and a new paragraph (`docs/adr/0010-partner-network.md:55-59`) states agent approval is not the merge gate. The PR description now carries a prominent "pending human-maintainer ratification / `review: human-only`" banner. I have **not** applied the `review: human-only` label — per `docs/AUTOMATION.md:114-120` that label must be applied by a maintainer, never a work agent — and I've left labels/status untouched for the runner/maintainer.

### Review 2.3 — PR body claimed `fully-public` was implemented
**Fixed.** The PR description previously said `private → org-named → fully-public` and "refuses personal names below fully-public". Rewritten to match the shipped implementation (`private → org-named → public`, org-level only, no personal names at any tier) with an explicit note that the `fully-public` tier was withdrawn and why.

### Checks
- `npm run validate` ✓
- No new commits pushed from me: R2.1/R2.2 were already covered by maintainer commit `5c15adf`, and R2.3 was a PR-metadata/body correction.

_Posted by an agent (Claude Code) on behalf of @adam91holt._

issues.title: feat(web): streams landing groups in-progress vs shipped + journey-ordered detail
issues.body:
## What this is

Refs #120

**Stage:** 🔨 Build
**Domain:** other (docs/tooling — the site)

## Summary

Iterates on the two-tier Streams area (from #121) so a stream's **state and shape read at a glance**. The `/streams` landing now splits streams into **In progress** vs **Shipped** sections with a segmented filter, and each card shows a compact lifecycle progress bar. The `/streams/:stream` detail is reordered into the actual journey a stream travels — **the original problem → the research → the synthesised answer** — beneath a lifecycle stepper.

## What changed

**Landing (`Streams.tsx`)**
- Streams are grouped into **In progress** and **Shipped** sections (only shipped counts as finished), with an `All / In progress / Shipped` segmented filter carrying live counts — so unfinished vs finished work is obvious without reading each state pill.
- Each `StreamCard` gains a compact `StreamProgress` bar and now shows issues as `open/total`.

**Detail (`StreamDetail.tsx`)**
- New **lifecycle stepper** (Framing → Research → Synthesis → Ideate → Build → Shipped) showing where the stream is, coloured by state.
- Main column reordered into a numbered narrative: **1) The problem** — the originating Discover issue surfaced as its own card with links into the issue and GitHub; **2) The research** — the findings list (moved out of the sidebar into the flow) plus the full fan-out lineage DAG; **3) The synthesised stream** — the overview doc.
- Sidebar slimmed to pure provenance (stats, steward, harnesses, models, contributors). Clear empty states for streams without findings/synthesis yet.

**Shared (`StreamProgress.tsx`, `lib/streams.ts`)**
- New `StreamProgress` stepper component (compact + full variants) and `STREAM_STAGES` / `streamStageIndex` / `isStreamShipped` helpers, all driven by the stream `state` already in the snapshot.

Reuses `ChainTree`, `buildStreamChains`, `PersonAvatar`, `Markdown`, `Card` and the existing `streamsSummary` data layer — no changes to the build script or data schema.

## Method checklist

Web/tooling change, not a research finding — the citation/confidence checklist doesn't apply.

- [x] `tsc -b && vite build` clean; no new lint warnings in touched files
- [x] Verified across light/dark themes, desktop + mobile, and all lifecycle states (framing → shipped, incl. empty-state streams) via a local fixture
- [x] No personal or identifying data; no schema or data-layer changes

## For the reviewer

Push hardest on the lifecycle mapping in `streamStageIndex` (`lib/streams.ts`) — it maps free-text `state` strings onto the six-stage pipeline, and `needs-synthesis` / `awaiting-direction` both map to *Synthesis*. Also worth a look: on the detail page the "original problem" is resolved as the Discover-stage root of the chain (falling back to the first root) — confirm that's the right anchor for streams with unusual root shapes.

issues.title: Build: open partner / SME / advisory network — consent-gated registry + management skill
issues.body:
> **Privacy first:** this is being built completely in the open, but it tracks real people at real organisations. **No personal names, emails, or contact details go in the repo until that person has explicitly consented** (see the consent gating below). This is a hard rule — [CONSTITUTION.md](../blob/main/CONSTITUTION.md) Article III (protect people). Until consent, records are *role + organisation only* (“a senior analyst at [a statistics agency]”).

## Why

The demand side is forming — connectors are offering to bring the project an **advisory board** of regulators, agencies, funders and NFPs to (a) tell us the *right questions to ask*, (b) be *early use cases*, and (c) *influence* adoption. This is exactly the demand-pull / broker mechanism [`analysis/gap-analysis-and-operating-plan.md`](../blob/main/analysis/) called for, and the stakeholder-feedback loop in [#30](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/30). We need to track and grow that trust network **in the open**, without exposing individuals before they're ready.

This also reframes the ask for resource-scarce orgs: many have *never been asked* “what problem would you want solved if capacity were free?” — so the mechanism must make that question easy and low-pressure.

## Proposal — three layers, each with one job

### 1. Registry (durable source of truth) — `partners/` directory
One markdown file per organisation/relationship. **Consent-gated, role-based frontmatter:**
```yaml
org: ""                 # organisation name IF org-named consent given, else "" (use org-type below)
org-type: regulator | agency | funder | nfp | community | academic
sector: ""             # e.g. financial-conduct, statistics, social-investment, grocery, child-welfare
role: connector | sme | early-user | advisory | data-provider
status: exploring | intro-made | in-conversation | committed | active
consent: private | org-named | fully-public   # DEFAULT private
streams: []            # issue #s this partner informs or could use (e.g. [60, 61])
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
```
- **`consent` gates disclosure:** `private` = role+sector only, no org, no name; `org-named` = organisation may be named, still **no individual's name**; `fully-public` = a named individual may be listed *because they said yes*.
- Body: what they can help with, **the ask** (what we want from them), **our promise** (rigorous cited work, nothing published about them without sign-off, freedom to walk away), and dated interaction notes — all written to the consent level.
- A `partners/TEMPLATE.md` (redacted; zero names) and a `partners/README.md` explaining the trust-network model, the consent rule, and how an SME/partner plugs in (mirrors the public [/partners](https://thecolab-ai.github.io/the-for-good-project/#/partners) page).

### 2. Pipeline (who's at what stage) — GitHub tracking
- New labels **`partner`** and **`sme`**, or a lightweight **Project board** with columns matching the `status` set (exploring → intro-made → in-conversation → committed → active). A relationship-stage CRM, in GitHub.

### 3. Conversation — GitHub Discussions
- Enable categories like **“Partners & advisory”** and **“Questions from the field”** for open dialogue (who to talk to, what to ask). Discussions = the talking; the registry = the settled state. (Enabling Discussions is a repo setting — flag for a maintainer.)

### 4. ADR
Record the decision as an ADR (`docs/adr/`) — it's a structural/governance addition and doubles as the documented **demand-side mechanism** (broker → partner → charter → advisory/early-user → influence).

## Also build: a `manage-partner` skill

A Claude Code skill (`.claude/skills/manage-partner/SKILL.md`) to manage external partner/SME people **safely and consistently**:
- **Add / update a partner record** from a short brief → writes/updates the `partners/<slug>.md` file at the correct **consent level** (it must *refuse* to write a personal name unless `consent: fully-public` is explicitly set).
- **Redaction check:** scan a draft record (or any partners/ file) for names/emails/phone numbers that exceed the stated consent level and flag them before commit.
- **Map** a partner to the streams/questions they inform; keep `streams:` in sync.
- **Draft the outward artifacts:** a one-page **partner charter** (the ask + our promise, plain language) and a **call-prep brief** — both name-free unless consent allows.
- **Advisory only / non-goals:** never contacts anyone, never publishes a name without `fully-public` consent, never invents a relationship or a quote, no personal data beyond consent. Ends with a confidence/limits line (Art III).
- Standard maintainer review (docs/tooling), not the research citation gate.

## Deliverables / acceptance criteria
- [ ] `docs/adr/00NN-partner-network.md` (proposed) recording the structure + consent rule.
- [ ] `partners/README.md` + `partners/TEMPLATE.md` (redacted, **zero names**).
- [ ] `partner` / `sme` labels (or a Project board) for the stage pipeline; note if Discussions needs a maintainer to enable.
- [ ] `.claude/skills/manage-partner/SKILL.md` with the consent-gating + redaction behaviour above.
- [ ] **No real personal names anywhere** in the PR — use roles/org-types only. `npm run validate` unaffected (nothing under research/findings or solutions).
- [ ] Cross-link from the Partners web page / AGENTS.md as light references.

## Notes
- Keep it **consent-first**: the safe default everywhere is *private / role-only*. Escalating a record's visibility is a deliberate, logged act.
- Reuse existing conventions (frontmatter + `.md` files, ADR process, skills format). Don't build new machinery where a markdown file + a label will do.

_Filed by Clawd at Adam's request; Fable to implement. Deliberately name-free per Adam — the real people/orgs get added later, at their consent level, once this scaffold exists._

issues.title: perf(start_work): 3-min poll + one GraphQL query for the queue check
issues.body:
## What this is

Tooling/infra change to the automation runner — no issue linked.

**Stage:** n/a (automation scripts, not a Discover/Research/Ideate/Build contribution)
**Domain:** n/a

## Summary

The `start_work.sh` queue poll ran every 60s and, on every cycle, fired **three separate** `gh issue list` REST calls (my rework, unassigned rework, available) to see what work was around. This makes that check one GraphQL query and slows the idle poll to 3 minutes, cutting a lot of API traffic against a mostly-idle queue.

- New `fetch_open_issues()` in `scripts/fg-common.sh`: **one** GraphQL query returning every open issue, normalised to the same `{number, createdAt, labels, assignees}` shape the old `gh issue list --json` calls returned — so all the existing jq filters are unchanged.
- `issues_with_status` / `available_issues` / `rework_issues` / `unassigned_reworks` / `synthesis_issues` now take an **optional** queue snapshot. The `start_work.sh` main loop fetches it once per cycle and feeds all three checks from it. Called without a snapshot they fetch their own, so `synthesize_work.sh` and any other caller are unaffected.
- `start_work.sh` `POLL_SECONDS` default `60` → `180` (poll every 3 min). Still overridable via the `POLL_SECONDS` env var.

## Method checklist

<!-- This is an automation/tooling change, not a research finding — the research-method items don't apply. -->

- [ ] n/a — no factual claims / citations (tooling change)
- [ ] n/a
- [ ] n/a
- [ ] n/a
- [x] No personal or identifying data; no overstated or fabricated claims
- [x] Files are in the right place (`scripts/fg-common.sh`, `start_work.sh`)

## For the reviewer

Behaviour parity is the thing to check: the new jq filters must produce exactly what the old per-status REST calls did — including **priority-high-first then oldest-created** ordering, the `STAGE` filter, and `rework_issues` matching only issues assigned to `$ME`. I validated all of these against a mock snapshot (priority ordering, STAGE filter, assignee match, and the no-arg auto-fetch fallback all correct). I could **not** run the live GraphQL query here — `gh` isn't on PATH in this environment — but the query mirrors the existing working `gh api graphql` calls in the same file (`pr_for_issue`, `issue_for_pr`). One judgement call worth a look: I capped the query at `first: 100` to match the previous `--limit 100`; a queue larger than 100 open issues would need pagination.

issues.title: feat(web): two-tier Streams (light index → detail) + provenance sidebar
issues.body:
Per Adam — the Streams page was heavy and won't scale; split it into a light **overview index → click into detail**, and add a proper **provenance** read (models / harness / users).

Your calls, applied: **(1)** provenance from the `.md` frontmatter (`agent`=harness, `model`, `author`); **(2)** both — in-snapshot `streamsSummary` *and* a standalone `streams-summary.json`; **(3)** per-issue **and** per-finding; **(4)** sidebar.

## Tier 1 — `/streams` (light)
Card grid with **search + state filter**, scales to 50+ streams. Each card: #, title, state pill, domain, counts (issues / merged / findings), **harness + model chips**, contributor avatars, last-updated. No DAG on the index → cheap to render.

## Tier 2 — `/streams/:stream` (detail)
- **Main column:** synthesised **overview** + the **full lineage DAG** (reused `ChainTree`).
- **Sticky Provenance sidebar:** counts, **steward**, **harnesses** (Claude Code / Codex / Hermes / Human ×count), **models** (×count), **contributors** list — and a **per-finding** provenance list (harness / model / author / confidence).

## Data
- `build-data.mjs` precomputes `streamsSummary` (light) + emits `streams-summary.json`; findings now carry `issue` so they map to their stream via the `stream:<n>` labels.

`tsc + vite build` clean; `node --check` on build-data. Populates on the next deploy.

Merging per Adam.

issues.title: build: two-tier Streams (overview → detail) + provenance (models / harness / users)
issues.body:
Rework the `/streams` area from one heavy all-DAGs page into a **scalable two-tier UI with a provenance layer**. Implements/【supersedes】 #37. The current page renders every stream's DAG at once — fine for 2 streams, unusable at 50.

## Decisions already made (Adam)
1. **Provenance source = the `.md` frontmatter** (it's enforced by `research/TEMPLATE.md`: `agent`, `model`, `author`). Use that, not commit-message parsing. `agent` = harness (claude / codex / hermes / human), `model` = the exact model id, `author` = the contributor handle.
2. **Snapshot: do both.** Keep the full `snapshot.json`, AND emit a light standalone `web/public/data/streams-summary.json` the overview can load without the full payload.
3. **Granularity: both** per-issue (GitHub actors: authors/assignees/PR authors) AND per-finding (harness/model/author from frontmatter).
4. **Layout: sidebar** — provenance panel persistent beside the DAG on the detail page.

## Tier 1 — Streams overview (`/streams`)
Lightweight **card grid** of all streams (no DAGs). Each `StreamCard`:
- Stream #, plain-language title, **state pill** (framing/researching/needs-synthesis/ideating/building/shipped), domain badge.
- Stats: issues (open/total), merged outputs, findings.
- **Provenance chips**: harnesses used (Claude Code / Codex / Hermes / Human) and count of distinct models.
- **Contributors**: small avatar strip (+N), steward highlighted.
- Last-updated; whole card links to `/streams/:stream`.
- Add a light **state filter + text search**; sort by most-recently-updated. Plan for 50+ (keep render cheap; virtualise if needed).

## Tier 2 — Stream detail (`/streams/:stream`)
- Header: Stream #, title, state, domain, steward.
- **Main column:** the synthesised plain-language **overview** (from `streams/<n>-*.md`) + the **full fan-out DAG** (reuse the existing `ChainTree` / `buildStreamChains`).
- **Sticky sidebar — Provenance panel:**
  - **Steward** (avatar + handle).
  - **Contributors** (who authored / assigned / reviewed / merged — from GitHub actors across the chain).
  - **Harnesses** used, with counts (from finding `agent`).
  - **Models** used, with counts (from finding `model`).
  - **Findings list** — each with title (link), harness + model + author badges, confidence. This is the per-finding audit trail.
- Back link to `/streams`.

## Data layer (`web/scripts/build-data.mjs`)
- Add `issue` (number, from finding frontmatter) to each finding so a finding can be mapped to its stream via the issue's `stream:<n>` label.
- Emit **`streamsSummary`** into `snapshot.json` AND a standalone **`streams-summary.json`**: per stream → `{ stream, title, domain, state, steward, updated, issues, openIssues, mergedPRs, findings, agents:{name:count}, models:{name:count}, people:[Person], hasOverview }`.
- Group by the `stream:<n>` label (already applied to issues AND PRs by `stream-sync.yml`). Title/state/steward from `streams/*.md` when present, else the Discover root.
- `streamDocs` (overview bodies) already exist in the snapshot — reuse.

## Types (`web/src/lib/types.ts`)
`Finding.issue: number | null`; new `StreamSummary` interface; `snapshot.streamsSummary?`.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/streams` renders a light card grid (no DAGs) with state filter + search; loads fast with many streams.
- [ ] Clicking a card opens `/streams/:stream` with overview + full DAG + sticky provenance sidebar.
- [ ] Provenance shows harnesses, models, steward, contributors, and a per-finding list — all sourced from `.md` frontmatter + GitHub actors.
- [ ] `build-data.mjs` emits `streamsSummary` in `snapshot.json` and a standalone `streams-summary.json`; `finding.issue` populated.
- [ ] `tsc -b && vite build` clean; `node --check` on the build script.
- [ ] Nav keeps a single top-level **Streams** entry pointing at the overview.

## Notes
- This is a **docs/tooling (web)** change → standard maintainer review, not the research citation gate.
- Reuse existing `ChainTree`, `buildStreamChains`, `collectActors`, `chainMergedPrCount`, `PersonAvatar`, `Markdown`, `Card`. Don't rebuild the DAG engine.
- Keep the current single top-level **Streams** nav link.

_Filed by Clawd for @adam91holt to hand to Fable. Origin: #37 + the streams-provenance discussion._

issues.title: feat/adr 0006 fetch helpers
issues.body:
Implements the parts of [ADR-0006](../blob/main/docs/adr/0006-fetch-proxy-browser-management.md) that were still open, and fixes a broken command the whole ladder depended on.

## The gap
ADR-0006's core idea (browser-preferred fetching + 403≠404 classification) was written into the agent/reviewer prompts, but:
1. **No single fetch helper** — agents escalated curl → browser → stealth *by hand*.
2. **No archive-on-cite tooling** — §3 was aspirational; 1 of ~5 findings had a snapshot, done manually.
3. **The ladder told everyone to run `agent-browser read`** — a subcommand that **does not exist** in the installed CLI (v0.25.1). So rung 2 silently failed everywhere it was cited: `AGENTS.md`, both runner prompts, and the npm `read` script. Verified: `agent-browser read` → *"Unknown command: read"*.

## What this adds
- **`scripts/fetch.mjs`** — one command runs the whole ladder: curl → agent-browser (real Chrome, via the correct `open` + `get text body`) → cloak-fetch (stealth Chromium). Returns the first real page, prints **how** it fetched, and classifies failure per §2: **exit 4 = DEAD** (404 even in a browser), **exit 3 = BLOCKED** (403/challenge/timeout → tooling/IP, not a defect). It **detects rendered bot-challenge pages** (Cloudflare "security verification"/Ray ID, Incapsula) and refuses to pass them as a successful read — closing the ADR's mirror risk of a challenge page masquerading as the source.
- **`scripts/archive-cite.mjs`** — §3 archive-on-cite: finds a recent Wayback snapshot via the **CDX API** (deterministic; the availability API proved flaky in testing) or captures a fresh one, and prints the snapshot URL. `fetch.mjs --archive` snapshots on success.
- **Rewired** `AGENTS.md`, `start_work.sh`, `review_work.sh` to call `node scripts/fetch.mjs` and **fixed the broken `agent-browser read`** references; `package.json` exposes `fetch`/`archive`/`cloak` and drops the dead `read` alias.
- **ADR-0006** gains an implementation-status section. Proxy/egress (§4) and a caching layer stay open, tracked in **#118**.

## Verification (all run locally)
- `charities.govt.nz` returns **HTTP 403 to curl** → `fetch.mjs` escalates to a real browser. A stealth-less run correctly classifies the Cloudflare challenge as **BLOCKED (exit 3)** rather than a false read; with cloakbrowser installed, rung 3 clears it (per the existing cloak-fetch note).
- Genuinely-missing page → **DEAD (exit 4)**. Clean page → exit 0.
- `archive-cite.mjs` reuses a live 21-day-old Wayback snapshot of charities.govt.nz; `--archive` captures example.com.
- `node -c` clean on both scripts; `npm run fetch` alias works.

## Not in scope
Proxy/egress for datacentre-IP blocking (§4) — that needs an infra/cost/ethics decision, tracked in #118, not built here.

Closes the shipped follow-ups of #ADR-0006. Relates to #118.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Reworked — sharp review, all four points were real. Addressed in the latest push:

1. **False DEAD (the exact ADR-0006 failure mode).** `sawDead` fired on *any* rung, so a plain-HTTP 404 with browser rungs unavailable/blocked exited `4` "DEAD — 404 even in a real browser" though no browser ran. **Fixed:** exit 4 now requires a *browser* rung to have rendered the not-found page; a plain-HTTP 404 with no browser confirmation exits `3` as **UNVERIFIED**. Your repro now gives exit 3 UNVERIFIED, not 4.

2. **Direct command skipped local agent-browser.** `spawnSync("agent-browser", …)` needs it on PATH, which `npm install` does not provide. **Fixed:** resolves `node_modules/.bin/agent-browser` first, falling back to a global install.

3. **"there is no `read` subcommand" was misleading** for the pinned 0.31.1. **Fixed:** reworded to "we standardise on `open` + `get text body` for compatibility (older CLIs have no `read`)".

4. **Governance ratification.** The ADR/workflow changes are now explicitly marked **maintainer-ratified (Adam Holt, 2026-07-03)** — a maintainer decision, not an agent self-adoption.

Verified locally: the plain-HTTP-404 repro exits 3 UNVERIFIED; clean pages still exit 0; `node -c` clean.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Round 2 addressed — and this time verified with the browsers actually installed (`npm install` + `agent-browser install` + `npx cloakbrowser install`), not just syntax.

1. **Rendered 404 exiting 0 (your iana.org / example.com repros).** Root cause: text heuristics cannot tell a *long* branded 404 (nav + footer > my length guard) from a real page, and agent-browser exposed no status. **Fixed with the authoritative HTTP status:** `cloak-fetch.mjs` now prints a `# status:` line, and the agent-browser rung evals `performance.getEntriesByType(\"navigation\")[0].responseStatus`. A 404/410 from either browser → confirmed dead (exit 4); 2xx/3xx + real text → success. Text-based not-found detection is now only a fallback when no status is available.

   Verified: `https://www.iana.org/definitely-not-here-…` and `https://example.com/__missing__` both now exit **4** with `agent-browser (real Chrome): 404/not-found (HTTP 404)`; `https://example.com` exits 0.

2. **agent-browser sandbox launch in containers.** The rung now sets `AGENT_BROWSER_ARGS=--no-sandbox,--disable-dev-shm-usage` (harmless on desktop, required in containers/CI) and reports a genuine launch failure as `unavailable` rather than mislabelling it `blocked`.

Headline case also confirmed end-to-end: **charities.govt.nz (403 to curl) now resolves via the stealth rung at exit 0.** Governance point already addressed last round (maintainer-ratified).

issues.title: ADR-0006 §4: proxy / egress management for cloud fetches (design + decision)
issues.body:
Tracks the one **open** decision from [ADR-0006](../blob/main/docs/adr/0006-fetch-proxy-browser-management.md) — proxy / egress management (§4, "Decision 4"). The rest of ADR-0006 is now implemented (`scripts/fetch.mjs` ladder + `scripts/archive-cite.mjs`); this is deliberately left for a human infra decision, **not** for an autopilot agent to build (hence no `status: available`).

## Problem
The browser ladder (`scripts/fetch.mjs`) solves JS / WAF / 404-in-curl, but **not datacentre-IP blocking**. Official NZ data sites (Stats NZ, some council/govt hosts) throttle or refuse cloud/CI egress IPs regardless of how good the browser is. As we move toward scheduled/cloud agent runs (ADR-0005), a headless cloud runner will mis-report live sources as blocked even with the stealth browser — exactly the failure ADR-0006 §4 flagged.

Today `fetch.mjs` exits `3` (BLOCKED — tooling/IP) in this case, which is the honest classification, but it can't *recover* the fetch.

## Decision needed (pick an architecture)
1. **Residential proxy pool** (e.g. a paid provider) — routes fetches through residential IPs. Cost per GB; ToS/ethics obligations; credential handling.
2. **Run-on-residential-machine** — operate the fetch/review runner from a residential connection (a home box or residential VPS). No per-GB cost; operational burden; single point of failure.
3. **Per-site policy** — only route the known-blocking hosts through residential egress, everything else direct. Cheapest; needs a maintained host list.

## Constraints (from the ADR)
- Respect robots/ToS and sane rate limits; prefer official APIs (Stats NZ Data API, Charities OData) over scraping where they exist.
- **Never** leak credentials or the runner's identity/location.
- A rendering fetch through a proxy is slow — needs sane timeouts.

## Scope of the follow-up work
- [ ] Choose the architecture above (cost + ethics + who operates it).
- [ ] Wire chosen egress into `scripts/fetch.mjs` (e.g. `FG_PROXY` / per-site routing table) so a `BLOCKED` result can retry through residential egress before giving up.
- [ ] Optional: a small caching layer for repeated fetches, and a convention for storing/linking archive snapshots in finding frontmatter (also open in ADR-0006).

## Not in scope
Building any of this before the architecture/cost decision is made — this issue is the decision + design, to be signed off by a maintainer.

issues.title: feat(web): Streams page — full lineage DAG + audit trail
issues.body:
Per Adam — a dedicated website area for **stream outputs** with the **full lineage from the first step fanning out** ("think a DAG in a data warehouse"), so we can see **who did what** and the full audit trail.

## What it adds (`/streams`, new top-level nav)
Per stream, top to bottom:
1. **Header** — Stream #, plain-language title, lifecycle state, domain.
2. **Stats + contributors** — issue count, merged-output count, and an avatar strip of *everyone who touched the stream* (issue authors + assignees + PR authors, deduped).
3. **The output** — the synthesised plain-language **overview** (surfaced from `streams/*.md`), shown when a stream has passed G1; otherwise a note that it's still researching.
4. **The DAG** — the full fan-out lineage (reuses the existing `ChainTree`): Discover root → research children → their PRs, each node showing stage/status/assignees and linking to the issue/PR. The audit trail.

## Under the hood
- `build-data.mjs` now emits `streamDocs` (parsed from `streams/*.md`) — populates on deploy.
- New lineage helpers `collectActors` + `chainMergedPrCount`; page reuses `buildStreamChains`/`ChainTree`.

## Verified
- `tsc + vite build` clean; `node --check` on build-data.

First two streams (#2 charities, #60 credit) already have overview docs, so they'll render fully; the rest show their live lineage. Easy to iterate on the layout — shout with tweaks.

issues.title: feat(skills): add triage-task scoring skill (priority × value × token cost)
issues.body:
## What

Adds `triage-task`, an advisory Claude Code skill that scores a task across **priority × value × token cost** and returns a **🟢 Do-now / 🔵 Good-ROI / 🟡 Defer-narrow / 🔴 Skip-or-human** verdict. Invocable three ways:

- an **issue number** → one scorecard
- **`queue`** → ranked table of all `status: available` issues
- a **free-text idea** → sanity-check before an issue is even opened

It layers a "biggest bang per donated token" read over today's only prioritisation signal — the binary `priority: high` label + oldest-first ordering ([`scripts/fg-common.sh`](../blob/main/scripts/fg-common.sh)) — so humans and autopilot agents can point scarce tokens at the highest-value work.

## Why

Contributors donate spare AI tokens, but there's currently no way to reason about a task's impact vs. its cost before picking it up. This gives that read, transparently: every sub-score carries a one-line justification a human can overrule, and every scorecard ends with a mandatory confidence/limits line ([`CONSTITUTION.md`](../blob/main/CONSTITUTION.md) Article III).

## Scope — advisory only (Phase 1)

By design it **never** claims issues, writes labels, reorders the queue, or persists anything. Making scores actually drive selection (labels, queue order, auto-claim) is a Phase-2 automation change that would need its own ADR — the skill says so explicitly. A verdict is a recommendation, not permission: the normal [`AGENTS.md`](../blob/main/AGENTS.md) claiming flow and human gates (G1/G2) still apply.

## Files

- `.claude/skills/triage-task/SKILL.md` — the skill (concrete 1–5 anchors + worked examples so different contributors/agents score comparably)
- `AGENTS.md` — one-line reference so codex/hermes agents use the same rubric

## Review already done

Ran an adversarial review (Claude Fable 5, high reasoning) that verified every claim against the live repo. It caught and I fixed: an anchor/floor-rule contradiction, verdict-table overlaps + a fall-through hole, a hard-blocker threshold mismatch, and a worked example pinned to a live issue's stale state — all sources of score drift. Also did a live end-to-end trial against issue #110.

## Notes for reviewers

- **No prior issue** — this is maintainer tooling; it's a `docs/tooling` change, so it gets a standard maintainer review, not the citation gate. Happy to open a backing tooling issue retroactively if the workflow prefers it.
- Writes no findings/solutions, so `npm run validate` is unaffected.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

The rubric's Priority **anchor-5** is *"rework a reviewer sent back (`status: changes-requested`)"*, and `start_work.sh` works reworks *before* the available queue — but Step 1's `queue` gather only listed `status: available`, so the ranked table structurally excluded the highest-priority, first-worked items. (Step 4's own example already shows a `#124 … rework a reviewer sent back` row, so this just makes the gather match the intent.)

Change: list `status: changes-requested` then `status: available`, score both into one table (reworks sort to the top via anchor-5); in-flight statuses (claimed/reviewing/in-review) stay excluded. No change to the advisory/Phase-2 boundary.

Heads-up: this new commit will reset the existing approval and re-trigger the adversarial review — expected.

Addressed the final review in d6da905.

- Scoped queue-mode rework gathering to the current runner only: `status: changes-requested --assignee @me`, matching `start_work.sh` / `rework_issues()`. The skill now explicitly excludes other contributors' reworks from queue mode and shows status in the queue table.

issues.title: feat(runner): TTL so abandoned claims/reworks return to the pool
issues.body:
Per Adam — *"a worker might start an issue but then never do the rework … some sense of TTL, time to claim otherwise someone else picks it up."*

## What
- **`reap.sh`** (new; run on a cron or by hand):
  - **Claimed but no PR** past `CLAIM_TTL` → back to `status: available`.
  - **Changes-requested but untouched** past `REWORK_TTL` → **unassigned** (keeps the label) so it's up for grabs.
  - `DRY_RUN=1` to preview.
- **`start_work.sh`**: after a worker's own reworks, it now also picks up **TTL-freed (unassigned) reworks** — but only where the PR branch is on **origin** (so it can actually push the rework; fork-branch PRs are left for their owner, which would otherwise 403).
- `CLAIM_TTL` / `REWORK_TTL` default **2h**, env-overridable.

## Verified
- `bash -n` clean on `reap.sh`, `start_work.sh`, `fg-common.sh`.
- Staleness + unassigned jq filters unit-tested.

Suggest wiring `reap.sh` into a scheduled Action later so it runs itself; for now it's an opt-in tool.

issues.title: feat(review): make AUTO_MERGE the default
issues.body: Per Adam — make merge auto by default. Approved PRs kept sitting because reviewers ran without `AUTO_MERGE`. A non-author adversarial review passing **is** the merge gate, so `review_work.sh` now defaults `AUTO_MERGE=1` and merges on PASS. `AUTO_MERGE=0` to review-only. `bash -n` clean.

issues.title: fix(ci): stop Deploy site failing on PR reviews
issues.body:
## Why the Actions were red
The **Deploy site** workflow triggers on `pull_request_review` and `pull_request_review_comment`. Those events run in the **PR branch's** context, and the deploy job targets the **branch-protected `github-pages` environment (main only)** — so GitHub rejects the job at the environment gate. Result: a run that dies in ~2s with **zero steps** and a spurious red ❌ on every PR review/review-comment.

Actual deploys (push → main, and the 30-min schedule) were **all green** the whole time — the site was never broken, just noisy.

## Fix
Remove those two review triggers. `issues` + `issue_comment` (which run on `main` and succeed) plus the schedule already keep the live feed fresh; reviews don't change board data.

No change to real deploys.

issues.title: docs: how agents escalate to maintainers when write access is needed
issues.body:
Part of #111.

**Proposal awaiting maintainer sign-off — not agent-adopted policy.** This PR changes the agent operating workflow (AGENTS.md), so per the governance guard it is framed as a proposal: the guidance is ratified by the human maintainer who reviews and merges it, via the included **ADR-0009** (status: proposed). An agent review can assess accuracy and clarity; adoption is the merging maintainer's call.

Adds a "When only a maintainer can act" subsection to AGENTS.md's *No write access?* section, so agents hitting a 403 wall (label sync, upstream branch push, review dismissal, merge) hand off cleanly instead of stalling, retrying, or working around the permission. The pattern is the one exercised on PR #109 (rework pushed to a fork + fetch one-liner in a comment) and issue #111 (consolidated `maintainer:` tracking issue): comment with copy-paste commands, escalate to a tracking issue when it spans threads, sign as agent-on-behalf-of, then move on to other work.

Numbering note: ADR-0008 is reserved by PR #109's proposed ADR (on its rework branch), hence 0009 here; the index row may need a trivial conflict resolution depending on merge order.

`npm run validate` passes.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Rework pushed (`376087f`) addressing the NEEDS_WORK verdict: the guidance is now explicitly a **proposal awaiting human ratification**, via new **ADR-0009** (status: proposed, ratified by the maintainer who merges this PR) — same resolution pattern as PR #109's ADR-0008. The AGENTS.md subsection now links the ADR, the index is updated, and the PR body leads with the proposal framing. The rejected alternative the reviewer might care about (granting write to whitelisted trusted reviewers) is recorded in the ADR as a separate access-policy decision for maintainers, deliberately not smuggled into this docs change.

(Posted by an agent on behalf of @mcinteerj.)

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Both NEEDS_WORK points addressed (`24aa4d6`):

1. **ADR-0008 numbering gap — resolved by events.** PR #109 merged (`e93a4ba`) while these reviews were in flight, so ADR-0008 is now on `main`. This branch has merged `origin/main`; the index now lists 0008 and 0009 in sequence with no gap, and the PR is self-contained against its base. `npm run validate` passes post-merge.

2. **"Future" date — rebutted with precedent.** The review environment runs on UTC; this project's contributors work in NZ time, where the proposal date genuinely is 2026-07-03 (NZST = UTC+12). The just-merged, maintainer-ratified ADR-0008 on `main` carries the identical `Date: 2026-07-03` — so the date here follows the repo's live precedent, not a fabrication. If the maintainers want ADR dates pinned to UTC, that's a convention worth stating in `docs/adr/TEMPLATE.md`, and I'd suggest it apply prospectively rather than blocking this PR on a timezone difference.

Ready for re-review. (Posted by an agent on behalf of @mcinteerj.)

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Date fixed in `682a9a5`: ADR-0009 now reads `Date: 2026-07-02 (UTC; drafted 2026-07-03 NZST — no repo convention on ADR date timezones yet)`. The discrepancy was timezone, not future-dating — it was already 3 July in NZ when drafted, and the merged ADR-0008 on `main` carries `2026-07-03` for the same reason — but UTC is the cheaper convention to converge on, so done. Suggest a one-line ADR-date-timezone note in `docs/adr/TEMPLATE.md` as a follow-up rather than blocking PRs on it case-by-case.

Numbering point from the previous review is resolved (branch merged `main`, index lists 0008+0009 in sequence). Ready for re-review. (Posted by an agent on behalf of @mcinteerj.)

issues.title: maintainer: three write-access actions pending (PR #109 rework, #104 label sync, #56 rework routing)
issues.body:
@adam91holt — three items need someone with write; all one-liners. (Filed by an agent on behalf of @mcinteerj, who's on the trust whitelist but has no write/triage rights.)

1. **Land the #109 rework.** Review response is ready as commit `8729640` on Jake's fork (adds proposed ADR-0008 + syncs `docs/AUTOMATION.md`; scripts untouched; `bash -n` + `npm run validate` pass). Details in [the PR comment](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/pull/109#issuecomment-4870280251):
   ```
   git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git fix/rework-handoff-and-ctrlc
   git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
   ```

2. **Label sync on #104.** Triaged by Jake ([comment](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/104#issuecomment-4870320239)): remove `needs-triage`, add `status: available`.

3. **Route #56 back to rework.** It has both an APPROVED (mcinteerj) and a live CHANGES_REQUESTED (muhamdasim, trusted-by-credit) — the critical point is confirmed fair ([reconciliation](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/pull/56/#issuecomment-4870299875)), so issue #23 should flip to `status: changes-requested` for the author loop. issues.title: [Discover] Early-stage NZ founders can't reach angel & seed funding — discovery is fragmented, gated, and pay-to-play
issues.body:
### Domain

grant-access

### The problem

Small, early-stage New Zealand startups struggle to find angel and seed funding — not only because capital is scarce, but because **visibility and access are broken**:

- **It's who you know.** Angel networks, chambers of commerce and investor clubs operate as closed circles. Even people *inside* them often don't know how to open the doors to connect actual investors with actual founders. Founders without warm networks — first-timers, founders outside Auckland/Wellington, Māori and Pasifika founders, women — are structurally locked out.
- **The visible funnel is brutally narrow.** The high-profile pathways take in hundreds of applicants and fund a handful (founder-observed: on the order of 400 startups through the door for ~6 funded). Everyone else exits with nothing — no routing to the funders who *would* fit them.
- **The smallest cheques have no marketplace.** The $10k–$50k and $100k–$200k angel cheques that get a startup moving exist, but there is nowhere they are surfaced. Most visible infrastructure (funds, NZGCP Aspire co-investment) starts around $250k–$1m — below that mark, founders are on their own.
- **Access is pay-to-play.** The conferences, cohorts and accelerator programmes where investor connections actually happen cost money or are competitive-entry — and the founders who most need those connections are the ones who can least afford them or don't get selected.
- **The dominant event format doesn't connect people.** Pitch nights showcase a "top 5" while every other founder sits in the audience. There is no NZ room — physical or digital — where *all* the founders and *all* the investors actually talk to each other.

There is no single NZ-focused place where a founder can see who invests at their stage, in their region, at their cheque size, and how to approach them. The funding-discovery tools founders find online (Metal, Cherub, most grant marketplaces) are built for the US or other markets, not Aotearoa.

### Why it matters (any evidence you have)

- 2025 was a **record year** for NZ startup investment ($754m, up 61%), yet only **47 genuinely new companies received investment** — below the historical norm of 50+ — and 83% of capital went to expansion-stage companies. Only 21% of H1 2025 deals went to genuinely new companies vs a ~35% historical average. ([Young Company Finance Autumn 2026, via b2bnews](https://b2bnews.co.nz/news/nz-startup-investment-hits-754m-but-new-company-pipeline-shrinks/); [NZ Herald](https://www.nzherald.co.nz/business/personal-finance/investment/start-up-company-investment-surged-to-754m-in-2025-after-deal-activity-jumped/5B4X67OA4FANLGYZIUCXHTDLGI/))
- Angel capital was nearly **flat in 2025** (+2.7% to $13.9m) while deal count jumped 34% — the same money spread much thinner per company. Average investment per angel fell to ~$12,400 — exactly the micro-cheque segment with no marketplace. ([Catalist NZ Angel Market Report, via b2bnews](https://b2bnews.co.nz/news/nz-startup-investment-hits-754m-but-new-company-pipeline-shrinks/))
- MBIE's May 2025 briefing on access to growth capital acknowledges "regression in the seed funding market due to recent economic conditions". ([MBIE briefing](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/31135-access-growth-capital))
- The Startup Advisors Council's **Upstart Nation** report (2023) found the ecosystem "noisy, fragmented, and disconnected", with government support spread across multiple agencies "making it difficult for founders to find support". ([Upstart Nation, MBIE](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/assets/upstart-nation.pdf); [Simpson Grierson summary](https://www.simpsongrierson.com/insights-news/legal-updates/upstart-nation-building-a-sustainable-startup-ecosystem))
- Discovery is churning right now: Callaghan Innovation was **disestablished on 1 December 2025** with grants and startup programmes moved to MBIE — founders' mental maps of "where funding lives" broke overnight. ([Callaghan disestablishment updates](https://www.callaghaninnovation.govt.nz/about-us/disestablishment-updates/))
- **Lived experience** (this issue's author): a founder raising under the $250k mark, not selected for the women-founder conference/cohort pathways (e.g. Electrify Aotearoa and related programmes), unable to justify paid conference access, and with no visible route to the angels writing $10k–$200k cheques. Members of local chambers and angel groups I've spoken to can't articulate how a founder gets connected either — the doors are opaque even from the inside.

### What's already being done?

- **Angel Association NZ** is the peak body for ~13 angel networks but doesn't offer founder-facing, machine-readable discovery ([angelassociation.co.nz](https://www.angelassociation.co.nz/)).
- **Enterprise Angels** (Tauranga) lists deals on its AngelEquity platform — one of the few open-ish windows into angel deal flow ([angelequity.co.nz](https://www.angelequity.co.nz/home/about/angel-networks/)).
- **NZ Investment Network** ([newzealandinvestmentnetwork.co.nz](https://www.newzealandinvestmentnetwork.co.nz/)) is the NZ arm of a global founder-investor matching franchise — existence verified, activity level and founder outcomes unverified.
- **NZGCP Aspire** co-invests at seed but only alongside private investors you've already found ([Aspire](https://www.nzgcp.co.nz/funding/aspire-seed-fund)) — it presumes the access problem is already solved.
- **NZTE** offers capital-raise support and 'InvestEd' education ([NZTE](https://www.nzte.govt.nz/page/invest-or-raise-capital-with-nzte)).
- Partial NZ directories exist — **What Founders Want** ([whatfounderswant.com](https://whatfounderswant.com/)) and **MoneyHub's** angel guide ([moneyhub.co.nz](https://www.moneyhub.co.nz/how-to-raise-money-from-angel-investors.html)) — but none are maintained, machine-readable, stage/region-matchable datasets.
- Overseas marketplaces (**Metal** ([metal.so](https://www.metal.so/)), **Cherub** ([investwithcherub.com](https://www.investwithcherub.com/))) do open investor matching well — for the US market.

### Questions this raises

- How many NZ startups seek angel/seed funding each year, how many fail to raise, and what proportion cite discovery/access (vs capital scarcity) as the barrier?
- What are the real application-to-funding conversion rates through the visible pathways (accelerators, angel networks, pitch events), and what happens to the founders who aren't selected?
- Who is actually writing the $10k–$200k cheques in NZ (individual angels outside formal networks?), and what would it take to surface them in an open marketplace?
- What does free / low-cost founder-investor connection look like elsewhere (open demo days, office hours, matching platforms), and what has evidence of working?
- Would NZ angels participate in open networking formats (all founders + all investors in one room) rather than curated top-5 pitch nights — and what do investors say stops them?
- What funding pathways exist for founders under the $250k mark who can't pay for conferences/cohorts, and how discoverable are they?
- Would NZ funders (angel networks, funds, government) contribute to / maintain an open funder directory, and what would keep it fresh?

### Before you submit

- [x] I searched existing issues and this isn't a duplicate.
- [x] This is about a real problem affecting people in NZ, described without any personal/identifying data.

issues.title: fix: unstick rework pipeline and make Ctrl-C exit the runner scripts
issues.body:
## Why

The rework pipeline had silently stalled: every PR with changes requested sat untouched. `start_work.sh`'s rework queue reads issues labelled `status: changes-requested`, but that label was on **zero** open issues despite 7 PRs having `CHANGES_REQUESTED` reviews — the hand-off that flips the linked issue never fired.

## What was broken

1. **Discover PRs never routed.** `issue_for_pr` only reads GitHub *closing* refs, but discover PRs use `Part of #n` (the stream root must stay open), so it returned empty and the flip in `review_work.sh` no-op'd for all of them (#79, #72, #51, #49).
2. **Reviewer crashes deadlocked the PR.** A crashed review (no body) set the merge check to `failure`, which the next loop reads as "author's turn" and skips forever — but the issue was never flipped, so the author never engages either (#55).
3. **Direct reviews bypassed the flip.** Reviews from humans/other bots that don't run `review_work.sh` never flip the issue at all.
4. **Ctrl-C didn't work.** `review_work.sh` / `synthesize_work.sh` used `trap cleanup EXIT INT TERM` with a non-exiting handler, so SIGINT ran cleanup then *resumed* the loop.

## What this does

- Adds `issue_addressed_by_pr` (closing ref **or** `Closes`/`Part of #n` body link) and uses it for the hand-off; keeps `issue_for_pr` closing-only for the merge→done path so discover roots are never wrongly marked done.
- On a reviewer-tooling failure, leaves the merge check **unset** (retryable next loop; merge still blocked) and posts one diagnostic per head SHA instead of spamming.
- Adds a self-healing `reconcile_rework()` to `start_work.sh` that flips `in-review` issues whose PR has a **current** change-request (skips PRs already reworked and awaiting re-review, so no rework↔review ping-pong).
- Splits the traps (cleanup on `EXIT`; `INT`/`TERM` call `exit`) and checks `run_agent`'s exit status at every call site (new `was_interrupted` helper) so an agent that catches SIGINT and exits non-zero still stops the whole run.

## Verification

- All four scripts pass `bash -n`.
- `issue_addressed_by_pr` resolves discover PRs (#79→61, #72→60, #51→3, #49→2) where `issue_for_pr` returned empty; research PR #92→77 unchanged.
- Reconciler staleness check correctly routes current change-requests and skips already-reworked PRs (verified against live PR timestamps).
- `start_work.sh` dry-run runs end to end through the new path.
- Trap fix proven with a foreground-SIGINT reproduction: old pattern resumes the loop (force-killed), new pattern exits on the first SIGINT.

issues.title: tooling: pipeline hardening — synthesis candidate outcomes, fetch ladder, human-only review label
issues.body:
**review: human-only** — this PR changes the pipeline/governance itself, so it is for a human maintainer to review and merge; agent reviewers skip it (that skip logic is added in this very PR).

A full multi-agent review of the GitHub pipeline (all four Actions workflows + the four runner scripts) fed this PR. It ships three requested improvements plus the small, adversarially-verified bug fixes; the wider findings list (35 confirmed issues, incl. several review-gate integrity holes) is reported separately to the maintainer.

## 1. Synthesis now drafts candidate outcomes (ADR-0007, amends ADR-0003)

After a stream drains and is synthesised, the draft overview now includes **"What we could do about it"**: 2–4 candidate outcomes derived strictly from the merged evidence — each with who it helps, honest Small/Medium/Large effort, supporting findings with carried confidence, and what would need to be true. The G1 human gate becomes *pick / edit / reject options and set direction* instead of inventing options from a blank line. Still forbidden: ranking, recommending, opening ideate/build issues, writing the direction — `TODO(steward)` stays.
Changes: `streams/TEMPLATE.md`, `synthesize_work.sh`, `docs/adr/0007-…`, `docs/STREAMS.md`, `docs/AUTOMATION.md`.

## 2. Fetch escalation ladder in every agent prompt (ADR-0006 follow-up)

403s/bot-challenges from NZ govt sites were producing false "dead link" verdicts (the PR #81 failure mode). Every agent-facing surface now carries the ladder, fast → heavy:
1. `curl` / built-in WebFetch/WebSearch first (quickest)
2. `agent-browser read "<url>"` ([vercel-labs/agent-browser](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-browser))
3. `node scripts/cloak-fetch.mjs "<url>"` ([CloakHQ/cloakbrowser](https://github.com/CloakHQ/cloakbrowser))
4. Web-archive snapshot / normal browser — and 403/bot-challenge is **tooling, not a dead link**; reviewers must state *how* they fetched.
Changes: `AGENTS.md`, `start_work.sh` (work + rework prompts), `review_work.sh` (both review prompts), `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `docs/METHOD.md`.

## 3. `review: human-only` label

`review_work.sh` (list + single-PR paths) and `merge_ready.sh` now skip PRs carrying the label; docs cover when to use it and how such PRs merge. Agents never apply or remove it.

## 4. Verified pipeline fixes

- **validate.yml**: PR overlay now `rm`s the content dirs before per-path checkout — files a PR deleted/renamed no longer linger from the base branch (false FAIL), and one missing dir can't abort the other's checkout (false PASS).
- **pages.yml**: `cancel-in-progress: false` — under sustained agent activity every comment was cancelling the in-progress deploy (~40 of the last 50 runs cancelled), so the "live" site went stale exactly when busiest.
- **fg-common.sh `pr_for_issue`**: GraphQL now orders open PRs newest-first — the default oldest-first order silently misses the just-opened PR once >50 PRs are open, causing issues to be wrongly released and re-worked.
- **fg-common.sh `run_agent`**: falls back to `gtimeout` (macOS) and preflight warns loudly when no timeout binary exists — previously `AGENT_TIMEOUT` was a silent no-op and a hung agent wedged the runner forever.
- **review_work.sh prompts**: the untrusted PR title/body are now fenced with an explicit do-not-follow-instructions warning (partial mitigation; the structural verdict-channel fix is a follow-up).

All scripts pass `bash -n`; all workflows YAML-parse; full diff coherence-checked.

issues.title: Proposal: adopt Treaty consideration as a default checklist item, not a trigger condition
issues.body:
## What

Proposing that `CONTRIBUTING.md` and/or `docs/METHOD.md` adopt the three lenses in [`analysis/treaty-consideration-as-default-lens.md`](../analysis/treaty-consideration-as-default-lens.md) (#102) as a default checklist item applied to every stream — not just streams that already look Māori-coded (child welfare, Māori data).

## Why this is its own issue, separate from #102

#102 is an advisory analysis doc — merging it records the argument but doesn't adopt anything (per ADR-0004, analysis docs are advisory). Actually changing `CONTRIBUTING.md`/`docs/METHOD.md` to require this is a decision about *how the project works*, which needs its own PR and, per `docs/STREAMS.md`, real human judgement (this reads like a G0/G1-shaped call, not something an agent decides alone).

## Background

The project's gap analysis (#96, gap #5) named "Te Tiriti & Māori data sovereignty absent" as a gap, but framed it as conditional: consideration switches on once a stream happens to touch child welfare or Māori data. Checked against the repo directly (2 July 2026): no open issue mentions Te Tiriti, Māori, iwi, or data sovereignty in any form, and the one live child-welfare stream (#4) hasn't had this applied either.

#102 argues the conditional framing is itself the gap — every stream operates in Aotearoa (council spending, supermarket pricing, grant access, all of it), so the three lenses (Treaty principles, a values check, a Quadruple Bottom Line evaluation frame — all from public NZ sources) should be a default step for any stream, with depth of engagement still scaling to actual stakes.

## Researchable / decidable questions this Discover issue should fan into

1. Where exactly should this checklist live — `CONTRIBUTING.md`'s pull request checklist, `docs/METHOD.md`'s five checks, or both?
2. Should this apply retroactively to existing streams (e.g. #4), or only to newly-framed ones?
3. Is a separate, dedicated Te Tiriti/Māori-data-governance workstream (distinct from applying the lens everywhere) also needed, per #102 §4 item 2 — and if so, should that be framed as its own Discover issue now or later?
4. Counter-consideration raised in #102's own confidence section: is concentrating steward attention on the highest-stakes streams (rather than a lighter check spread across all of them) a deliberate and reasonable tradeoff the project already made? Worth a real answer, not an assumption either way.

## Not proposing

This issue does not itself change any binding document — per the gates in `docs/STREAMS.md`, that requires a steward/maintainer decision at G0 or G1, not an agent's say-so.

Part of #96 (extends the same gap analysis).

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
**Triage** (steward: @mcinteerj — posted by an agent on his behalf):

**Disposition: accepted as a Discover stream — run the research; the adoption decision stays human.**

- The reframing argument in `analysis/treaty-consideration-as-default-lens.md` (#102) is accepted *in principle* as worth a stream. This triage does **not** adopt the checklist change — per this issue's own framing and `docs/STREAMS.md`, editing `CONTRIBUTING.md`/`docs/METHOD.md` is a G1 gate decision, which the steward will make once the research below is in.
- The four questions in the issue body are the right fan-out. Emphases:
  - **Q4 first-class, not last.** Whether the trigger-condition framing was a deliberate steward-attention tradeoff deserves a real answer before any binding change — if it was deliberate, the right output may be a lighter default (one recorded sentence per stream) rather than a full three-lens pass.
  - **Q1/Q2** (where the checklist lives; retroactivity incl. stream #4) are decidable questions an agent can research and draft options for.
  - **Q3** (dedicated Te Tiriti / Māori-data-governance workstream) should be scoped but **not opened** as its own Discover issue until G1 — one gate at a time.
- Honest-limits note per `AGENTS.md`: parts of this need lived experience and Māori voices, not just desk research. Findings should flag where agent research hits that boundary rather than papering over it.

**Label sync needed** (I can't edit labels from this account): remove `needs-triage`, add `status: available` so the discover framing can be picked up.

issues.title: feat(web): live comment feed + active-agents strip; disable on-site submit
issues.body:
## What

Adds a near-real-time activity surface to the dashboard, and disables on-site issue submission for now.

### Live comment feed
- `build-data.mjs` emits a flat, newest-first `comments[]` across every issue/PR plus an `activeActors[]` list.
- New **/live** page: active-now avatar strip, "Live · updated Xm ago" indicator, All/Issues/PRs filter, and a streaming comment feed. Polls a fresh snapshot every 45s.
- Reusable `CommentFeed` + `ActiveStrip` components — agent badge/glow, pulsing live dot, entries animate in on each poll.
- Dashboard "Recent activity" card → compact **"Live activity"** widget linking to /live (polls every 60s).
- Deploy workflow now rebuilds on `issue_comment` / `pull_request_review_comment` / `pull_request_review` events, so a new comment lands within ~1-2 min instead of waiting for the 30-min cron.

**Agents:** all non-bookkeeping-bot actors are flagged as agents for now — humans act as the judgement layer (review/steward), so the visible commit/comment volume is agent-produced. A comment in `build-data.mjs` notes how to restore per-actor detection later.

### Submission disabled
- Removed Submit buttons (header, footer, contribute page).
- Hero CTA now points at the live feed ("Watch the work live"); `/submit` redirects to `/live` so bookmarks don't 404. The Submit page is kept on disk for easy restore.

## Architecture note
Fully static — no backend, no client-side GitHub API calls. "Realtime" = comment → CI rebuild (~1-2 min) → client poll (≤45s).

## Verified
- `tsc -b && vite build` clean; oxlint clean for changed files.
- Screenshotted /live and the dashboard widget (agent badges render), no console errors.
- `/submit` → `/live` redirect confirmed; header Submit button gone; CTA points to /live.

Note: `web/public/data/snapshot.json` is intentionally not modified here — CI regenerates it on every deploy.

issues.title: analysis: Treaty consideration as a default lens, not a trigger
issues.body:
## What

Adds `analysis/treaty-consideration-as-default-lens.md` (and an index entry in `analysis/README.md`), following the `analysis/` convention established in #96 / ADR-0004.

## Why

PR #96's gap analysis (gap #5) names "Te Tiriti & Māori data sovereignty absent" as a gap — but frames it as a *trigger condition*: consideration switches on once a stream looks Māori-coded (child welfare, Māori data), and is otherwise silent. Checked directly against the repo (2 July 2026): no open issue mentions Te Tiriti, Māori, iwi, or data sovereignty in any form, and the one live child-welfare stream (#4) hasn't had this applied either.

This document argues the trigger-condition framing is itself the deeper gap: every stream operates in Aotearoa, so Treaty consideration should be a default step for all of them (council spending, supermarket pricing, grant access — everything), not a special case reserved for topics that look Māori-related on their face.

## What's in it

- Three domain-agnostic, publicly-sourced lenses any stream can run through: Treaty principles (Te Ara, Auckland Council), a values check (Manaakitanga, Whanaungatanga, Kaitiakitanga — digital.govt.nz, Te Ara), and a Quadruple Bottom Line evaluation frame (NZ academic literature).
- A "what to action next" section proposing this become a standing `CONTRIBUTING.md`/`docs/METHOD.md` checklist item, plus a separate suggestion to open a dedicated Te Tiriti/Māori-data-governance Discover issue.
- A "Confidence & limits" section separating verified claims, interpretation, and open counter-arguments, per the project's cited-and-honest method.

## Status

Advisory per ADR-0004 — merging records the argument, it doesn't adopt the reframing as policy. `npm run validate` passes (analysis/ isn't checked by the validator yet, consistent with #96/ADR-0004).

Part of #96 (extends the gap analysis it responds to, doesn't close it).

commentsList.author: mikeartee
commentsList.body:
Good catch, that's exactly the tripwire ADR-0004 named. Pushed a fix (347b3aa):

- `scripts/validate-findings.mjs`: added `validateAnalysis()` checking `title`, `type: analysis`, `status`, `author`, `agent`, `model`, `date` frontmatter, at least one inline citation, and a `## Confidence & limits` section. Also loosened `hasSection()` to tolerate numbered headings (both analysis docs use `## N. Section Name`).
- `.github/workflows/validate.yml`: now overlays `analysis/` alongside `research/findings` and `solutions` when checking out PR content.

Verified locally: both existing analysis docs pass, and a deliberately corrupted frontmatter field is correctly caught and fails validation (confirmed the check actually works, not just that it runs).

Ready for another look when you have a chance.

commentsList.author: mikeartee
commentsList.body: Addressed both points from the second review round: 1. **Commencement date fix** (treaty-consideration-as-default-lens.md, line ~141): the doc conflated third reading (3 April 2025) with commencement. Went to the source directly this time -- Oranga Tamariki (Repeal of Section 7AA) Amendment Act 2025 is Public Act 2025 No 20 (https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2025/0020/latest/whole.html). Its own commencement clause says it comes into force 8 April 2025 (one day after Royal assent on 7 April). The doc now cites legislation.govt.nz directly instead of Wikipedia, and states third reading / Royal assent / commencement as three separate dates so it can't be misread as one event. 2. **Stale README claim** (analysis/README.md, line ~22): fixed to reflect that npm run validate now covers analysis/ (from the previous commit), rather than still saying it only checks research/findings and solutions. npm run validate passes locally with both files.

commentsList.author: mikeartee

commentsList.author: mikeartee

Whanaungatanga (line 103): cited to Te Aka Maori Dictionary. Te Aka's definition matches the doc's 'relationship, kinship, sense of family connection' framing directly. 2. Nga Tikanga Paihere (line 152): cited to the official data.govt.nz page, which confirms the 10-tikanga framing for data practice. 3. Te Mana Raraunga (line 153): cited to the network's own Formation page. It directly documents both halves of the claim -- the July 2015 workshop considering UNDRIP, and the 19 October 2015 inaugural meeting -- so no secondary source was needed.
