issues.title: docs(adr): accept ADR-0005 & 0006 (cleaned) + Decisions page on the site
issues.body:
Per Adam: **merge ADR-0005 & 0006, cleaned up, and put the ADRs on the public site.**

## ADRs
- **ADR-0005** (agent execution environment — containerise + sandboxed auto modes) and **ADR-0006** (fetch / proxy / browser management) tidied and marked **`accepted`** by maintainer decision (Adam). Index updated.
- This **consolidates and supersedes PRs #99 and #100** (avoids the index-conflict of merging both separately). Closing those in favour of this one.

## Website
- New **Decisions** page at `/decisions` (in the Community nav) that renders every ADR — number, status pill, date, and full text — straight from `docs/adr/`.
- `build-data.mjs` now emits an `adrs` array parsed from `docs/adr/*.md`; it populates on each deploy (the Pages workflow runs build-data before the build), so the page fills in automatically as ADRs are added.

## Verified
- `tsc + vite build` clean; `node --check` on build-data; ADR field-parsing spot-checked against real files.

Admin-merging on Adam's say-so (he asked to merge these tonight).

issues.title: docs(adr): ADR-0006 — fetch, proxy & browser management (proposed)
issues.body:
Per Adam ("create an ADR for proxy / browser management — I have good ideas for this, but I need some sleep"). Framed as a **proposal** so you can drop your ideas straight into the Open Questions when you're rested — merging only records it.

## Why
Motivating incident: **PR #81**. The finding was sound (DIA figures later reproduced to the dollar) but got **4× NEEDS_WORK** largely because reviewers' `curl` hit **404/403** on bot-protected govt sites and JS/CDN pages — false "dead link" rejections. A browser-rendering reviewer confirmed the links resolved and it merged on the same commit. Weak fetch tooling → false rejections (and the mirror risk: a reviewer who can't fetch waves through a genuinely dead link).

## Proposal
1. A **shared fetch layer** that prefers **real browser rendering** over plain `curl` (JS, redirects, WAF challenges, PDFs).
2. **Classify failures:** 404 = real dead link; **403 / challenge / timeout = likely tooling/IP**, retry via browser/archive before flagging a citation.
3. **Archive-on-cite** for fragile/date-stamped URLs; prefer **official APIs** over scraping.
4. **Proxy/egress management** framework (residential where datacentre IPs are blocked) — exact mechanism left **open for Adam's design input**.

Related to **ADR-0005** (execution env / container); this is the fetch layer that lives inside it.

## Reviewer note
Governance ADR → wants a **human maintainer decision**, not an agent approval. `Status: proposed`. Index note flags that ADR-0005 is in a separate open PR (minor index reconcile when both merge).

commentsList.author: thecolab-clawd
commentsList.body: Superseded by #101, which merges ADR-0006 as accepted (cleaned up) alongside ADR-0005 and the site Decisions page.

issues.title: docs(adr): ADR-0005 — agent execution environment & permission modes (proposed)
issues.body:
Per Adam's steer ("I don't like dangerously skip perms, we should be running on auto mode… we could try to containerise it"). I spun up two research agents to pin the current facts, then wrote this up as a **proposed** ADR for a maintainer to accept/reject — it does **not** enact anything on merge.

## The gist
The runner currently uses blanket bypass flags on the host (`bypassPermissions` / `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox`). Proposal:

1. **Container = the sandbox.** A hardened image (non-root inside, no host mounts, dropped caps, seccomp, resource limits) makes blanket bypass *acceptable* — the blast radius is the throwaway container, not the host. Also fixes the `bypassPermissions`-refuses-root friction.
2. **On the host, default to sandboxed *auto* not bypass:** Codex `--sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval never` (real auto + sandbox, no root issue); Claude `--permission-mode acceptEdits` (bypass only inside the container). Both stay env-overridable.
3. **Where runs live:** default = container on a contributor's **residential** machine — keeps IPs residential *and* tokens distributed. Cloud/CI is fine for *review* but risky for *research* (datacentre-IP blocks on NZ gov/Stats sites) and re-centralises token cost. Prefer official NZ APIs over scraping.
4. **Creds:** scoped GitHub App / fine-grained token, least privilege, never baked into the image.

Research sources are cited inline in the ADR (Claude Code permission modes/headless; Codex sandboxing; Docker AI governance; GitHub fine-grained PATs).

## Note for the reviewer
This is an **ADR / governance change** — per the new review classification it wants a **human maintainer decision**, not an agent approval. It's framed as `Status: proposed` and merging only records the proposal. Follow-ups (write the Dockerfile, microVM-vs-Docker for untrusted PRs, optional hosted review runner) are listed, not decided.

Happy to implement the quick win immediately on request — flipping the **Codex default** to `--sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval never` is a safe, isolated change that gives "auto mode" now.

commentsList.author: thecolab-clawd
commentsList.body: Superseded by #101, which merges ADR-0005 as accepted (cleaned up) alongside ADR-0006 and the site Decisions page.

issues.title: fix(scripts): default agents to sandboxed auto mode (not permission bypass)
issues.body:
Per Adam — we shouldn't be defaulting to `--dangerously-*` / `bypassPermissions`. Switch the runner defaults to **sandboxed auto modes**:

| agent | was | now |
|---|---|---|
| codex | `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` | **`--full-auto`** (workspace-write sandbox, auto-approve, no prompts) |
| claude | `--permission-mode bypassPermissions` | **`--permission-mode acceptEdits`** |
| hermes | `--yolo` (unchanged; override via `HERMES_FLAGS`) | — |

Bonus: `acceptEdits` **runs as root**, unlike `bypassPermissions`/`--dangerously-skip-permissions` — so it also fixes the “can't run as root” wall a couple of people hit.

Still fully overridable per-run via `CODEX_FLAGS` / `CLAUDE_PERMISSION_MODE` / `HERMES_FLAGS`.

## Please smoke-test before the whole fleet adopts
I can't exercise the live CLIs from here. The one thing to confirm: under claude `acceptEdits` (headless `-p`), the reviewer can still run its **read tools** (git diff, file reads, WebFetch to check citations) and write `.fg-review.md` without stalling. Run `MAX=1 PR=<n> ./review_work.sh claude` once and check it posts a real review.

**Fallback if a claude run stalls on tool prompts:** set `CLAUDE_PERMISSION_MODE=bypassPermissions` (non-root only), or we add `--allowedTools` in a follow-up. codex `--full-auto` is a well-established safe auto mode and should just work.

`bash -n` clean.

issues.title: feat(review): classify PRs (method vs standard) + claim-based selection
issues.body:
Addresses two things from the For Good chat (Adam).

## 1. Distinguish pipeline work from standard changes
The reviewer treated *every* PR as a research finding — so a working doc (e.g. the gap analysis #96) or a bash script got held to "cite every claim, two independent sources." That's a category error and it's why #96 ping-ponged.

Now `review_work.sh` classifies each PR by the paths it changes:
- **`research/findings/**` or `solutions/**` → the full adversarial research method** (unchanged).
- **everything else (docs, tooling, site, analysis, `.github`) → a standard maintainer review**: correctness, convention-fit, honest framing (proposals marked as proposals, not “adopted”), safety. **No citation gate.**
- **Governance guard:** structural changes (ADR status, CONTRIBUTING, gates, review/merge rules) are flagged as needing a **human maintainer decision**, not an agent approval — an agent shouldn't self-ratify how the project works.

## 2. Claim-based selection (the race + starvation)
Runners listed open PRs in the same order and all started at the front — so one PR got 5 reviews while ~20 got none.
- Reviewers **claim** a PR via a `status: reviewing` label before working it; others skip a freshly-claimed PR.
- Stale claims (> `REVIEW_CLAIM_TTL`, default 1800s) are taken over (crashed runner).
- Candidate PRs are **shuffled** so runners spread across the queue.
- Claim released on every exit path + on interrupt.

## Verified
- `bash -n` clean on both scripts.
- Classifier + shuffle logic unit-sanity-tested.
- New `status: reviewing` label added to `labels.yml` (and created on the repo).

Note: small residual claim race remains (two runners reading no-label at the exact same instant), but combined with the shuffle it removes the front-of-list stampede that was the real problem.

issues.title: docs: add /analysis folder + audience-by-extension convention
issues.body:
## What

Adds a project-level `analysis/` folder for gap analyses and operating plans — analysis *of the project itself*, distinct from `research/` (cited findings about NZ's problems). Seeds it with the **July 2026 Gap Analysis & Operating Plan** in two formats.

## Files

- `analysis/gap-analysis-and-operating-plan.md` — canonical, LLM-readable source
- `analysis/gap-analysis-and-operating-plan.pdf` — human-readable rendered companion
- `analysis/README.md` — folder purpose + the convention
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` — new "Where things go" row + a subsection documenting the convention

## The convention: audience is signalled by extension

Paired documents share **one kebab-case slug**; the audience is implied by the extension, with no `-human`/`-llm` suffix:

- **`.md` is the canonical source of truth** — edit, review, diff, and cite it.
- **`.pdf` is a read-only rendered companion**, generated from the `.md`. Never hand-edited.
- If the two disagree, **the Markdown wins** — regenerate the export.

This keeps the project's one-source-of-truth principle (`MANIFESTO.md`) intact: Markdown is the database, everything else is a view of it.

## Notes

- `scripts/validate-findings.mjs` only walks `research/findings` and `solutions`, so `analysis/` files need no frontmatter — `npm run validate` passes unchanged.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

issues.title: research: quantify take-up gap for NZ child/family entitlements
issues.body:
Closes #93. Part of #4. Stream: #4.

**Stage:** Research (child-welfare, stream #4).

## What this answers
How many *eligible* NZ families actually receive the main child/family entitlements, and where the take-up gap sits — quantifying the avoidable harm this stream is about, complementing #24 (what exists / eligibility) and #25 (where families go / where it breaks).

## Headline
- WFF take-up ~**87%** overall (2020 tax year), stable 83–90% since 2007 — but **~100% for beneficiary families vs 75–85% for working (non-beneficiary) families**. The gap is a *working-family* problem.
- Accommodation Supplement (MSD Income Support Survey) shows the same shape via an **awareness gap**: only 81% of potentially-eligible people had heard of it, and understanding that non-beneficiary workers qualify was far lower (~37% low-income, 20.3% mid, 5.7% high) than for beneficiaries.
- Best Start (2018) lifted WFF take-up for families with very young children ~**5pp** — proactive/streamlined enrolment closes part of the gap.

## Method / honesty
- Existing official NZ estimates only (MSD, IRD, SIA Hub) — no new data, no personal data.
- Confidence **Medium**: the *pattern* (gap concentrated among working families) is corroborated by two independent MSD datasets; the specific 87% rests on one MSD study and stops at the 2020 tax year — flagged explicitly.
- 'What would change this' calls out the need for a post-2020 estimate, a real AS take-up rate, and a **frontline/lived-experience human** to confirm the mechanism (awareness vs complexity vs distrust) before anything is designed.

## Checklist
- [x] Linked issue (Closes #93) + parent (Part of #4)
- [x] Used research/TEMPLATE.md, filed under research/findings/child-welfare/
- [x] Every claim sourced; load-bearing pattern claim has two independent sources
- [x] Confidence marked; single-source figure flagged
- [x] Stated what would change the conclusion / what's unverified
- [x] No personal data
- [x] Passes `npm run validate`

commentsList.author: muhamdasim
commentsList.body:
Thanks for the adversarial review — every point checked out against the source (I re-fetched Findings Pack 7 and verified pages 9–22). Pushed 8c385b1 fixing all four required items:

1. **Misread/inverted understanding figures (MAJOR)** — removed. The old sentence conflated p.11's "who can receive" showcard results: 79.8% understood low-income non-beneficiaries qualify (I'd cited ~37%), 37.0%/42.7%/20.3% was the yes/no/don't-know split for *middle*-income, and 5.7% was "said yes for high-income" where "no" is the correct answer. Also folded in p.12 (understanding high and consistent at 67–91%, no significant beneficiary vs non-beneficiary difference). The finding no longer makes the "non-beneficiaries understand far less" claim.

2. **Omitted take-up figure (MAJOR)** — fixed. Now reports **43.9% self-reported receipt** among the potentially eligible (p.14) with self-report/apparent-eligibility caveats, and deleted the claims that no AS take-up rate was found.

3. **Awareness overstated as the mechanism (MODERATE)** — reconciled with p.17: lack of awareness is only ~⅓ (33.6%) of non-take-up. Added the mistaken-ineligibility driver — 35.9% of aware non-recipients thought they were ineligible (p.20), 60.7% because they believed they earned too much despite measured income below threshold (p.22).

4. **Load-bearing Source 2 (MODERATE)** — the claims table now cites **p.15** (respondents not on a main benefit statistically significantly less likely to self-report receiving AS), which genuinely corroborates the working-family pattern.

Minor items: removed the standalone "85.3% aware" error, added fieldwork dates (Jun–Dec 2022, 1,852 respondents), softened the Best Start causal claim to match the source's "it appears that…", and flagged Source 3 (IRD) as background-only. `npm run validate` passes.

commentsList.author: muhamdasim
commentsList.body:
Thanks for the thorough adversarial reviews — they were right on the Accommodation Supplement section. Pushed fixes (commits `8c385b1` + `f36f7da`); the WFF half, which both reviews confirmed verified verbatim, is unchanged. Point by point:

1. **Misread/inverted understanding figures (MAJOR)** — Removed the incorrect `37% / 20.3% / 5.7%` framing entirely. The finding now states understanding that a non-beneficiary can receive the payment was **high and consistent across sub-groups (67–91%), with no statistically significant beneficiary/non-beneficiary difference** (p.11–12), which is what the source actually says.
2. **Omitted 43.9% take-up figure (MAJOR)** — Now the headline of the AS section: **43.9% self-reported receipt among the potentially eligible** (56.1% not), in both the executive answer and evidence body. Removed the incorrect "no take-up rate found" claim.
3. **"Awareness" overstated as the mechanism (MODERATE)** — Now quantified per the source: **~⅓ unaware (33.6%)**, **~⅓ aware but wrongly believe themselves ineligible (35.9% of aware non-recipients, most citing income)**, remainder aware-but-not-applying. Reframed from "awareness gap" to a mix of awareness + mistaken self-assessed ineligibility.
4. **Wrong Source-2 evidence (MODERATE)** — The load-bearing claims table now cites the correct **p.15** statistic (non-beneficiaries statistically significantly *less* likely to self-report receiving AS), not the misread p.11 understanding figures.
5. **Minor** — Removed the erroneous "(85.3% aware)"; added the June–December 2022 fieldwork date to Source 2; labelled the IRD source as background/not cited for a figure; and restored the source's own "it appears that…" hedge on the Best Start ~5pp claim. Also removed two stray `</content>`/`</invoke>` artifact lines at the end of the file (`f36f7da`). `npm run validate` passes.

The overall conclusion is unchanged and now rests on correctly-read evidence. Ready for re-review when you have a moment — @mcinteerj @adam91holt.

commentsList.author: muhamdasim
commentsList.body:
Re-review addressed in `52a4d25` — thanks @adam91holt, these were fair; all five are about not letting the AS evidence carry more than it can. Fixes:

1. **AS not child-specific / independence (load-bearing)** — Reframed the Accommodation Supplement throughout as a **broader-population (working-age 18–64, low-to-mid income, *not* only families with children) *supporting analogue***, not independent corroboration of the child/family pattern. Load-bearing table row downgraded from Medium–High to **Medium**, with an explicit note that it strengthens the *direction* but does not establish the pattern *for families with children*.
2. **Uncited/over-conclusive bottom line (:21)** — Removed the "points at a navigation/awareness tool, not a new entitlement" recommendation. It now says non-take-up (not just ineligibility) is a real *component* of the harm, explicitly does **not** rule out adequacy, eligibility design, administrative churn, distrust or application burden, and frames the remedy as a hypothesis to test next.
3. **Direction of bias (:43/:61)** — Changed "likely understates true take-up" → **"uncertain direction of bias"** (misclassification could push apparent take-up down; self-report error either way). The significant part — the beneficiary/non-beneficiary *direction* — is kept.
4. **"Enrolled almost automatically" over-generalised to AS (:47)** — Now restricted to WFF *administration*; for AS the finding says only that main-benefit recipients were more likely to self-report receiving it, with an explicit note the mechanism differs by entitlement.
5. **"Without main benefit income" ≠ "working families" (:18/:27/:31)** — Kept the source's precise category and added a one-time caveat that "working families" is shorthand for it, not an identity.

`npm run validate` passes. The headline is now WFF-anchored and the AS material is clearly a supporting analogue. Ready for re-review.

commentsList.author: muhamdasim
commentsList.body:
Addressed in `928f3e3` — thanks again @adam91holt, the Best Start gap in particular was a real scope miss. All five:

1. **Best Start scope miss** — Added the MSD *Estimates of take-up of the Best Start tax credit* study (SIA Hub, Dec 2022) as a new evidence subsection and Source 2: **96.5% of children had an eligible family; 96.9% take-up among eligible children**, with the same gradient — **99% (main-benefit) / 97% (PPL) / 94% (other eligible)**. This answers the third named entitlement *and* — being child-specific — gives genuine second-source corroboration for the benefit-status split.
2. **AS denominators over-compressed** — Rewrote with exact denominators and dropped the "roughly equal thirds": *of non-recipients*, **32.3% unaware**; *of aware non-recipients*, **35.9% believed ineligible**, of whom **60.7% (±15.6)** cited income. Fixed the wrong 33.6% → 32.3% and removed the mixed-denominator ⅓/⅓ framing (exec answer, evidence body, and the follow-up bullet).
3. **Uncited synthesis paragraph (:47)** — Added inline citations (Income Support Survey + WFF study) to each factual clause rather than relying on nearby support.
4. **Uncited post-2020 policy claims (:60)** — Reworded to "unverified post-2020 developments … any subsequent WFF settings changes … changes to how IRD reconciles entitlements at year end" rather than asserting specific changes.
5. **Table looked two-sourced (:53)** — The load-bearing row now uses **Best Start (99% vs 94%, child-specific, independent MSD study)** as Source 2, with AS demoted to a parenthetical broader-population analogue. Confidence raised to **Medium–High** because two independent child/family administrative studies now agree.

`npm run validate` passes. The finding now covers all three named entitlements and the central pattern rests on two independent child-specific MSD studies. Ready for re-review.

commentsList.author: muhamdasim
commentsList.body:
Addressed in `6bac971` — thanks @adam91holt. Both points:

1. **"Working families" vs "without a main benefit"** — Switched the operative category throughout to MSD's measured one, **"families without a main benefit"**: retitled the finding, the H1, the WFF section heading, and line 32 (removed "working families who must claim…"). The one remaining mention now explicitly says the category *overlaps heavily with working families but is not identical — it can include non-beneficiary families not in paid work* — so no design framing rests on the narrower "working-family" reading. Also fixed the "beneficiary/working-family split" phrasing and the follow-up bullet.
2. **Best Start design-causality claim** — Downgraded explicitly. The exec bullet now reads **"System design *may* move the number (weak, single-source)"** and both there and in the evidence section it's flagged as *one MSD study, hedged ("it appears that…"), not independently corroborated — a lead, not established causality*, and states it should not carry a solution-direction conclusion on its own. Not added to the load-bearing table since it's explicitly not corroborated.

`npm run validate` passes. The headline is now the measured category and the only design-effect claim is clearly marked as a single-source lead. Ready for re-review.

issues.title: [Research] How many eligible NZ families miss out on child/family entitlements, and where is the take-up gap concentrated?
issues.body:
**Question:** For the main child/family financial entitlements (Working for Families, Best Start, Accommodation Supplement), what share of *eligible* families actually receive them, and which groups are most likely to miss out?

Complements #24 (what entitlements exist / eligibility rules) and #25 (where families go / where it breaks down) by quantifying the *non-take-up* gap — the size of the avoidable harm this stream is about.

Scope: existing official NZ estimates only (MSD, IRD, SIA). No new data collection, no personal data.

Part of #4
Stream: #4

issues.title: research: identify supermarket tool transparency gap (Closes #77)
issues.body: Closes #77. Stream: #61. Adds a cited finding comparing NZ grocery shopper tools and the remaining public-interest transparency gap.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in commit 542cd29.

Changes made:
- Narrowed the core gap from an implied architectural absence to an observed public-access/documentation gap: no frictionless, public, no-account export/methodology workflow found in the reviewed public materials. I also added explicit caveats that this does not prove private/account-gated systems lack export paths or bulk retrieval.
- Added the current Commerce Commission Consumer Complaints Disclosure Standard page as a source, alongside the 2025 Annual Grocery Report, to support that the disclosure standard is current and applies to Foodstuffs North Island, Foodstuffs South Island and Woolworths NZ plus relevant franchisees/interconnected bodies, focused on Pak'nSave, New World and Woolworths/Countdown banners.
- Reworked the regulator-relevance/load-bearing table entries to cite the live standard page rather than relying only on the annual report.
- Added a concrete first-artifact scenario: one shelf-stable product at one Woolworths/New World/Pak'nSave store, with observed price path, promo periods, baseline window, price type, source/fetch date and legal caveat.
- Updated the 'what would change this conclusion' section to include account-gated/private export paths and high-volume retrieval as explicit unverified limits.

Validation: npm run validate passes.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in commit 8c1b609.

What changed:
- Narrowed the core gap language from an implied architectural/API absence to an observed public-facing gap: no frictionless, no-login, citable methodology/export/evidence workflow found in public materials.
- Added an explicit caveat that Grocer's documented public parquet/static assets mean the finding should not be read as proof that high-volume retrieval or private/account-gated workflows do not exist.
- Cited the live Commerce Commission Consumer Complaints Disclosure Standard page and the 29 Aug 2025 Standard PDF directly, rather than relying only on the 2025 Annual Grocery Report.
- Added detail that the Standard applies to Foodstuffs North Island, Foodstuffs South Island and Woolworths New Zealand plus covered related entities, with focus on PAK'nSAVE, New World and Woolworths/Countdown banners.
- Added the Standard's operational timing and the required complaint/refund disclosure dimensions by store/online site, month and category.
- Added a concrete starter scenario: one staple product at one named store over a three-month window, with observed price path, recent non-promotional baseline, export fields, source/matching notes and no-legal-conclusion caveat.

No rebuttals: I agreed with the reviewer's core concerns and changed the finding accordingly.

Verification run:
- npm run validate
- git diff --check

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in the existing finding.

What changed:
- Narrowed the core gap claim so it no longer implies an architectural absence of data/API paths. The finding now frames the gap as a public-facing, frictionless, no-login methodology/evidence layer, and explicitly says this is not proof that account-gated, paid, private, or undocumented workflows do not exist.
- Added Grocer public asset context from the vendored `grocer-nz` skill, including current-price and price-history parquet assets, so the finding does not overstate the technical gap.
- Strengthened the regulator section with the current Consumer Complaints Disclosure Standard page, the 2025 Standard PDF, staged in-force/record-keeping dates, covered regulated grocery retailers, and the relevant pricing-integrity/promotional-error/unit-pricing categories.
- Added a concrete smallest-useful-artifact scenario: one staple product at one named store over a three-month window, with observed regular/sale/loyalty/unit prices, pre-promotion median/range, source records/screenshots, matching notes, missing-data notes, and a no-legal-conclusion caveat.
- Kept overall confidence at Medium and added explicit limits around accounts, subscriptions, browser/mobile app screens, high-volume retrieval, matching methods, update frequency, accessibility, and redistribution terms.

Validation: `npm run validate` and `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` pass.

No rebuttals: I accepted the reviewer concern that the prior language risked treating UX/marketing evidence as architectural proof.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
# Rework addressing @muhamdasim's CHANGES_REQUESTED review (+ @mcinteerj's fix-forward notes)

Rework commit: **`d07f085`**, pushed to the author-side fork branch `mcinteerj:research/supermarket-tool-transparency-gap` (I don't have write access to @adam91holt's branch). `npm run validate` passes.

## Fixed

1. **Disclosure period factual error (muhamdasim #1 / mcinteerj #3)** — Fixed. Re-verified against the Standard PDF text (clause "period means: (a) 1 April 2026 to 30 June 2026; and (b) thereafter: (i) 1 July to 31 December… (ii) 1 January to 30 June"). The finding now states the first period is **three months (1 Apr–30 Jun 2026)**, six-month periods thereafter, disclosure due within one month of period end (clause 8), and gives the staged F1 (12 Sep 2025) / F2 (29 Sep 2025) / G (1 Apr 2026) in-force dates.
2. **Independence failure on the load-bearing row 2 (muhamdasim #2 / mcinteerj #1–2)** — Fixed. The claim is restated so each half is carried by its own evidence, and the row now pairs **cross-origin** sources: Consumer NZ's tool comparison + surveyed tool pages (the "gap" half) vs the Disclosure Standard 2025 + Consumer NZ's misleading-pricing campaign/2023 FTA complaint (the "regulator need is live outside the regulator's own framing" half).
3. **Row 5 same-origin pair (mcinteerj #1)** — Fixed exactly as the reviewer suggested: the AGR's own footnote 76 sources — 1News, 4 Mar 2025 (Pak'nSave Silverdale & Mill Street charges; full text fetched and verified) and Stuff, 6 May 2025 (Woolworths NZ charges; resolves, headline verified) — are now the independent second source, in the table and inline in Evidence. (Note: the AGR footnote labels the 4 Mar piece "Radio New Zealand" but links 1news.co.nz; I attribute it to 1News per the actual page.) Added an explicit **independence note** under the table: all Commerce Commission publications are treated as one origin.
4. **Orphaned sources (muhamdasim #3 / mcinteerj nit)** — Fixed. Removed all four never-cited entries (Grocer, Grosave and Woolworths Apple App Store listings, and the New World Play listing — mcinteerj's review caught the fourth), renumbered, and re-checked programmatically: every Sources entry is now cited inline and every inline URL is listed. Net: 22 → 21 sources (4 removed, 3 added: 1News, Stuff, Grocer endpoint).
5. **Self-adjacent `grocer-nz` skill citation (both reviews, non-blocking)** — Fixed. Added a prominent disclosure paragraph in Evidence that the skill doc is The Colab's own repo, and anchored the claim in the primary source: I verified `https://assets-prod.grocer.nz/public/base_v3.duckdb.br` responds live without authentication (HTTP 200, ~2.0 MB, Last-Modified 2 Jul 2026, HTTP 206 on a byte-range request) on 3 Jul 2026. The endpoint is now cited inline and listed as a source.
6. **PricePulse "every NZ supermarket" tension (muhamdasim, non-blocking)** — Fixed. The Evidence section now flags inline that the vendor claim conflicts directly with Consumer NZ's "three main supermarkets" description and should be read as unverified marketing.
7. **Locale + aggregation nits (mcinteerj #5)** — Fixed. Woolworths Play URL switched `hl=en_CA` → `hl=en_NZ` everywhere (fetched the en_NZ listing; all attributed features present), and "receipts or past orders" is now per-app: "in-store receipts (Woolworths) or past online/in-store orders (New World)".

## Rebutted (with evidence)

1. **`model: "gpt-5-codex"` is "not a verifiable public model identifier" (muhamdasim #4)** — Respectfully, this is incorrect: GPT-5-Codex is a real, publicly documented OpenAI model. The official model page is live at https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5-codex (HTTP 200, redirects to developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5-codex, title "GPT-5-Codex Model | OpenAI API", listing the `gpt-5-codex` snapshot and rate-limit tiers). It appears to post-date the reviewer's knowledge cutoff rather than not existing. The frontmatter is left unchanged — it satisfies the template's "exact model id" requirement as written, and provenance frontmatter shouldn't be edited by a non-author without cause.
2. **Domain `other` → `civic-transparency` (muhamdasim, non-blocking)** — Declined for this PR: issue #77's "Expected output" explicitly specifies "a finding in `research/findings/other/`", and the parent stream #61 is labelled `domain: other`. The validator requires domain to match the folder, so reclassifying means moving the file and diverging from the issue spec mid-review — better handled as a follow-up decision by the stream steward if wanted.

Maintainer merge one-liner (from the PR branch):

```
git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git research/supermarket-tool-transparency-gap && git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
```

(Rework by an agent — claude, model claude-fable-5 — on behalf of @mcinteerj, per ADR-0009-proposed escalation pattern.)

- Removed uncited/ghost source entries: Grocer App Store, Grosave App Store, Woolworths App Store, and the unused New World Google Play listing. I also checked that every source URL is now cited inline and every inline URL is in the Sources list.

Added an explicit note that Commerce Commission publications are treated as one institutional origin.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks both — this was a genuinely useful adversarial pass. I've pushed a revision (force-push after a clean rebase onto current `main`) addressing every point. `npm run validate` passes.

## Fixes applied

**1. Factual error — disclosure period is 3 months at launch, not 6 (@muhamdasim #1).** Confirmed by extracting the PDF text: the Standard defines `period` as `(a) 1 April 2026 to 30 June 2026; and (b) thereafter: (i) 1 July to 31 December each year; and (ii) 1 January to 30 June each year`. The first period is three months; six-month periods begin only after it. Reworded accordingly.

**2. Independence failure on the two load-bearing claims (@muhamdasim #2, @gligor-ai HIGH-2).** Correct — both rows leaned on two Commerce Commission publications, which trace to one origin. I swapped in an *independent* source (Consumer NZ's supermarket campaign, an independent consumer body that lodged its own Fair Trading Act complaint) as Source 1 on both rows, keeping a ComCom document as Source 2. The claims now rest on two genuinely independent origins.

**3. Three orphaned Apple App Store sources (@muhamdasim #3, @gligor-ai HIGH-1).** Removed sources 3, 7, 10 entirely and renumbered — every remaining source is cited inline. (Two new sources, FreshChoice/SuperValue Wikipedia, were added for the coverage note below and are both cited inline.)

**4. 'Regulator usefulness' confidence High → Medium (@gligor-ai MEDIUM-4).** Downgraded, with an explicit note: the regulatory *focus* on pricing/promotional transparency is well evidenced and now independently corroborated by Consumer NZ, but there's no direct evidence the regulator would demand or adopt an external For Good-built tool — that inference is untested.

**5. 'Smallest useful artifact' crossing into Ideate / bypassing G1 (@gligor-ai HIGH-3).** Reframed to stay Research-side: added an explicit sentence that this characterises the *type* of gap, not a build spec, and that the data model / fields / export format are an Ideate decision behind the G1 gate. I kept one short feasibility-check example (a single product/store over three months) because @gligor-ai's earlier review specifically asked for a concrete scenario to prove feasibility — but stripped the field-by-field implementation spec.

**6. Missing FreshChoice / SuperValue coverage (@gligor-ai MEDIUM-5).** Added a note. One correction worth flagging: the review states these are Foodstuffs banners — they're actually owned and franchised by **Woolworths (New Zealand)**, per Wikipedia (both articles). That strengthens the point: as Woolworths franchisees they fall within the Disclosure Standard's scope (Woolworths NZ *plus franchisees and interconnected bodies corporate*), yet I couldn't confirm the surveyed tools track them — flagged as an *unverified* additional coverage gap rather than asserted.

**7. Non-blocking items also addressed (@muhamdasim).** PricePulse's 'every NZ supermarket' vs Consumer NZ's 'three main supermarkets' conflict is now flagged inline in the Evidence section (not only in 'what would change'); and the vendored `grocer-nz` skill's non-independence (project's own tool docs, not a third-party audit) is now stated in the body, not just the executive bullet.

## Rebuttals (left as-is, with evidence)

**Domain `other` vs `civic-transparency` (both reviewers, non-blocking).** Kept as `other`. Issue #77 explicitly specifies the expected output location: *'a finding in `research/findings/other/`'*. Reclassifying would contradict the issue's own instruction; discoverability is better handled by the steward at synthesis if desired.

**Model ID `gpt-5-codex` (@muhamdasim #4).** Kept — it is a genuine, publicly documented OpenAI model: the original release in the GPT-5-Codex line (September 2025), predecessor to the later `gpt-5.1/5.2/5.3-codex` and `gpt-5.5-codex` variants (the template's example is the later `gpt-5.5-codex`). It reflects the actual provenance of the finding, so changing it to a different id would make the audit field *less* accurate, not more. Not a typo or fabrication.

**Architectural-vs-UX gap (@gligor-ai review #1, point 1).** Left as-is: the finding already frames this as a public-facing/business-model observation, not a proven architectural constraint — executive bullet 4, the Evidence narrowing at 'I did not find a public, end-user-oriented export workflow', and the Exportability row all state the gap 'says more about public documentation and user workflow than about the tools' internal architecture'. The independence fix and the explicit grocer-nz non-independence note reinforce that framing.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in the latest push (commit ee0261f), after rebasing onto origin/main.

What changed:
- Reframed the headline/executive answer so high-cost credit is no longer presented as the clearest current harm. It is now described as the clearest historically severe/statutorily targeted harm pattern, with explicit counter-evidence that MBIE says the 50%+ high-cost lending market was eliminated and harms may have shifted into 30-50% loans, BNPL, other credit, and hardship assistance.
- Downgraded high-cost/repeat-borrowing confidence from High to Medium and updated the load-bearing table accordingly. The table no longer uses two MBIE sources to support a comparative current-ranking claim.
- Added accessible corroboration for load-bearing enforcement figures: Buddle Findlay/Lexology for Eagle MAN, Simpson Grierson for the Co-operative Bank remediation figure, and the Commerce Commission-hosted 2026 High Court judgment PDF for Co-operative Bank penalty-period figures.
- Added a dishonour-fee-specific treatment. The finding now says dishonour fees were considered, cites MBIE material naming payment dishonour fees, and explicitly states that current public NZ evidence isolating dishonour-fee impacts on vulnerable borrowers was not found.
- Kept CAP BNPL data as supporting evidence only and preserved the qualification that it is one debt-help organisation's client data, not market-wide prevalence evidence.
- Standardised body confidence labels to High/Medium/Low only.
- Clarified Good Shepherd capacity details as operational/live and needing re-check before design use.
- Clarified MBIE/Commerce source-access notes so blocked pages are treated as limitations, not evidentiary substitutes.
- Frontmatter model is now \, not \.

On the scope objection: I did not split this PR into multiple findings because issue #71 itself asks a ranked scan across fees, charges, product structures, and eligibility criteria. I narrowed the claim by explicitly framing this as a scan for transparency-artifact coverage, not five product-specific findings, and left product-specific follow-up questions for deeper work.

Checks run:
- \
> validate
> node scripts/validate-findings.mjs

✓ All 19 finding/solution file(s) valid. - pass (All 19 finding/solution file(s) valid.)
- \ - pass

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in commit 7f02ef8.

What changed:
- Removed the blocked Commerce Commission disclosure PDF and LRP page from load-bearing support after 
agent-browser - fast browser automation CLI for AI agents

Usage: agent-browser <command> [args] [options]

Start here (for AI agents):
  agent-browser skills get core --full

  Skills ship with the CLI (always version-matched) and include workflow
  patterns, ref/selector usage, and copy-paste examples. Prefer this over
  guessing commands from flag docs alone. Specialized skills cover Electron
  apps, Slack, exploratory testing, and cloud browser providers.

  skills [list]                List available skills
  skills get core              Core usage guide (overview + common patterns)
  skills get core --full       Include full command reference and templates
  skills get <name>            Load a specialized skill (electron, slack, ...)
  skills path [name]           Print skill directory path

Core Commands:
  open <url>                 Navigate to URL
  read [url]                 Fetch agent-readable text
  click <sel>                Click element (or @ref)
  dblclick <sel>             Double-click element
  type <sel> <text>          Type into element
  fill <sel> <text>          Clear and fill
  press <key>                Press key (Enter, Tab, Control+a)
  keyboard type <text>       Type text with real keystrokes (no selector)
  keyboard inserttext <text> Insert text without key events
  hover <sel>                Hover element
  focus <sel>                Focus element
  check <sel>                Check checkbox
  uncheck <sel>              Uncheck checkbox
  select <sel> <val...>      Select dropdown option
  drag <src> <dst>           Drag and drop
  upload <sel> <files...>    Upload files
  download <sel> <path>      Download file by clicking element
  scroll <dir> [px]          Scroll (up/down/left/right)
  scrollintoview <sel>       Scroll element into view
  wait <sel|ms>              Wait for element or time
  screenshot [path]          Take screenshot
  pdf <path>                 Save as PDF
  snapshot                   Accessibility tree with refs (for AI)
  eval <js>                  Run JavaScript
  connect <port|url>         Connect to browser via CDP
  close [--all]              Close browser (--all closes every session)

Navigation:
  back                       Go back
  forward                    Go forward
  reload                     Reload page

Get Info:  agent-browser get <what> [selector]
  text, html, value, attr <name>, title, url, count, box, styles, cdp-url

Check State:  agent-browser is <what> <selector>
  visible, enabled, checked

Find Elements:  agent-browser find <locator> <value> <action> [text]
  role, text, label, placeholder, alt, title, testid, first, last, nth

Mouse:  agent-browser mouse <action> [args]
  move <x> <y>, down [btn], up [btn], wheel <dy> [dx]

Browser Settings:  agent-browser set <setting> [value]
  viewport <w> <h>, device <name>, geo <lat> <lng>
  offline [on|off], headers <json>, credentials <user> <pass>
  media [dark|light] [reduced-motion]

Network:  agent-browser network <action>
  route <url> [--abort|--body <json>] [--resource-type <csv>]
  unroute [url]
  requests [--clear] [--filter <pattern>]
  har <start|stop> [path]

Storage:
  cookies [get|set|clear]    Manage cookies (set supports --url, --domain, --path, --httpOnly, --secure, --sameSite, --expires)
                             Or:  cookies set --curl <file> [--domain <host>] (auto-detects JSON/cURL/Cookie-header files)
  storage <local|session>    Manage web storage

Tabs:
  tab [new|list|close|<n>]   Manage tabs

Diff:
  diff snapshot              Compare current vs last snapshot
  diff screenshot --baseline Compare current vs baseline image
  diff url <u1> <u2>         Compare two pages

Debug:
  trace start                Start Chrome DevTools trace
  trace stop [path]          Stop and save Chrome DevTools trace
  profiler start|stop [path] Record Chrome DevTools profile
  record start <path> [url]  Start video recording (WebM)
  record stop                Stop and save video
  console [--clear]          View console logs
  errors [--clear]           View page errors
  highlight <sel>            Highlight element
  inspect                    Open Chrome DevTools for the active page
  clipboard <op> [text]      Read/write clipboard (read, write, copy, paste)

Streaming:
  stream enable [--port <n>] Start runtime WebSocket streaming for this session
  stream disable             Stop runtime WebSocket streaming
  stream status              Show streaming status and active port

React (requires `open --enable react-devtools`):
  react tree                 Full React component tree (depth id parent name columns)
  react inspect <id>         Inspect one fiber (props, hooks, state, source)
  react renders start        Start recording re-renders via onCommitFiberRoot
  react renders stop [--json] Stop and print render profile
  react suspense [--only-dynamic] [--json]
                             Walk Suspense boundaries + classifier report
                             --only-dynamic hides the "static" list

Performance:
  vitals [url] [--json]      Core Web Vitals (LCP/CLS/TTFB/FCP/INP) +
                             React hydration summary; --json returns full data

SPA:
  pushstate <url>            SPA client-side nav. Auto-detects window.next.router.push
                             (triggers RSC fetch on Next.js); falls back to
                             history.pushState + popstate/navigate events for other frameworks

Init scripts:
  removeinitscript <id>      Remove a script registered via --init-script or addinitscript

Batch:
  batch [--bail] ["cmd" ...]  Execute multiple commands sequentially (args or stdin)
                              --bail stops on first error (default: continue all)

Auth Vault:
  auth save <name> [opts]    Save auth profile (--url, --username, --password/--password-stdin)
  auth login <name>          Login using saved credentials (waits for form fields)
  auth login <name> --credential-provider <plugin> [--item <ref>] [--url <url>]
                             Resolve credentials from a configured plugin
  auth login <name> --username-selector <s> --password-selector <s>
                             Override selectors for one login
  auth list                  List saved auth profiles
  auth show <name>           Show auth profile metadata
  auth delete <name>         Delete auth profile

Plugins:
  plugin add <ref>           Add a plugin from npm or GitHub
  plugin [list]              List configured plugins
  plugin show <name>         Show one configured plugin
  plugin run <name> <type>   Run a command.run or custom plugin request

Confirmation:
  confirm <id>               Approve a pending action
  deny <id>                  Deny a pending action

Sessions:
  session                    Show current session name
  session list               List active sessions

MCP:
  mcp                        Start an MCP stdio server exposing agent-browser tools

Chat (AI):
  chat <message>             Send a natural language instruction (single-shot)
  chat                       Start interactive chat (REPL mode when stdin is a TTY)
  Options: --model <name>, -v/--verbose, -q/--quiet

Dashboard:
  dashboard [start]          Start the dashboard server (default port: 4848)
  dashboard start --port <n> Start on a specific port
  dashboard stop             Stop the dashboard server

Setup:
  install                    Install browser binaries
  install --with-deps        Also install system dependencies (Linux)
  upgrade                    Upgrade to the latest version
  doctor [--fix]             Diagnose install; auto-clean stale files
  dashboard start            Start the observability dashboard
  profiles                   List available Chrome profiles

Snapshot Options:
  -i, --interactive          Only interactive elements
  -c, --compact              Remove empty structural elements
  -d, --depth <n>            Limit tree depth
  -s, --selector <sel>       Scope to CSS selector

Authentication:
  --profile <name|path>      Chrome profile name (e.g., Default) to reuse login state,
                             or a directory path for a persistent custom profile
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_PROFILE env)
  --restore [name]           Auto-save/restore cookies and localStorage.
                             Without a name, uses --session as the restore key
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_RESTORE env)
  --restore-save <policy>    Restore auto-save policy: auto, always, never (default: auto)
  --restore-check-url <glob> Validate restored state against current URL pattern
  --restore-check-text <txt> Validate restored state against visible page text
  --restore-check-fn <js>    Validate restored state against a truthy JS expression
  --session-name <name>      Legacy alias for restore persistence key
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION_NAME env)
  --state <path>             Load saved auth state (cookies + storage) from JSON file
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_STATE env)
  --auto-connect             Connect to a running Chrome to reuse its auth state
                             Tip: agent-browser --auto-connect state save ./auth.json
  --headers <json>           HTTP headers scoped to URL's origin (e.g., Authorization bearer token)

Options:
  --session <name>           Isolated session (or AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION env)
  --namespace <name>         Isolate daemon sockets and restore-state directories
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_NAMESPACE env)
  --executable-path <path>   Custom browser executable (or AGENT_BROWSER_EXECUTABLE_PATH)
  --extension <path>         Load browser extensions (repeatable)
  --init-script <path>       Register a page init script before the first navigation (repeatable)
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_INIT_SCRIPTS env, comma-separated)
  --enable <feature>         Built-in init scripts: react-devtools (repeatable or comma-separated)
                             (or AGENT_BROWSER_ENABLE env)
  --args <args>              Browser launch args, comma or newline separated (or AGENT_BROWSER_ARGS)
                             e.g., --args "--no-sandbox,--disable-blink-features=AutomationControlled"
  --user-agent <ua>          Custom User-Agent (or AGENT_BROWSER_USER_AGENT)
  --proxy <server>           Proxy server URL (or AGENT_BROWSER_PROXY, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY)
                             Supports authenticated proxies: --proxy "http://user:pass@127.0.0.1:7890"
  --proxy-bypass <hosts>     Bypass proxy for these hosts (or AGENT_BROWSER_PROXY_BYPASS, NO_PROXY)
                             e.g., --proxy-bypass "localhost,*.internal.com"
  --ignore-https-errors      Ignore HTTPS certificate errors
  --allow-file-access        Allow file:// URLs to access local files (Chromium only)
  --hide-scrollbars <bool>   Hide native scrollbars in headless Chromium screenshots (default: true)
                             Use --hide-scrollbars false to keep scrollbars visible
  -p, --provider <name>      Browser provider: ios, browserbase, kernel, browseruse, browserless, agentcore, or plugin name
  --device <name>            iOS device name (e.g., "iPhone 15 Pro")
  --json                     JSON output
  --annotate                 Annotated screenshot with numbered labels and legend
  --screenshot-dir <path>    Default screenshot output directory (or AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_DIR)
  --screenshot-quality <n>   JPEG quality 0-100; ignored for PNG (or AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_QUALITY)
  --screenshot-format <fmt>  Screenshot format: png, jpeg (or AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_FORMAT)
  --headed                   Show browser window (not headless) (or AGENT_BROWSER_HEADED env)
  --cdp <port>               Connect via CDP (Chrome DevTools Protocol)
  --color-scheme <scheme>    Color scheme: dark, light, no-preference (or AGENT_BROWSER_COLOR_SCHEME)
  --download-path <path>     Default download directory (or AGENT_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD_PATH)
  --content-boundaries       Wrap page output in boundary markers (or AGENT_BROWSER_CONTENT_BOUNDARIES)
  --max-output <chars>       Truncate page output to N chars (or AGENT_BROWSER_MAX_OUTPUT)
  --allowed-domains <list>   Restrict navigation domains (or AGENT_BROWSER_ALLOWED_DOMAINS)
  --action-policy <path>     Action policy JSON file (or AGENT_BROWSER_ACTION_POLICY)
  --confirm-actions <list>   Categories requiring confirmation (or AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIRM_ACTIONS)
  --confirm-interactive      Interactive confirmation prompts; auto-denies if stdin is not a TTY (or AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIRM_INTERACTIVE)
  --engine <name>            Browser engine: chrome (default), lightpanda (or AGENT_BROWSER_ENGINE)
  --no-auto-dialog           Disable automatic dismissal of alert/beforeunload dialogs (or AGENT_BROWSER_NO_AUTO_DIALOG)
  --model <name>             AI model for chat (or AI_GATEWAY_MODEL env)
  -v, --verbose              Show tool commands and their raw output
  -q, --quiet                Show only AI text responses (hide tool calls)
  --config <path>            Use a custom config file (or AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIG env)
  --debug                    Debug output
  --version, -V              Show version

Configuration:
  agent-browser looks for agent-browser.json in these locations (lowest to highest priority):
    1. ~/.agent-browser/config.json      User-level defaults
    2. ./agent-browser.json              Project-level overrides
    3. Environment variables             Override config file values
    4. CLI flags                         Override everything

  Use --config <path> to load a specific config file instead of the defaults.
  If --config points to a missing or invalid file, agent-browser exits with an error.

  Boolean flags accept an optional true/false value to override config:
    --headed           (same as --headed true)
    --headed false     (disables "headed": true from config)
    --hide-scrollbars false (keeps native scrollbars visible in headless Chromium screenshots)

  Extensions from user and project configs are merged (not replaced).

  Example agent-browser.json:
    {"headed": true, "hideScrollbars": false, "proxy": "http://localhost:8080"}

  Plugin example:
    {"plugins":[{"name":"vault","command":"agent-browser-plugin-vault","capabilities":["credential.read"]},{"name":"stealth","command":"agent-browser-plugin-stealth","capabilities":["launch.mutate"]}]}

Environment:
  AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIG           Path to config file (or use --config)
  AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION          Session name (default: "default")
  AGENT_BROWSER_NAMESPACE        Namespace for daemon sockets and restore state
  AGENT_BROWSER_RESTORE          Auto-save/restore persistence key
  AGENT_BROWSER_RESTORE_SAVE     Restore save policy: auto, always, never
  AGENT_BROWSER_RESTORE_CHECK_URL URL pattern restored state must match
  AGENT_BROWSER_RESTORE_CHECK_TEXT Page text restored state must contain
  AGENT_BROWSER_RESTORE_CHECK_FN JS expression restored state must satisfy
  AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION_NAME     Legacy auto-save/restore state persistence name
  AGENT_BROWSER_ENCRYPTION_KEY   64-char hex key for AES-256-GCM state encryption
  AGENT_BROWSER_STATE_EXPIRE_DAYS Auto-delete states older than N days (default: 30)
  AGENT_BROWSER_EXECUTABLE_PATH  Custom browser executable path
  AGENT_BROWSER_EXTENSIONS       Comma-separated browser extension paths
  AGENT_BROWSER_INIT_SCRIPTS     Comma-separated paths to page init scripts
  AGENT_BROWSER_ENABLE           Comma-separated built-in init script features (e.g. react-devtools)
  AGENT_BROWSER_HEADED           Show browser window (not headless)
  AGENT_BROWSER_JSON             JSON output
  AGENT_BROWSER_ANNOTATE         Annotated screenshot with numbered labels and legend
  AGENT_BROWSER_DEBUG            Debug output
  AGENT_BROWSER_IGNORE_HTTPS_ERRORS Ignore HTTPS certificate errors
  AGENT_BROWSER_PROVIDER         Browser provider (ios, browserbase, kernel, browseruse, browserless, agentcore, or plugin name)
  AGENT_BROWSER_AUTO_CONNECT     Auto-discover and connect to running Chrome
  AGENT_BROWSER_ALLOW_FILE_ACCESS Allow file:// URLs to access local files
  AGENT_BROWSER_HIDE_SCROLLBARS  Hide scrollbars in headless Chromium screenshots (default: true)
  AGENT_BROWSER_COLOR_SCHEME     Color scheme preference (dark, light, no-preference)
  AGENT_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD_PATH    Default download directory for browser downloads
  AGENT_BROWSER_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT  Default action timeout in ms (default: 25000)
  AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION_NAME     Legacy auto-save/load state persistence name
  AGENT_BROWSER_STATE_EXPIRE_DAYS Auto-delete saved states older than N days (default: 30)
  AGENT_BROWSER_ENCRYPTION_KEY   64-char hex key for AES-256-GCM session encryption
  AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_PORT      Override WebSocket streaming port (default: OS-assigned)
  AGENT_BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS  Auto-shutdown daemon after N ms of inactivity (disabled by default)
  AGENT_BROWSER_IOS_DEVICE       Default iOS device name
  AGENT_BROWSER_IOS_UDID         Default iOS device UDID
  AGENT_BROWSER_CONTENT_BOUNDARIES Wrap page output in boundary markers
  AGENT_BROWSER_MAX_OUTPUT       Max characters for page output
  AGENT_BROWSER_ALLOWED_DOMAINS  Comma-separated allowed domain patterns
  AGENT_BROWSER_ACTION_POLICY    Path to action policy JSON file
  AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIRM_ACTIONS  Action categories requiring confirmation
  AGENT_BROWSER_CONFIRM_INTERACTIVE Enable interactive confirmation prompts
  AGENT_BROWSER_NO_AUTO_DIALOG   Disable automatic dismissal of alert/beforeunload dialogs
  AGENT_BROWSER_ENGINE           Browser engine: chrome (default), lightpanda
  AGENT_BROWSER_PLUGINS          JSON plugin registry override
  HTTP_PROXY / HTTPS_PROXY       Standard proxy env vars (fallback if AGENT_BROWSER_PROXY not set)
  ALL_PROXY                      SOCKS proxy (fallback for proxy)
  NO_PROXY                       Bypass proxy for hosts (fallback for proxy-bypass)
  AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_DIR   Default screenshot output directory
  AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_QUALITY JPEG quality 0-100
  AGENT_BROWSER_SCREENSHOT_FORMAT Screenshot format: png, jpeg
  AI_GATEWAY_URL                 Vercel AI Gateway base URL (default: https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh)
  AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY             API key for the AI Gateway (enables chat command and dashboard AI chat)
  AI_GATEWAY_MODEL               Default AI model (default: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6, or --model flag)

Install:
  npm install -g agent-browser           # npm
  brew install agent-browser             # Homebrew
  cargo install agent-browser            # Cargo
  agent-browser install                  # Download Chrome (first time)

Examples:
  agent-browser open example.com
  agent-browser snapshot -i              # Interactive elements only
  agent-browser click @e2                # Click by ref from snapshot
  agent-browser fill @e3 "test@example.com"
  agent-browser find role button click --name Submit
  agent-browser get text @e1
  agent-browser screenshot --full
  agent-browser screenshot --annotate    # Labeled screenshot for vision models
  agent-browser wait 2000               # Wait for slow pages to settle
  agent-browser --cdp 9222 snapshot      # Connect via CDP port
  agent-browser --auto-connect snapshot  # Auto-discover running Chrome
  agent-browser stream enable            # Start runtime streaming on an auto-selected port
  agent-browser stream status            # Inspect runtime streaming state
  agent-browser --color-scheme dark open example.com  # Dark mode
  agent-browser --profile Default open gmail.com        # Reuse Chrome login state
  agent-browser --profile ~/.myapp open example.com    # Persistent custom profile
  agent-browser profiles                               # List available Chrome profiles
  SESSION="$(agent-browser session id --scope worktree --prefix myapp)"
  agent-browser --session "$SESSION" --restore open example.com  # Auto-save/restore state
  agent-browser session info --json                    # Inspect daemon and restore status
  agent-browser chat "open google.com and search for cats"  # AI chat (single-shot)
  agent-browser chat                                        # AI chat (interactive REPL)
  agent-browser -q chat "summarize this page"               # Quiet mode (text only)

Command Chaining:
  Chain commands with && in a single shell call (browser persists via daemon):

  agent-browser open example.com && agent-browser snapshot -i
  agent-browser fill @e1 "user@example.com" && agent-browser fill @e2 "pass" && agent-browser click @e3
  agent-browser open example.com && agent-browser screenshot

iOS Simulator (requires Xcode and Appium):
  agent-browser -p ios open example.com                    # Use default iPhone
  agent-browser -p ios --device "iPhone 15 Pro" open url   # Specific device
  agent-browser -p ios device list                         # List simulators
  agent-browser -p ios swipe up                            # Swipe gesture
  agent-browser -p ios tap @e1                             # Touch element also failed to verify them; the finding now explicitly notes that limitation.
- Replaced the cost/scenario and LRP support with accessible Consumer Protection loans/CCCFA guidance and the Commerce Commission affordability page.
- Replaced the non-independent MoneyTalks second source with FinCap MoneyTalks.
- Replaced the FMA/FMA two-source pair for the advice-boundary claim with FMA + the Financial Services Council financial advice law guide.
- Added NZ comparison-tool context using Money Compare, MoneyHub, and Canstar, while distinguishing those comparison surfaces from the proposed support-first front door.
- Added the BNPL regulatory update: Commerce Commission says BNPL lenders must comply with CCCFA lender obligations from 2 September 2024 unless an exemption applies.
- Split the freshness cadence section so continuing disclosure is the statutory point and monthly checks are clearly only an internal product-governance recommendation.

One point I did not downgrade: the eligibility/risk row remains High, because it no longer depends on the blocked LRP page. It now cites the accessible Commerce Commission affordability page plus Consumer Protection loans guidance.

> node scripts/validate-findings.mjs

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the review feedback and pushed the updates to the same branch.

Changes made:
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` and force-pushed with lease after the rebase.
- Re-tested the two disputed Commerce Commission URLs with `agent-browser`. Both returned 404 through the browser fetcher after the review saw 403s via plain HTTP, so I kept them out of load-bearing support and recorded that limitation explicitly in the finding.
- Removed the unverified Commerce Commission disclosure PDF / LRP page from the load-bearing evidence path and replaced those claims with accessible Consumer Protection, Commerce Commission affordability/BNPL/register, FSPR, Companies Office, FMA, FSC, MSD, and FinCap sources.
- Replaced the non-independent FMA/FMA support for the financial-advice boundary with FMA plus Financial Services Council legal-guide support, and kept the confidence at Medium.
- Replaced the non-independent MoneyTalks support with MSD plus FinCap MoneyTalks support.
- Kept/downgraded confidence marks where the evidence is not enough for High, especially provenance and advice-boundary claims.
- Split the statutory continuing-disclosure obligation from the monthly freshness cadence, making clear that monthly checks are an internal product-governance recommendation, not a NZ statutory interval.
- Added existing NZ comparison-tool context using verified public pages: Money Compare, MoneyHub, Canstar, Finance.co.nz, and Glimp. I did not include unverified named examples where I could not find a stable public source in this pass.
- Added BNPL regulatory context: BNPL contracts became consumer credit contracts from 2 September 2024 unless exemptions apply.
- Added current regulator context: FMA says CCCFA regulatory responsibility transferred from the Commerce Commission to FMA on 1 July 2026.

Validation: `npm run validate` passes (All 18 finding/solution file(s) valid).

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the latest requested changes in commit aa71466 after rebasing onto current main.

What changed:
- Updated the provider-validation model for the 1 July 2026 CCCFA transfer: the minimum data model now requires FMA consumer-credit provider licence/deemed-licence status where applicable, and the Commerce Commission certified-lender register is framed only as transition provenance, not the current validation model. Added FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ citations.
- Reframed the personalised-ranking / advice-boundary point as a legal-risk control, not a settled consumer-credit-specific legal conclusion. The finding now says scenario outputs should avoid ranked or personalised "best product" recommendations at MVP stage until qualified legal review confirms the boundary.
- Added the advice-boundary citations directly to the executive answer so that the load-bearing "not ranked recommendations" point is cited where it appears.
- Changed the freshness cadence paragraph into an explicit unvalidated pilot assumption tied to change-based disclosure evidence, and cited the Consumer Protection CCCFA page inline. It no longer presents monthly checks as an NZ statutory requirement.
- Kept the earlier fixes from the first review: blocked Commerce Commission PDF/LRP sources are removed from load-bearing support, FinCap is used as the independent MoneyTalks source, BNPL regulatory treatment is noted, and existing NZ comparison-tool context is included.

Validation: npm run validate passes (All 18 finding/solution file(s) valid).

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested review fixes in the PR branch.

Changes made:
- Removed the Commerce Commission disclosure PDF and lender-responsibility-principles page from load-bearing support after following the fetch ladder: plain fetch returned 403 in review, and \ returned HTTP 404 for both on 2 July 2026. Replaced those supports with accessible Consumer Protection/FMA/FSPR/Companies Office/FinCap/FSC sources.
- Updated the provider-validation model for the 1 July 2026 CCCFA transfer: the finding now requires FMA consumer credit provider licence/deemed-licence status where applicable and treats Commerce Commission certification as transition provenance only, not the current validation model.
- Replaced non-independent MoneyTalks support with FinCap MoneyTalks as the second source.
- Reframed the personalised ranking/advice boundary as an unresolved legal-risk control, not a proven consumer-credit-specific legal conclusion; kept confidence at Medium and added the FMA/FSC citations inline in the executive answer and evidence.
- Split the freshness cadence into statutory change-based disclosure versus an explicit unvalidated internal pilot assumption; removed any implication that monthly checks are a NZ statutory rule.
- Added existing NZ comparison-tool context and current BNPL regulatory treatment using accessible sources; removed Canstar and MBIE citations after fetch issues.

CONFLICT (add/add): Merge conflict in research/findings/other/credit-help-front-door-requirements.md reported HEAD up to date.

issues.title: feat(web): high-level system diagram on /partners
issues.body:
Per Adam: a very-high-level diagram (HTML + existing shadcn/brand components) showing how the system works, on the `/partners` page.

**The flow:** a real problem → **becomes a stream** → *broken down, then broken down again* (max 2 levels, shown as a little tree) → **synthesise** → **ideate** → **build**.

- A **“human” badge** marks the synthesise / ideate / build gates — makes the “AI does the volume, people decide what matters” point visible at a glance.
- Responsive: horizontal on desktop, stacks with down-chevrons on mobile.
- New `SystemDiagram` component; dropped into Partners after “The idea in one minute”.

`tsc + vite build` clean.

issues.title: research: frame supermarket transparency stream (Part of #61)
issues.body:
Part of #61. Stream: #61. Adds a cited discover framing artifact and opened research child issues #74, #75, #76, #77, and #78 for the supermarket transparency stream.

## What this is

Part of #61
Stream: #61

**Stage:** Discover
**Domain:** other

## Summary

This frames the supermarket price and promotion transparency stream around a data-rights and methodology-first approach. It records the official Stats NZ aggregate baseline, confirms the Grocer/current-retailer data route is technically tractable through small smoke tests, and avoids retailer-naming claims until coverage, rights, law, and measurement standards are researched.

Fan-out opened:
- #74 data coverage, history depth, and reuse rights
- #76 representative shelf-level basket versus Stats NZ indexes
- #75 reference-pricing and promotion-cycle methodology
- #78 NZ law and regulator powers
- #77 current shopper tools and transparency gap

## Method checklist

- [x] Every factual claim has a source (inline)
- [x] Surprising / load-bearing claims have two independent sources (or I flagged where they do not)
- [x] Confidence marked High / Medium / Low
- [x] I stated what would change the conclusion and what I could not verify
- [x] No personal or identifying data; no overstated or fabricated claims
- [x] Files are in the right place and follow the relevant template

## For the reviewer

Push hardest on the data-rights/republication framing and whether the discover artifact belongs under `research/findings/other/` for this stage. I used that path because the validator only covers findings/solutions and stream overview docs are explicitly steward-owned.

issues.title: research: Map NZ law and regulator powers for misleading supermarket pricing and promotions
issues.body:
Part of #61. Stream: #61.

Question: What do the Fair Trading Act, Consumer Protection guidance, Commerce Commission guidance, and grocery-regulation materials say about misleading prices, specials, was/now comparisons, unit pricing, and the Grocery Commissioner’s enforcement or monitoring role?

Why it matters: any transparency artifact or policy brief must distinguish lawful price changes from potentially misleading representations, and must route evidence to the right regulator desk.

Where to look: Commerce Commission grocery pages at https://www.comcom.govt.nz/regulated-industries/grocery/; ComCom pricing guidance; MBIE Consumer Protection misleading-pricing pages; Grocery Industry Competition Act 2023; Fair Trading Act 1986; ComCom annual grocery reports and relevant cases.

Expected output: a finding in `research/findings/other/` with a practical enforcement-line summary, quoted legal tests only where necessary, and clear flags for where legal review is needed.

issues.title: research: Identify the supermarket transparency gap left by current NZ shopper tools
issues.body:
Part of #61. Stream: #61.

Question: What do existing NZ grocery comparison and price-history tools already show shoppers, and what transparency or education gap remains for a public-interest artifact?

Why it matters: The For Good Project should not duplicate a working shopper app. It should identify the smallest useful gap: education, methodology, public-interest reporting, regulator-ready evidence, or an accessibility layer.

Where to look: Grocer.nz, GroceryMate/Grosave/Price Pulse if available, retailer apps, Consumer NZ comparisons such as https://www.consumer.org.nz/shopping/supermarkets/the-3-best-grocery-price-comparison-tools-in-new-zealand, and Commerce Commission consumer-transparency priorities.

Expected output: a finding in `research/findings/other/` comparing features, price-history visibility, exportability, education content, accessibility, data provenance, and gaps for households and regulators.

issues.title: research: Compare representative shelf-level grocery prices with Stats NZ food price indexes
issues.body:
Part of #61. Stream: #61.

Question: For a small representative basket, how do real per-product/per-store prices in available grocery data compare with Stats NZ food-price index movement over the same period, and where do they diverge?

Why it matters: Stats NZ indexes are the official aggregate baseline, but household-facing transparency needs to explain what a shopper sees at shelf/product/store level without misusing index numbers as average prices.

Where to look: `.skills/skills/grocer-nz/SKILL.md` for product/store history; `.skills/skills/stats-nz/SKILL.md` for CPI/FPI/SPI baseline; Stats NZ methodology pages including https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/item/nz.govt.stats/749d8c27-1bed-45fb-b941-a1905aee632f.

Expected output: a finding in `research/findings/other/` with a defensible basket definition, exact product/store/date provenance, comparison method, charts/tables if useful, and explicit limits on representativeness.

issues.title: research: Define a defensible method for detecting supermarket reference-pricing and promotion cycles
issues.body:
Part of #61. Stream: #61.

Question: How should this project measure possible was/now reference-pricing patterns, promotional cycles, and short-lived price increases in supermarket price history without asserting retailer intent?

Why it matters: naming a retailer for misleading pricing is legally and reputationally sensitive. The stream needs a bulletproof method before publishing any product/store examples.

Where to look: `.skills/skills/grocer-nz/SKILL.md` history and current price fields; Commerce Commission pricing guidance at https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/dealing-with-typical-situations/selling-goods-and-services/pricing-your-products-or-services/; MBIE Consumer Protection misleading-pricing guidance; analogous regulator methods such as ACCC supermarket pricing cases only as comparison, not NZ law.

Expected output: a finding in `research/findings/other/` that proposes metrics, minimum evidence thresholds, date-window rules, confidence labels, and a publication standard for exact product/store/date examples.
