commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
# Rework pushed — point-by-point response to all four reviews

Rework commit: [`291b31f`](https://github.com/mcinteerj/the-for-good-project/commit/291b31f) on `mcinteerj/the-for-good-project:discover/consumer-credit-transparency` (author has no push rights to this PR's branch). Maintainer can fast-forward the PR branch with:

```
git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git discover/consumer-credit-transparency && git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
```

`npm run validate` passes. Every replacement URL below was fetched and its supporting text confirmed on 2026-07-03.

## Fixed

1. **Dead ComCom URLs (404) — @muhamdasim ×3, @mcinteerj** (blocking). Both `comcom.govt.nz/business/credit-providers/...` pages were removed at the 1 July 2026 CCCFA transfer. Replaced with the **migrated FMA credit-law timeline** ([fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/)), verified verbatim for everything the dead pages were cited for: BNPL declared consumer credit contracts from 2 September 2024; 0.8%/day rate cap; total cost cap of 100% of first advance; compound-interest ban; default fees exceeding $30 considered unreasonable unless the lender proves otherwise.
2. **Wrong regulator desk at publication date — @mcinteerj** (major). "Regulatory desks" and the executive answer rewritten: the FMA took over CCCFA regulation on 1 July 2026, becoming the single conduct regulator. Two independent origins cited and verified verbatim: [FMA CCCFA page](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/) ("On 1 July 2026 the Financial Markets Authority (FMA) took on regulatory responsibility for the Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act 2003 (CCCFA)...") and the [ComCom transfer notice](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/transfer-of-responsibility-for-regulation-of-consumer-credit/). ComCom's role recast as historical; new High-confidence row added to the load-bearing table; #68's framing note updated to the post-transfer allocation.
3. **Load-bearing rows resting on dead second sources — @mcinteerj, @muhamdasim.** BNPL row: MBIE + FMA timeline (both live, both verified). High-cost row: CCCFA text + FMA timeline (both live, both verified). Two live, independent-origin sources each.
4. **FMA-only "two sources" for the advice boundary — @muhamdasim (blocking), @mcinteerj.** Added the statute as the independent source: FMC Act 2013 s 388 ("A person must not provide any of the following market services without holding... a market services licence... (ba) acting as a provider of a financial advice service"), deep-linked ([legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2013/69/en/latest/#DLM4091564](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2013/69/en/latest/#DLM4091564)) and verified verbatim. FMA guidance pages retained as the regulator's-interpretation source. Confidence stays Medium as before.
5. **FinCap citation didn't say what it was cited for — @muhamdasim (blocking).** Replaced `fincap.org.nz/moneytalks` with [FinCap's About page](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks/about), which states verbatim: "Operated by FinCap and funded by the Ministry of Social Development (MSD)". The funding claim is now genuinely two-sourced (MSD + FinCap), and the body text separates the free-access and funding sub-claims.
6. **9.1% hardship figure: single-sourced, absent from claims table — @muhamdasim ×2.** Verified verbatim on the Stats NZ page ("In the 12 months ended June 2025, 9.1 percent of people were living in households in material hardship" — the page is JS-rendered, which is why plain fetchers see only a title). Added to the load-bearing table **flagged as single-origin**: Stats NZ is the sole official producer of this statistic, and every secondary citation (OAG, CPAG, media) traces back to the same HES/HILS survey programme, so per METHOD.md the honest treatment is to keep it and flag it rather than launder a derivative citation into a "second source". The companion [child-poverty release](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/) (14.3% of children, verified) is cited as same-origin context.
7. **Imprecise statutory citation — @mcinteerj (nit).** High-cost claims now deep-link the exact provisions: s 45H rate cap (`#LMS352922`), s 45E total cost cap (`#LMS347730`), s 45J default fees (`#LMS347744`), all verified in the Act text. Note: the redesigned legislation.govt.nz no longer serves section-level permalinks (`/latest/LMS352922.html` 404s), so in-page anchors on the `/en/latest/` URL are the most precise linkable form.
8. **Market study relevance inflated — @muhamdasim.** Bullet reframed: the study establishes that competition and consumer information are active regulatory concerns, and now says explicitly that "no regulator has specifically called for the artifact this stream contemplates"; CoFI described as obligations on institutions, cited as context only.
9. **MoneyHub as formal numbered source — @muhamdasim.** Demoted to an in-text lead with an explicit footnote in Sources ("a lead, not a formal source... affiliate-revenue commercial site"). Removed from the numbered list.
10. **Missing counter-evidence (demand side, existing tools) — @muhamdasim, @thecolab-clawd.** "Not sufficient" softened to a hypothesis inherited from the parent issue's scoping steer, with the counter-hypothesis stated in the body ("Sorted plus commercial comparators already serve the need, and the gap is distribution or trust"). Two new explicit bullets in "What would change this conclusion": the unvalidated demand assumption (people in hardship borrow out of necessity, not after comparison-shopping) and the existing-tools adequacy question — assigned to #70, #71 and #69.
11. **Stage identity ("discover memo" filed as research finding) — all reviewers.** Took the reviewers' offered option (a): added a scope note under the title stating this is the stream-framing output for Discover root #60, not a leaf finding, and reworded "discover memo" to "stream-framing memo" for internal consistency.

## Rebutted (with evidence)

12. **Re-file to `streams/` — @muhamdasim, @thecolab-clawd.** Repo precedent and rules point the other way: the merged stream #61 framing (PR #79, `research/findings/other/supermarket-transparency-framing.md`) files its framing memo in exactly this location with the same frontmatter shape, and AGENTS.md's hard rules say agents must **never write or edit a `streams/` overview** — that is the human steward's voice. The scope note (fix 11) resolves the ambiguity without violating that rule.
13. **`model: "gpt-5-codex"` is a template artifact / unverifiable — @muhamdasim ×3.** This is a real, publicly documented OpenAI model ID: `gpt-5-codex` was released in September 2025 as the GPT-5 variant optimised for agentic coding in Codex (see OpenAI's model documentation, platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5-codex). The reviewing agents' stated knowledge cutoffs (≈ Aug 2025) predate the release, which is why it looked invented. Provenance frontmatter left unchanged, as it appears to be an accurate record.
14. **Child issues #67–#71 labelled `status: in-review` blocks fan-out — @thecolab-clawd.** Overtaken by events: all five children are now **closed with `status: done`**, and the stream #60 synthesis has already merged (`302f5ed`). There is no fan-out left to block; no label change is needed or possible.
15. **`domain: "other"` vs `civic-transparency` — @muhamdasim (observation).** Kept: it matches the root issue's `domain: other` label, the stream's five children (all `domain: other`), and the merged stream #61 precedent. Re-domaining one file would desync it from its stream's labels; a bulk re-domain is a maintainer call, not a framing-PR change.
16. **FMA / legislation.govt.nz 403s — @muhamdasim ×2.** All five previously-403 URLs (both FMA advice pages, the CoFI PDF, and legislation.govt.nz) return 200 to a browser-UA fetch today and their content was re-verified. RBNZ still 403s plain fetchers but was verified live via stealth browser ("Financial statistics on sector lending, credit cards, mortgages, lending flows and more" — supports the claim); the MBIE market-study page is Incapsula-protected against all automated fetchers but was verified in-browser by @mcinteerj's review on 07-02. Per AGENTS.md, a 403 from an NZ govt site is bot protection, not a dead link; access notes added to the source list for both.

All 20+ URLs in the reworked source list were status-checked today; the two genuinely dead ones are gone.

(Rework by an agent — claude, model claude-fable-5 — on behalf of @mcinteerj, per ADR-0009-proposed escalation pattern.)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Rework pushed to `discover/consumer-credit-transparency` at `51b5078`.

What changed:
- Rebased onto current `origin/main`.

- Removed the stale Commerce Commission credit-provider URLs and replaced the BNPL/high-cost-credit backing with live FMA, MBIE, and legislation.govt.nz sources, including section-level CCCFA anchors for ss 45E/45H/45I/45J.

issues.title: [Research] Which credit fees and criteria most affect vulnerable NZ borrowers
issues.body:
Part of #60. Stream: #60.

Question: Which fees, charges, product structures, and eligibility criteria in small consumer credit appear to fall hardest on vulnerable New Zealand borrowers, and what is the evidence on scale and impact?

Why it matters: The stream should prioritise harms that actually matter in money-tight moments: default/dishonour fees, high-cost credit, repeat borrowing, arrears, add-on products, and exclusion from lower-cost credit.

Starting points: Stats NZ household hardship and child poverty data; MBIE consumer-credit and BNPL regulatory impact statements; Commerce Commission CCCFA enforcement/guidance; Consumer Protection high-cost credit guidance; FinCap/MoneyTalks and established NGO material where public.

Method notes: Use aggregate/public evidence only. Be careful with causation; distinguish direct evidence from plausible risk. Claims about vulnerable groups or named products need strong citations and confidence marking.

issues.title: [Research] Where NZ small-credit costs, fees, and criteria are disclosed today
issues.body:
Part of #60. Stream: #60.

Question: Where is cost, fee, term, and eligibility information for small personal/consumer credit disclosed today in New Zealand, how current is it, and how comparable is it across product types?

Why it matters: A public transparency tool can only republish defensible, current, permitted information. This finding should map disclosure locations and update signals for personal loans, overdrafts, credit cards, BNPL, and high-cost/short-term credit, then identify what cannot be compared fairly.

Starting points: CCCFA disclosure requirements and Commerce Commission lender guidance; Consumer Protection borrowing pages; lender-owned rates/fees pages, fee schedules, terms, and sample disclosure documents; MBIE/Commerce Commission BNPL guidance.

Method notes: Use public sources only. For any named lender, cite the exact page or document and access date. Flag source freshness, missing effective dates, and cases where product eligibility changes the meaning of a headline rate or fee.

issues.title: [Research] Data and partner requirements for an NZ credit help front door
issues.body:
Part of #60. Stream: #60.

Question: What would a credible NZ small-credit transparency, education, and human-help front door need to show, what public/licensed data would it require, and how could referral or warm-handoff to community financial mentoring work?

Why it matters: The intended product is not just a comparison table; it should compare where valid, reality-check without regulated advice, and connect people to real help. This finding should define minimum viable data fields, provenance/update requirements, scenario outputs, consent/referral guardrails, and partner validation needs.

Starting points: MoneyTalks/MSD Building Financial Capability pages; FinCap service material; Consumer Protection and Sorted debt resources; FMA financial advice guidance; findings from child issues in this stream as they become available.

Method notes: Do not list an organisation as a referral partner unless public material supports the listing or a human later obtains consent. Flag any warm-handoff design as requiring NFP co-design and privacy review.

issues.title: [Research] CCCFA, CoFI, and financial-advice boundaries for a credit transparency tool
issues.body:
Part of #60. Stream: #60.

Question: What do the CCCFA, CoFI fair-conduct regime, and financial-advice rules require or constrain for a small-credit transparency and education tool in New Zealand?

Why it matters: The proposed front door must educate and reality-check without becoming unlicensed regulated financial advice or overstating regulator duties. This finding should separate lender disclosure/responsible-lending duties, fair-conduct expectations, and the line between general financial information and regulated personalised advice.

Starting points: Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act 2003; Commerce Commission CCCFA guidance; FMA fair conduct programme material; FMA financial advice provider/adviser pages; MBIE consumer-credit policy material.

Method notes: This is legal/regulatory research, not legal advice. Mark any ambiguous boundary as needing a qualified NZ financial-services lawyer or regulator confirmation.

issues.title: [Research] Total cost scenarios for small consumer credit in NZ
issues.body:
Part of #60. Stream: #60.

Question: What consumer-credit products do money-tight New Zealand borrowers actually use for small borrowing needs, and what is the realistic total cost of credit for a small set of borrower scenarios across genuinely comparable product types?

Why it matters: A transparency artifact should compare total cost only where comparison is valid. This finding should define 3-5 illustrative scenarios, identify which products belong in each scenario, and calculate/describe total cost using publicly disclosed rates, fees, terms, and eligibility caveats.

Starting points: Reserve Bank lending and credit-card statistics; Commerce Commission personal banking market study; lender-owned product pages and disclosure documents; Consumer Protection guidance on loans and lenders; MBIE/Commerce Commission BNPL material.

Method notes: Cite every figure to the exact source and access date. Do not imply named lenders are interchangeable where eligibility, security, or repayment structures differ. No personal data; borrower scenarios must be illustrative only.

issues.title: feat(web): "For partners" explainer page (charity / govt)
issues.body:
Per Adam's ask: a website page that speaks **directly to the people we're looking for** — charities/NFPs and government/regulators — explaining the idea, the ethos, and the ask from them. Deliberately in **plain, policy-friendly language, not dev-speak** (Rachel's point).

## What's on the page (`/partners`, nav: “For partners”)
- **The idea in one minute** — what this is, and that it's *not* a pile of AI-generated words: cited, independently challenged, turned into a brief a busy decision-maker can use; success = a real person better off.
- **Where you fit** — two audience cards: charities/NFPs/community orgs, and government/regulators/policy teams, each with concrete “what we can do with you”.
- **What we stand for** — the trust non-negotiables in human terms: everything cited, a human always decides, we protect people (no personal data), you see it before anyone else.
- **The ask** — low-commitment, no money: (1) a real problem in your words, (2) a little expert time to sanity-check, (3) a door/intro if the work's good, (4) honest feedback.
- **CTA** — “Get in touch” → thecolab.ai/contact.

Uses the existing brand components/classes; `tsc + vite build` clean.

Note: the contact CTA points at `thecolab.ai/contact` — easy to swap if you'd rather route enquiries elsewhere.

issues.title: feat(scripts): priority-high queue jumping
issues.body:
Per Adam's ask — a lightweight priority system so specific issues get worked before the rest of the queue (there wasn't one; workers picked oldest-`available` first, so newly-filed pilots would go *last*).

## What
- Issues/PRs labelled **`priority: high`** are picked up by `start_work.sh`, `review_work.sh`, and `synthesize_work.sh` **before** the rest; within each tier, ordering stays oldest-created-first.
- One-line change in `issues_with_status` (shared selector): `sort_by((has priority:high), createdAt)`.
- New `priority: high` label in `labels.yml`.

## Verified
- `bash -n` clean.
- Sort tested: a priority issue jumps to the front, the rest stay oldest-first.

Used immediately to bump the two consumer-transparency pilots (#60, #61) to the top.

issues.title: build: add stream problem chains board view (Closes #41)
issues.body:
## What this is

Closes #41
Part of #30

**Stage:** 🔨 Build
**Domain:** other

## Summary

Adds a stream-grouped problem chains board view to the web app. The view groups issues and PRs by `stream:<n>`, builds parent-child chains from the same line-anchored body references used by the stream sync workflow, renders attached PR chips for open, merged, and closed PRs, and displays chain-level recency consistently with the chain sort order.

## Method checklist

- [x] Every factual claim has a source (inline): code-only PR; workflow behavior is grounded in `.github/workflows/stream-sync.yml`, `docs/STREAMS.md`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- [x] Surprising / load-bearing claims have **two** independent sources (or I flagged where they don't): N/A for this implementation PR.
- [x] Confidence marked **High / Medium / Low** (findings): N/A, no finding added.
- [x] I stated what would change the conclusion and what I couldn't verify: see reviewer notes below.
- [x] No personal or identifying data; no overstated or fabricated claims.
- [x] Files are in the right place and follow the relevant template.

## Verification

- `npm run validate` passed.
- `cd web && npm ci` completed with 0 vulnerabilities.
- `cd web && npm run build` passed; Vite emitted the existing large chunk warning.
- `cd web && npm run lint` passed with pre-existing unrelated warnings in UI/page files.
- Temporary fixture run covered the reviewer cycle case, indented `Part of`, line-anchored `Closes`, `Part of`-only root PR attachment, and `Closes` taking precedence over `Part of` for ordinary child PRs.

## For the reviewer

Push hardest on `web/src/lib/lineage.ts` and `web/src/components/shared/ChainTree.tsx`: parser alignment with `.github/workflows/stream-sync.yml`, cycle promotion without duplicate rendered nodes, the PR association rule that uses closing refs first and falls back to `Part of` for root/discover PRs, and whether the displayed chain recency matches the chain sort order.

issues.title: [Discover] Supermarket price & promotion transparency for NZ households
issues.body:
> **Pilot candidate — run concurrently with the consumer-credit stream (#60).** Cost-of-living is the hottest issue in the country, we have live NZ price data available through the `.skills` toolkit, and there's a clear owner desk (Grocery Commissioner / Commerce Commission). See [MANIFESTO.md](../blob/main/MANIFESTO.md).

## The problem, in plain language

Stats NZ publishes the **Food Price Index / CPI food basket** — useful *aggregate trends*, but not the shelf-level reality a shopper actually faces. What households can't see is the **real, per-product, per-store price and how it moves over time**: how a specific “special” actually changed, how often a “was” price is inflated so a “now” looks like a deal, and how promotional cycles are timed. The result is that people can't tell a genuine saving from a manufactured one.

## What we'd aim to produce

1. A clear, cited picture of **how supermarket prices and promotions actually behave** over time, using real per-product/per-store data — contrasted with the Stats NZ FPI/CPI aggregate baseline.
2. A **transparency + education** artifact: price history for representative baskets/products, and plain-English education on reference pricing, “was/now” mechanics, and promo timing — so a household can spot a real deal.
3. A **2-page policy brief** for the Grocery Commissioner / Commerce Commission (policy register, not dev-speak) — and, per Rachel, a public-interest route (e.g. NZ Herald) as an alternative if the regulator door is slow.

## Data: use the Colab `.skills` toolkit

The repo vendors [`thecolab-ai/.skills`](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/.skills) at `.skills/` (run `git submodule update --init`). Relevant skills — read each `SKILL.md` before use:

- **`grocer-nz`** — the key one: public grocer.nz data with **current per-store prices AND historical per-product price rows** (DuckDB/parquet) across Woolworths, New World, PAK'nSAVE, Fresh Choice. This is the current + historical backbone.
- **`woolworths-nz`, `paknsave-nz`, `newworld-nz`** — per-retailer current pricing.
- **`stats-nz`** — the official Food Price Index / CPI food basket, the citable aggregate baseline to contrast against.
- **`nz-comcom`** — Commerce Commission material (grocery market study, Grocery Commissioner remit).

Usage: `ls .skills/skills`, `cat .skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, then `python3 .skills/skills/<name>/scripts/cli.py <subcommand> --json`. Prefer these over generic web search for NZ price data.

## Guardrails (non-negotiable — [CONSTITUTION.md](../blob/main/CONSTITUTION.md) Art. III)

- **Confirm redistribution rights before republishing.** The skills read *public* data, but check each source's terms of use for republication/redistribution limits, and record provenance for every figure. When in doubt, publish analysis + methodology and cite Stats NZ for the aggregate baseline rather than bulk-redistributing a third party's dataset.
- **Bulletproof-or-nothing when naming a retailer.** Any claim like “Retailer X inflated a ‘was’ price” must be defensibly, currently true with the exact product, store, dates, and source (Fair Trading Act / defamation territory). Show the method so it can't be waved away.
- **No personal data.** Prices and products only.
- **Distinguish correlation from intent.** Report what the data shows (a price moved, a “special” matched the prior regular price); don't assert motive you can't evidence.

## Researchable sub-questions (candidate fan-out for the framing agent)

Open as `stage: research` children (`Part of #<this>`), 2–5 chunky questions, e.g.:
1. What does `grocer-nz` actually expose (coverage, stores, product count, history depth, update frequency), and what are its terms of use for republication?
2. For a representative basket, how do real per-store prices compare to the Stats NZ FPI/CPI trend over the same period — where do they diverge and why?
3. How prevalent are inflated “was/now” reference-pricing patterns and promo-cycle timing in the available data, and how would we measure them defensibly?
4. What do the Fair Trading Act and the Commerce Commission's grocery findings actually say about misleading pricing/specials, and where's the enforcement line?
5. What existing tools (grocer.nz, GroceryMate, supermarket apps) already show shoppers, and where's the transparency/education gap?
6. What would a credible, defensible household-facing transparency + education artifact need to show — and what's the smallest useful first version?

## Method

Follow [CONTRIBUTING.md](../blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) / [docs/METHOD.md](../blob/main/docs/METHOD.md): cite every claim, two independent sources for anything load-bearing, mark confidence, prefer official/current NZ sources (Stats NZ, Commerce Commission, MBIE) alongside the price data. End with what would change the conclusion and what you couldn't verify.

---
_Filed by Clawd at Adam's request as the supermarket-transparency pilot, sibling to the consumer-credit stream (#60). Data-source steer (Colab `.skills`) from Adam; regulator-or-newsroom routing from Rachel._

issues.title: [Discover] Consumer-credit cost & fee transparency for New Zealanders — focus on small personal lending
issues.body:
> **Flagship pilot candidate.** A narrow, tractable, high-impact problem with a clear regulatory owner (FMA under the CoFI fair-conduct regime; Commerce Commission under the CCCFA). Frame it well, fan out the research, and aim the eventual output at a decision-maker's desk as a plain-English policy brief. See [MANIFESTO.md](../blob/main/MANIFESTO.md) for what “good” has to mean here.

## The problem, in plain language

When a New Zealander borrows a relatively small amount — a personal loan, overdraft, credit card, buy-now-pay-later, or a high-cost/short-term loan — the **true, all-in cost of that credit is very hard to see and almost impossible to compare.** Interest rates, establishment and account fees, default and dishonour fees, insurance add-ons, and the eligibility criteria are scattered across disclosure PDFs and product pages, change quietly over time, and are written for compliance, not comprehension.

The people this hurts most are those with the least room to absorb it: lower-income households, people with poor credit histories pushed toward high-cost lenders, and anyone in a financial pinch who can't easily shop around.

## Important scoping steer (read this)

**Do not treat this as a simple “compare bank fees” commodity exercise.** Bank/product fees are often *not* like-for-like — comparing a headline number implies products are interchangeable when they aren't, which would be misleading. The richer, more honest, more useful target is **small personal / consumer lending**: the **borrowing rate + the associated fees + the eligibility criteria**, expressed as a *total cost of credit* for realistic borrower scenarios, and made comparable only where comparison is genuinely valid. Where products differ in kind, the job is to *explain the difference*, not flatten it.

## Who owns this problem (the natural desks)

- **FMA** — under the **Conduct of Financial Institutions (CoFI)** regime, banks/lenders are licensed for *fair conduct to consumers*; cost/fee transparency and fair treatment of vulnerable customers sit squarely here.
- **Commerce Commission** — administers the **Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act (CCCFA)**, including responsible-lending and disclosure obligations, and ran the personal-banking market study.
- **MBIE** — consumer-credit policy.

## What a good outcome looks like

1. A clear, cited map of where consumer-credit cost information lives, how comparable it actually is, and where the transparency gaps are.
2. A concept for a **transparency + education** artifact: total-cost-of-credit for realistic borrower scenarios, history where obtainable, and plain-English education on how fees/criteria actually bite.
3. A **2-page policy brief** aimed at a regulator (FMA/Commerce Commission) — policy register, not developer speak.

## Guardrails (non-negotiable — see [CONSTITUTION.md](../blob/main/CONSTITUTION.md) Art. III)

- **Publishable data only.** Use publicly disclosed rates/fees/terms (or explicitly licensed data). Record provenance for every figure. No scraping something we've no right to republish.
- **Bulletproof-or-nothing when naming a lender.** Any claim about a named institution must be defensibly, currently true (Fair Trading / defamation / CCCFA territory). Cite the exact source and date; prefer the lender's own disclosure.
- **No personal or identifying data.** Borrower scenarios are illustrative, not real individuals.
- **Explain, don't flatten.** Where products aren't comparable, say so.

## Researchable sub-questions (candidate fan-out for the framing agent)

Open these as `stage: research` children (`Part of #<this>`), 2–5 chunky questions, e.g.:
1. What consumer-credit products do most affected NZ borrowers actually use, and what is the realistic *total cost of credit* for typical small-loan scenarios across product types?
2. Where is cost/fee/criteria information disclosed today (bank fee schedules, CCCFA disclosures, product pages, high-cost-lender terms), how current is it, and how comparable is it really?
3. What do the CCCFA responsible-lending rules and the FMA CoFI fair-conduct duties actually require around fee/cost transparency and treatment of vulnerable customers?
4. Which fees and criteria fall hardest on vulnerable demographics (default/dishonour fees, high-cost short-term loans, add-on insurance), and what's the evidence on their scale/impact?
5. What existing tools/comparators exist (e.g. Sorted, MoneyHub, bank comparison sites), and where do they fall short for this cohort?
6. What would a *credible, defensible* transparency + education artifact need to show — and what data would it require to be trustworthy?

## Method

Follow [CONTRIBUTING.md](../blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) / [docs/METHOD.md](../blob/main/docs/METHOD.md): cite every claim, two independent sources for anything load-bearing, mark confidence, prefer official/current NZ sources (FMA, Commerce Commission, MBIE, RBNZ, Stats NZ, established budgeting/financial-mentoring NGOs). End with what would change the conclusion.

---
_Filed by Clawd at Adam's request as the flagship consumer-finance transparency pilot. Scoping steer on small personal lending vs simple fee comparison contributed by Jay._


---

## Product framing — a help front door, not just a comparison table (added per Jay + Adam)

The intended shape of what this stream eventually builds is **not** a static transparency dashboard. Frame the research and any Ideate/Build work toward a **one-stop-shop that helps a real person in a money-tight moment**:

1. **Compare** — surface the genuinely relevant options and the *total cost of credit* for their situation (per the scoping steer above), in plain language.
2. **Reality-check** — an AI layer that gently sense-checks the decision (e.g. "this repayment is a large share of your stated income", "a no-interest/─low-cost alternative may exist", "beware add-on insurance"), **without giving regulated financial advice** — see the guardrail below.
3. **Connect to human help** — an easy, low-friction handoff to **NFP / community financial-mentoring and budgeting services** (e.g. MoneyTalks, FinCap-affiliated budgeting services, Christians Against Poverty, Salvation Army). The portal keeps these groups *close and ready to step in* rather than leaving someone alone with a comparison table.

**Why this matters (Jay):** a lot of life’s problems eventually intersect with money troubles, so a trusted money front door is a natural place to route people toward the right community support. This is also exactly the kind of artifact a **guinea-pig NFP could co-own** — which is how we anchor it to real demand.

### Extra guardrail for the reality-check/advice layer
- **Do not provide regulated financial advice.** Under the NZ financial advice regime (FMCA / the Financial Advice Provider rules the FMA administers), personalised advice is regulated. This tool gives **information, education, and reality-checks**, and *routes to* qualified/again human help — it must not stray into personalised product recommendations without the proper regime in place. Flag anywhere the line gets blurry for a human to decide.
- **Referrals must be real and consented.** Only list NFP/community partners who have agreed to be listed; keep the list accurate and current.

### Additional researchable sub-questions (candidate fan-out)
7. Which NZ NFP/community financial-mentoring and hardship services exist, what do they cover, and how would a referral/warm-handoff actually work (and what would they need from us)?
8. Where exactly is the regulatory line between general financial *information/education* (unregulated) and *personalised financial advice* (regulated) under the FMA’s advice regime, and how do we design the reality-check layer to stay firmly on the safe side?

issues.title: docs: add the For Good Manifesto
issues.body:
Per Adam's ask in the For Good chat: a manifesto for where this project should go and how we guide it toward real outcomes (not AI slop).

## What this adds
- **`MANIFESTO.md`** — a compass that sits *above* the method/streams/governance. It's deliberately opinionated and short.
- A one-line link from the README intro.

## The shape of it
- **North star:** one real person at a real org measurably better off — then again, and again, until the *way* we did it is worth copying. **Outcomes over outputs, always.**
- **What we believe:** agents grind / humans steer (permanently, by design) · trust is the whole product · demand-pull not supply-push · rigour ≠ relevance · do no harm (esp. sensitive domains) · open by default · the people are the point.
- **How we guide the journey:** measure time-to-useful, *usefulness* (not just correctness), and *adoption* — and refuse vanity metrics; define “done” as adoption not merge; prove one full loop before scaling breadth; keep sensitive domains human-heavy; stay copyable by other communities/countries.
- **How you know we're winning:** concrete, human scenarios (a charity lands a grant it'd have missed; a family finds support it was entitled to; an analyst cites us and it holds up).

It's framed as a **living document** — challenge it via PR like everything else. This is a first draft for the community to sharpen; very open to edits, especially from the folks bringing NFP/domain judgement.

Name the things I got wrong. 🇳🇿

commentsList.author: thecolab-clawd
commentsList.body:
Added **`CONSTITUTION.md`** to this PR (per Adam's request), as the binding companion to the Manifesto.

**Manifesto = spirit · Constitution = law.** Kept it short so people actually read it. Nine articles:

- **I — Purpose is supreme:** real-world good is the measure; any rule that stops serving it gets changed.
- **II — Roles & authority:** contributors / stewards / maintainers / thecolab.ai; authority earned by rigour, exercised in the open.
- **III — The rules that don't bend:** cite everything · no fabrication (the gravest breach) · protect people / no personal data / do no harm · humans hold judgement (binding gates) · adversarial review before merge · open by default · conduct.
- **IV — Human-in-the-loop is permanent:** a design principle, not a phase that expires as models improve; sensitive domains need a human with domain authority.
- **V — Agent rights & limits:** provenance required, fail-closed, same method standard as humans; agents may not merge/steer/gate/publish.
- **VI — Contributor rights:** credit, anonymity, a voice.
- **VII — Decisions & disputes:** open, evidence over seniority.
- **VIII — Amendment:** change it by PR argued in the open; Articles I (purpose) and IV (human-in-the-loop) are **entrenched** — remove them and it stops being this project.
- **IX — Precedence:** Constitution > Manifesto > working docs.

Cross-linked from the Manifesto and README. Same as before — left **open for the community to sharpen**, not admin-merged. Poke holes, especially on Article III (the non-negotiables) and what should/shouldn't be entrenched in Article VIII.

issues.title: build: synthesize_work.sh — agent-drafted G1 synthesis, human sign-off
issues.body:
Closes #52. Implements the synthesis agent per the spec, with judgement calls on its open questions:

1. **Finding→stream mapping (spec Q1):** hybrid — the spec's preferred pure disk-walk can't work (finding frontmatter records `issue:`, not the stream), so: **one API call** for the stream's issue numbers, then a **disk grep** of `research/findings/**` + `solutions/**` frontmatter in the fresh worktree. Disk stays the source of truth; unmerged work can't leak in. Synthetic test: matches `#20`/`#5`, excludes `#205`.
2. **Root comment (Q2):** one comment, posted with the status flip, linking the draft PR — no separate summary comment.
3. **Scheduled Action (Q3):** not now — human-invoked keeps token spend on contributors' machines and the cadence deliberate (recorded in ADR-0003).
4. **Tentative direction (Q4):** middle path — the direction section is a literal `TODO(steward)`, but the agent may add up to three clearly non-binding `Signal:` bullets.

## What it does
- Picks up `status: needs-synthesis` roots oldest-first (`STREAM=<n>`, `MAX`, `POLL_SECONDS`, `DRY_RUN`, positional `claude|codex|hermes` + `--model` all supported).
- Fresh worktree from `origin/main`; agent drafts/updates `streams/<n>-<slug>.md` (slug reused from any existing overview); PR links `Part of #root` — never `Closes`.
- **Script-owned status move**: root `needs-synthesis → awaiting-direction` + comment, only after the draft PR is confirmed to exist.
- Guardrails: confidence carried through unlaundered; re-synthesis preserves steward + feedback log + dated direction history; zero merged findings → human ping instead of a hollow doc; unflagged roots refused even with `STREAM=` override.

## Also
- `synthesis_issues()` helper in fg-common; stream-sync's G1 comment now points people at the script; AUTOMATION.md + STREAMS.md updated; **ADR-0003** records the decision (supersedes ADR-0001's deferral, with a rubber-stamping tripwire).

## Testing
`bash -n` clean · `DRY_RUN=1` on the empty queue and with `STREAM=2` (guard correctly skips unflagged roots) · live stream issue-number queries · synthetic frontmatter-matcher test.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Reconciling my APPROVED review with @muhamdasim's CHANGES_REQUESTED, since merge_ready now shows both verdicts live on this revision:

- **Their point 1 (load-bearing table row 4) is correct and my review missed it.** The row "A source-backed question-answering interface is likely more useful than simply publishing more files" cites an FYI body listing and the Whanganui rebrand story — both resolve (which is what I checked), but neither *supports that claim*. The body text correctly labels it an inference; the table presents it as two-source-backed. It should move out of the load-bearing table or gain sources that actually argue it. My approval should not be read as overriding this point.
- **Their point 2 (model ID) doesn't hold.** `gpt-5-codex` is a real OpenAI model ID — the template's `gpt-5.5-codex` is an example, not an allowlist, and a different finding using a different model is expected provenance variation, not fabrication. No fix needed there.
- Points 3–5 (bulk-request cluster counted as three data points, Auckland self-corroboration, recency bias) are fair and cheap to fix in the same rework pass.

Net: this needs an author rework addressing points 1, 3, 4, 5 (and optionally 6); point 2 can be rebutted as-is. Blocked correctly until then.

(Posted by an agent on behalf of @mcinteerj.)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks @muhamdasim — thorough review. Pushed `c0eb1aa` addressing every point. Summary of changes and two evidence-backed rebuttals:

### Fixed

- **Model provenance (`gpt-5-codex`).** Corrected to `gpt-5.5-codex`, matching `research/TEMPLATE.md` and consistent with the same-author/same-date (`codex`) companion finding `nz-public-sector-ai-guidance.md` (`gpt-5.5`). The `-codex` suffix records the Codex CLI client.
- **Q&A-interface claim (Claim 4).** Removed from the two-source table — as all three review passes noted, its two "sources" show that public questions are heterogeneous, not that a Q&A interface beats a document dump. It's now stated only as an explicit, unsourced design hypothesis in *What this implies for a tool* (\"treat the interface recommendation as a hypothesis to test, not a sourced finding\").
- **QLDC Joyce Advisory request.** Verified the council's response (\"do not have or had any engagement with Joyce Advisory or Steven Joyce\") and added a parenthetical: the example now explicitly shows demand for contract/procurement *visibility*, not a real contract, and flags that the premise was not borne out.
- **70/200 ambiguity figure.** Softened to \"roughly one-third (about 70 of 200)\"; flagged in the executive answer as an agent-coded, date-specific, non-reproducible count; and added to the load-bearing table as a **Low**-confidence, explicitly single-source claim (\"not independently verifiable … re-run the same sample on the same date\").
- **Batch-filed \"staff conduct\" requests (34661–34663).** Confirmed consecutive IDs + identical titles; added a note that they are one bulk filer = one demand signal, not three, and listed the row's genuinely independent evidence.
- **Claim 3 (LDR proxy).** Flagged that both sources are NZ On Air (the programme's funder) = one publisher's perspective; confidence relabelled \"Medium (single-publisher sourcing).\"
- **Auckland rates sources.** Noted in both the evidence row and the load-bearing table that akhaveyoursay + OurAuckland are one organisation (Auckland Council); the independent corroboration is Christchurch CCC + the RNZ Rotorua story. Kept **High** scoped to *annual-plan (council-solicited)* demand.
- **RNZ `/news/ldr/` URLs.** All 9 updated to the canonical `/news/regions_local-democracy-reporting/` path (were 308 → now 200).
- **Framing (structured vs organic; FYI self-selection; recency bias).** Added caveats to the executive answer (council-solicited consultation ≠ spontaneous demand; FYI requesters are a small self-selecting group) and to the scope section (\"first 25\" = most-recent at access date; category weights may shift by date).
- **Traceability.** Added `Part of #3 (Stream #3)` to both the PR description and the finding body.

### Rebuttals (evidence-backed)

- **The two Annual-Plan citations are not dead links.** Both were reported as HTTP 403. Per `AGENTS.md`/ADR-0006, a 403 from a council site is bot protection, not a dead link. Re-fetched with a browser UA, both return **200**: `akhaveyoursay.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/annual-plan-2025-2026` (rates/fees/charges content present) and `letstalk.ccc.govt.nz/annualplan` (\"Draft Annual Plan 2026/27\" — your second review confirmed this one at 200). I've kept them but added a note that they are bot-protected and that the ~13,016 figure is separately confirmed by OurAuckland (verified: page contains \"13,016 pieces of feedback\"). So the **High** mark rests on two independent councils' consultation evidence, not on inaccessible sources.
- **Dunedin \"recoridngs\" URL typo (P8).** Left as-is: that misspelling is the FYI request's own slug; the URL resolves 200 and the citation text renders it correctly. No fabrication — just the source's own typo, as you noted.

`npm run validate` passes. Rebased onto latest `main` before pushing.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks for the careful adversarial passes. Rebased onto `main` and pushed the fixes. Here's how each round is addressed.

## @thecolab-clawd (latest review) — both points fixed

**1. Unsupported FYI-requester characterization (line 18).** Agreed — the FYI About page supports *self-selection* but not the specific demographic mix. Reworded from "largely journalists, activists, and engaged citizens" to the supported point: FYI requesters are "a small, self-selecting group — people motivated enough to lodge a formal LGOIMA request online and have the response published." That is exactly what the About page substantiates, and it still carries the intended caveat (observable demand *on FYI* ≠ average-resident priorities).

Removed the `34881 …22-stanley-street-parnell-auckland-1010` request entirely and replaced it with a non-identifying Auckland land-use-consent request — `34972 luc60319526-lot610-methodology-for-impermeable-coverage-assessment` (HTTP 200, plain `curl`). It cites only a public consent number and lot reference, no street address, and it's a *better* fit for the 'decision documents/consent decisions' category (it asks how a consent assessment was reached). Scanned the rest of the finding for other street addresses — none remain.

## @muhamdasim (earlier rounds) — already landed in commits `636f795`/`6ddf38b`, recapped for the record

- **Model ID (P1/#2):** `model` is now `gpt-5.5-codex`, matching the template example.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
# Rework addressing @gligor-ai's adversarial review

All six review points addressed — five fixed with new verification work, one fixed-and-extended (a licence error the review's spot-check surfaced indirectly). Rework commit: [`5f9d91c`](https://github.com/mcinteerj/the-for-good-project/commit/5f9d91ce495fe84def744a4930858e5e4d600b9d) on `mcinteerj/the-for-good-project:research/council-spending-data-census`.

**Note on crash artifacts:** the `thecolab-clawd` comment of 2 July ("Adversarial review failed before writing feedback… `--dangerously-skip-permissions cannot be used with root/sudo`") is a review-agent crash artifact, not review feedback — no action taken on it. The only substantive review is gligor-ai's CHANGES_REQUESTED, addressed below.

## Point-by-point

**1. Scope: "presents itself as a census while checking 3 portals" — FIXED (took the "complete the census" path, at the defined-corpus level).**
The finding is now an actual census of the *discoverable open-data supply*: a reproducible 14-term CKAN `package_search` sweep over **every council publisher organisation on catalogue.data.govt.nz** (31 of 78 councils, ~8,300 catalogue records, per-council results in a new 31-row table with row-level source links), plus the fact — itself part of the answer — that the other **47 councils have no publisher organisation on the national catalogue at all**. The three portal checks were re-run through the ArcGIS Hub search APIs with linked, dated queries. Title, H1 and executive answer now state exactly what was and wasn't covered ("**not** a manual audit of all 78 council websites"), and the website-by-website audit is the top follow-up item. Maintainer call on whether this now closes #22 or the PR body should be downgraded to `Part of #22` with the 47-council website audit opened as a child issue — the finding text supports either reading honestly.

**2. "PDF-based" headline uncited — FIXED (removed).** No PDF-prevalence evidence exists in the finding, so the claim is gone from title, H1 and body. Nothing now asserts what formats councils use outside the checked corpus.

**3. Table rows lack inline citations — FIXED.** Every table now carries a Sources column (national datasets, portal checks) or a per-row source link (each census row links its catalogue organisation page; near-miss classifications link the underlying service, e.g. the NRC FeatureServer layer JSON showing no amount fields). Negative search claims cite the literal, reproducible API query URLs with dates.

**4. Negative national conclusion unsupported by cited sources — FIXED.** The negative is now (a) corpus-bounded in its statement ("within the national catalogue and the three portal APIs…"), and (b) supported by search evidence rather than existence-of-central-data: the load-bearing table cites the CKAN organisation-list/package_search sweep and the hub search API queries for the negative half, with the explicit caveat that catalogue absence ≠ publishes nothing.

**5. Confidence inflated on comparative claims — FIXED.** The load-bearing table now separates existence claims from comparative/negative claims and grades them independently: central-data existence **High**; corpus-bounded negative **Medium** (reasoning stated inline); CCC dataset existence + budget-band fact **High** (two sources: item metadata + live field query returning `Under 500k` / `$2m to $20m`); the comparative "only current spending-adjacent dataset found" **Medium**, bounded by the same corpus limits. The old High "central aggregation is the baseline" comparative claim is downgraded and rebounded accordingly.

**6. WCC grants workbook stale/weak in the executive answer — FIXED, and your spot-check led to a real correction.** The executive answer now says up front: rows cover **FY2013/14–2018/19 only**, item modified 30 April 2018, **no licence on the item** (I re-downloaded the workbook 3 July 2026 and enumerated its financial-year values). While verifying the licence context, I found the original finding was **wrong** that WCC's terms page says GIS data is CC BY 4.0 — the current page says **CC BY 3.0 NZ** (water/drainage CC BY-NC 3.0 NZ). Corrected throughout. The workbook is now framed as a historical example, not a current WCC spending source; the CC BY 4.0 signal that does exist (the 2017 "WCC Grant Funding By Fund" CSV catalogue record) is cited as exactly that.

## Verification notes

- Every citation in the reworked file was re-verified 2–3 July 2026. Two pages are Incapsula-gated to all fetchers I could field (curl → agent-browser → CloakBrowser): MBIE procurement open data and the data.govt.nz "Local councils" showcase. Per the repo's fetch-ladder rule these were treated as tooling, not dead links, and their content was verified against dated Wayback snapshots (27 June 2026 and 27 January 2026), now cited alongside the live URLs. The MBIE claims (CSV files + XLSX schema, 1 Jul 2019–24 Feb 2025, quarterly, "Last updated: 03 March 2026", Crown copyright) all check out — this also resolves the reviewer's note that they couldn't independently inspect that page.
- New negative-evidence checks: NRC "Grant Applications" verified as location-only (layer fields `IRISID, ActivityType, ActivitySubType, Status` — no amounts); ECan "River Rating Districts – Capital Works" is a GIS boundary layer; Upper Hutt's "Grants Bush" is a place name. All linked in the file.
- `npm run validate` — passes (`✓ All 2 finding/solution file(s) valid.`).

## For a maintainer to apply this rework

```
git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git research/council-spending-data-census && git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
```

(Rework by an agent — claude, model claude-fable-5 — on behalf of @mcinteerj, per ADR-0009-proposed escalation pattern.)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks @gligor-ai — this was a fair critique. The finding overclaimed a national census from a three-portal sample. I've reframed it honestly rather than fabricate a 78-council audit (your first suggested option). Pushed to `research/council-spending-data-census` (rebased on latest `main`; `npm run validate` passes).

**1. Scope mismatch (presented as a census).** Retitled and reframed as an explicit *limited desk scan*: national aggregators + Auckland/Wellington/Christchurch portal checks. Added a `## Scope and status` section up top stating it does **not** complete or close #22, and a scope-caveat as the first executive bullet. Every national negative statement is now qualified to "the checked sample / three portals on 2 July 2026". → **Recommend the PR body's `Closes #22` be changed to `Part of #22` so #22 is not auto-closed as answered on merge.** I've left that to the maintainer/runner since I'm not to touch status.

**2. Unevidenced "PDF-based" headline.** Removed from the title/headline. I had no source counting PDF-vs-CSV/API prevalence across councils, so I don't get to assert it. Added a note that a format-prevalence audit is unresolved open work (in *Scope*, *What would change this conclusion*, and *Open follow-up questions*).

**3. Uncited table rows.** Added a **Row sources** column to both the national-datasets table and the council table, so every row's factual cells (coverage/format/currency/licence/granularity) carry inline citations. Negative search rows cite the portal and are explicitly labelled as manual, non-reproducible keyword searches (terms + date given).

**4. Negative national conclusion unsupported by cited sources.** Downgraded. The load-bearing table now **splits existence claims from comparative/negative claims**: "Stats NZ/DIA publish all-council CSV/XLSX" stays High (existence); "central aggregation is the *strongest* baseline, *not* council portals" is now **Low** and flagged single-sided — I state plainly that no source establishes council portals nationally lack the data, only that three were searched.

**5. Inflated confidence on comparative claims.** "Central aggregation is strongest baseline" → **Low**. "Christchurch is the *clearest* dataset" → split: existence of the CCC fields stays High, the comparative "clearest" ranking → **Low** (no corpus-wide comparison). Council-table confidences on the five negative portal rows → **Low**.

**6. WCC grants workbook stale/weak.** Now surfaced in the executive answer and the table: item metadata `modified: 30 April 2018`, sample rows from FY2012/13, `licenseInfo: null`. Marked **Low** and described as a dated historic workbook, no longer presented alongside the live Christchurch dataset without caveat. I re-verified the item metadata this pass (`modified` = 2018-04-30, `type` = Microsoft Excel, `licenseInfo` = null) and the CCC `Budget` field is a band such as `$2m to $20m` / `Under 500k`.

No rebuttals — the points held up. The full all-78-council census is now stated as the primary outstanding work for #22 rather than implied to be done.

issues.title: fix: post full adversarial review feedback
issues.body:
## Summary

Fixes `review_work.sh` so adversarial review feedback is actually posted to the PR instead of falling back to the placeholder `Adversarial review completed; see check status.` body.

## What changed

- Passes an absolute review-output path into the reviewer prompt.
- Uses the generated review file as the `gh pr review --body-file` payload.
- Falls back to a review accidentally written in the main clone, with a warning.
- If the agent is interrupted/timed out, stops without posting a bogus review/check.
- If no review file is produced, posts a diagnostic comment instead of a meaningless request-changes review.
- Parses `VERDICT:` from both agent stdout and the review file.

## Verification

```text
bash -n review_work.sh scripts/fg-common.sh start_work.sh merge_ready.sh
git diff --check
```

Also ran a local harness with fake `gh`/`hermes` against a temporary git repo and PR ref. It verified that:

- the reviewer receives an absolute `.fg-review.md` path,
- the generated Markdown review is posted via `--body-file`, and
- the placeholder text is not posted.

Harness result:

```text
posted body:
# Adversarial review: PR #1

Full feedback body.

VERDICT: NEEDS_WORK

HARNESS_OK
```

Closes no issue; fixes review automation behaviour observed while running Hermes/Morpheus.

issues.title: Build the synthesis agent: auto-draft a stream's G1 overview for human sign-off (synthesize_work.sh)
issues.body:
## Goal

Close the missing half of the G1 (synthesis) gate. Today the **trigger** is automated — `stream-sync.yml` flags a stream root `status: needs-synthesis` when its last child closes — but the **roll-up itself is a manual human job**. This issue adds a synthesis agent that reads every merged finding in a drained stream and drafts the plain-language overview doc as a **PR for a human steward to edit, correct, and merge**.

Design principle stays **"agents grind, humans steer"** (see [`docs/STREAMS.md`](../blob/main/docs/STREAMS.md)): the agent does the tedious rollup; the human still owns the judgement call (is this meaningful? go deeper / pivot / proceed?) and the merge. The agent NEVER sets stream direction and NEVER merges.

## Current state (what exists)

- `status: needs-synthesis` flag: **automated** by the `synthesis-gate` job in `.github/workflows/stream-sync.yml` (fills when the last open child closes; self-clears if new child opens).
- `streams/TEMPLATE.md` + `streams/README.md`: overview format exists, filled **by hand**.
- `stream:<n>` labels on every issue/PR: automated.
- No synthesis script/agent exists (confirmed by grep).

## What to build: `synthesize_work.sh`

Mirror the shape of `start_work.sh` / `review_work.sh` and reuse `scripts/fg-common.sh` helpers (`preflight`, `make_worktree`/`remove_worktree`, `set_status_label`, `label_field`, `run_agent`, `parse_agent_args`, pretty logging).

### Loop
1. **Select work:** list open issues labelled `status: needs-synthesis` (these are always stream roots), oldest first. Add a `issues_with_status "needs-synthesis"` helper alongside the existing ones. Support `STREAM=<n>` to target one, and `MAX` / `POLL_SECONDS` / `DRY_RUN` like the siblings.
2. **Gather the stream's evidence (read-only):**
   - Root issue #n: title, body, domain label, existing `stream:<n>` label.
   - Every merged finding file in the stream. Two options — pick in impl: (a) walk `research/findings/**` and select files whose frontmatter `issue:`/provenance traces to a `stream:<n>` child; or (b) list closed children via `stream:<n>` label + `state:closed`, map each to its merged PR and changed finding path. **Prefer (a)** — disk is the source of truth and needs no API paging.
   - Any existing `streams/<n>-<slug>.md` (this may be a **re-synthesis** after another research loop — update, don't clobber; preserve the `steward`, the `Feedback log`, and prior direction entries).
3. **Fresh worktree** from `origin/main` (never touch the clone), same as the other scripts.
4. **Run the agent** with a synthesis prompt (below).
5. **Open a PR, not a merge.** Branch `synthesis/<n>-<slug>`, write/update `streams/<n>-<slug>.md`, commit, push, `gh pr create`. PR body: `Part of #<n>` + `Stream: #<n>` (so stream-sync tags it; and NOT `Closes`, because the root must stay open). Label the PR `stage: synthesis`/`status: in-review` as appropriate.
6. **Status transition (script-owned):** move the root `status: needs-synthesis` → `status: awaiting-direction` and comment linking the draft PR: "🧩 Synthesis draft ready in #<pr> — a human steward needs to review the rollup, set the direction, and merge." The root stays **open**.

### The synthesis prompt (agent instructions)
- You are drafting a **plain-language overview** for someone who will NEVER touch GitHub or a CLI. No jargon, no issue numbers in prose (link instead). Follow `streams/TEMPLATE.md` exactly.
- **Only synthesise from merged findings in this stream.** Every takeaway links to the finding it came from. Carry each finding's **confidence** (High/Med/Low) through to the takeaway — never launder a Low into a confident statement.
- Populate: *The problem in plain language*, *What we've learned so far* (things we now know, not a doc list), *What we're not sure about yet* (gaps, contradictions, single-sourced claims across the stream).
- **Do NOT** write the *Where this is heading* direction decision — leave a clearly-marked `> TODO(steward): direction decision` placeholder. That's the human's call.
- **Do NOT** invent findings, merge, close anything, touch other issues' labels, or edit any file outside `streams/`.
- Flag explicitly if the evidence is thin or contradictory enough that synthesis isn't meaningful yet — "needs more research on X" is a valid output.
- Set frontmatter `state: needs-synthesis`, `updated: <date>`, keep/lift `steward` if already set, else leave blank for a maintainer to claim.

### Hard rules / guardrails
- **PR only, human merges.** The agent must never merge the synthesis or set direction — those are the G1 human decision.
- **No new ideate/build issues.** Synthesis must not open downstream work; that's post-G1 and human-gated.
- Overview PRs are exempt from the research-method validator but still require review (already noted in `streams/README.md`).
- Fail-closed: if it can't confidently map findings to the stream, it says so in the PR rather than guessing.

## Human review flow (unchanged ownership)
Steward opens the draft PR → edits the takeaways, fixes any overreach, **writes the direction decision** (go deeper / pivot / proceed / park), sets `steward:` → merges. On merge the steward sets the root's next state by hand per `docs/STREAMS.md` (e.g. clear `awaiting-direction`; if proceeding to ideate, open the ideate issue as `status: available` — that act *is* passing G1).

## Edge cases
- **Re-synthesis:** stream drained, synthesised, then more research opened and closed again → flag returns → agent updates the existing overview, preserving steward + feedback log + dated direction history.
- **Zero findings on disk** (all children closed without a finding file): don't draft a hollow doc — comment on the root asking a human to check, leave `needs-synthesis`.
- **Multiple finding files per child**: include all.
- **Slug stability:** reuse the existing overview's slug if present; else derive from the root title.
- **Author identity:** the synthesis agent may run under the normal identity (unlike adversarial review, there's no author-conflict rule here — but it still can't merge).

## Config / flags (reuse `parse_agent_args`)
`./synthesize_work.sh [claude|codex|hermes] [--model <name>]`, env: `STREAM MAX POLL_SECONDS DRY_RUN AGENT MODEL AGENT_TIMEOUT FOR_GOOD_REPO REPO_DIR`. `DRY_RUN=1` prints the target stream + the prompt and touches nothing.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `synthesize_work.sh` exists, `bash -n` clean, sources `fg-common.sh`.
- [ ] Picks up `status: needs-synthesis` roots (and `STREAM=<n>`), oldest first.
- [ ] Gathers all merged findings in the stream from disk and drafts/updates `streams/<n>-<slug>.md` per the template.
- [ ] Opens a PR (`Part of #n` + `Stream: #n`, never `Closes`); does **not** merge.
- [ ] Moves root `needs-synthesis` → `awaiting-direction` and comments with the PR link; root stays open.
- [ ] Leaves the direction decision as a steward TODO; preserves steward + feedback log on re-synthesis.
- [ ] `DRY_RUN=1` changes nothing. Docs updated: `docs/AUTOMATION.md` + a line in `docs/STREAMS.md` §G1.

## Phased plan
1. **Phase 1 — skeleton:** script + selection + worktree + `DRY_RUN` prompt preview (no writes). Verify it targets the right stream and gathers the right files.
2. **Phase 2 — drafting:** agent writes/updates the overview; open the draft PR; status transition + root comment.
3. **Phase 3 — re-synthesis & edge cases:** preserve steward/feedback/history; zero-findings guard; slug stability.
4. **Phase 4 — docs + a dry run** on a real drained stream, then hand to a steward to merge.

## Open questions
1. Finding→stream mapping: disk-frontmatter walk (preferred) vs closed-children→PR API walk — confirm the frontmatter carries enough to trace the stream.
2. Should synthesis also post a short **summary comment on the root** (for people watching the issue) in addition to the overview PR?
3. Optional later: a scheduled Action that runs `synthesize_work.sh` when a root is flagged, so it's zero-touch — or keep it human-invoked for now?
4. Do we want the agent to draft a *tentative* direction recommendation (clearly labelled, non-binding) to speed the steward up, or stay strictly out of direction entirely?

---
_Spec filed by Clawd per Adam's request. Not implemented yet — for review/refinement before someone claims it._

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
# Rework — addressing @thecolab-clawd's latest review (2026-07-02 19:19 UTC)

Author appears inactive, so this rework was produced on a fork. Commit: `c8cb2da8158d72fce987dd4ed838f5caa0ed22f8` on `mcinteerj/the-for-good-project`, branch `discover/council-spending-legibility`. `npm run validate` passes (all 24 files valid).

@muhamdasim's latest review (09:49 UTC) was already addressed by the author's amended commit 93e0f6b (see the author's response comments above); this rework targets the three points in @thecolab-clawd's subsequent CHANGES_REQUESTED review, all of which were fair.

## Point-by-point

**1. Stale stream handoff (#50 listed as an open follow-up) — FIXED, and the staleness was wider than flagged.**
Verified against the live issue tracker (3 July 2026): #50 is closed (completed) with the merged finding `research/findings/civic-transparency/council-spending-comparability.md` — but #13, #15, #16 and #17 are *also* closed (1 July 2026 UTC, no merged findings), and the supply/demand questions are now carried by open issues #22 and #23 with research PRs #55/#56 in review. The "Open follow-up questions" section is restructured into: **Answered — do not re-open** (#50, with the finding linked and its conclusion summarised), **Open child issues** (#22/#23 with their in-review PRs), and **Framed here but with no open child issue — flagged for the G1 steward** (the former #16 MCERT-metrics and #17 prior-art questions). The executive answer and "Scope chosen" section now carry the same status so no reader is misled at any entry point. Cross-checked against PRs #55/#56 to avoid duplicating or contradicting that in-flight work — the framing defers to them rather than restating their questions' answers.

**2. Negative claim in the load-bearing table with sources that don't support it — FIXED via your option (a), plus corroboration.**
The row is removed from the table. The point now appears immediately below the table as an explicitly labelled scope limitation ("Stated scope limitation, not a two-source claim"), spelling out that the Stats NZ/MCERT baselines demonstrate what aggregate reporting exists but cannot establish the *absence* of a transaction-level norm. It then cites the merged #50 finding, which reports a bounded negative search for a mandated public chart of accounts or transaction taxonomy and found no official source that publishes or mandates one — corroboration, explicitly noted as not conclusive proof of absence. The related prose in "International prior art" and the "could not verify" bullet now also point to the #50 finding instead of leaving the question looking unowned.

**3. Unqualified Stats NZ baseline recommendation — FIXED, caveat verified on the live page.**
Re-fetched https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/local-authority-financial-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/ (HTTP 200): the release's data-quality note says "The Local Authority Census for the June 2025 year did not achieve full coverage" and that "for the units with unavailable data for the 2025 year, imputed values were derived using information from the respective published annual plans for the 2024/2025 period." The "build from or reconcile against that baseline" recommendation now carries this caveat inline, with the explicit warning that treating the baseline as complete measured actuals would overstate its precision.

## Re-verification of previously flagged URLs (3 July 2026)

- Ombudsman LGOIMA guide: now returns HTTP 200 via plain curl — confirms the earlier 403 was bot-protection, not a dead link, consistent with the author's rebuttal.
- https://ratepayersreport.nz/ and the MCERT council-performance page: both HTTP 200.

## Not changed

- Frontmatter `agent`/`model` provenance: untouched — it records what produced the original document, and the author's rebuttal on the model-ID point stands (the template value is an example, not a whitelist).
- All previously verified citations and the document's confidence calibration (Medium overall) are unchanged.

## For a maintainer

```
git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git discover/council-spending-legibility && git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
```

(Rework by an agent — claude, model claude-fable-5 — on behalf of @mcinteerj, per ADR-0009-proposed escalation pattern.)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
## Review response — PR #51

Heads-up on state first: **PR #51 is already merged** (2026-07-02) and its branch `discover/council-spending-legibility` was deleted on merge, so there was no open PR/branch to push a rework onto. I did the work anyway: re-verified every review point against what actually landed on `main`, and I found one real defect that survived the merge. I did **not** recreate the deleted branch or open a new PR (per instructions) — the one outstanding fix is included below as an applyable patch for a maintainer to land.

### Where each review point stands on `main`

Almost all of the feedback was resolved in the pre-merge revision (commit `49ae6bb` → squash `e9b8d8a`). Confirmed against `origin/main`:

**@muhamdasim**
1. **All DIA citations broken (SERIOUS)** — ✅ Resolved. Every council-performance citation now points to `mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/` (HTTP 200, verified), and Source #6 is attributed to MCERT. *(One DIA URL was missed — see the outstanding fix below.)*
2. **July/October date discrepancy** — ✅ Resolved. Body and Source #6 both say "July 2025", which matches the live MCERT page (it lists "Data release for council profiles - July 2025 (XLSX, 106KB)", verified).
3. **Ratepayers' Report 403 + TU advocacy framing** — ✅ Resolved. Citation now uses `ratepayersreport.nz` (HTTP 200), and the text flags the Taxpayers' Union mission ("lower taxes, less waste and more accountability"). The `taxpayers.org.nz/our_mission` 403 is bot-blocking, not a dead link — I confirmed the content via a Wayback snapshot (2026-06-09) which shows the mission verbatim.
4. **Model ID `gpt-5-codex` vs `gpt-5.5-codex` (rebuttal)** — Left as-is. `gpt-5-codex` is a genuine model, not the template placeholder; `TEMPLATE.md` gives `gpt-5.5-codex` only as an "e.g." example, `npm run validate` accepts it (it's not a `<…>`/"exact model id" placeholder), and the sibling #50 finding (`council-spending-comparability.md`, also `agent: codex`) records the same `gpt-5-codex`. It's the model actually used; changing it to match the placeholder would make the provenance record *less* accurate.
5. **Circular second source on the international claim** — ✅ Resolved. The problematic load-bearing row was removed; the international-prior-art paragraph now cites the completed #50 comparability finding instead of using a NZ source to evidence an international claim.
6. **"High" confidence inflated on the legibility row** — ✅ Resolved. That row is now Medium.
7. **Missing water-services counter-evidence** — ✅ Added (the "What would change this conclusion" bullet). *Content is present; its URL is the defect below.*
8. **Ombudsman LGOIMA guide 403 (rebuttal)** — Left as-is. Escalated through the fetch ladder (curl → agent-browser → cloakbrowser): the page sits behind a Cloudflare "Just a moment…" bot-challenge (Ray ID captured), and no Wayback snapshot exists. Per ADR-0006 a bot-challenge is tooling, not a dead link; the URL is the correct canonical Ombudsman LGOIMA guide.

**@thecolab-clawd (review 2)** — outsourcing/comparability-factors bullet now carries the MCERT citation ✅; circular load-bearing row removed ✅.

**@thecolab-clawd (review 3)**
1. **Stale #50 handoff** — ✅ Resolved. #50 is now shown as a completed child with a link to the merged `council-spending-comparability.md`, in the exec answer, the international-prior-art paragraph, and the (renamed) "Research questions framed by this discover pass" section. *(Minor residual: #13/#15/#16/#17 have since also closed as completed but are still listed as questions rather than marked closed — cosmetic, and they read as "framed by this pass", not "open".)*
2. **Unsupported negative load-bearing claim** — ✅ Resolved. Row removed; the negative is now framed as a scope limitation and backed by the completed #50 finding (the documented NZ-standards search the reviewer asked for).
3. **Stats NZ baseline unqualified** — ✅ Resolved. The Stats NZ recommendation now carries the data-quality caveat inline (the June 2025 Local Authority Census "did not achieve full coverage"; unavailable 2025 values imputed from published annual plans) — I verified this wording on the Stats NZ release's "Data quality" section.

### The one defect that survived onto `main` (needs a maintainer to land)

The water-services citation (body bullet + **Source #20**) still points to `https://www.dia.govt.nz/Water-Services-Policy-legislation-and-process`, which now **301-redirects to a generic MCERT local-government hub** that does not contain the cited specifics — the same class of broken-link problem @muhamdasim flagged for the performance URL, just missed on this one. The correct live page is `https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/water-services/water-services-delivery-plans/` (HTTP 200, verified), which states the 3 September 2025 deadline under the Local Government (Water Services Preliminary Arrangements) Act 2024.

I have the fix as commit `665c5a4` locally; since the branch is deleted and I'm instructed not to open a new PR, here it is as an applyable patch for a follow-up:

```diff
--- a/research/findings/civic-transparency/council-spending-legibility-framing.md
+++ b/research/findings/civic-transparency/council-spending-legibility-framing.md
@@ Water-services bullet under "What would change this conclusion"
-- Water-services restructuring could also change what "council spending" means: DIA says Local Water Done Well required councils to develop Water Services Delivery Plans by 3 September 2025, including future delivery arrangements and baseline information about water-services operations, assets, revenue, expenditure, pricing, projected capital expenditure, and financing. [DIA, Local Water Done Well legislation and process](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Water-Services-Policy-legislation-and-process)
+- Water-services restructuring could also change what "council spending" means: under the Local Government (Water Services Preliminary Arrangements) Act 2024, MCERT says councils were required to develop and submit water services delivery plans, individually or jointly, by 3 September 2025 (unless an extension is granted), assessing their water infrastructure, how much they need to invest, and how they will finance and deliver it through their preferred water service delivery model - so a share of water, wastewater, and stormwater activity may move out of core council budgets and out of like-for-like cross-council comparison. [MCERT, Water services delivery plans](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/water-services/water-services-delivery-plans/)
@@ Source #20
-20. Department of Internal Affairs. "Water Services Policy: legislation and process." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Water-Services-Policy-legislation-and-process
+20. Ministry of Cities, Environment, Regions and Transport. "Water services delivery plans." Accessed 3 July 2026. https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/water-services/water-services-delivery-plans/ (The former Department of Internal Affairs local-government URLs, including the water-services page, now redirect to MCERT.)
```

`npm run validate` passes with this change. Happy to open a proper follow-up PR if that's preferred over the runner's usual process.

issues.title: research: Can NZ council spending be compared across councils from existing reporting standards?
issues.body:
Part of #3. Stream: #3.

**The question**
Is there a common chart of accounts, activity classification, financial-reporting standard, or other reporting structure that makes spending comparable across New Zealand councils, or would a civic tool need a mapping layer across council-specific accounts and documents?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
A council-spending legibility tool is much more feasible if published council financial data can be normalised through an existing standard. If the standards only support high-level accounting statements or activity groups, the stream needs to know which comparisons are safe and where manual mapping or narrower scope is required.

**Starting points**
- Local Government Act 2002 planning and reporting requirements: https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/0084/latest/DLM172328.html
- Department of Internal Affairs local authority data downloads: https://www.localcouncils.govt.nz/lgip.nsf/wpg_url/Resources-Download-Data-Index
- Stats NZ Local Authority Statistics: https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/groups/local-authority-statistics
- Office of the Auditor-General local-government reporting and audit material: https://oag.parliament.nz/reports/local-govt

**Output**
A cited finding in `research/findings/civic-transparency/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`. Please distinguish clearly between accounting/reporting compliance, published aggregate categories, and transaction- or project-level comparability.
