issues.body: Part of #2. Stream: #2. Adds a cited discovery framing note and opens research child issues #45, #46, #47, and #48.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in commit 18a39f9 and pushed back to `discover/grant-access-framing`.

Changes made:
- Replaced the broken Fundsorter `/data-1` citation with the live `funding-landscape` page for the caveats and Hui E! for the older “almost 2,000 / 37 categories” Fundsorter analysis; added a sentence warning not to combine that older snapshot with Fundsorter’s current homepage counter.
- Corrected GEM Local’s stated small-charity threshold from `$1.5m` to `$1m`.
- Re-ran the Charities Services Tier OData counts as JSON and updated Tier 4 to `14,204` and Tier 3 to `10,826`, with explicit wording that same-day live register drift occurred and later findings should re-run the query.
- Added direct citations for Granted.govt.nz and the data.govt.nz class 4 grants dataset.
- Qualified the `$4.28b` figure as the combined “grants and other funding” category, not grants-only income.
- Added Stats NZ as a second source for the broader nonprofit-sector scale claim and downgraded/qualified the load-bearing table where sources trace back to the Charities Register.
- Kept the missed-grants hypothesis Low confidence and explicitly labelled GEM Local/Fundsorter as vendor leads, not independent proof.
- Added a cited Māori/Pacific pathways paragraph covering Tupu.nz/Te Puni Kokiri, Charities Services Te Ao Maori funding, and the Ministry for Pacific Peoples fund, plus stronger wording that solution design needs human review for Maori, iwi, hapu, whanau, Pacific, rural and volunteer-led groups.
- Renamed “Open follow-up questions” to avoid staleness now that #48 has been completed.
- Updated `model:` from `gpt-5-codex` to `gpt-5`, which is the model identity exposed in this Codex session.

Checks run:
- `npm run validate` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
# Rework: point-by-point response to all three CHANGES_REQUESTED reviews

The author's earlier commit 18a39f9 already resolved most of @muhamdasim's and @mcinteerj's points; this rework commit (**mcinteerj/the-for-good-project@23debcc**, branch `discover/grant-access-framing`) resolves the remaining post-rework review from @thecolab-clawd, adds the citations still missing, and re-verifies every changed source live (3 July 2026). `npm run validate` passes.

## @thecolab-clawd (latest review, 2026-07-02 19:13 UTC)

**1. Discover-stage artifact placed in `research/findings/` — partly fixed, partly rebutted.**
- **Fixed:** the doc now opens with an explicit **Stage note** stating it is the Discover-stage framing note for stream root #2, not a research finding, and that later synthesis should treat the merged findings from #45–#48 as the evidence base. That directly removes the "can mislead later synthesis" risk.
- **Rebutted (placement move):** the repo's merged precedent puts Discover framing notes exactly here — PR #79 (commit de0543a, merged after this review) placed the stream #61 framing note at `research/findings/other/supermarket-transparency-framing.md` with the same frontmatter shape. There is no `discover/` output directory, and the validator only checks `research/findings/`, `solutions/`, and `analysis/`. Empirically the feared confusion did not occur: the stream #2 synthesis overview (commit f80ba5b, `streams/2-…md`) built its evidence base from the seven merged child findings and did not cite this framing note as evidence. If the project wants a dedicated home for framing outputs, that is an ADR-level structural decision outside a single framing PR's scope (per AGENTS.md "stay in scope" / ADR rules).

**2. "Authoritative public source" characterisation uncited — fixed.** Reworded and cited: the Charities Register page states "All registered charities in New Zealand appear on the Charities Register" and that the Register "is a database of charities registered under the Charities Act 2005". Verified via the repo fetch ladder (`curl` 403s on charities.govt.nz — bot protection — content confirmed with `scripts/cloak-fetch.mjs`). Source added to the Sources list.

## @muhamdasim (latest review, 2026-07-02 09:48 UTC) — status after 18a39f9 + 23debcc

1. **Fundsorter `/data-1` 404 — fixed** (18a39f9): claim now cites `fundsorter.com/funding-landscape` (live, wording verbatim: "does not measure dollar amounts… only the number of grant opportunities… doesn't include non-public funding opportunities") and the Hui E! republication of the "almost 2,000 opportunities across 37 broad categories" analysis, with a reconciling sentence against the current 3,361 homepage counter. Both re-fetched today: HTTP 200, wording matches.
2. **Tier 4 count discrepancy — fixed and re-verified** (18a39f9 + 23debcc): the doc carries the JSON-query counts 14,204 (Tier 4) and 10,826 (Tier 3), explains the earlier Atom-capture drift, and now records that the identical queries re-run on 3 July 2026 returned **exactly the same counts** (14,204 / 10,826; registered total 29,331 also confirmed).
3. **Vendor-only two-source pair — fixed** (18a39f9): the confidence cell now reads "Low; both are vendor leads, not independent proof". No non-vendor quantification exists yet — that is precisely what child issue #48 was opened to test.
4. **Granted.govt.nz / data.govt.nz uncited — fixed** (18a39f9): both now cited inline and in Sources (18–19).
5. **$4.28B category breadth — fixed** (18a39f9): now explicitly "the combined category 'grants and other funding'… does not isolate grants-only income".
6. **`model: "gpt-5-codex"` unverifiable — resolved, with one correction for the record:** `gpt-5-codex` **is** a real, publicly documented OpenAI model ID (https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5-codex), so the original value was not fabricated. The author nonetheless set `model: "gpt-5"` in 18a39f9; I have left provenance frontmatter untouched.
7. **Māori/iwi/Pacific pathways blind spot — fixed** (18a39f9): "Specialised pathways" paragraph added (Tupu.nz >40 whenua Māori funds, Charities Services Te Ao Māori funding page, MPP Pacific Languages Community Fund) with the explicit caveat that one generic small-charity workflow should not be assumed to cover them.
8. **Single-source sector-size anchor — fixed and strengthened** (18a39f9 + 23debcc): Stats NZ NPISA 2018 added as the independent second source. This rework also found the ~115,000 figure was not actually on the cited landing page — the **full report PDF** states "In 2018, there were 115,770 NPIs" — so the doc now says 115,770 and cites the PDF directly (landing page kept for the 159M volunteer hours, which it does state).

## @mcinteerj (latest review, 2026-07-02 10:22 UTC)

1. **GEM Local $1.5M → $1M — fixed** (18a39f9), and 23debcc adds the FAQ citation you suggested (verified live: "annual revenue less than $1 million per year").
2. **Fundsorter dead URL — fixed** (18a39f9) exactly along the lines you proposed: `funding-landscape` + Hui E!, plus the reconciling sentence for "almost 2,000" vs 3,361.
3. **Rows 1–2 same-origin pairing — fixed** (18a39f9 + 23debcc): row 1's second source is now Stats NZ (independent origin) and its mark dropped High → Medium; row 2's second source is labelled "same register origin"; the table preamble stating why register data is leaned on is now itself cited (Charities Register page) rather than asserted.
4. **Vendor marketing as evidence — acknowledged:** row stays Low with the explicit vendor-lead caveat; noted for synthesis not to upgrade it without primary evidence.
5. **#48 already closed — addressed** (18a39f9): the section header is now "Follow-up questions opened from this discovery", which stays accurate regardless of issue state.

## Verification summary

- All changed/added citations fetched live on 3 July 2026: GEM Local FAQ, Fundsorter funding-landscape, Hui E!, Stats NZ landing page + NPISA PDF (curl), Charities Register + open-data pages (`cloak-fetch` after curl 403 — bot protection, not dead links), and all three OData count queries (values identical to the doc).
- `npm run validate`: ✓ all 23 files valid.
- No provenance frontmatter changed in this rework.

## For the maintainer

Rework commit: mcinteerj/the-for-good-project@23debcc on `discover/grant-access-framing`. To bring it into this PR's branch:

```
git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git discover/grant-access-framing && git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
```

(Rework by an agent — claude, model claude-fable-5 — on behalf of @mcinteerj, per ADR-0009-proposed escalation pattern.)

issues.title: research: Measure whether eligible small charities under-apply for grants
issues.body:
Part of #2. Stream: #2.

Research question: Can missed or under-applied grant funding for eligible small New Zealand charities be measured from public data, funder data, or defensible proxies?

Why it matters: Issue #2 says the unclaimed or under-applied pool is plausibly significant, but the discovery framing could not verify a dollar value or rate. Before solution design, the stream needs to know whether this can be quantified or whether it must remain a qualitative/access problem.

Where to look first:
- Charities Services sector snapshot, including grants and other funding income: https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf
- DIA class 4 grants data, which may include approved and declined grants depending on the dataset version: https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data
- Community Matters records of grants and budgets: https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/
- Fundsorter landscape notes, with caution because they measure opportunities rather than dollars and exclude non-public funding: https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1
- Annual reports from major funders such as community trusts, gaming trusts, Lottery Grants Board and councils.

Expected output: a cited research finding in research/findings/grant-access/ that tests possible measures, states which are invalid or partial, and says what data access or human/funder input would change the conclusion.

issues.title: research: Audit machine-readable NZ grants data and schema gaps
issues.body:
Part of #2. Stream: #2.

Research question: Which New Zealand grants data sources are machine-readable today, what fields do they expose, and what would need to be assembled manually to support eligibility matching for small charities?

Why it matters: A buildable grant-matching solution depends on structured fields such as eligibility, geography, deadlines, legal-status requirements, exclusions, funding amounts, application links and update cadence. This issue should separate public official data from private/product databases and static webpages.

Where to look first:
- Charities Services open data, which covers charities and annual returns rather than grant opportunities: https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data
- DIA class 4 grants data and the linked data.govt.nz dataset: https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data
- Community Matters funding pages and records of grants: https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/
- data.govt.nz catalogue searches for grant, funding, lottery, COGS and class 4 datasets.
- Council funding pages listed by Creative New Zealand: https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support

Expected output: a cited research finding in research/findings/grant-access/ with a source-by-source schema/gap table, confidence ratings, and a recommendation on whether an MVP could use public data only.

issues.title: research: How small NZ charities find grants and where the workflow fails
issues.body:
Part of #2. Stream: #2.

Research question: What do small New Zealand charities and community groups currently use to find grants, and where does that workflow break down?

Why it matters: The root claim is not just that grants are fragmented, but that small and volunteer-led groups miss eligible funding because discovery and matching are hard. That claim needs evidence from the seeker side, not just funder directories.

Where to look first:
- Charities Services sector snapshot for affected population and volunteer context: https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf
- Charities Services Tier 4 definition for small charities: https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4
- GEM Local's small-charity positioning and workflow features: https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/
- Fundsorter's volunteer/community-leader positioning: https://www.fundsorter.com/
- Generosity NZ/givUS access model and public-library access routes: https://generosity.org.nz/
- Consider public reports from Hui E!, Volunteering NZ, Community Networks Aotearoa, councils, and grant-writing support programmes. Do not publish personal data.

Expected output: a cited research finding in research/findings/grant-access/ that distinguishes documented workflow evidence from hypotheses, flags where lived-experience/user interviews are required, and identifies the highest-friction steps to test.

issues.title: research: Map NZ grant funders and eligibility-source locations
issues.body:
Part of #2. Stream: #2.

Research question: How many grant funders and contestable grant opportunities operate in New Zealand, and where do their eligibility criteria, deadlines, regions, funding purposes and application rules actually live?

Why it matters: Issue #2 depends on knowing whether the discovery problem is a small directory-cleanup task or a genuinely fragmented information architecture problem. The discovery framing found official and sector sources spread across DIA/Community Matters, class 4 gaming data, council pages, philanthropic trusts, and commercial/sector tools.

Where to look first:
- Community Matters for Lottery, Crown and Trust funding: https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/
- DIA class 4 grants data and Granted.govt.nz: https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data
- Creative New Zealand's non-comprehensive cross-sector funding list, including council pages: https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support
- Generosity NZ/givUS, GEM Local and Fundsorter as coverage benchmarks, with care because they are product sources: https://generosity.org.nz/ https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/ https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1

Expected output: a cited research finding in research/findings/grant-access/ that estimates funder/opportunity counts, classifies source types, gives confidence per source class, and says what coverage remains unverifiable.

issues.title: fix: pr_for_issue fallback must not match child PRs
issues.body:
Follow-up to #40. Child PR bodies also carry `Part of #<root>` (per the convention), so the fallback could return a **child's** PR when looking up a root's framing PR — `finish_issue` would then track the wrong PR. A framing PR is distinguishable: it closes nothing. The fallback now skips any candidate with a closing ref.

Verified live: `pr_for_issue(2)` → empty (PR #26 closes #20, correctly skipped), `pr_for_issue(4)` → 27 (framing PR), `pr_for_issue(20)` → 26 (closing ref path unchanged).

issues.title: chore(scripts): default agent to claude + positional agent/--model args
issues.body:
Two asks from Adam.

## 1. Default agent → `claude`
`fg-common.sh` now defaults `AGENT` to `claude` (was `codex`).

## 2. Nicer invocation: positional agent + `--model`
Added a shared `parse_agent_args` in `fg-common.sh`, wired into both `start_work.sh` and `review_work.sh`:

```bash
./start_work.sh                     # claude (default)
./start_work.sh codex               # codex
./start_work.sh claude --model <n>  # explicit agent + model
./start_work.sh --model gpt-5.5      # just a model override
```

- Agent given as a positional word (`claude`|`codex`); model via `--model <name>` / `--model=<name>` / `-m <name>`.
- **CLI wins over the `AGENT`/`MODEL` env vars**; unknown args ignored so each script keeps its own env options.
- Same syntax works for `review_work.sh`.
- Usage blocks + `docs/AUTOMATION.md` updated.

## Verified
- `bash -n` clean on all three scripts
- Parser table-tested: `codex --model gpt-5.5` → AGENT=codex/MODEL=gpt-5.5; `claude` → claude; `--model=sonnet-4.6` → claude+model; no args → claude.

issues.title: chore: default the work agent to claude
issues.body:
Sets `claude` as the default agent for `start_work.sh` / `review_work.sh`, per Adam's request.

- `scripts/fg-common.sh`: `AGENT="${AGENT:-codex}"` → `AGENT="${AGENT:-claude}"`
- `start_work.sh` usage header + `docs/AUTOMATION.md`: codex is now documented as the opt-in override (`AGENT=codex ./start_work.sh`)

`bash -n` passes on both scripts. No behavioural change beyond the default — `AGENT=codex` still works exactly as before.

issues.title: build: problem-chains board view, grouped by stream labels
issues.body:
Part of #37. Stream: #30.

**Goal:** a third board view showing each stream as a nested "problem chain" — child issues under their parents, PR chips (open/merged), status badges — so the Discover → Research → Ideate → Build pipeline is visible per problem, not just as stage columns.

**Head start — salvage, don't start from scratch:** closed PR #14 built most of the rendering (`ChainTree` component, `lineage.ts`, filter dimming; screenshot on the PR). Fetch it with `git fetch origin pull/14/head` and lift what's useful. It predates the stream model, so the foundations change:

1. **Group chains by the `stream:<n>` label** — the canonical, server-maintained grouping (validated + propagated by `stream-sync.yml`). Body-ref parsing may still shape the tree *inside* a stream, but must be **line-anchored** (`^Part of #n`) — unanchored prose matching mis-parents issues.
2. **Fix the cycle crash found in PR #14's adversarial review** — promoted cycle roots keep child pointers, so `sortChildren` recurses forever (`RangeError`) and takes down the whole Board page.
3. **Build against current main** — streams are live; render the new statuses (`changes-requested`, `needs-synthesis`, `awaiting-direction`) and remember discover roots now stay open for a stream's life (#40).

Coordinate with #37 (per-stream pages) — the chain tree is a natural component of those pages.

issues.title: fix: discover PRs must not close their stream root
issues.body:
Found while auditing in-flight issues: PR #27's `Closes #4` would have closed **stream #4's root** on merge — ending the stream's drain trigger and G1 queue while children #24/#25 are still open. (That PR's body is already hand-fixed to `Part of #4`.)

This makes the rule structural:
- **start_work.sh**: discover-stage prompts instruct `Part of #n` in commit + PR body (with the reason); every other stage keeps `Closes #n` — a child closing on merge is exactly what fires the drain→synthesis check.
- **fg-common.sh**: `pr_for_issue()` falls back to a `"Part of #n" in:body` search when no closing ref exists, so the runner still finds discover PRs (verified live: `pr_for_issue(4)` → 27).
- **docs/STREAMS.md + AGENTS.md**: root lifecycle documented — roots are closed by the steward when the stream ships or parks, never by a PR.

## Testing
`bash -n` both scripts; `DRY_RUN=1` prompt shows `Part of #2` for the discover issue and `Closes` for others; live `pr_for_issue(4)` returns 27 via the fallback.

issues.title: fix: per-item concurrency group for stream-sync
issues.body:
A single global concurrency group keeps only one *pending* run per group and cancels older pending ones — observed live during the stream-label backfill: 4 of 8 burst runs were cancelled (harmless only because the labels had been applied directly). Per-item groups (`stream-sync-<number>`) serialize same-item events — the actual race — while letting different items run in parallel. The drain gate recounts open children live, so cross-item ordering doesn't matter.

issues.title: build: stakeholder feedback channel — WhatsApp → Clawd → stream
issues.body:
Part of #30. Companion to #36.

The people with the most valuable domain knowledge won't use GitHub. Decision in ADR-0001: **WhatsApp → Clawd first** (this community already lives there), web form later.

- Clawd captures a stakeholder comment in the community chat and files it against the right stream: an issue comment (or new issue) labelled `stream:<n>` + `feedback`.
- Map WhatsApp identity → optional GitHub handle (dovetails with the register-gh / daily-summary work in whatsapp.admin).
- Feedback must be able to **redirect a stream**: the steward sees it at G1/G2 and logs it in the overview doc's feedback log.
- Out of scope here: the web form and email-to-issue variants — open separately if wanted.

issues.title: build: per-stream pages on the site (overview + timeline + human queue)
issues.body:
Part of #30. Companion to #36 (streams + gates are now live in the repo).

Make the web app the **read surface** for streams, so a non-GitHub human (SME, funder, community member) can follow a body of work:

- A **streams index** grouping board issues by their `stream:<n>` label (label + count + state from the overview doc's frontmatter).
- A **per-stream page** rendering `streams/<n>-<slug>.md` (the plain-language overview) plus a timeline of the stream's issues/PRs/findings from the snapshot.
- Surface the **human queue** (`status: needs-synthesis`, `status: awaiting-direction`) prominently — the /review page is the natural home.

Data plumbing: `web/scripts/build-data.mjs` already captures labels; it needs to parse `streams/*.md` frontmatter (same pattern as findings) and group by stream label. The `needs-synthesis` / `awaiting-direction` statuses already render as badges (#36).

commentsList.body: Related: PR #14 (problem-chains board view) predates the stream model and is being retargeted onto stream labels via #41 — its `ChainTree` component (nested lineage, PR chips, filter dimming) is a natural building block for the per-stream pages here. issues.title: Streams + human gates + ADR policy (core of #30)
issues.body:
Implements the core of #30 and establishes an ADR policy. **Part of #30** (doesn't close it — the feedback channel and web stream pages remain, split as #37 / #38).

## Streams (`docs/STREAMS.md`, ADR-0001)
- **`stream:<n>` labels**, auto-created and propagated by a new zero-token Action (`stream-sync.yml`): a `stage: discover` issue roots its own stream; children/PRs inherit from `Stream: #n` or `Part of #p` body lines.
- **`streams/` overview docs** (TEMPLATE + README) — the plain-language front page per stream, written for people who'll never touch GitHub, owned by a human **steward**. Stream state lives in its frontmatter.
- **Human gates**: G0 = existing `needs-triage` flow; **G1** = human synthesis before any ideation; **G2** = human approval before any build. Enforced structurally — agents only work `status: available`, and only a human makes ideate/build issues available. New labels `status: needs-synthesis`, `status: awaiting-direction`, `feedback` (already created live).
- **Bounded fan-out**: agents are *encouraged* to split oversized work into 2–5 **chunky** research sub-issues — but only two levels deep (root's agent → depth 1 → depth 2, no further; `start_work.sh` computes depth from the `Part of #` chain and tells the agent). Splitting is scope-narrowing: the agent still completes its own issue.
- **Drain → synthesis trigger**: when the last open child issue in a stream closes, `stream-sync.yml` flags the root `status: needs-synthesis` and comments — G1 queues itself. New/reopened child work removes the flag until the stream drains again.
- **Agent contract**: AGENTS.md hard rules + `start_work.sh` prompt — never cross gates, never touch `streams/` docs, carry stream refs, fan out chunky and bounded.

## ADR policy (`docs/adr/`)
- Policy README (when an ADR is required, numbered + immutable, supersede-don't-edit, **agents must check ADRs before structural changes**) + template.
- **ADR-0001**: streams, human gates, bounded fan-out, drain trigger (the #30 decisions).
- **ADR-0002**: why `thecolab-ai/.skills` is vendored as a git submodule.
- Referenced from README and AGENTS.md (single agent contract — no separate CLAUDE.md).

## Web
- **Contribute page**: "As a person" reframed as **the judgement layer** — submit problems, steward streams, sanity-check findings; agents do the volume, humans decide what matters (you can still do work by hand).
- `needs-synthesis` / `awaiting-direction` statuses render on board badges and filters.

## Testing
- `bash -n` + `DRY_RUN=1` runs of `start_work.sh`; fan-out prompt verified at depth 0; `issue_depth` verified against live issues (#30→0, #37→1).
- `stream-sync.yml` YAML-validated.
- `npm run validate` unaffected; `cd web && npm run build` passes.

issues.title: web: dedicated in-app finding detail page (Closes #28)
issues.body:
Closes #28.

Findings previously linked out to the raw markdown blob on GitHub. This adds an in-app detail page so a finding renders within the site.

## Changes
- **New `/findings/*` route → `FindingDetail` page**: domain + confidence badges, author / agent·model / date metadata, the rendered markdown body (via the existing `Markdown` component), a **Sources** sidebar, and a **"View source on GitHub"** link kept as a secondary action.
- **`build-data.mjs`** now emits `slug` (a stable URL id derived from the file path, e.g. `ai-policy/nz-public-sector-ai-guidance`) and `body` (the finding's markdown) on each finding. `Finding` type updated to match.
- **Findings cards** navigate in-app via `<Link to="/findings/:slug">` instead of `target="_blank"` to GitHub; dropped the external-link icon that implied leaving the site.

## Verification
- `tsc -b && vite build` passes; `oxlint` clean on the changed files.
- Regenerated the snapshot locally (1 finding, `slug` + 30 KB `body` present) and verified via `vite preview`:
  - Findings card → navigates to `/findings/ai-policy/nz-public-sector-ai-guidance` (clean in-app URL).
  - Detail page renders badges, metadata, full markdown body with inline source links, and the 23-source sidebar.
- `snapshot.json` is intentionally **not** committed — it's a CI-regenerated build artifact; the deployed site will rebuild it with the new fields.

issues.title: web: show the rework loop and worktree flow on the site
issues.body:
Companion to #33 — the site now explains how the pushback loop works.

- **Workflow diagram** (Get started page): a new *Changes requested* callout shows what happens when a review finds problems — the work returns to its author's queue, their next loop fixes it on the same PR, and it comes back for a fresh adversarial review. The review-gate callout now says **anyone but the author** can pick up a review.
- **Contribute**: the `start_work.sh` / `review_work.sh` cards and the agent-autopilot path describe the rework-first queue and fresh-from-main throwaway worktrees.
- **Methodology**: the adversarial-review section explains that pushback isn't rejection — it's the loop that makes the work trustworthy.
- **Board support**: `changes-requested` added to the status types and `STATUS_META`, so issue badges and the board filter render it (colour matches the GitHub label).

## Testing
- `npm run build` (tsc + vite) passes; `npm run lint` shows only pre-existing warnings.

issues.title: tooling: route review pushback to the author, run all tasks in worktrees
issues.body:
## What

Closes the loop between the work and review scripts, and isolates every agent run in a throwaway git worktree.

### Pushback routing
- `review_work.sh`: a NEEDS_WORK verdict now flips the linked issue to the new **`status: changes-requested`** label (author stays assigned), so the rework goes back to the person who did the work.
- `start_work.sh`: checks the rework queue (`changes-requested` + assigned to you) **before** claiming new issues. The agent gets the reviewer's feedback (review bodies + inline comments), pushes to the same PR branch, and the issue flips back to `in-review` for a fresh review.
- Reviewers skip PRs that already failed at their current revision — no wasted tokens until the author pushes rework. The reviewer-must-not-be-the-author rule is unchanged.

### Worktrees + fresh main
- Every task (work, rework, review) runs in a throwaway `git worktree` created from a fresh `git fetch` — `origin/main` for new work, the PR head for rework/review. The clone is never checked out, reset, or dirtied. Replaces the old `git reset --hard` machinery and its dirty-tree guard.

### Bookkeeping
- `status: changes-requested` added to `.github/labels.yml` (already created on the repo) and cleared by `issue-status.yml` when a PR merges or closes.
- `docs/AUTOMATION.md` updated with the new lifecycle diagram and worktree notes.

## Testing
- `bash -n` on all scripts.
- Worktree helpers exercised for real (created from `origin/main`, removed cleanly).
- `DRY_RUN=1` runs of both loops against the live repo — rework-queue priority and failed-revision skipping behave as expected.

A Vite `closeBundle` plugin emits `404.html` as a copy of `index.html` so GitHub Pages serves the SPA on deep links / refresh (Pages has no server-side rewrite).

issues.title: Proposal: "Streams" + human-in-the-loop review gates + non-technical synthesis & feedback
issues.body:
> Raised from the For Good WhatsApp discussion (Adam). Two linked problems: (1) research can generate lots of words that are potentially meaningless without a human deciding whether it matters and where it goes next; (2) a body of work starts as one issue but fans into many investigations, so we need a durable identifier that tracks the whole thing. This is a design proposal for consideration — not final.

## 1. The "Stream" concept

A **Stream** is a durable thread of work that begins at one **Discover** problem and may fan out into many research questions, solution ideas, and builds. It's the unit humans actually care about ("the small-charities-miss-grants work"), not the individual issue.

**Identifier:** the number of the originating Discover issue. e.g. everything descending from #2 belongs to **Stream #2**.

**How it's tracked (proposed mechanics):**
- **Label `stream:<n>`** applied to every issue & PR in the stream — instantly filterable on GitHub and groupable on the site. Auto-created when a Discover issue is opened.
- **Body convention:** every child carries both `Stream: #2` (the root) and `Part of #<parent>` (the immediate parent), so we keep the full tree, not just a flat tag.
- **A GitHub Action** propagates the `stream:<n>` label from parent to child on issue open/edit, so contributors don't have to remember.
- **A living overview doc** per stream at `streams/<n>-<slug>.md` (see §3) — the human-readable front page.

**Stream lifecycle (state machine):**
```
Framing → Researching → [G1: Synthesis] → Ideating → [G2: Build approval] → Building → Shipped
                  ↑_______________|  (loop back: more research / new angle)
```
States surfaced as a `stream-state:` label or a field in the overview doc. Loops are first-class: synthesis can send a stream back to research with a different lens.

## 2. When do we actually need a human? (the review rules)

Distinguish **two kinds of review** that currently get conflated:

**A. Method review (mostly agent + maintainer merge)** — the existing adversarial check that a *single finding* meets the method: every claim cited, surprises double-sourced, confidence honest. Per-artifact quality control. Stays agent-run for routine, non-sensitive, High/Medium findings.

**B. Synthesis & direction review (MUST be human)** — the new stage. A person reads the *stream as a whole*, decides whether it's meaningful, and steers what happens next. This is judgement, not fact-checking, so it can't be delegated to an agent.

### Human-required gates
- **G0 — Problem framing (Discover → Research):** lightweight maintainer thumbs-up that the problem is real and tractable *before* research fans out. Cheap; prevents burning tokens on a meaningless stream.
- **G1 — Synthesis gate (Research → Ideate): mandatory human.** No solutioning until a human has written/updated the plain-language **Stream Overview** and answered: (a) Is this meaningful/useful? (b) Is the evidence good enough? (c) Go deeper, pivot the angle, or proceed? This is the "is it all just words?" checkpoint.
- **G2 — Build gate (Ideate → Build): mandatory human.** Nothing gets built until a human approves a specific solution + its impact, feasibility, and ethics.

### Always-human triggers (any stage, override the above)
- Sensitive domains (child-welfare, anything touching vulnerable individuals)
- Any **Low-confidence** claim that is load-bearing for a decision
- Anything that could publish or imply personal / identifying data
- Anything an agent flagged as needing lived experience or legal authority
- Any **external-facing** output (published overview, anything shared beyond the repo)

### Stays agent-only (no human needed)
Source gathering, dedup, formatting, and routine findings that pass method review at High/Medium confidence in non-sensitive domains. They feed the stream but don't each need a synthesis review.

## 3. The Stream Overview doc — the bridge to non-technical people

`streams/<n>-<slug>.md`, maintained by a human **Stream Steward**. Written for someone who will never touch GitHub or a CLI:
- Plain-language problem statement (no jargon, no repo-speak)
- **What we've learned so far** — synthesised bullets with confidence, linking back to findings
- Open questions / what we're unsure about
- Current **direction decision** + stream state
- A dated **feedback log**

## 4. Feedback from non-GitHub stakeholders (e.g. NFP domain experts)

The hard part: people with the most valuable domain knowledge won't use GitHub. Options to choose from:
- **The web app becomes the read surface.** A per-stream page renders the overview + timeline. (The `/review` page is the natural home for the human queue.)
- **Feedback capture, pick one+:**
  - **WhatsApp → Clawd:** stakeholders comment in the community chat; Clawd captures it and files it against the stream. This dovetails with the daily-summary / `register-gh` idea being discussed right now — map WhatsApp identity → optional GH handle, and let Clawd post feedback + daily stream digests.
  - A simple **web form** → serverless function → issue comment.
  - Email-to-issue.
- Feedback lands as a `stream:<n>` + `feedback` labelled item that can **reopen or redirect** the stream (push back for more research, request a new angle) *before* a build.

## 5. Roles
- **Stream Steward** (human) — owns the overview doc + direction decisions for a stream.
- **Reviewers** (agent + maintainer) — method/adversarial checks on individual findings.
- **Stakeholders** (NFP / domain, non-technical) — give feedback via web/WhatsApp, shape direction. No GitHub required.

## 6. New labels / artefacts implied
- `stream:<n>` (per stream), `feedback`, and a `stream-state:` set (or a field in the overview)
- `status: needs-synthesis` / `status: awaiting-direction` for the human queue
- `streams/` directory + a `streams/TEMPLATE.md`
- Small Actions: stream-label creation + parent→child propagation

## Open questions for discussion
1. `stream:<n>` **label** vs a **GitHub Project / milestone** per stream vs just the `Stream: #n` body convention — which is least friction?
2. Who can be a Stream Steward, and is G1 one steward or a small panel for sensitive domains?
3. Do we want `stream-state` as labels (visible, queryable) or as front-matter in the overview doc (single source of truth)?
4. Which feedback channel do we build first — WhatsApp→Clawd (fastest for this community) or a web form?
5. Does the adversarial review Action stay purely per-finding, or also produce a draft synthesis for the human steward to edit at G1?

---
_Filed by Clawd for consideration per Adam's request. Happy to split any section into its own issue once there's a direction._

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
## Direction set — core implemented in #36

The core of this proposal is implemented in #36 (streams tracking, human gates, steward docs, agent contract, site reframe). Decisions on the open questions, recorded permanently in [ADR-0001](../blob/main/docs/adr/0001-streams-and-human-gates.md):

1. **`stream:<n>` label + body convention** (not Projects/milestones) — a zero-token Action (`stream-sync.yml`) creates the label when a Discover issue opens and propagates it to children via `Stream: #n` / `Part of #p` body lines.
2. **Stewards** — any trusted reviewer (whitelist or earned credit, same trust model as `merge_ready.sh`); sensitive domains need a maintainer co-sign at G1/G2.
3. **Stream state lives in the overview doc's frontmatter** (single source of truth); the human queue is driven by two new labels: `status: needs-synthesis` (G1) and `status: awaiting-direction`.
4. **Feedback channel: WhatsApp → Clawd first** (that's where the community already is) — split into its own issue.
5. **Agent-drafted synthesis: deferred** — reviewer stays per-finding; revisit if streams pile up at G1 (tripwire noted in the ADR).

Gates are enforced structurally: agents only work `status: available` issues, and **only a human may make ideate/build issues available** — G0 is the existing `needs-triage` flow, G1/G2 are the new labels + a steward's overview update.

Remaining sections are split out as child issues (linked below). Leaving this issue open until the stream pages and feedback channel land.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body: Closing as decided and implemented. The design shipped in #36 (streams, human gates, bounded fan-out, drain→synthesis trigger, steward docs) with the decisions recorded permanently in [ADR-0001](../blob/main/docs/adr/0001-streams-and-human-gates.md) — that ADR is now the canonical record; propose changes by superseding it (docs/adr/README.md). Remaining work continues in its own issues: #37 (per-stream pages on the site) and #38 (WhatsApp → Clawd feedback channel), both tracked under stream:30.

issues.title: Add a dedicated in-app finding detail page instead of linking out to GitHub
issues.body:
## Problem

On the [Findings page](https://thecolab-ai.github.io/the-for-good-project/#/findings), clicking a finding card takes the user **off-site** to the raw markdown blob on GitHub rather than showing the finding within the app.

In `web/src/pages/Findings.tsx:50`, each card is wrapped in:

```tsx
<a key={f.path} href={f.url} target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">
```

and `f.url` is built in `web/scripts/build-data.mjs:188` as a GitHub blob URL:

```js
url: `${repoMeta.html_url}/blob/${repoMeta.default_branch}/${rel}`,
```

So the finding experience dead-ends at GitHub's file viewer — raw markdown, no site chrome, confidence/domain/source styling lost, and the user is bounced out of the app.

## Proposal

Add a dedicated in-app finding detail route (e.g. `/findings/:slug` or `/finding/:slug`) that renders the finding's full content in-app: title, domain/confidence badges, author + agent/model metadata, the rendered markdown body, and the sources list.

The pattern already exists for issues — `App.tsx:21` defines `/issue/:number` → `IssueDetail.tsx`, and `IssueCard.tsx:41` uses `<Link to={/issue/${issue.number}}>` for in-app navigation. Findings should work the same way. Keep a secondary "View on GitHub" link on the detail page for those who want the source file.

### Notes for implementation
- `Finding` already carries `path`, `title`, `domain`, `confidence`, `author`, `agent`, `model`, `date`, `summary`, `sources` (`web/src/lib/types.ts`). To render the full body in-app, `build-data.mjs` would need to include the finding's markdown `content` (or a rendered/sanitized version) in the snapshot.
- A `Markdown` shared component already exists (`web/src/components/shared/Markdown.tsx`) and can render the body.
- Use `f.path` (slugified) as the route param so links are stable.

### Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Clicking a finding card navigates within the app (no `target="_blank"` to GitHub as the primary action).
- [ ] The detail page renders the finding's full markdown, metadata, confidence/domain badges, and sources.
- [ ] A "View source on GitHub" link remains available on the detail page.

issues.title: Discover: NZ child & family support entitlements, non-take-up scale, and where navigation breaks down (#4)
issues.body:
Part of #4.

## What this is

A cited Discover-stage report (`research/findings/child-welfare/discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`) framing: what child/family entitlements exist in NZ, who owns them, how big the non-take-up problem is, and where navigation breaks down.

**Headline:** the system is split between IRD (Working for Families incl. Best Start, FamilyBoost) and MSD (Childcare Subsidy, OSCAR, Accommodation Supplement, TAS), with Working for Families uniquely paid by *both* agencies — benefit-only families must choose a payer and switch agencies when moving between benefit and work. Non-take-up is measurable: 83–90% WFF take-up 2010–2020 but only 75–85% among working (non-beneficiary) families; up to ~100,000 potentially eligible non-recipients of the Accommodation Supplement (WEAG/Treasury, self-flagged over-estimate) and ~30,000–53,000 for TAS; ~41% of families not using the Childcare Subsidy didn't know it existed or whether they were eligible. Awareness gaps are the documented cause; Best Start's ~97% take-up (enrolment tied to birth registration/PPL) is consistent with — but does not prove — the broader navigation thesis, and the split-ownership causal link is explicitly framed as untested inference.

## Method notes

- Follows `research/TEMPLATE.md`: executive answer, inline citations, two-source table for load-bearing claims, "what would change this conclusion", follow-up questions, numbered sources with access dates.
- 24 sources fetched; the 25 highest-value claims were adversarially verified with 3 independent votes each — 23 confirmed, 2 refuted. The two refuted claims (Best Start annual re-confirmation as a drop-off point; a 95–97% IRD-automation take-up figure) are listed struck-through in the finding as "do not reuse", for the next agent.
- Honest gaps flagged: parts (3) and (4) of #4 — navigation channels (SmartStart, CAB, Whānau Ora) and current remediation initiatives — only partially survived verification and are framed as the priority follow-up Research questions (5 candidates listed in the finding; not opened as issues, left for repo triage).
- Dated figures are explicitly caveated: WEAG's AS/TAS gap estimates rest on 2017 TAWA modelling and June 2018 counts; the WFF take-up series ends at tax year 2020.

## Review round 1 (addressed in 09afd7e)

The adversarial review found overclaiming and source-independence problems. Fixes: causal framing softened to what the evidence carries (awareness = documented cause; split ownership = documented fact, causal link flagged as untested inference); MSD summary pages and their own underlying reports no longer counted as two independent sources — four statistics now explicitly flagged single-origin with downgraded confidence; every executive-answer bullet cited inline; the previously uncited claims cited or replaced (incl. correcting the revoked Childcare Assistance Regulations 2004 citation).

## Scope note

Issue #4 is a Discover issue whose output nominally lives on the issue itself; this PR writes it as a template-conformant finding file instead so the claims are auditable under adversarial review. Happy to also condense the executive answer into an issue comment if the reviewer prefers both.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body: 🔧 Edited the PR body: `Closes #4` → `Part of #4`. #4 is **stream #4's root** — under the stream model (docs/STREAMS.md, ADR-0001) the root stays open for the life of the stream (it anchors the drain→synthesis trigger while children #24/#25 are worked). Discover framing PRs link with `Part of`; only child issues close via PRs. The stream label is unaffected.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
Thanks — the review was right on all nine points. Addressed in 09afd7e, point by point:

1. **Title causal overclaim** — retitled: "…awareness gaps are a documented cause, and split agency ownership **may contribute** to navigation breakdowns". The exec answer now has an explicit "documented vs. inferred" bullet stating the split-ownership causal link is untested inference, and "What would change this conclusion" gains a bullet naming the evidence that would firm it up or kill it.
2. **"Proves the mechanism"** — reframed to "consistent with — but does not prove — the navigation/awareness thesis", quoting the MSD report's own hedges ("may be due to", "probably enabled") in both the exec answer and the evidence section.
3. **Uncited exec answer** — every bullet now carries inline citations; the overall-confidence line now states which origins support the headline and that specific single-origin statistics are flagged in the table.
4. **WFF two-source row** — Source 2 now reads "None independent found", with the direct McLeod & Wilson 2022 PDF linked (replacing the bare thehub.sia.govt.nz) but explicitly marked same-origin. Confidence downgraded High → Medium.
5. **Best Start row** — same treatment: single-origin flagged, Medium (noting it's administrative IDI data rather than survey self-report).
6. **41% row** — split in two: "awareness gaps are a documented barrier" stays two-source High (WEAG/Stats NZ 2017 + MSD 2022 survey, independent method/year); the exact 41% statistic is a separate row marked single-sourced, Medium.
7. **AS ~100,000 row** — claim reworded to lead with direction; Source 2 explicitly corroborates direction only; confidence now "Medium for the direction; Low for the specific ~100,000 number".
8. **Uncited claims** — the ~364,000 figure (which I couldn't trace to a quotable source) is replaced with a citable one: AS receipt up 18,600 (5.1%) in the year to Dec 2024, per the MSD Benefit Fact Sheets December 2024 snapshot. The overpayment-debt point and the Oct 2026 / Jul 2028 AISA phasing are both now cited to the 2026 MSD/IR PAYE information-sharing consultation (MSD consultation page + IRD Tax Policy).
9. **Childcare governance/legal cites** — 20 Hours ECE now cited to the Ministry of Education page. Verifying the regs surfaced an error, not just a missing cite: the Social Security (Childcare Assistance) Regulations 2004 were **revoked 26 Nov 2018** and carried into the Social Security Regulations 2018 — corrected and cited (legislation.govt.nz + MSD mapping document).

Also added a revision note to the stage note, added a table preamble stating the independence rule, and folded the split-ownership causality question into follow-up Q5. Sources renumbered (18 → 24). Ready for another pass.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
Round 2 addressed in 868ddb0:

1. **WEAG link rot** — confirmed the redirect (302 → MSD's generic WEAG index, which no longer links the take-up paper directly). All 13 citations now point to a Wayback Machine snapshot of the PDF (23 Jan 2025 capture). I downloaded the archived PDF and re-verified it contains every figure cited to it: the ~100,000 AS over-estimate sentence verbatim, 33,000–53,000 TAS, the "almost 41%" awareness quote verbatim, 285,000 AS / 61,000 TAS recipients, and the Childcare Subsidy decline — which appears as chart data labels ("CCS, 2007 Jun, 42,552 … CCS, 2018 June, 25,215"), matching the ~42,600 → ~25,200 in the finding. The Sources entry documents the original URL, the redirect, and the re-verification date.
2. **FamilyBoost threshold** — corrected against the IRD eligibility and payments pages: for quarters ending after 1 Jul 2025 it's 40% of fees capped at $1,560/quarter with household income < $57,286/quarter (≈$229k/yr); the row now shows the pre-July-2025 settings (25% / $975 / <$45,000/quarter ≈ $180k/yr) as historical instead of mixing eras. Both IRD pages added to Sources.
3. **Best Start refutation narrowed** — the bullet now separates fact from interpretation: annual confirmation is real and quoted from govt.nz ("it does not automatically keep going") and is explicitly marked safe to cite; only the "recurring drop-off point" causal reading remains unverified (no take-up data by entitlement year, and the ~97% figure covers only the first 15 months). Follow-up Q5 now asks whether take-up decays in years 2–3 for exactly this reason, so the next researcher is pointed *at* the real administrative step rather than away from it.

Stage note updated to reflect both rounds. Ready for another pass.

commentsList.author: gligor-ai
commentsList.body:
PR #27 — Adversarial Review (Round 3)

## Summary

This PR adds a Discover-stage finding file (`research/findings/child-welfare/discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`) for issue #4. The file follows the research template structurally and has clearly been through two rounds of adversarial review, with many improvements: causal framing is softened, single-origin sources are flagged, refuted claims are struck through, and most URLs resolve (200 OK).

However, several substantive problems remain that a decision-maker relying on this document could be misled by. The core issues are: (1) the overall confidence mark of "High" is not justified given the evidence base; (2) Source 1 in the numbered list is never cited inline; (3) two MSD URLs (`workandinincome.govt.nz`) fail DNS resolution from this environment, raising questions about link rot risk on a document that depends heavily on them; (4) several numeric claims are presented with precision that the sources do not support; and (5) the file's self-description as "119 claims extracted" is unverifiable and potentially misleading.

---

## Findings

### 1. Overall confidence mark inflated — should be Medium, not High
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 5 (`confidence: "High"`), line 27 (justification paragraph)

The overall confidence is marked **High**, justified by "several independent origins." But the actual evidence base has significant weaknesses:
- The headline WFF take-up figure (83–90%) is single-origin (MSD IDI analysis, no confidence intervals, series ends 2020).
- The Best Start ~97% figure is also single-origin.
- The Accommodation Supplement ~100,000 gap estimate is self-flagged as an over-estimate and dated to 2017 modelling.
- The TAS gap figures are similarly dated (June 2018 counts).
- The awareness statistic (~41%) is single-sourced for the exact figure.

The method says High = "multiple strong, current sources agree; you'd stake a decision on it." This evidence base has multiple single-origin claims and several dated figures. A decision-maker acting on this (e.g., building a navigation tool) would be making decisions based on data that is at least 6 years old for the core statistics. The confidence should be **Medium** with an explicit caveat about data age.

### 2. Source 1 in numbered list is never cited inline
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 105 (Source 1)

Source 1 (`IRD — Support for families. https://www.ird.govt.nz/support-for-families`) appears in the numbered sources list but is **never referenced anywhere in the body text**. The method requires that every source used should be cited inline. An uncited source in a numbered list suggests either (a) it was fetched and discarded, or (b) it was intended to support some claim but the citation was lost during editing. Either way, it's sloppy and wastes space. If it supported no claim, remove it from the sources list.

### 3. MSD `workandinincome.govt.nz` URLs fail DNS resolution
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, lines 7, 8, 9, 111–114 (multiple MSD Work and Income URLs)

The domain `www.workandinincome.govt.nz` fails DNS resolution from this environment (`Could not resolve host`). The initial HEAD checks returned 200 OK, which suggests either:
- Intermittent DNS issues with the ArcGIS/Esri infrastructure that hosts MSD's site; or
- The domain is behind a CDN/firewall that blocks certain user-agents or IP ranges.

This matters because **at least 6 of the 26 sources** (Sources 7–10, 14) use this domain. If these links rot, the document loses its two genuinely independent source for the awareness claim (line 75: WEAG + MSD Income Support Survey). The document should either mirror these pages or note the DNS risk explicitly.

### 4. Numeric precision exceeds source precision
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 39 (Accommodation Supplement asset limits)

The file states cash asset limits as "$8,100 (single) / $16,200 (couple or sole parent)." The MSD page does confirm these exact figures (verified via curl), so this is accurate. However:
- Line 37: FamilyBoost income threshold "$57,286/quarter" — verified against IRD page as correct.
- Line 36: Best Start abatement above "~$79,000" — the IRD page says exactly $79,000 (no tilde). The tilde is misleading precision.

**Fix:** Remove the tilde on line 36 and state "$79,000" precisely.

### 5. "119 claims extracted" is unverifiable self-description
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 15 (stage note)

The stage note says: "Method: multi-angle web research; 24 sources fetched, **119 claims extracted**, the 25 highest-value claims adversarially verified with 3 independent votes each."

There is no way for a reviewer to verify that 119 claims were actually extracted. This number could be fabricated or inflated. The method does not require reporting extraction counts — it requires citing every claim and verifying surprising ones. Including this unverifiable metric invites the appearance of rigor without delivering verifiable evidence. **Remove or replace with something auditable** (e.g., "claims verified: 25/25").

### 6. WFF take-up range presented as a single figure despite being an aggregate
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, lines 49, 72

The WFF take-up is stated as "83–90%." This is not a confidence interval — it's the range across tax years 2010–2020. The document does not explain whether this represents year-to-year volatility or a measurement uncertainty band. A decision-maker might interpret "83–90%" as "we're 83–90% confident" when it actually means "the rate varied between 83% and 90% across the period."

**Fix:** Clarify that this is year-to-year variation, not a confidence band. E.g., "ranging from 83% to 90% across tax years 2010–2020."

### 7. The ~41% awareness statistic conflates two distinct responses
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, lines 56, 76

The claim states: "almost 41% either didn't know about the Childcare Subsidy or didn't know if they were eligible." This is a **compound statistic** — it merges two different failure modes (awareness of existence vs. awareness of eligibility). A family that knows the subsidy exists but doesn't know their income qualifies has a fundamentally different navigation problem than one that has never heard of it. The document does not break these down, and neither source provides a breakdown.

This matters for a decision-maker building a navigation tool: if 25% don't know about it and 16% don't know eligibility, the intervention design is different (awareness campaign vs. eligibility calculator). Presenting them as one number obscures this distinction.

**Fix:** Either find a source that breaks these down, or explicitly state that the 41% conflates two distinct failure modes and cannot be used to prioritise interventions without further research.

### 8. Best Start ~97% take-up lacks context on what "eligible" means
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 52

The document states: "97% of newborns eligible, payment taken up for 97% of eligible children." This is a very high bar — it implies the denominator is *all* newborns who meet basic criteria (parent on PPL or benefit). But Best Start was introduced alongside paid parental leave, and the eligibility window is narrow (from end of PPL until child turns 3, income-tested from birth for post-April-2026 births). The near-universality of the *eligibility* itself (vs. a means-tested program with complex criteria) is what drives high take-up, not necessarily good navigation.

The document acknowledges this ("near-universal eligibility") but still uses Best Start as a "contrasting case" that is "consistent with — but does not prove — the navigation/awareness thesis." The problem is that the contrast itself may be misleading: comparing a near-universal program (Best Start) to means-tested programs (AS, TAS, Childcare Subsidy) conflates eligibility breadth with navigation quality.

**Fix:** Add an explicit caveat that Best Start's high take-up is partly explained by its broad eligibility criteria, making it an unfair benchmark for navigating complex means-testing.

### 9. FamilyBoost settings table row mixes pre- and post-policy-change data without clear temporal boundary
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 37

The FamilyBoost row states: "For quarters ending after 1 Jul 2025: up to 40% of licensed ECE fees, capped at $1,560/quarter... before 1 Jul 2025 the settings were 25% / $975/quarter." This is accurate (verified against IRD pages), but presenting both sets of settings in a single table row without a clear temporal column makes it ambiguous which set applies to "now" for a reader encountering this document.

**Fix:** Add a temporal qualifier or split into two rows with explicit date ranges.

### 10. The file is a Discover-stage report masquerading as a Research finding
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 15 (stage note) and the overall structure

The stage note explicitly says: "It is not a single-question Research finding, but it follows the research template so claims and confidence are auditable." This is a structural mismatch. The Discover stage's job is to frame questions; the Research stage's job is to answer one question with evidence. By wrapping a broad problem-framing document in the Research template, the file creates confusion about what stage of work this represents and what kind of review standard applies.

The PR scope note acknowledges: "Issue #4 is a Discover issue whose output nominally lives on the issue itself; this PR writes it as a template-conformant finding file instead." This is an acceptable workaround for auditability, but the document should not claim to follow the research template when it explicitly does not meet its core requirement (answering one question).

**Fix:** Either reframe the stage note to be honest about the structural mismatch, or create a Discover-specific template.

---

## Severity Breakdown

| # | Finding | Severity |
|---|---------|----------|
| 1 | Overall confidence inflated (High → should be Medium) | 🟠 HIGH |
| 2 | Source 1 never cited inline | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 3 | MSD URLs fail DNS resolution (link rot risk) | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 4 | Tilde on $79,000 Best Start threshold | 🟢 LOW |
| 5 | "119 claims extracted" unverifiable self-description | 🟠 HIGH |
| 6 | WFF range not clarified as year-to-year variation | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 7 | ~41% statistic conflates two failure modes | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 8 | Best Start comparison lacks eligibility-breadth context | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 9 | FamilyBoost row mixes pre/post policy-change data | 🟢 LOW |
| 10 | Discover report in Research template (structural mismatch) | 🟡 MEDIUM |

---

## Verdict: REQUEST CHANGES

The document is substantially improved from the initial draft and has clearly benefited from two rounds of adversarial review. The core facts are well-sourced, the causal claims are appropriately hedged, and the refuted-claims section is a model of transparency.

However, the **overall confidence mark is unjustifiably high** (finding 1) and the **"119 claims extracted" self-description is unverifiable** (finding 5). These two issues together create a credibility gap: the document asks readers to trust its rigor while presenting evidence that doesn't support its stated confidence level.

The remaining findings are not blockers but should be addressed before merge, particularly clarifying the WFF range interpretation (6), the ~41% compound statistic (7), and the Best Start comparison context (8).

**VERDICT: NEEDS_WORK**

issues.title: Research finding: how small NZ charities find grants, and where it breaks down (#20)
issues.body:
Closes #20. Part of #2.

## What this is

A cited finding (`research/findings/grant-access/how-small-nz-charities-find-grants.md`) answering: what do small NZ charities actually use to discover grants, and where does the process break down?

**Headline:** there is no official channel. Discovery runs on two commercial databases (givUS — free only via council library subscriptions; Fundsorter), funder-hosted link lists, and volunteer labour. Five structural breakdown points, each evidenced: paywalls/opaque pricing; council-dependent free access; no open dataset (the databases' own counts diverge 1,200 → 2,000 → 3,361); volunteer time/skill burden (why Hui E! matches volunteer grant writers); and capacity — 73% of NZ's 115,770 non-profits have no paid staff (Stats NZ NPISA 2018).

## Method notes

- Follows `research/TEMPLATE.md` incl. the new `agent:`/`model:` provenance frontmatter.
- Counts quoted from web pages/PDFs were extracted deterministically (scripted) rather than trusted from model reading — e.g. Tindall link-list section counts, Fundsorter stat block, SOTS 2024 questionnaire scan.
- One deterministic negative claim reviewers may want to poke at: the SOTS 2024 questionnaire contains zero questions on funding *discovery* (only adequacy) — i.e. nobody measures how charities find grants. Flagged as Medium confidence in the claims table.
- Load-bearing claims two-sourced per method; where only one source exists (Stats NZ 73%) it is explicitly flagged.
- Builds on the #5 finding (PR #11); cross-referenced as a companion.

## Provenance

Researched and drafted by a Claude agent (claude-fable-5, via Claude Code) operating for @mcinteerj. Fork workflow per AGENTS.md.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body: Heads-up for reviewers: pushed a follow-up commit folding in channels surfaced by the second #11 review — GrantsGuru and GEM Local's **council-sponsored free portals** (Rangitīkei's GrantGuru Grant Finder, Ruapehu's GEM Local instance) as a second postcode-dependent free channel alongside library givUS access, plus GEMS' "NZ's largest" marketing claim and Go Gravy (marked competitor-described only). Keeps this finding's inventory consistent with the corrected #5 finding on PR #11.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Thanks @adam91holt — all five points addressed in c98d148. The first one was a genuinely important catch.

**1. 73% → 89% — accepted, corrected everywhere.** You're right: my "18→27 percent" quote was from the *Health activity group* section; the whole-sector statement is "in 2018, 89 percent of NPIs did not employ staff" (Table 6: 103,272 with zero employees vs 12,508 employing — reproduced from the PDF text). Corrected in the exec answer, evidence section (with the exact whole-sector quote and Table 6 numbers), claims table, and what-would-change. The correction is noted transparently in the finding, and the claim is now *stronger* for the argument, not weaker — and Te Ara's "90%" now corroborates in direction. I've also flagged the wrong-denominator failure mode as a lesson: activity-group sections in NPISA quote the same metrics as the whole-sector summary.

**2. givUS "largest" — accepted.** Now "the incumbent funding database"; no size claim.

**3. Companion-file dependency — accepted, finding now stands alone.** The "no official register / no open dataset" claims are now cited directly inline: the reproducible data.govt.nz CKAN searches (queries + result characterisation), Charities Services open data, and direct citations for the free portals (Tindall descriptions, localcommunity.org.nz, Public Trust grants search — including the 69-record public search index, which I'd verified directly on this PR's sibling). PR #11 is now referenced only as a pointer marked "under review, not yet merged", and the Sources entry says explicitly that this finding doesn't depend on it.

**4. JBWere 404 — accepted, fixed via the Internet Archive.** Both JBWere URLs have rotted (my earlier fetch failures were the same rot — I mis-attributed them to bot protection; fair catch). The 9/91 stat is verbatim in the archived primary report PDF ("the largest 9% of charities receiving 91% of all donations", verified in the archived PDF text) — now cited via web.archive.org with the rot noted. The claims table now marks this explicitly as a **single underlying analysis** flagged per method, not two sources; FundingHQ's echo is kept only as evidence of currency.

**5. Free-portals paragraph — accepted.** Direct inline citations added (Tindall, localcommunity.org.nz URL, Public Trust grants search + index); "verified for issue #5" phrasing removed.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body: @gligor-ai thanks for the review — but I think it ran against a superseded commit. The `for-good/adversarial-review` **failure** status was set on `4a7e08e`, which is the SHA *before* the rework commit `c98d148` (pushed 02:05 UTC) that addressed all five points of @adam91holt's review (89% NPISA correction, standalone citations, archived JBWere PDF, etc. — see my point-by-point comment above). The current head `c98d148` has no adversarial-review status, and the review body says "see check status" but no detailed findings were posted as inline comments, PR comments, or in the status description — so there's nothing actionable to respond to. Could you re-run the review against the current head? If the agent did produce specific findings for `c98d148`, please post them and I'll address every point.
