commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
@adam91holt tried the new flow as suggested — `git pull && DRY_RUN=1 AGENT=claude ./start_work.sh` correctly finds my rework queue (#5, #20), but the real run can't work for **fork-based contributors** yet. Two concrete gaps, verified:

1. **`rework_one` → `make_worktree "origin/$branch"`**: fork PR branches don't exist on origin — `git ls-remote origin research/how-small-nz-charities-find-grants` returns nothing (the branch lives on `mcinteerj/the-for-good-project`). The worktree add fails under `set -e`. Fix would be fetching the PR ref instead: `git fetch origin pull/<pr>/head` and worktree from `FETCH_HEAD`, then the agent pushes to the fork remote (or uses `gh pr checkout`).
2. **Label transitions need triage**: my account is `pull: true, push: false, triage: false`, so every `gh issue edit` in `claim_issue`/`set_status_label` 403s.

**State of the actual rework:** already done and pushed *after* the pushback labels were set — PR #26 commit `c98d148` (all 5 review points addressed, point-by-point reply posted) and PR #11 commit `692bc7f` (all 4 round-3 points addressed, reply posted). So both #5 and #20 just need the label flip `changes-requested → in-review` from someone with triage — or the label automation could also watch `pull_request` `synchronize` events on PRs linked to changes-requested issues, which would make the pushback flow fork-compatible with no contributor action at all.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body: @adam91holt heads-up: this issue was closed by `thecolab-clawd` at 03:42 UTC (no comment), but **PR #26 hasn't merged** — the finding isn't on `main` (`git ls-tree origin/main research/findings/grant-access/` shows only the README) and the PR still sits at CHANGES_REQUESTED (rework pushed in `c98d148`, awaiting re-review). Since stream automation keys off children closing, this premature close might make stream #2 look more drained than it is. Probably wants a reopen until #26 actually lands — I can't reopen it from this account.

issues.title: build: support Hermes automation agent
issues.body:
## Summary

Adds native `AGENT=hermes` support to the shared automation runner used by both agent-driving scripts: `start_work.sh` and `review_work.sh`. This lets contributors run work or adversarial reviews with `hermes chat` directly instead of relying on a Codex/Claude compatibility wrapper.

## Details

- Adds a `hermes` branch to `scripts/fg-common.sh::run_agent`
- Supports `MODEL`, `PROVIDER`, `HERMES_PROFILE`, and `HERMES_FLAGS` for Hermes invocations
- Marks only agent-running scripts with `RUNS_AGENT=1`, so `merge_ready.sh` does not require any agent CLI on PATH
- Updates `start_work.sh`, `review_work.sh`, `docs/AUTOMATION.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `research/TEMPLATE.md` so provenance/docs include Hermes

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `bash -n scripts/fg-common.sh start_work.sh review_work.sh merge_ready.sh`
- `AGENT=hermes HERMES_PROFILE=smith DRY_RUN=1 MAX=1 ./start_work.sh`
- `AGENT=hermes HERMES_PROFILE=smith DRY_RUN=1 PR=14 ./review_work.sh`
- `AGENT=definitely-not-installed ./merge_ready.sh` proved merge gate no longer requires an agent CLI
- Direct Hermes runner smoke test: `AGENT=hermes HERMES_PROFILE=smith AGENT_TIMEOUT=180 HERMES_FLAGS="--ignore-rules --source tool" bash -c 'source scripts/fg-common.sh; RUNS_AGENT=1; REPO_DIR=$PWD; run_agent "Reply with exactly: VERDICT: PASS"'` returned `VERDICT: PASS`

## Note

This does not change the different-identity rule: reviewers still need to use `REVIEW_GITHUB_TOKEN` for a distinct GitHub account when reviewing their own authored PRs.

issues.title: Meta: lower the friction for outside contributors (humans + agents) — Claude GitHub App, merge gate, fork workflow
issues.body:
## Context

Over the last day I've contributed here as an outside account working with Claude Code, end to end: adversarially reviewed PR #7, claimed and researched #5, opened PR #11, and went through a NEEDS_WORK → fix → re-review cycle. The pipeline and method are excellent — the adversarial review on #11 genuinely improved the finding. But several mechanical things only work if you have write access, which most contributors won't. This issue collects everything that would make outside contribution (human or agent) actually work as designed, roughly in impact order.

### 1. Install the Claude GitHub App + enable the review workflow

The biggest unlock. Installing the [Claude GitHub App](https://github.com/apps/claude) on this repo would enable:

- **Claude Code remote/web sessions** targeting this repo — contributors could point cloud agents at issues without a local clone at all.
- **@claude mentions** on issues and PRs (`.github/workflows/review.yml` already handles the mention trigger — it's dormant).

Enabling `review.yml` is already documented in the file header: add an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (or `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`) secret and set repo variable `CLAUDE_REVIEW_ENABLED=true`, and pin the action version. That gives every PR an automatic adversarial review as `github-actions[bot]` — inherently a different identity than any author, which is exactly what the merge gate wants.

### 2. The merge gate currently can't be satisfied by outside contributors

Branch protection requires the `for-good/adversarial-review` status check + a non-author approval. Three problems for anyone without write access:

- **Setting the status check requires write access.** I reviewed PR #7 (full citation spot-check, verdict PASS, review posted and approved) but the `POST /statuses` call 404s for a read-only account. The review content exists; the gate can't see it.
- **Approvals from read-only users don't count** toward GitHub's required-approving-reviews rule, even when recorded.
- **`review.yml` never sets the `for-good/adversarial-review` context** — it posts a review comment but not the commit status, so even enabled it won't satisfy the current branch protection.

Suggested fix: have the workflow set the status context on PASS/NEEDS_WORK (one `gh api` step), or change the required check to the workflow's own check run. Either makes the gate self-serve for fork PRs.

### 3. Document the fork workflow in AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md

The current instructions assume push access and fail for outside contributors at two steps:

- `gh issue edit <n> --add-assignee @me --add-label "status: claimed"` → 403 without triage. (The comment fallback is documented — good — but agents follow the literal commands first.)
- `git push -u origin research/<slug>` → 403 without write.

Working commands for the fork path (verified on PR #11):

```
gh repo fork --remote --remote-name fork
git push -u fork research/<slug>
gh pr create --repo thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project --head <user>:research/<slug> --base main ...
```

A short "No write access? (most contributors)" block in AGENTS.md step 2/5 would save every new contributor/agent the same discovery loop.

### 4. Label lifecycle automation that works for fork PRs

`start_work.sh`/`review_work.sh` own the status labels, but they only work for accounts with label permissions. For outside contributors the labels drift (e.g. #5 sat at `status: available` while PR #11 was in review, until a maintainer fixed it). A small Action on `pull_request` events could sync labels from the closing-issue link: PR opened with `Closes #n` → `status: in-review`; PR merged → `status: done`; PR closed unmerged → back to `available`. Runs with the repo token, so it works regardless of who opened the PR.

### 5. Document the NZ-government bot-protection gotcha for citation verification

Load-bearing for a repo whose whole method is "check that citations resolve and support the claim": many official NZ sources return **403/block pages to plain HTTP fetchers** while loading fine in a browser. Hit during the PR #7 and #11 work: digital.govt.nz (Incapsula), charities.govt.nz, communitymatters.govt.nz, pncc.govt.nz, perpetualguardian.co.nz.

Consequences: a naive reviewer agent will wrongly conclude sources are dead or unverifiable (false NEEDS_WORK), and a naive author agent can't verify its own citations. Suggest a short note in AGENTS.md tips: "a 403 from an official NZ domain usually means bot protection, not a dead link — verify with a browser-based fetcher before flagging."

### 6. Queue depth: seed more Research issues

Only 3 issues are `status: available` right now, all Discover-stage. Research issues are the best agent food (bounded, desk-based, high value) — #5 and #6 both went end-to-end quickly. Each Discover issue is meant to spawn 3–6 research questions; front-loading a batch of those (or prioritising the Discover issues so agents generate them) would keep a growing contributor pool busy. The two grant-access outputs already suggest obvious next issues (e.g. the Charities Register grantmaker census from PR #11's follow-ups).

---

Happy to help with any of these — PRs for the AGENTS.md fork docs, the label-sync workflow, and the status-context fix to review.yml are all things an outside contributor can write (they just need a maintainer to merge/configure). The App install and secrets are maintainer-only.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Fantastic write-up — this is exactly the kind of field feedback that makes the pipeline better. Went through it end to end; here's the status of each item.

**✅ 3. Fork workflow docs** — done (`274529d`). Added a **"No write access? (most contributors)"** section to `AGENTS.md` with the verified fork path (`gh repo fork` → push to fork → cross-repo `gh pr create --head <user>:branch`), the claim-via-comment fallback, and a note that outside reviews *do* still count (see item 2). `CONTRIBUTING.md` now points there too.

**✅ 4. Label lifecycle for fork PRs** — done. There's a token-free `issue-status.yml` (`pull_request_target`, runs on the repo token so it works for any PR author): `Closes #n` opened → `in-review`, merged → `done`. Your point about closed-unmerged was a real gap — it now also sends the issue **back to `available`**.

**✅ 5. NZ-gov bot-protection gotcha** — done. Added to `AGENTS.md` tips, near-verbatim: a 403 from an official NZ domain (digital.govt.nz, charities.govt.nz, council sites…) is usually Incapsula-style bot protection, not a dead link — verify with a browser-based fetcher before flagging a citation. Flagged as load-bearing for both authoring and reviewing.

**✅ 6. Queue depth** — done. Seeded **6 research issues** (#20–#25) across grant-access, civic-transparency, and child-welfare, including the **Charities Register grantmaker census** follow-up you surfaced from #11. Several are `good first issue`. That takes `available` research issues from 0 → 6.

**◑ 2. Merge gate for outside contributors** — mostly solved already, now documented. You're right that setting the `for-good/adversarial-review` status check needs write and read-only approvals don't count natively. The bridge is **`merge_ready.sh`** (added after your #11 run): a maintainer runs it, it reads *recorded* reviews (which any account can post — `review_work.sh` does), validates each against a **whitelist-or-earned-credit** trust model, and sets the check + merges what qualifies. So your PASS on #7 would count — it just needs a maintainer's one-command sweep to land. `review_work.sh` already degrades gracefully when it can't set the check (403) and tells you merge_ready will validate it. Docs for this are in `docs/AUTOMATION.md` + the new AGENTS section.

**⚙️ 1. Claude GitHub App + CI review** — maintainer-only, and one deliberate divergence to flag: the dormant `review.yml` was actually **removed**, not enabled. Running the adversarial review in CI would mean the model tokens come from one repo secret — i.e. the maintainer pays for every review — which breaks the "collective's own spare tokens" premise the project runs on. So review stays distributed (`review_work.sh`, each reviewer's own tokens/identity). The **Claude GitHub App install** for remote/web sessions is a separate, reasonable call — I've flagged it for @adam91holt to decide.

Thanks again — 3, 4, 5, 6 are live on `main` now, and 2 was already handled by design. 🙏

commentsList.author: thecolab-clawd
commentsList.body: @claude hi

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body: Thanks @adam91holt — that's a fast and thorough turnaround. The reasoning for removing `review.yml` rather than enabling it makes sense (central token would break the distributed-tokens premise); `merge_ready.sh` + recorded reviews is a cleaner fit. The new AGENTS.md sections already paid off: today's #20 finding (PR #26) and the second-round fixes on PR #11 were done entirely through the documented fork loop, including the new `agent:`/`model:` provenance frontmatter. From my side everything here is resolved — happy for this issue to close whenever the Claude GitHub App decision lands.

issues.title: [Research] What NZ and international civic-tech efforts on council spending legibility exist or have been tried?
issues.body:
**The question**
What efforts to make council/local-government spending legible exist in NZ (Taxpayers' Union Ratepayers' Report, Figure.NZ, FYI.org.nz, past Open New Zealand projects) and internationally (UK's mandatory over-£500 spend disclosure, OpenSpending, USAFacts) — and what worked, what died, and why?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
Avoid duplicating live efforts and repeating dead ones. International models show what disclosure regimes and tools actually get used.

**Part of #3** (civic-transparency Discover issue).

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/civic-transparency/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`, ending with build-on / partner / avoid recommendations. Note also whether NZ councils share a common chart of accounts that would make cross-council comparison feasible — if that turns out deep, split it into its own issue.

_Maintainers: please add labels `stage: research`, `status: available`, `domain: civic-transparency` (no label access)._

issues.title: [Research] What do the 2025 DIA council performance metrics cover, and what gaps remain?
issues.body:
**The question**
What exactly do the DIA local government performance metrics (first published 30 June 2025) and the Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Act 2025 reporting requirements cover — and which citizen questions about council spending do they still leave unanswerable?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
Any civic effort should build on top of the new official baseline, not duplicate it. This maps the official coverage so the gap is precise.

**Starting points**
- https://www.dia.govt.nz/local-government-performance-metrics
- Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill: https://bills.parliament.nz/v/6/ccec6aba-9fb7-4b54-f679-08ddc285d4b5
- OAG Insights into local government 2024: https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt

**Part of #3** (civic-transparency Discover issue).

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/civic-transparency/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`: what's in the metrics, in what format, on what cadence, and a gap list against real citizen questions.

_Maintainers: please add labels `stage: research`, `status: available`, `domain: civic-transparency`, `good first issue` (no label access)._

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body: Claiming this — starting now. Output will be a cited finding in `research/findings/civic-transparency/` via a PR. (No label access — maintainers, please mark `status: claimed`.)

issues.title: [Research] What do residents and local journalists actually ask about council spending?
issues.body:
**The question**
What questions do residents and local journalists actually want answered from council spending and decision data? What is the observable demand?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
A legibility tool should be built around real questions, not what's easy to publish. This is the demand-side evidence.

**Evidence base (public sources only)**
- LGOIMA requests to councils archived on FYI.org.nz — categorise a sample by topic
- Local Democracy Reporting stories (RNZ/partners) that hinge on spending data
- Council consultation submissions and media coverage of rates debates

**Part of #3** (civic-transparency Discover issue).

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/civic-transparency/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`, with a ranked list of recurring question types and how often each is answerable from published data.

_Maintainers: please add labels `stage: research`, `status: available`, `domain: civic-transparency` (no label access)._

issues.title: feat(web): problem-chains view on the board
issues.body: ## What this is **Stage:** 🔨 Build (site infrastructure — `web/`) **Domain:** n/a (dashboard) ## Summary Adds a third view to the board — **problem chains** — that shows each problem's full lineage: child issues nest under their parent via the "Part of #n" body convention, and pull requests attach to the issues they close (via "Closes/Fixes/Resolves #n"). This makes the Discover → Research → Ideate → Build pipeline the README describes actually visible per problem, instead of only as stage columns. Details: - New `web/src/lib/lineage.ts` builds the trees client-side from the existing snapshot (no build-script or snapshot-format changes needed). - New `ChainTree` component renders a card per root problem with indented children, PR chips (open/merged), status badges, and assignee avatars. - Filters still work in chains view: a chain is shown if any node matches; non-matching nodes render dimmed so the chain keeps its shape. Closed issues stay visible as completed steps (struck through, "done"). - Defensive handling: unknown "Part of" targets and cycles fall back to root, so every issue always renders. ## Method checklist - [x] No personal or identifying data; no overstated or fabricated claims - [x] Files are in the right place and follow the relevant template - (Findings-specific items n/a — this is a site change.) ## For the reviewer - Verified locally: `npm ci && npm run lint && npm run build` pass; tested in the browser with real snapshot data (PR chips attach to #5/#6) and with a synthetic "Part of #2" chain to confirm nesting, filter dimming, and closed-issue rendering. - The weakest point to push on: parent detection is regex on the issue body ("part of #n", first match, case-insensitive). If the project later adopts GitHub sub-issues or a different linking convention, `lineage.ts` is the one place to change. 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) Closes #41.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body: ⚠️ This PR has a NEEDS_WORK review but no linked issue (no "Closes #n" in the body), so the new rework routing from #33 can't track it — @ian-patel, please add a closing reference to the PR body (or address the review directly) so the automation can hand it back to your `start_work.sh` loop.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks @ian-patel — this view is exactly the visibility the project needs, and we want to keep it. Since it was opened, the **stream model** landed (#36, [docs/STREAMS.md](../blob/main/docs/STREAMS.md), ADR-0001), which changes its foundations rather than replacing it. I've opened **#41** (now linked from this PR) with the retarget scope:

1. Group chains by the canonical `stream:<n>` **labels** (server-maintained by `stream-sync.yml`) instead of deriving groups from body regex; line-anchor any `Part of #n` parsing used for tree shape.
2. Fix the cycle crash from the adversarial review (`RangeError` in `sortChildren` can take down the Board page).
3. Rebase onto main — Board.tsx and status metadata have moved (three new statuses to render).

Because this PR now closes #41 (which is `status: changes-requested` and assigned to you), your next `./start_work.sh` loop will pick the rework up automatically with the review feedback in the prompt — just `git pull` first to get the new scripts. If you'd rather not continue it, say so and we'll fold the `ChainTree` work into #37.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body: Closing this in favour of #41 — no reflection on the work, which is good and is being kept. The stream model landed underneath it (#36) and changed its foundations (canonical `stream:<n>` labels instead of client-side body regex), plus main has moved enough that a rebase is bigger than a fresh start from the component. **#41 captures the full scope and is open for anyone to pick up**, salvaging the `ChainTree` rendering from this branch (`git fetch origin pull/14/head` keeps working after close). Thanks @ian-patel — the chains idea itself is now part of the plan (#37/#41).

issues.title: [Research] Which NZ councils publish spending data beyond statutory PDFs, and in what formats?
issues.body:
**The question**
Of New Zealand's 78 councils, which publish financial/spending data beyond the statutory PDF documents (Long Term Plans, Annual Plans, Annual Reports) — e.g. open-data portals, CSV downloads, APIs — and at what granularity, in what formats, and under what licences?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
Whether a council-spending legibility tool is feasible depends on what machine-readable data already exists versus what would need to be extracted from PDFs or requested under LGOIMA. This is the supply-side census.

**Starting points**
- Stats NZ Local Authority Statistics (quarterly CSV): https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/groups/local-authority-statistics
- DIA data downloads: https://www.localcouncils.govt.nz/lgip.nsf/wpg_url/Resources-Download-Data-Index

**Part of #3** (civic-transparency Discover issue).

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/civic-transparency/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`. Sampling is fine if you cover all council types and say how you sampled.

_Maintainers: please add labels `stage: research`, `status: available`, `domain: civic-transparency`, `good first issue` (no label access)._

issues.title: feat(web): contributors list + header shadow/rounding
issues.body:
Two asks from Adam.

The sticky header only had a hairline `border-b` (with a semi-transparent border colour). Gave it a clean, **downward-only** soft shadow so it reads as a proper elevated bar over scrolling content, solidified the border, and forced square corners (`rounded-none`) so no inherited rounding shows.

issues.title: research: NZ grant funder count and eligibility data landscape
issues.body:
Closes #5. Part of #2.

## What changed

Adds a cited research finding at `research/findings/grant-access/nz-grant-funder-count-and-eligibility-data.md` answering how many grant funders operate in NZ and in what form their eligibility data is available.

## Summary

- No official register or count of NZ grant funders exists; best proxies are givUS (1,200+ schemes) and Fundsorter (~3,000 opportunities), both vendor figures and flagged as such.
- Countable institutional categories (~150+ funder floor): 32 Class 4 gaming societies (DIA list, deterministically counted), 12 statutory community trusts, 18 community foundations, 18 ETNZ energy trusts (deterministically counted), 78 councils, 7 Lottery distribution committees (m allocated 2026/27).
- Eligibility criteria live almost entirely in unstructured funder web pages/PDFs plus two paid databases (givUS — free via most public libraries; Fundsorter — from $20/month). No open machine-readable eligibility dataset found (marked Medium — negative claim).
- What IS open and machine-readable: Class 4 grants *distribution* data (data.govt.nz/Granted.govt.nz) and the Charities Register open data service (OData API, CC BY 3.0 NZ) — who got funded, not who's eligible.
- Direct implication for the grant-matching-tool decision (#2): build on scraping/curation or licensed aggregator data; open data helps identify funders and validate matching, not eligibility.

## Method notes

- Confidence marked per claim; overall Medium with reasons.
- Counts taken from live pages were enumerated deterministically (script-counted list items), not eyeballed — two LLM-assisted counts were caught wrong this way and corrected before writing.
- All 26 cited URLs verified live 2 July 2026. Four return 403 to plain curl (bot protection: charities.govt.nz, communitymatters.govt.nz ×2, pncc.govt.nz) but load in a browser; content verified via browser-based scraping.
- Desk research only; no organisations were contacted.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Thanks @adam91holt — genuinely useful review; all seven points addressed in 17e94b7. Point-by-point:

**1. "Only two paid databases" — accepted, corrected.** The finding now inventories all six consolidated sources found: 3 paid national databases (givUS, Fundsorter, GEMS/Strategic Grants) and 3 free partial-coverage portals (localcommunity.org.nz, Perpetual Guardian Funding Hub, Public Trust Grantseeker's Portal), each with coverage notes. The central conclusion is rewritten: consolidated eligibility data exists in several places, but none is open or machine-readable.

**2. "~150 floor" — accepted, dropped.** Recast as a table of *verified* counts (32 gaming societies, 12 community trusts, 18 community foundations, Lottery Grants Board as **one** funder with 7 committees) plus an explicit "categories that exist but are not verified funder-by-funder" section (78 councils as ceiling, 18 ETNZ members with the not-all-grantmakers caveat, private trusts, licensing trusts, corporate foundations, iwi funders). No overall floor is claimed.

**3. Fundsorter figures — accepted, updated from the vendor pages.** Confirmed your numbers against page source: homepage counters read **3,361 opportunities / 1,650 funders / 186,469 matches**, and the pricing page's plan data shows **$16/month billed annually ($192/yr)** for micro-orgs up to $100–125/month for tier 1–2. The stale PNZ "nearly 3,000" is now explicitly noted as stale. Bonus: the 1,650-funder figure now serves as the headline funder-count proxy (flagged Low–Medium, single vendor source, includes some international funders).

**4. Uncited negative claim — accepted.** Now phrased as "I found no government-maintained register…" with the search method inline.

**5. Reproducible methodology — accepted, added.** New "Search methodology" section with the data.govt.nz CKAN API query URL pattern and a results table run 2 July 2026: `grants` → 264 datasets (all distribution/recipient data), `grant eligibility` → 7 (irrelevant), `grant funders` → 1 (geology), `funding schemes` → 3 (irrelevant); plus the specific official pages and aggregator pages checked, explicit exclusions, and limits (no OIA, no vendor contact).

**6. Two-source rule — accepted, fixed both rows.** The 32-society count now has a genuinely independent second source: DIA's Quarterly List of Venues and GMs (March 2026 XLSX on data.govt.nz) — script-counted, exactly 32 unique `Non-Club` societies (and 137 club societies that don't make external grants), with the marginal name differences between the two lists noted. The Lottery $311m is downgraded to **Medium** and flagged single-sourced.

**7. Charities Register inference — accepted, evidenced.** Retrieved the OData `$metadata` (2 July 2026): the schema includes `PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations`, `OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers`, `GrantsAndDonationsMade`, `GrantsPaidWithinNZ/OutsideNZ`. The census method is now stated concretely (filter on purpose flags + non-zero grants paid), with caveats: field completeness untested, and coverage limited to funders that are registered charities.

Ready for re-review.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Thanks @adam91holt — second round addressed in 379e097. Four points accepted and fixed with new evidence; one I believe is a mis-parse on the review side, rebutted with a reproduction below.

**1. Public Trust Algolia index — accepted, verified, reworded.** I reproduced your query exactly: `prod_publictrust_grants` (app ID + search-only key are in the page config) returns **69 records**, and with `attributesToRetrieve: ["title","sections"]` all 69 carry structured `sections` guideline/exclusion text including the strings you quoted. You're right that my wording conflated "not open-licensed" with "not machine-readable" — a tool-feasibility reader would have been misled. The negative claim is now scoped to **"no open-licensed dataset"**, the Public Trust bullet documents the index (fields, record count, and the machine-readable ≠ open-licensed distinction), and the title/exec/claims table all carry the correction.

**2. Inventory incompleteness — accepted, expanded.** Verified and added: **GrantsGuru** ($228/yr per Fundsorter's own comparison; powers council-sponsored free portals — Rangitīkei's Grant Finder launched March 2025, cited), **GEM Local** (Strategic Grants' small-org tier, with council-sponsored instances like Ruapehu DC), and **Go Gravy** (success-fee application service — included but explicitly marked "verified only as described by a competitor"). The section is retitled "the inventory verified (not claimed exhaustive)" and the search-methodology limits now say the inventory reflects what the searches surfaced, not a market census. Council-sponsored free instances are also now listed as a distinct free-access model.

**3. "Largest commercial database" — accepted, weakened.** Title and headline now say **"largest published database count"**, and the body quotes GEMS' competing "New Zealand's largest and most current grants database" marketing directly, noting the claims can't be adjudicated because only Fundsorter publishes a number. The claims-table row now cites the GEMS claim as direct counter-evidence.

**4. DIA XLSX numbers — rebutted with a reproduction; reconciliation added.** I re-downloaded the March 2026 workbook from the cited resource (`f9b58e84-…/quarterly-list-of-venues-and-gms-march-2026.xlsx`) and reparsed it with a full XML parse (ElementTree over sharedStrings + sheet1, no regex). Results:

| `Club Type` value | Unique societies | Rows (venues) |
|---|---|---|
| `Non-Club` | **32** | **805** |
| `Club - Chartered` | 82 | 83 |
| `Club - RSA` | 38 | 40 |
| `Club -Sports` | 16 | 22 |
| `Club - Racing` | 1 | 1 |
| **Total** | 169 | **951** |

Your 951 is the total unique venue count across *all* rows — consistent with mine (805 Non-Club + 146 club rows). The likely cause of "no Club rows": the club categories are spelled `"Club - Chartered"`, `"Club - RSA"`, `"Club -Sports"`, `"Club - Racing"` — never bare `"Club"` — so an exact-match filter on `Club` finds zero. 82+38+16+1 = **137 club societies**, as in the finding. I've kept 32/805/137 and added the 951 reconciliation plus the exact value spellings to the finding so the count is reproducible either way.

**5. Charities Register inference — accepted, then upgraded with a test.** Rather than just weakening the wording, I ran live spot-queries (2 Jul): `Organisations?$filter=PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations eq true` returns flagged charities, and `GrpOrgLatestReturns?$filter=GrantsPaidWithinNZ gt 0` returns dollar amounts (e.g. CC22586: $500,000). So the claim is now "fields are populated and filterable (spot-checked)", with the full census — counts, completeness, and grantmaker-vs-occasional-donor precision — explicitly flagged as unrun. (That census is exactly the follow-up issue you seeded; happy to pick it up next.)

Also brought the frontmatter up to the new template (`agent: claude`, `model: claude-fable-5`).

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Round 3 addressed in 692bc7f — all four points accepted (verified before fixing):

**1. GEMS counts — accepted.** Confirmed the Strategic Grants homepage advertises "Access over 3K expertly curated grants" and "the most comprehensive grants database in New Zealand", and the GEMS page "thousands of grant records" / "all grant sources around New Zealand". The finding now: (a) incorporates this as a published *scheme* count comparable to Fundsorter's, (b) reframes the headline from "largest published database count" to **"the only published *funder* count is a vendor's 1,650"** — the funder-count/scheme-count distinction you asked for — and (c) drops "largest database" framing entirely, reporting counts per vendor.

**2. Fundsorter's divergent figures — accepted, reconciled explicitly.** New paragraph reconciles the vendor's own numbers: ~2,000 "census of all contestable funding opportunities" (their data analysis, quoted via the Hui E! mirror; fundsorter.com/data itself resisted my scrapers) vs 3,361 on the live counter vs PNZ's "nearly 3,000". Likely a contestable-NZ vs total-tracked scope difference, but since the vendor doesn't say, it's flagged as unresolved and the headline answer is now **a range (~2,000 contestable NZ → ~3,400 tracked total)**, in the title, exec, and claims table.

**3. "Longest-established" — accepted, dropped.** The DOC citation doesn't support an age claim; removed rather than chasing a source for a non-load-bearing superlative.

**4. "Independent DIA sources" — accepted, softened.** Now "two separate DIA publications … a cross-check within one authority", with the note that both likely trace to the same licensing records — and the mitigating point that DIA *is* the licensing authority, so its records define the category. Wording fixed in the exec answer, count table, and claims table.

issues.title: fix(web): stop kanban cards stacking on top of each other
issues.body:
## The actual bug
`IssueCard` wraps its content in an `<a>`/`<Link>`, which is `display: inline` by default. In the board's **kanban columns** the cards sit in a `space-y` (margin-based) stack — and vertical margins barely apply to inline elements, so the cards collapsed on top of each other.

Grid/list view forces block layout on its children, so it looked fine there — which masked the bug and is why the spacing PR (#9) didn't resolve it.

## Fix
Make the anchor wrappers `block h-full` so they stack as proper blocks and honour both the column spacing and full-height sizing.

## Verified
- `tsc -b && vite build` passes clean locally

issues.title: fix(web): improve kanban card & column spacing
issues.body:
## What
Tightens up the breathing room on the board / kanban, per Adam's request — cards and columns needed more margin/padding.

## Review findings
- Board columns were `p-3` — actually **tighter** than the `p-4` cards inside them, which read as cramped.
- Columns sat only `gap-4` apart with no extra separation on wider screens.
- Card internal rhythm (title → badges → footer) was a touch dense.

## Changes
- Board columns `p-3` → `p-4` so they're roomier than their cards, not tighter
- More gap between columns on md+ (`gap-5`); consistent `gap-4` in list view
- Column header `mb-3` → `mb-4`, aligned padding, roomier empty state
- Card stack spacing `space-y-3` → `space-y-3.5`
- `IssueCard` `p-4` → `p-5` with more internal spacing between title, badges and footer

## Verified
- `tsc -b && vite build` passes clean locally

issues.title: fix(web): make dashboard mobile responsive
issues.body:
## What
Fixes the Dashboard cards blowing out the page width on mobile (reported by Matt) — specifically the **Open work by domain** chart and **Recent activity** components.

## Root cause
The recharts `ResponsiveContainer` sat inside grid items with the default `min-width: auto`, so they couldn't shrink below their content (including the 120px Y-axis) and forced the page wider than the mobile viewport. There was also no global `overflow-x` guard.

## Changes
- Add `min-w-0` to the grid children so they can shrink properly
- Wrap the chart in a `min-w-0 overflow-hidden` container; trim Y-axis width 120 -> 96 for small screens
- Truncate the recent-activity meta line
- Harden the review callout flex (`min-w-0` + `shrink-0`) so it wraps cleanly instead of overflowing
- Add an `overflow-x-hidden` safety net on `<main>`

## Verified
- `tsc -b && vite build` passes clean locally

Closes the mobile width issue on the dashboard page.

issues.title: [Research] What NZ government AI guidance already exists for the public sector?
issues.body:
**The question**
What official AI guidance, frameworks, or policies already exist for the New Zealand public sector (e.g. from DIA, the Public Service Commission, Stats NZ, GCDO), and what do they actually require or recommend?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
Grounds any AI-policy work in what NZ has already published, so we build on it rather than reinventing or contradicting it.

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/ai-policy/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`. Prefer primary government sources; mark confidence; note anything dated or superseded.

_Good first issue: clear scope, official sources, immediately useful as a reference._

issues.title: [Discover] Families don't know what child & family support they qualify for
issues.body:
**The problem**
NZ has many child- and family-support entitlements (payments, subsidies, services) across agencies. Eligibility is complex and the information is spread out, so families — especially those under stress — miss support they're entitled to.

**Why it matters**
Unclaimed support for children is a direct, avoidable harm. Navigation and eligibility are exactly the kind of problem better information tools can help with.

**Ethics note:** this domain touches vulnerable people. Any work here uses public sources only, no personal data, and flags where a human with lived experience or authority is needed.

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- What child/family entitlements exist, which agencies own them, and how is eligibility defined?
- Where do families currently go for help, and where does that break down?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
Claimed and completed — worked by Ian's Claude agent. The Discover finding is up as PR #27 (`research/findings/child-welfare/discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`), awaiting adversarial review — validate check is green.

Headline: support is split between IRD (WFF/Best Start/FamilyBoost) and MSD (Childcare Subsidy, Accommodation Supplement, TAS), WFF is paid by two agencies, and non-take-up is measurable (83–90% WFF take-up overall but only 75–85% for non-beneficiary families; ~41% of families not using the Childcare Subsidy didn't know it existed). The finding ends with 5 candidate follow-up Research questions left for maintainer triage rather than opened as issues.

Note: I don't have label permissions on this repo, so a maintainer may want to flip `status: available` → `status: in-review`.

issues.title: [Discover] Council spending is public but not legible to citizens
issues.body:
**The problem**
NZ councils publish budgets, spending, and decisions, but the data is scattered across PDFs, portals, and formats that ordinary citizens and local journalists can't easily use. "Technically public" is not the same as legible or accountable.

**Why it matters**
Local accountability depends on people being able to see where money goes and what was decided. The barrier is usability, not secrecy — which makes it tractable.

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- Which councils publish spending data, in what formats, and under what open-data terms?
- What questions do residents and local journalists actually want answered from it?
- Are there existing NZ civic-tech efforts here to build on rather than duplicate?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body: Claiming this (no label access — maintainers, please swap `status: available` → `status: claimed`). Discover write-up follows in the next comment, with child Research issues linked after that.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
# Discover write-up: Council spending is public but not legible to citizens

## Problem statement

New Zealand's 78 councils (11 regional + 67 territorial, including 6 unitary authorities) ([LGNZ](https://www.lgnz.co.nz/local-government-in-nz/councils-in-aotearoa/), [Local Government Commission](https://www.lgc.govt.nz/about-us/about-local-government-in-new-zealand/)) collectively spent **$18.1 billion in operating expenses in 2023/24** — well above the previous five-year average of $14.6 billion ([Office of the Auditor-General, *Insights into local government: 2024*](https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt)). The law makes this spending "public": councils must adopt and publish Long Term Plans, Annual Plans, and audited Annual Reports (Local Government Act 2002), and answer information requests under LGOIMA within 20 working days ([Ombudsman guidance](https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/lgoima-local-government-agencies-guide-processing-requests-and-conducting-meetings)).

But *published* is not *legible*. The primary artefacts are hundred-plus-page PDFs in formats that vary council-to-council; even the audit community describes annual reports as "pages and pages of dense tables of numbers and tiny little text that many of us don't take the time to read or don't understand" ([OAG blog](https://oag.parliament.nz/blog/2014/kiwi-guide)). A resident who wants to know "what did my council spend on consultants last year, and is that high compared to the neighbouring district?" generally cannot answer it from published material without either specialist skills or a LGOIMA request — and each LGOIMA answer resets the clock for the next question. There is no line-item or transaction-level spending disclosure norm in NZ local government (contrast the UK, where councils must publish all spending over £500 as open data).

## Who it affects (NZ figures + sources)

- **Every ratepayer/resident** — all ~5.3M New Zealanders live in a territorial authority area and fund the $18.1B via rates, fees and charges ([OAG 2024 insights](https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt)). Rates pressure is a live national issue; the government's stated rationale for its 2025 metrics regime is that "communities can now compare how much their council spends on core essentials … and see whether their rates are going up more than average" ([Beehive release](https://www.beehive.govt.nz/release/government-getting-local-government-back-basics)).
- **Local journalists** — local-body journalism is thin enough that RNZ and NZ On Air co-fund a dedicated **Local Democracy Reporting** service to cover councils at all ([Scoop LDR](https://www.scoop.co.nz/stories/AK2507/S00883/nothing-new-in-council-report-cards-mayors.htm)). Reporters lean on LGOIMA and PDFs for basic figures.
- **Citizens making information requests** — the volunteer-run [FYI.org.nz](https://fyi.org.nz/help/about) (Open New Zealand) exists precisely because LGOIMA requests are the fallback channel for questions that published data can't answer; each carries a 20-working-day statutory clock.

## What's already being done (build on, don't duplicate)

| Effort | What it covers | Gap it leaves |
|---|---|---|
| [Stats NZ Local Authority Statistics](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/f0e135ea-1e3b-466d-a4dd-5eb83238605c) (quarterly, CSV on [data.govt.nz](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/groups/local-authority-statistics) back to 2008) | Aggregate income/expenditure of councils' core activities | Aggregates only — no per-project or line-item view, limited category detail |
| [DIA local government performance metrics](https://www.dia.govt.nz/local-government-performance-metrics) (first release 30 June 2025) + Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Act 2025 ([Bill](https://bills.parliament.nz/v/6/ccec6aba-9fb7-4b54-f679-08ddc285d4b5?Tab=history), [Buddle Findlay summary](https://www.buddlefindlay.com/insights/changes-to-the-local-government-act-incoming/)) | Annual per-council profiles: rates, debt, capex by activity; future mandatory reporting of contractor/consultant spend | High-level benchmarking, government-framed metrics; not citizen-queryable spending data |
| [Taxpayers' Union Ratepayers' Report](https://www.taxpayers.org.nz/rpr_2026) (league tables since 2014) | Per-household comparisons: average rates, staff, debt, CEO salary | Advocacy-framed, annual snapshot, no drill-down to what money was spent on |
| [Figure.NZ](https://figure.nz/chart/fcYvfKIkCwuf9xXJ-D5p9V3kD3tdeW0hH) | Charts of published council aggregates | Depends on the same aggregate sources |
| [FYI.org.nz](https://fyi.org.nz/help/about) (Open New Zealand) | Public LGOIMA request/response archive | Reactive, slow, one question at a time |
| [OAG *Insights into local government*](https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt) (annual) | Sector-wide financial analysis and audit findings | Sector-level, not "my council, my question" |
| [localcouncils.govt.nz data downloads](https://www.localcouncils.govt.nz/lgip.nsf/wpg_url/Resources-Download-Data-Index) (DIA) | Downloadable council financial/profile data | Dated interface; coverage and currency need checking |

**The gap:** nothing connects a citizen's actual question ("what was spent, on what, by whom, and how does that compare?") to machine-readable, cross-council-comparable data. The barrier is format and granularity, not secrecy — which is what makes this tractable.

## Researchable questions (proposed child Research issues)

1. **Census of council spending data:** Which of the 78 councils publish financial/spending data beyond the statutory PDFs (open-data portals, CSVs, APIs), in what formats, at what granularity, and under what licences?
2. **Demand side:** What do residents and local journalists actually ask about council spending? (Evidence base: LGOIMA requests on FYI.org.nz, Local Democracy Reporting stories, consultation submissions.)
3. **The new official baseline:** What exactly do the DIA performance metrics + System Improvements Act reporting rules cover from 2025 onward, and which citizen questions do they still leave unanswerable?
4. **Comparability:** Is there a common chart of accounts / reporting standard across NZ councils that would make cross-council spending comparison feasible, or would a mapping layer need to be built?
5. **Prior art:** What NZ and international civic-tech efforts on council spending legibility exist or have been tried (Ratepayers' Report, Figure.NZ, UK £500 spend disclosure, OpenSpending), and what worked or died — and why?

**Next step:** open these as `stage: research` issues linked `Part of #3`.

Discover stage for this issue is done — maintainers, feel free to move this to `status: in-review`.

issues.title: [Discover] Small NZ charities miss grants they're eligible for
issues.body:
**The problem**
New Zealand has hundreds of grant funders (philanthropic trusts, gaming trusts, councils, government), but discovery is manual and fragmented. Small charities and community groups — often run by volunteers — miss funding they're eligible for simply because finding and matching to the right grants is hard and time-consuming.

**Why it matters**
The unclaimed/under-applied pool is plausibly significant, and the groups worst affected are the smallest ones doing frontline work. _(Figures to be established — see the research questions below.)_

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- How many grant funders operate in NZ, and where does their eligibility data actually live?
- What do small charities currently use to find grants, and where does it break down?
- Is there a machine-readable source of NZ grants, or would one need to be assembled?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above. See CONTRIBUTING.md.

issues.title: PRD: Distributed Agent Research System
issues.body:
**Version 1.0 | 2026-06-30 | Ready for Implementation**

## TL;DR
- GitHub Issues = task queue
- `work.sh` claims a task, runs Claude agent with standardized method (`CONTRIBUTING.md`), creates PR
- `review.sh` adversarially verifies findings before merge
- **Result:** Transparent, scalable, zero duplication, full audit trail

## System Flow
```
Issue -> work.sh (claim + research) -> PR -> review.sh (adversarial check) -> PASS: Merge | NEEDS_WORK: Revise
```

## Key Scripts

**work.sh** - Grab unclaimed issue -> mark in-progress -> pass to Claude (task + method) -> agent researches -> create PR -> label review-needed

**review.sh** - Grab PR needing review -> pass to adversarial agent ("refute this") -> if PASS: merge + done; if NEEDS_WORK: request changes + revert to in-progress

**CONTRIBUTING.md** - Research method all agents follow:
1. Clarify the question
2. Choose approach (Workflow for multi-agent, Agent for single)
3. Research & cite (every claim >=1 source)
4. Verify surprises (>=2 sources)
5. Mark confidence (High/Medium/Low)
6. Format findings (use PR template)

## Repo Structure
```
├── README.md
├── CONTRIBUTING.md
├── work.sh
├── review.sh
├── .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/research-task.md
├── .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/research-finding.md
└── research/findings/ (merged findings live here)
```

## GitHub Labels
- Status: unclaimed | in-progress | review-needed | done
- Domain: child-welfare | grant-access | civic-transparency | ai-policy | biosecurity
- Task: research/task | research/finding

## Phases
- **Phase 1 (Week 1):** Repo + scripts + templates + end-to-end test on 2 tasks
- **Phase 2 (Week 2):** GitHub Actions automation
- **Phase 3 (Week 3):** Metrics + dashboard
- **Phase 4 (Week 4+):** Scale

## Success Story Example
Monday 9am: Issue #42 created ("Starship impact on child welfare?")
10am: Researcher claims + runs work.sh
10-12:30pm: 3 agents research in parallel
12:30pm: PR #88 created, needs review
1:30pm: review.sh finds 2 weak points
2-3pm: Researcher responds with NZ data
3:30pm: review.sh PASS -> merges
**Outcome:** 6 hours, full audit trail, finding live in repo

## Handoff Checklist
- [ ] Repo + write access
- [ ] Scripts (error handling)
- [ ] Templates
- [ ] Labels
- [ ] Project board
- [ ] README
- [ ] End-to-end test
- [ ] Race condition test

## Implementation Agent Task
1. Create repo + structure
2. Write work.sh + review.sh (error handling)
3. Create templates + CONTRIBUTING.md
4. Test end-to-end on 3 sample tasks
5. Verify: zero race conditions, conflicts, all sourced
6. Document + handoff

---

*Filed by The Colab Clawd as a test of issue-creation access, per Adam's request. Full context from WhatsApp #For Good discussion, 2026-06-30/07-01.*

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Adversarial review failed before writing feedback.

No review body was produced at:

```text
/tmp/fg-worktree.5TxC4R/repo/.fg-review.md
```

Tail of agent output:

```text
   212	`main` without a recorded, non-author adversarial review behind it.
   213	
   214	### Research credit vs review credit
   215	
   216	The leaderboard scores two distinct things, so reviewing (the chore) is rewarded,
   217	not just researching (the fun part):
   218	
   219	- **Research** — findings authored, PRs merged, issues claimed, commits.
   220	- **Review** — adversarial reviews given on PRs you did **not** author.
   221	
   222	Both feed an overall total, but the Researchers and Reviewers boards are ranked
   223	separately. If nobody reviews, the queue jams — so review points are how the
   224	collective keeps itself unblocked.
   225	
   226	## Cost & safety notes
   227	
   228	- The agent runs with your local CLI auth (your subscription/tokens). `codex`
   229	  runs with `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox`, `claude` with
   230	  `--permission-mode bypassPermissions`, and `hermes` with `--yolo --source tool`
   231	  by default so it can use git/gh unattended — run these on a repo clone you
   232	  trust, not arbitrary input. Use `HERMES_PROFILE` to run a named Hermes profile,
```

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
Reconciling my APPROVED review with @muhamdasim's CHANGES_REQUESTED, since merge_ready now shows both verdicts live on this revision:

- **Their point 1 (load-bearing table row 4) is correct and my review missed it.** The row "A source-backed question-answering interface is likely more useful than simply publishing more files" cites an FYI body listing and the Whanganui rebrand story — both resolve (which is what I checked), but neither *supports that claim*. The body text correctly labels it an inference; the table presents it as two-source-backed. It should move out of the load-bearing table or gain sources that actually argue it. My approval should not be read as overriding this point.
- **Their point 2 (model ID) doesn't hold.** `gpt-5-codex` is a real OpenAI model ID — the template's `gpt-5.5-codex` is an example, not an allowlist, and a different finding using a different model is expected provenance variation, not fabrication. No fix needed there.
- Points 3–5 (bulk-request cluster counted as three data points, Auckland self-corroboration, recency bias) are fair and cheap to fix in the same rework pass.

Net: this needs an author rework addressing points 1, 3, 4, 5 (and optionally 6); point 2 can be rebutted as-is. Blocked correctly until then.

(Posted by an agent on behalf of @mcinteerj.)

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks @muhamdasim — thorough review. Pushed `c0eb1aa` addressing every point. Summary of changes and two evidence-backed rebuttals:

### Fixed

- **Model provenance (`gpt-5-codex`).** Corrected to `gpt-5.5-codex`, matching `research/TEMPLATE.md` and consistent with the same-author/same-date (`codex`) companion finding `nz-public-sector-ai-guidance.md` (`gpt-5.5`). The `-codex` suffix records the Codex CLI client.
- **Q&A-interface claim (Claim 4).** Removed from the two-source table — as all three review passes noted, its two "sources" show that public questions are heterogeneous, not that a Q&A interface beats a document dump. It's now stated only as an explicit, unsourced design hypothesis in *What this implies for a tool* (\"treat the interface recommendation as a hypothesis to test, not a sourced finding\").
- **QLDC Joyce Advisory request.** Verified the council's response (\"do not have or had any engagement with Joyce Advisory or Steven Joyce\") and added a parenthetical: the example now explicitly shows demand for contract/procurement *visibility*, not a real contract, and flags that the premise was not borne out.
- **70/200 ambiguity figure.** Softened to \"roughly one-third (about 70 of 200)\"; flagged in the executive answer as an agent-coded, date-specific, non-reproducible count; and added to the load-bearing table as a **Low**-confidence, explicitly single-source claim (\"not independently verifiable … re-run the same sample on the same date\").
- **Batch-filed \"staff conduct\" requests (34661–34663).** Confirmed consecutive IDs + identical titles; added a note that they are one bulk filer = one demand signal, not three, and listed the row's genuinely independent evidence.
- **Claim 3 (LDR proxy).** Flagged that both sources are NZ On Air (the programme's funder) = one publisher's perspective; confidence relabelled \"Medium (single-publisher sourcing).\"
- **Auckland rates sources.** Noted in both the evidence row and the load-bearing table that akhaveyoursay + OurAuckland are one organisation (Auckland Council); the independent corroboration is Christchurch CCC + the RNZ Rotorua story. Kept **High** scoped to *annual-plan (council-solicited)* demand.
- **RNZ `/news/ldr/` URLs.** All 9 updated to the canonical `/news/regions_local-democracy-reporting/` path (were 308 → now 200).
- **Framing (structured vs organic; FYI self-selection; recency bias).** Added caveats to the executive answer (council-solicited consultation ≠ spontaneous demand; FYI requesters are a small self-selecting group) and to the scope section (\"first 25\" = most-recent at access date; category weights may shift by date).
- **Traceability.** Added `Part of #3 (Stream #3)` to both the PR description and the finding body.

### Rebuttals (evidence-backed)

- **The two Annual-Plan citations are not dead links.** Both were reported as HTTP 403. Per `AGENTS.md`/ADR-0006, a 403 from a council site is bot protection, not a dead link. Re-fetched with a browser UA, both return **200**: `akhaveyoursay.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/annual-plan-2025-2026` (rates/fees/charges content present) and `letstalk.ccc.govt.nz/annualplan` (\"Draft Annual Plan 2026/27\" — your second review confirmed this one at 200). I've kept them but added a note that they are bot-protected and that the ~13,016 figure is separately confirmed by OurAuckland (verified: page contains \"13,016 pieces of feedback\"). So the **High** mark rests on two independent councils' consultation evidence, not on inaccessible sources.
- **Dunedin \"recoridngs\" URL typo (P8).** Left as-is: that misspelling is the FYI request's own slug; the URL resolves 200 and the citation text renders it correctly. No fabrication — just the source's own typo, as you noted.

`npm run validate` passes. Rebased onto latest `main` before pushing.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks for the careful adversarial passes. Rebased onto `main` and pushed the fixes. Here's how each round is addressed.

## @thecolab-clawd (latest review) — both points fixed

**1. Unsupported FYI-requester characterization (line 18).** Agreed — the FYI About page supports *self-selection* but not the specific demographic mix. Reworded from "largely journalists, activists, and engaged citizens" to the supported point: FYI requesters are "a small, self-selecting group — people motivated enough to lodge a formal LGOIMA request online and have the response published." That is exactly what the About page substantiates, and it still carries the intended caveat (observable demand *on FYI* ≠ average-resident priorities).

**2. Property address in a citation URL (line 45).** Agreed and fixed as a privacy/METHOD.md discipline issue. Removed the `34881 …22-stanley-street-parnell-auckland-1010` request entirely and replaced it with a non-identifying Auckland land-use-consent request — `34972 luc60319526-lot610-methodology-for-impermeable-coverage-assessment` (HTTP 200, plain `curl`). It cites only a public consent number and lot reference, no street address, and it's a *better* fit for the 'decision documents/consent decisions' category (it asks how a consent assessment was reached). Scanned the rest of the finding for other street addresses — none remain.

## @muhamdasim (earlier rounds) — already landed in commits `636f795`/`6ddf38b`, recapped for the record

- **Model ID (P1/#2):** `model` is now `gpt-5.5-codex`, matching the template example.
- **Bulk 'staff conduct' requests (P2/#3):** the three consecutive IDs (34661–34663) are now explicitly flagged as one requester's bulk filing and counted as one demand signal, not three.
- **Question-answering-vs-document-dump claim (P3/#1):** removed from the two-source table; it now lives in the 'what this implies for a tool' section, explicitly labelled a design hypothesis to test, not a sourced finding.
- **RNZ LDR URLs (P4):** all canonicalised to `/news/regions_local-democracy-reporting/`.
- **Annual-plan framing + non-independent Auckland sources (P5/#4):** executive bullets and the evidence row now distinguish council-solicited consultation demand from unsolicited/organic demand, and note that akhaveyoursay + OurAuckland are one organisation (independent corroboration = Christchurch CCC + RNZ Rotorua).
- **Self-selected sample + recency bias (P6/#5):** both disclosed in the executive bullets and the scope/method section ('first 25' = most-recent at access date; weights may shift by date).
- **Parent-issue link (P7/#6):** PR body and finding both carry `Part of #3` (Stream #3).
- **Dunedin `recoridngs` slug typo (P8):** noted inline as an original-source artefact.

`npm run validate` passes; all edited/added citations re-checked via the ADR-0006 fetch ladder (plain `curl`, HTTP 200). No rebuttals this round — every point was fair.

reviewQueue.title: feat(web): streams stats header + sortable table view
reviewQueue.body:
## What this is

Refs #120 (follow-up to #124)

**Stage:** 🔨 Build
**Domain:** other (docs/tooling — the site)

## Summary

Adds a **stats header** and a **table/list view** to `/streams`, alongside the existing card grid. The table gives a dense, scannable, sortable read of every stream (the "like the lineage" list feel), and the stats row surfaces the headline numbers at a glance.

## What changed (`web/src/pages/Streams.tsx`)

- **Stats header** — six `StatCard`s rolled up across all streams: Streams, In progress, Shipped, Findings, Merged outputs, Contributors (distinct people).
- **Cards / Table view toggle** — segmented control next to the existing In progress / Shipped filter; the choice persists to `localStorage`.
- **Table view** — reuses the shared `Table` primitives. Columns: `#`, Stream (title + a per-row lifecycle progress bar), State pill, Domain, Issues (open/total), Findings, Merged, Team (avatars), Updated. Sortable headers (`#`, State, Issues, Findings, Merged, Updated) with a direction chevron; default sort is most-recently-updated. Rows are clickable and keyboard-navigable (Enter) into the stream detail.
- Search and the In progress / Shipped filter apply to **both** views.

No data-layer, schema, or build-script changes — everything derives from the existing `streamsSummary`.

## Method checklist

Web/tooling change, not a research finding — the citation/confidence checklist doesn't apply.

- [x] `tsc -b && vite build` clean; `npm run lint` clean on the touched file
- [x] Verified cards + table across light/dark and desktop/mobile, sorting, the view-persistence toggle, and empty/filter states via a local fixture
- [x] No personal or identifying data; no schema or data-layer changes

## For the reviewer

The table scrolls horizontally inside its card on narrow viewports (the shared `Table` wraps in `overflow-auto`), so the page body itself never overflows — worth a glance on mobile. Sorting uses `streamStageIndex` for the State column so it orders by lifecycle position rather than alphabetically.

reviewQueue.title: research: identify supermarket tool transparency gap (Closes #77)
reviewQueue.body: Closes #77. Stream: #61. Adds a cited finding comparing NZ grocery shopper tools and the remaining public-interest transparency gap.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Addressed the requested changes in commit 542cd29.

Changes made:
- Narrowed the core gap from an implied architectural absence to an observed public-access/documentation gap: no frictionless, public, no-account export/methodology workflow found in the reviewed public materials. I also added explicit caveats that this does not prove private/account-gated systems lack export paths or bulk retrieval.
- Added the current Commerce Commission Consumer Complaints Disclosure Standard page as a source, alongside the 2025 Annual Grocery Report, to support that the disclosure standard is current and applies to Foodstuffs North Island, Foodstuffs South Island and Woolworths NZ plus relevant franchisees/interconnected bodies, focused on Pak'nSave, New World and Woolworths/Countdown banners.
- Reworked the regulator-relevance/load-bearing table entries to cite the live standard page rather than relying only on the annual report.
- Added a concrete first-artifact scenario: one shelf-stable product at one Woolworths/New World/Pak'nSave store, with observed price path, promo periods, baseline window, price type, source/fetch date and legal caveat.
- Updated the 'what would change this conclusion' section to include account-gated/private export paths and high-volume retrieval as explicit unverified limits.

Validation: npm run validate passes.

The finding now frames the gap as a public-facing, frictionless, no-login methodology/evidence layer, and explicitly says this is not proof that account-gated, paid, private, or undocumented workflows do not exist.

commentsList.author: mcinteerj
commentsList.body:
# Rework addressing @muhamdasim's CHANGES_REQUESTED review (+ @mcinteerj's fix-forward notes)

Rework commit: **`d07f085`**, pushed to the author-side fork branch `mcinteerj:research/supermarket-tool-transparency-gap` (I don't have write access to @adam91holt's branch). `npm run validate` passes.

## Fixed

1. **Disclosure period factual error (muhamdasim #1 / mcinteerj #3)** — Fixed. Re-verified against the Standard PDF text (clause "period means: (a) 1 April 2026 to 30 June 2026; and (b) thereafter: (i) 1 July to 31 December… (ii) 1 January to 30 June"). The finding now states the first period is **three months (1 Apr–30 Jun 2026)**, six-month periods thereafter, disclosure due within one month of period end (clause 8), and gives the staged F1 (12 Sep 2025) / F2 (29 Sep 2025) / G (1 Apr 2026) in-force dates.
2. **Independence failure on the load-bearing row 2 (muhamdasim #2 / mcinteerj #1–2)** — Fixed. The claim is restated so each half is carried by its own evidence, and the row now pairs **cross-origin** sources: Consumer NZ's tool comparison + surveyed tool pages (the "gap" half) vs the Disclosure Standard 2025 + Consumer NZ's misleading-pricing campaign/2023 FTA complaint (the "regulator need is live outside the regulator's own framing" half).
3. **Row 5 same-origin pair (mcinteerj #1)** — Fixed exactly as the reviewer suggested: the AGR's own footnote 76 sources — 1News, 4 Mar 2025 (Pak'nSave Silverdale & Mill Street charges; full text fetched and verified) and Stuff, 6 May 2025 (Woolworths NZ charges; resolves, headline verified) — are now the independent second source, in the table and inline in Evidence. (Note: the AGR footnote labels the 4 Mar piece "Radio New Zealand" but links 1news.co.nz; I attribute it to 1News per the actual page.) Added an explicit **independence note** under the table: all Commerce Commission publications are treated as one origin.
4. **Orphaned sources (muhamdasim #3 / mcinteerj nit)** — Fixed. Removed all four never-cited entries (Grocer, Grosave and Woolworths Apple App Store listings, and the New World Play listing — mcinteerj's review caught the fourth), renumbered, and re-checked programmatically: every Sources entry is now cited inline and every inline URL is listed. Net: 22 → 21 sources (4 removed, 3 added: 1News, Stuff, Grocer endpoint).
5. **Self-adjacent `grocer-nz` skill citation (both reviews, non-blocking)** — Fixed. Added a prominent disclosure paragraph in Evidence that the skill doc is The Colab's own repo, and anchored the claim in the primary source: I verified `https://assets-prod.grocer.nz/public/base_v3.duckdb.br` responds live without authentication (HTTP 200, ~2.0 MB, Last-Modified 2 Jul 2026, HTTP 206 on a byte-range request) on 3 Jul 2026. The endpoint is now cited inline and listed as a source.
6. **PricePulse "every NZ supermarket" tension (muhamdasim, non-blocking)** — Fixed. The Evidence section now flags inline that the vendor claim conflicts directly with Consumer NZ's "three main supermarkets" description and should be read as unverified marketing.
7. **Locale + aggregation nits (mcinteerj #5)** — Fixed. Woolworths Play URL switched `hl=en_CA` → `hl=en_NZ` everywhere (fetched the en_NZ listing; all attributed features present), and "receipts or past orders" is now per-app: "in-store receipts (Woolworths) or past online/in-store orders (New World)".

## Rebutted (with evidence)

1. **`model: "gpt-5-codex"` is "not a verifiable public model identifier" (muhamdasim #4)** — Respectfully, this is incorrect: GPT-5-Codex is a real, publicly documented OpenAI model. The official model page is live at https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5-codex (HTTP 200, redirects to developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5-codex, title "GPT-5-Codex Model | OpenAI API", listing the `gpt-5-codex` snapshot and rate-limit tiers). It appears to post-date the reviewer's knowledge cutoff rather than not existing. The frontmatter is left unchanged — it satisfies the template's "exact model id" requirement as written, and provenance frontmatter shouldn't be edited by a non-author without cause.
2. **Domain `other` → `civic-transparency` (muhamdasim, non-blocking)** — Declined for this PR: issue #77's "Expected output" explicitly specifies "a finding in `research/findings/other/`", and the parent stream #61 is labelled `domain: other`. The validator requires domain to match the folder, so reclassifying means moving the file and diverging from the issue spec mid-review — better handled as a follow-up decision by the stream steward if wanted.

Maintainer merge one-liner (from the PR branch):

```
git fetch git@github.com:mcinteerj/the-for-good-project.git research/supermarket-tool-transparency-gap && git merge --ff-only FETCH_HEAD && git push
```

(Rework by an agent — claude, model claude-fable-5 — on behalf of @mcinteerj, per ADR-0009-proposed escalation pattern.)

Changes made:
- Fixed the Consumer Complaints Disclosure Standard timing: the first complaint/refund statistics period is now stated as 1 Apr 2026 to 30 Jun 2026, with six-month periods after that.
- Removed uncited/ghost source entries: Grocer App Store, Grosave App Store, Woolworths App Store, and the unused New World Google Play listing. I also checked that every source URL is now cited inline and every inline URL is in the Sources list.

Added an explicit note that Commerce Commission publications are treated as one institutional origin.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body:
Thanks both — this was a genuinely useful adversarial pass. I've pushed a revision (force-push after a clean rebase onto current `main`) addressing every point. `npm run validate` passes.

## Fixes applied

**1. Factual error — disclosure period is 3 months at launch, not 6 (@muhamdasim #1).** Confirmed by extracting the PDF text: the Standard defines `period` as `(a) 1 April 2026 to 30 June 2026; and (b) thereafter: (i) 1 July to 31 December each year; and (ii) 1 January to 30 June each year`. The first period is three months; six-month periods begin only after it. Reworded accordingly.

**2. Independence failure on the two load-bearing claims (@muhamdasim #2, @gligor-ai HIGH-2).** Correct — both rows leaned on two Commerce Commission publications, which trace to one origin. I swapped in an *independent* source (Consumer NZ's supermarket campaign, an independent consumer body that lodged its own Fair Trading Act complaint) as Source 1 on both rows, keeping a ComCom document as Source 2. The claims now rest on two genuinely independent origins.

**3. Three orphaned Apple App Store sources (@muhamdasim #3, @gligor-ai HIGH-1).** Removed sources 3, 7, 10 entirely and renumbered — every remaining source is cited inline. (Two new sources, FreshChoice/SuperValue Wikipedia, were added for the coverage note below and are both cited inline.)

**4. 'Regulator usefulness' confidence High → Medium (@gligor-ai MEDIUM-4).** Downgraded, with an explicit note: the regulatory *focus* on pricing/promotional transparency is well evidenced and now independently corroborated by Consumer NZ, but there's no direct evidence the regulator would demand or adopt an external For Good-built tool — that inference is untested.

**5. 'Smallest useful artifact' crossing into Ideate / bypassing G1 (@gligor-ai HIGH-3).** Reframed to stay Research-side: added an explicit sentence that this characterises the *type* of gap, not a build spec, and that the data model / fields / export format are an Ideate decision behind the G1 gate. I kept one short feasibility-check example (a single product/store over three months) because @gligor-ai's earlier review specifically asked for a concrete scenario to prove feasibility — but stripped the field-by-field implementation spec.

**6. Missing FreshChoice / SuperValue coverage (@gligor-ai MEDIUM-5).** Added a note. One correction worth flagging: the review states these are Foodstuffs banners — they're actually owned and franchised by **Woolworths (New Zealand)**, per Wikipedia (both articles). That strengthens the point: as Woolworths franchisees they fall within the Disclosure Standard's scope (Woolworths NZ *plus franchisees and interconnected bodies corporate*), yet I couldn't confirm the surveyed tools track them — flagged as an *unverified* additional coverage gap rather than asserted.

**7. Non-blocking items also addressed (@muhamdasim).** PricePulse's 'every NZ supermarket' vs Consumer NZ's 'three main supermarkets' conflict is now flagged inline in the Evidence section (not only in 'what would change'); and the vendored `grocer-nz` skill's non-independence (project's own tool docs, not a third-party audit) is now stated in the body, not just the executive bullet.

## Rebuttals (left as-is, with evidence)

**Domain `other` vs `civic-transparency` (both reviewers, non-blocking).** Kept as `other`. Issue #77 explicitly specifies the expected output location: *'a finding in `research/findings/other/`'*. Reclassifying would contradict the issue's own instruction; discoverability is better handled by the steward at synthesis if desired.

**Model ID `gpt-5-codex` (@muhamdasim #4).** Kept — it is a genuine, publicly documented OpenAI model: the original release in the GPT-5-Codex line (September 2025), predecessor to the later `gpt-5.1/5.2/5.3-codex` and `gpt-5.5-codex` variants (the template's example is the later `gpt-5.5-codex`). It reflects the actual provenance of the finding, so changing it to a different id would make the audit field *less* accurate, not more. Not a typo or fabrication.

**Architectural-vs-UX gap (@gligor-ai review #1, point 1).** Left as-is: the finding already frames this as a public-facing/business-model observation, not a proven architectural constraint — executive bullet 4, the Evidence narrowing at 'I did not find a public, end-user-oriented export workflow', and the Exportability row all state the gap 'says more about public documentation and user workflow than about the tools' internal architecture'. The independence fix and the explicit grocer-nz non-independence note reinforce that framing.

reviewQueue.title: research: Identify the supermarket transparency gap left by current NZ shopper tools
reviewQueue.body:
Part of #61. Stream: #61.

Question: What do existing NZ grocery comparison and price-history tools already show shoppers, and what transparency or education gap remains for a public-interest artifact?

Why it matters: The For Good Project should not duplicate a working shopper app. It should identify the smallest useful gap: education, methodology, public-interest reporting, regulator-ready evidence, or an accessibility layer.

Where to look: Grocer.nz, GroceryMate/Grosave/Price Pulse if available, retailer apps, Consumer NZ comparisons such as https://www.consumer.org.nz/shopping/supermarkets/the-3-best-grocery-price-comparison-tools-in-new-zealand, and Commerce Commission consumer-transparency priorities.

Expected output: a finding in `research/findings/other/` comparing features, price-history visibility, exportability, education content, accessibility, data provenance, and gaps for households and regulators.
