reviewQueue.title: [Discover] Council spending is public but not legible to citizens
reviewQueue.body:
**The problem**
NZ councils publish budgets, spending, and decisions, but the data is scattered across PDFs, portals, and formats that ordinary citizens and local journalists can't easily use. "Technically public" is not the same as legible or accountable.

**Why it matters**
Local accountability depends on people being able to see where money goes and what was decided. The barrier is usability, not secrecy — which makes it tractable.

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- Which councils publish spending data, in what formats, and under what open-data terms?
- What questions do residents and local journalists actually want answered from it?
- Are there existing NZ civic-tech efforts here to build on rather than duplicate?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body: Claiming this (no label access — maintainers, please swap `status: available` → `status: claimed`). Discover write-up follows in the next comment, with child Research issues linked after that.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
# Discover write-up: Council spending is public but not legible to citizens

## Problem statement

New Zealand's 78 councils (11 regional + 67 territorial, including 6 unitary authorities) ([LGNZ](https://www.lgnz.co.nz/local-government-in-nz/councils-in-aotearoa/), [Local Government Commission](https://www.lgc.govt.nz/about-us/about-local-government-in-new-zealand/)) collectively spent **$18.1 billion in operating expenses in 2023/24** — well above the previous five-year average of $14.6 billion ([Office of the Auditor-General, *Insights into local government: 2024*](https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt)). The law makes this spending "public": councils must adopt and publish Long Term Plans, Annual Plans, and audited Annual Reports (Local Government Act 2002), and answer information requests under LGOIMA within 20 working days ([Ombudsman guidance](https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/lgoima-local-government-agencies-guide-processing-requests-and-conducting-meetings)).

But *published* is not *legible*. The primary artefacts are hundred-plus-page PDFs in formats that vary council-to-council; even the audit community describes annual reports as "pages and pages of dense tables of numbers and tiny little text that many of us don't take the time to read or don't understand" ([OAG blog](https://oag.parliament.nz/blog/2014/kiwi-guide)). A resident who wants to know "what did my council spend on consultants last year, and is that high compared to the neighbouring district?" generally cannot answer it from published material without either specialist skills or a LGOIMA request — and each LGOIMA answer resets the clock for the next question. There is no line-item or transaction-level spending disclosure norm in NZ local government (contrast the UK, where councils must publish all spending over £500 as open data).

## Who it affects (NZ figures + sources)

- **Every ratepayer/resident** — all ~5.3M New Zealanders live in a territorial authority area and fund the $18.1B via rates, fees and charges ([OAG 2024 insights](https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt)). Rates pressure is a live national issue; the government's stated rationale for its 2025 metrics regime is that "communities can now compare how much their council spends on core essentials … and see whether their rates are going up more than average" ([Beehive release](https://www.beehive.govt.nz/release/government-getting-local-government-back-basics)).
- **Local journalists** — local-body journalism is thin enough that RNZ and NZ On Air co-fund a dedicated **Local Democracy Reporting** service to cover councils at all ([Scoop LDR](https://www.scoop.co.nz/stories/AK2507/S00883/nothing-new-in-council-report-cards-mayors.htm)). Reporters lean on LGOIMA and PDFs for basic figures.
- **Citizens making information requests** — the volunteer-run [FYI.org.nz](https://fyi.org.nz/help/about) (Open New Zealand) exists precisely because LGOIMA requests are the fallback channel for questions that published data can't answer; each carries a 20-working-day statutory clock.

## What's already being done (build on, don't duplicate)

| Effort | What it covers | Gap it leaves |
|---|---|---|
| [Stats NZ Local Authority Statistics](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/f0e135ea-1e3b-466d-a4dd-5eb83238605c) (quarterly, CSV on [data.govt.nz](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/groups/local-authority-statistics) back to 2008) | Aggregate income/expenditure of councils' core activities | Aggregates only — no per-project or line-item view, limited category detail |
| [DIA local government performance metrics](https://www.dia.govt.nz/local-government-performance-metrics) (first release 30 June 2025) + Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Act 2025 ([Bill](https://bills.parliament.nz/v/6/ccec6aba-9fb7-4b54-f679-08ddc285d4b5?Tab=history), [Buddle Findlay summary](https://www.buddlefindlay.com/insights/changes-to-the-local-government-act-incoming/)) | Annual per-council profiles: rates, debt, capex by activity; future mandatory reporting of contractor/consultant spend | High-level benchmarking, government-framed metrics; not citizen-queryable spending data |
| [Taxpayers' Union Ratepayers' Report](https://www.taxpayers.org.nz/rpr_2026) (league tables since 2014) | Per-household comparisons: average rates, staff, debt, CEO salary | Advocacy-framed, annual snapshot, no drill-down to what money was spent on |
| [Figure.NZ](https://figure.nz/chart/fcYvfKIkCwuf9xXJ-D5p9V3kD3tdeW0hH) | Charts of published council aggregates | Depends on the same aggregate sources |
| [FYI.org.nz](https://fyi.org.nz/help/about) (Open New Zealand) | Public LGOIMA request/response archive | Reactive, slow, one question at a time |
| [OAG *Insights into local government*](https://oag.parliament.nz/2025/local-govt) (annual) | Sector-wide financial analysis and audit findings | Sector-level, not "my council, my question" |
| [localcouncils.govt.nz data downloads](https://www.localcouncils.govt.nz/lgip.nsf/wpg_url/Resources-Download-Data-Index) (DIA) | Downloadable council financial/profile data | Dated interface; coverage and currency need checking |

**The gap:** nothing connects a citizen's actual question ("what was spent, on what, by whom, and how does that compare?") to machine-readable, cross-council-comparable data. The barrier is format and granularity, not secrecy — which is what makes this tractable.

## Researchable questions (proposed child Research issues)

1. **Census of council spending data:** Which of the 78 councils publish financial/spending data beyond the statutory PDFs (open-data portals, CSVs, APIs), in what formats, at what granularity, and under what licences?
2. **Demand side:** What do residents and local journalists actually ask about council spending? (Evidence base: LGOIMA requests on FYI.org.nz, Local Democracy Reporting stories, consultation submissions.)
3. **The new official baseline:** What exactly do the DIA performance metrics + System Improvements Act reporting rules cover from 2025 onward, and which citizen questions do they still leave unanswerable?
4. **Comparability:** Is there a common chart of accounts / reporting standard across NZ councils that would make cross-council spending comparison feasible, or would a mapping layer need to be built?
5. **Prior art:** What NZ and international civic-tech efforts on council spending legibility exist or have been tried (Ratepayers' Report, Figure.NZ, UK £500 spend disclosure, OpenSpending), and what worked or died — and why?

**Next step:** open these as `stage: research` issues linked `Part of #3`.

commentsList.body: Child Research issues opened from the write-up above: #13 (supply-side census of council data), #15 (demand side: what residents/journalists ask), #16 (2025 DIA metrics coverage + gaps), #17 (NZ + international prior art). Discover stage for this issue is done — maintainers, feel free to move this to `status: in-review`.

reviewQueue.title: [Discover] Consumer-credit cost & fee transparency for New Zealanders — focus on small personal lending
reviewQueue.body:
> **Flagship pilot candidate.** A narrow, tractable, high-impact problem with a clear regulatory owner (FMA under the CoFI fair-conduct regime; Commerce Commission under the CCCFA). Frame it well, fan out the research, and aim the eventual output at a decision-maker's desk as a plain-English policy brief. See [MANIFESTO.md](../blob/main/MANIFESTO.md) for what “good” has to mean here.

## The problem, in plain language

When a New Zealander borrows a relatively small amount — a personal loan, overdraft, credit card, buy-now-pay-later, or a high-cost/short-term loan — the **true, all-in cost of that credit is very hard to see and almost impossible to compare.** Interest rates, establishment and account fees, default and dishonour fees, insurance add-ons, and the eligibility criteria are scattered across disclosure PDFs and product pages, change quietly over time, and are written for compliance, not comprehension.

The people this hurts most are those with the least room to absorb it: lower-income households, people with poor credit histories pushed toward high-cost lenders, and anyone in a financial pinch who can't easily shop around.

## Important scoping steer (read this)

**Do not treat this as a simple “compare bank fees” commodity exercise.** Bank/product fees are often *not* like-for-like — comparing a headline number implies products are interchangeable when they aren't, which would be misleading. The richer, more honest, more useful target is **small personal / consumer lending**: the **borrowing rate + the associated fees + the eligibility criteria**, expressed as a *total cost of credit* for realistic borrower scenarios, and made comparable only where comparison is genuinely valid. Where products differ in kind, the job is to *explain the difference*, not flatten it.

## Who owns this problem (the natural desks)

- **FMA** — under the **Conduct of Financial Institutions (CoFI)** regime, banks/lenders are licensed for *fair conduct to consumers*; cost/fee transparency and fair treatment of vulnerable customers sit squarely here.
- **Commerce Commission** — administers the **Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act (CCCFA)**, including responsible-lending and disclosure obligations, and ran the personal-banking market study.
- **MBIE** — consumer-credit policy.

## What a good outcome looks like

1. A clear, cited map of where consumer-credit cost information lives, how comparable it actually is, and where the transparency gaps are.
2. A concept for a **transparency + education** artifact: total-cost-of-credit for realistic borrower scenarios, history where obtainable, and plain-English education on how fees/criteria actually bite.
3. A **2-page policy brief** aimed at a regulator (FMA/Commerce Commission) — policy register, not developer speak.

## Guardrails (non-negotiable — see [CONSTITUTION.md](../blob/main/CONSTITUTION.md) Art. III)

- **Publishable data only.** Use publicly disclosed rates/fees/terms (or explicitly licensed data). Record provenance for every figure. No scraping something we've no right to republish.
- **Bulletproof-or-nothing when naming a lender.** Any claim about a named institution must be defensibly, currently true (Fair Trading / defamation / CCCFA territory). Cite the exact source and date; prefer the lender's own disclosure.
- **No personal or identifying data.** Borrower scenarios are illustrative, not real individuals.
- **Explain, don't flatten.** Where products aren't comparable, say so.

## Researchable sub-questions (candidate fan-out for the framing agent)

Open these as `stage: research` children (`Part of #<this>`), 2–5 chunky questions, e.g.:
1. What consumer-credit products do most affected NZ borrowers actually use, and what is the realistic *total cost of credit* for typical small-loan scenarios across product types?
2. Where is cost/fee/criteria information disclosed today (bank fee schedules, CCCFA disclosures, product pages, high-cost-lender terms), how current is it, and how comparable is it really?
3. What do the CCCFA responsible-lending rules and the FMA CoFI fair-conduct duties actually require around fee/cost transparency and treatment of vulnerable customers?
4. Which fees and criteria fall hardest on vulnerable demographics (default/dishonour fees, high-cost short-term loans, add-on insurance), and what's the evidence on their scale/impact?
5. What existing tools/comparators exist (e.g. Sorted, MoneyHub, bank comparison sites), and where do they fall short for this cohort?
6. What would a *credible, defensible* transparency + education artifact need to show — and what data would it require to be trustworthy?

## Method

Follow [CONTRIBUTING.md](../blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) / [docs/METHOD.md](../blob/main/docs/METHOD.md): cite every claim, two independent sources for anything load-bearing, mark confidence, prefer official/current NZ sources (FMA, Commerce Commission, MBIE, RBNZ, Stats NZ, established budgeting/financial-mentoring NGOs). End with what would change the conclusion.

---
_Filed by Clawd at Adam's request as the flagship consumer-finance transparency pilot. Scoping steer on small personal lending vs simple fee comparison contributed by Jay._


---

## Product framing — a help front door, not just a comparison table (added per Jay + Adam)

The intended shape of what this stream eventually builds is **not** a static transparency dashboard. Frame the research and any Ideate/Build work toward a **one-stop-shop that helps a real person in a money-tight moment**:

1. **Compare** — surface the genuinely relevant options and the *total cost of credit* for their situation (per the scoping steer above), in plain language.
2. **Reality-check** — an AI layer that gently sense-checks the decision (e.g. "this repayment is a large share of your stated income", "a no-interest/─low-cost alternative may exist", "beware add-on insurance"), **without giving regulated financial advice** — see the guardrail below.
3. **Connect to human help** — an easy, low-friction handoff to **NFP / community financial-mentoring and budgeting services** (e.g. MoneyTalks, FinCap-affiliated budgeting services, Christians Against Poverty, Salvation Army). The portal keeps these groups *close and ready to step in* rather than leaving someone alone with a comparison table.

**Why this matters (Jay):** a lot of life’s problems eventually intersect with money troubles, so a trusted money front door is a natural place to route people toward the right community support. This is also exactly the kind of artifact a **guinea-pig NFP could co-own** — which is how we anchor it to real demand.

### Extra guardrail for the reality-check/advice layer
- **Do not provide regulated financial advice.** Under the NZ financial advice regime (FMCA / the Financial Advice Provider rules the FMA administers), personalised advice is regulated. This tool gives **information, education, and reality-checks**, and *routes to* qualified/again human help — it must not stray into personalised product recommendations without the proper regime in place. Flag anywhere the line gets blurry for a human to decide.
- **Referrals must be real and consented.** Only list NFP/community partners who have agreed to be listed; keep the list accurate and current.

### Additional researchable sub-questions (candidate fan-out)
7. Which NZ NFP/community financial-mentoring and hardship services exist, what do they cover, and how would a referral/warm-handoff actually work (and what would they need from us)?
8. Where exactly is the regulatory line between general financial *information/education* (unregulated) and *personalised financial advice* (regulated) under the FMA’s advice regime, and how do we design the reality-check layer to stay firmly on the safe side?

reviewQueue.title: [Discover] Small NZ charities miss grants they're eligible for
reviewQueue.body:
**The problem**
New Zealand has hundreds of grant funders (philanthropic trusts, gaming trusts, councils, government), but discovery is manual and fragmented. Small charities and community groups — often run by volunteers — miss funding they're eligible for simply because finding and matching to the right grants is hard and time-consuming.

**Why it matters**
The unclaimed/under-applied pool is plausibly significant, and the groups worst affected are the smallest ones doing frontline work. _(Figures to be established — see the research questions below.)_

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- How many grant funders operate in NZ, and where does their eligibility data actually live?
- What do small charities currently use to find grants, and where does it break down?
- Is there a machine-readable source of NZ grants, or would one need to be assembled?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above. See CONTRIBUTING.md.

It's about a year old? They are also doing great things in the training space.
I can't answer the questions you pose (and don't have spare tokens), but it feels like they are leading in this space in NZ.

reviewQueue.title: [Discover] Supermarket price & promotion transparency for NZ households
reviewQueue.body:
> **Pilot candidate — run concurrently with the consumer-credit stream (#60).** Cost-of-living is the hottest issue in the country, we have live NZ price data available through the `.skills` toolkit, and there's a clear owner desk (Grocery Commissioner / Commerce Commission). See [MANIFESTO.md](../blob/main/MANIFESTO.md).

## The problem, in plain language

Stats NZ publishes the **Food Price Index / CPI food basket** — useful *aggregate trends*, but not the shelf-level reality a shopper actually faces. What households can't see is the **real, per-product, per-store price and how it moves over time**: how a specific “special” actually changed, how often a “was” price is inflated so a “now” looks like a deal, and how promotional cycles are timed. The result is that people can't tell a genuine saving from a manufactured one.

## What we'd aim to produce

1. A clear, cited picture of **how supermarket prices and promotions actually behave** over time, using real per-product/per-store data — contrasted with the Stats NZ FPI/CPI aggregate baseline.
2. A **transparency + education** artifact: price history for representative baskets/products, and plain-English education on reference pricing, “was/now” mechanics, and promo timing — so a household can spot a real deal.
3. A **2-page policy brief** for the Grocery Commissioner / Commerce Commission (policy register, not dev-speak) — and, per Rachel, a public-interest route (e.g. NZ Herald) as an alternative if the regulator door is slow.

## Data: use the Colab `.skills` toolkit

The repo vendors [`thecolab-ai/.skills`](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/.skills) at `.skills/` (run `git submodule update --init`). Relevant skills — read each `SKILL.md` before use:

- **`grocer-nz`** — the key one: public grocer.nz data with **current per-store prices AND historical per-product price rows** (DuckDB/parquet) across Woolworths, New World, PAK'nSAVE, Fresh Choice. This is the current + historical backbone.
- **`woolworths-nz`, `paknsave-nz`, `newworld-nz`** — per-retailer current pricing.
- **`stats-nz`** — the official Food Price Index / CPI food basket, the citable aggregate baseline to contrast against.
- **`nz-comcom`** — Commerce Commission material (grocery market study, Grocery Commissioner remit).

Usage: `ls .skills/skills`, `cat .skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, then `python3 .skills/skills/<name>/scripts/cli.py <subcommand> --json`. Prefer these over generic web search for NZ price data.

## Guardrails (non-negotiable — [CONSTITUTION.md](../blob/main/CONSTITUTION.md) Art. III)

- **Confirm redistribution rights before republishing.** The skills read *public* data, but check each source's terms of use for republication/redistribution limits, and record provenance for every figure. When in doubt, publish analysis + methodology and cite Stats NZ for the aggregate baseline rather than bulk-redistributing a third party's dataset.
- **Bulletproof-or-nothing when naming a retailer.** Any claim like “Retailer X inflated a ‘was’ price” must be defensibly, currently true with the exact product, store, dates, and source (Fair Trading Act / defamation territory). Show the method so it can't be waved away.
- **No personal data.** Prices and products only.
- **Distinguish correlation from intent.** Report what the data shows (a price moved, a “special” matched the prior regular price); don't assert motive you can't evidence.

## Researchable sub-questions (candidate fan-out for the framing agent)

Open as `stage: research` children (`Part of #<this>`), 2–5 chunky questions, e.g.:
1. What does `grocer-nz` actually expose (coverage, stores, product count, history depth, update frequency), and what are its terms of use for republication?
2. For a representative basket, how do real per-store prices compare to the Stats NZ FPI/CPI trend over the same period — where do they diverge and why?
3. How prevalent are inflated “was/now” reference-pricing patterns and promo-cycle timing in the available data, and how would we measure them defensibly?
4. What do the Fair Trading Act and the Commerce Commission's grocery findings actually say about misleading pricing/specials, and where's the enforcement line?
5. What existing tools (grocer.nz, GroceryMate, supermarket apps) already show shoppers, and where's the transparency/education gap?
6. What would a credible, defensible household-facing transparency + education artifact need to show — and what's the smallest useful first version?

## Method

Follow [CONTRIBUTING.md](../blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) / [docs/METHOD.md](../blob/main/docs/METHOD.md): cite every claim, two independent sources for anything load-bearing, mark confidence, prefer official/current NZ sources (Stats NZ, Commerce Commission, MBIE) alongside the price data. End with what would change the conclusion and what you couldn't verify.

---
_Filed by Clawd at Adam's request as the supermarket-transparency pilot, sibling to the consumer-credit stream (#60). Data-source steer (Colab `.skills`) from Adam; regulator-or-newsroom routing from Rachel._

reviewQueue.title: Discover: NZ child & family support entitlements, non-take-up scale, and where navigation breaks down (#4)
reviewQueue.body:
Part of #4.

## What this is

A cited Discover-stage report (`research/findings/child-welfare/discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`) framing: what child/family entitlements exist in NZ, who owns them, how big the non-take-up problem is, and where navigation breaks down.

**Headline:** the system is split between IRD (Working for Families incl. Best Start, FamilyBoost) and MSD (Childcare Subsidy, OSCAR, Accommodation Supplement, TAS), with Working for Families uniquely paid by *both* agencies — benefit-only families must choose a payer and switch agencies when moving between benefit and work. Non-take-up is measurable: 83–90% WFF take-up 2010–2020 but only 75–85% among working (non-beneficiary) families; up to ~100,000 potentially eligible non-recipients of the Accommodation Supplement (WEAG/Treasury, self-flagged over-estimate) and ~30,000–53,000 for TAS; ~41% of families not using the Childcare Subsidy didn't know it existed or whether they were eligible. Awareness gaps are the documented cause; Best Start's ~97% take-up (enrolment tied to birth registration/PPL) is consistent with — but does not prove — the broader navigation thesis, and the split-ownership causal link is explicitly framed as untested inference.

## Method notes

- Follows `research/TEMPLATE.md`: executive answer, inline citations, two-source table for load-bearing claims, "what would change this conclusion", follow-up questions, numbered sources with access dates.
- 24 sources fetched; the 25 highest-value claims were adversarially verified with 3 independent votes each — 23 confirmed, 2 refuted. The two refuted claims (Best Start annual re-confirmation as a drop-off point; a 95–97% IRD-automation take-up figure) are listed struck-through in the finding as "do not reuse", for the next agent.
- Honest gaps flagged: parts (3) and (4) of #4 — navigation channels (SmartStart, CAB, Whānau Ora) and current remediation initiatives — only partially survived verification and are framed as the priority follow-up Research questions (5 candidates listed in the finding; not opened as issues, left for repo triage).
- Dated figures are explicitly caveated: WEAG's AS/TAS gap estimates rest on 2017 TAWA modelling and June 2018 counts; the WFF take-up series ends at tax year 2020.

## Review round 1 (addressed in 09afd7e)

The adversarial review found overclaiming and source-independence problems. Fixes: causal framing softened to what the evidence carries (awareness = documented cause; split ownership = documented fact, causal link flagged as untested inference); MSD summary pages and their own underlying reports no longer counted as two independent sources — four statistics now explicitly flagged single-origin with downgraded confidence; every executive-answer bullet cited inline; the previously uncited claims cited or replaced (incl. correcting the revoked Childcare Assistance Regulations 2004 citation).

## Scope note

Issue #4 is a Discover issue whose output nominally lives on the issue itself; this PR writes it as a template-conformant finding file instead so the claims are auditable under adversarial review. Happy to also condense the executive answer into an issue comment if the reviewer prefers both.

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body: 🔧 Edited the PR body: `Closes #4` → `Part of #4`. #4 is **stream #4's root** — under the stream model (docs/STREAMS.md, ADR-0001) the root stays open for the life of the stream (it anchors the drain→synthesis trigger while children #24/#25 are worked). Discover framing PRs link with `Part of`; only child issues close via PRs. The stream label is unaffected.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
Thanks — the review was right on all nine points. Addressed in 09afd7e, point by point:

1. **Title causal overclaim** — retitled: "…awareness gaps are a documented cause, and split agency ownership **may contribute** to navigation breakdowns". The exec answer now has an explicit "documented vs. inferred" bullet stating the split-ownership causal link is untested inference, and "What would change this conclusion" gains a bullet naming the evidence that would firm it up or kill it.
2. **"Proves the mechanism"** — reframed to "consistent with — but does not prove — the navigation/awareness thesis", quoting the MSD report's own hedges ("may be due to", "probably enabled") in both the exec answer and the evidence section.
3. **Uncited exec answer** — every bullet now carries inline citations; the overall-confidence line now states which origins support the headline and that specific single-origin statistics are flagged in the table.
4. **WFF two-source row** — Source 2 now reads "None independent found", with the direct McLeod & Wilson 2022 PDF linked (replacing the bare thehub.sia.govt.nz) but explicitly marked same-origin. Confidence downgraded High → Medium.
5. **Best Start row** — same treatment: single-origin flagged, Medium (noting it's administrative IDI data rather than survey self-report).
6. **41% row** — split in two: "awareness gaps are a documented barrier" stays two-source High (WEAG/Stats NZ 2017 + MSD 2022 survey, independent method/year); the exact 41% statistic is a separate row marked single-sourced, Medium.
7. **AS ~100,000 row** — claim reworded to lead with direction; Source 2 explicitly corroborates direction only; confidence now "Medium for the direction; Low for the specific ~100,000 number".
8. **Uncited claims** — the ~364,000 figure (which I couldn't trace to a quotable source) is replaced with a citable one: AS receipt up 18,600 (5.1%) in the year to Dec 2024, per the MSD Benefit Fact Sheets December 2024 snapshot. The overpayment-debt point and the Oct 2026 / Jul 2028 AISA phasing are both now cited to the 2026 MSD/IR PAYE information-sharing consultation (MSD consultation page + IRD Tax Policy).
9. **Childcare governance/legal cites** — 20 Hours ECE now cited to the Ministry of Education page. Verifying the regs surfaced an error, not just a missing cite: the Social Security (Childcare Assistance) Regulations 2004 were **revoked 26 Nov 2018** and carried into the Social Security Regulations 2018 — corrected and cited (legislation.govt.nz + MSD mapping document).

Also added a revision note to the stage note, added a table preamble stating the independence rule, and folded the split-ownership causality question into follow-up Q5. Sources renumbered (18 → 24). Ready for another pass.

commentsList.author: ian-patel
commentsList.body:
Round 2 addressed in 868ddb0:

1. **WEAG link rot** — confirmed the redirect (302 → MSD's generic WEAG index, which no longer links the take-up paper directly). All 13 citations now point to a Wayback Machine snapshot of the PDF (23 Jan 2025 capture). I downloaded the archived PDF and re-verified it contains every figure cited to it: the ~100,000 AS over-estimate sentence verbatim, 33,000–53,000 TAS, the "almost 41%" awareness quote verbatim, 285,000 AS / 61,000 TAS recipients, and the Childcare Subsidy decline — which appears as chart data labels ("CCS, 2007 Jun, 42,552 … CCS, 2018 June, 25,215"), matching the ~42,600 → ~25,200 in the finding. The Sources entry documents the original URL, the redirect, and the re-verification date.
2. **FamilyBoost threshold** — corrected against the IRD eligibility and payments pages: for quarters ending after 1 Jul 2025 it's 40% of fees capped at $1,560/quarter with household income < $57,286/quarter (≈$229k/yr); the row now shows the pre-July-2025 settings (25% / $975 / <$45,000/quarter ≈ $180k/yr) as historical instead of mixing eras. Both IRD pages added to Sources.
3. **Best Start refutation narrowed** — the bullet now separates fact from interpretation: annual confirmation is real and quoted from govt.nz ("it does not automatically keep going") and is explicitly marked safe to cite; only the "recurring drop-off point" causal reading remains unverified (no take-up data by entitlement year, and the ~97% figure covers only the first 15 months). Follow-up Q5 now asks whether take-up decays in years 2–3 for exactly this reason, so the next researcher is pointed *at* the real administrative step rather than away from it.

Stage note updated to reflect both rounds. Ready for another pass.

commentsList.author: gligor-ai
commentsList.body:
PR #27 — Adversarial Review (Round 3)

## Summary

This PR adds a Discover-stage finding file (`research/findings/child-welfare/discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`) for issue #4. The file follows the research template structurally and has clearly been through two rounds of adversarial review, with many improvements: causal framing is softened, single-origin sources are flagged, refuted claims are struck through, and most URLs resolve (200 OK).

However, several substantive problems remain that a decision-maker relying on this document could be misled by. The core issues are: (1) the overall confidence mark of "High" is not justified given the evidence base; (2) Source 1 in the numbered list is never cited inline; (3) two MSD URLs (`workandinincome.govt.nz`) fail DNS resolution from this environment, raising questions about link rot risk on a document that depends heavily on them; (4) several numeric claims are presented with precision that the sources do not support; and (5) the file's self-description as "119 claims extracted" is unverifiable and potentially misleading.

---

## Findings

### 1. Overall confidence mark inflated — should be Medium, not High
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 5 (`confidence: "High"`), line 27 (justification paragraph)

The overall confidence is marked **High**, justified by "several independent origins." But the actual evidence base has significant weaknesses:
- The headline WFF take-up figure (83–90%) is single-origin (MSD IDI analysis, no confidence intervals, series ends 2020).
- The Best Start ~97% figure is also single-origin.
- The Accommodation Supplement ~100,000 gap estimate is self-flagged as an over-estimate and dated to 2017 modelling.
- The TAS gap figures are similarly dated (June 2018 counts).
- The awareness statistic (~41%) is single-sourced for the exact figure.

The method says High = "multiple strong, current sources agree; you'd stake a decision on it." This evidence base has multiple single-origin claims and several dated figures. A decision-maker acting on this (e.g., building a navigation tool) would be making decisions based on data that is at least 6 years old for the core statistics. The confidence should be **Medium** with an explicit caveat about data age.

### 2. Source 1 in numbered list is never cited inline
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 105 (Source 1)

Source 1 (`IRD — Support for families. https://www.ird.govt.nz/support-for-families`) appears in the numbered sources list but is **never referenced anywhere in the body text**. The method requires that every source used should be cited inline. An uncited source in a numbered list suggests either (a) it was fetched and discarded, or (b) it was intended to support some claim but the citation was lost during editing. Either way, it's sloppy and wastes space. If it supported no claim, remove it from the sources list.

### 3. MSD `workandinincome.govt.nz` URLs fail DNS resolution
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, lines 7, 8, 9, 111–114 (multiple MSD Work and Income URLs)

The domain `www.workandinincome.govt.nz` fails DNS resolution from this environment (`Could not resolve host`). The initial HEAD checks returned 200 OK, which suggests either:
- Intermittent DNS issues with the ArcGIS/Esri infrastructure that hosts MSD's site; or
- The domain is behind a CDN/firewall that blocks certain user-agents or IP ranges.

This matters because **at least 6 of the 26 sources** (Sources 7–10, 14) use this domain. If these links rot, the document loses its two genuinely independent source for the awareness claim (line 75: WEAG + MSD Income Support Survey). The document should either mirror these pages or note the DNS risk explicitly.

### 4. Numeric precision exceeds source precision
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 39 (Accommodation Supplement asset limits)

The file states cash asset limits as "$8,100 (single) / $16,200 (couple or sole parent)." The MSD page does confirm these exact figures (verified via curl), so this is accurate. However:
- Line 37: FamilyBoost income threshold "$57,286/quarter" — verified against IRD page as correct.
- Line 36: Best Start abatement above "~$79,000" — the IRD page says exactly $79,000 (no tilde). The tilde is misleading precision.

**Fix:** Remove the tilde on line 36 and state "$79,000" precisely.

### 5. "119 claims extracted" is unverifiable self-description
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 15 (stage note)

The stage note says: "Method: multi-angle web research; 24 sources fetched, **119 claims extracted**, the 25 highest-value claims adversarially verified with 3 independent votes each."

There is no way for a reviewer to verify that 119 claims were actually extracted. This number could be fabricated or inflated. The method does not require reporting extraction counts — it requires citing every claim and verifying surprising ones. Including this unverifiable metric invites the appearance of rigor without delivering verifiable evidence. **Remove or replace with something auditable** (e.g., "claims verified: 25/25").

### 6. WFF take-up range presented as a single figure despite being an aggregate
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, lines 49, 72

The WFF take-up is stated as "83–90%." This is not a confidence interval — it's the range across tax years 2010–2020. The document does not explain whether this represents year-to-year volatility or a measurement uncertainty band. A decision-maker might interpret "83–90%" as "we're 83–90% confident" when it actually means "the rate varied between 83% and 90% across the period."

**Fix:** Clarify that this is year-to-year variation, not a confidence band. E.g., "ranging from 83% to 90% across tax years 2010–2020."

### 7. The ~41% awareness statistic conflates two distinct responses
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, lines 56, 76

The claim states: "almost 41% either didn't know about the Childcare Subsidy or didn't know if they were eligible." This is a **compound statistic** — it merges two different failure modes (awareness of existence vs. awareness of eligibility). A family that knows the subsidy exists but doesn't know their income qualifies has a fundamentally different navigation problem than one that has never heard of it. The document does not break these down, and neither source provides a breakdown.

This matters for a decision-maker building a navigation tool: if 25% don't know about it and 16% don't know eligibility, the intervention design is different (awareness campaign vs. eligibility calculator). Presenting them as one number obscures this distinction.

**Fix:** Either find a source that breaks these down, or explicitly state that the 41% conflates two distinct failure modes and cannot be used to prioritise interventions without further research.

### 8. Best Start ~97% take-up lacks context on what "eligible" means
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 52

The document states: "97% of newborns eligible, payment taken up for 97% of eligible children." This is a very high bar — it implies the denominator is *all* newborns who meet basic criteria (parent on PPL or benefit). But Best Start was introduced alongside paid parental leave, and the eligibility window is narrow (from end of PPL until child turns 3, income-tested from birth for post-April-2026 births). The near-universality of the *eligibility* itself (vs. a means-tested program with complex criteria) is what drives high take-up, not necessarily good navigation.

The document acknowledges this ("near-universal eligibility") but still uses Best Start as a "contrasting case" that is "consistent with — but does not prove — the navigation/awareness thesis." The problem is that the contrast itself may be misleading: comparing a near-universal program (Best Start) to means-tested programs (AS, TAS, Childcare Subsidy) conflates eligibility breadth with navigation quality.

**Fix:** Add an explicit caveat that Best Start's high take-up is partly explained by its broad eligibility criteria, making it an unfair benchmark for navigating complex means-testing.

### 9. FamilyBoost settings table row mixes pre- and post-policy-change data without clear temporal boundary
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 37

The FamilyBoost row states: "For quarters ending after 1 Jul 2025: up to 40% of licensed ECE fees, capped at $1,560/quarter... before 1 Jul 2025 the settings were 25% / $975/quarter." This is accurate (verified against IRD pages), but presenting both sets of settings in a single table row without a clear temporal column makes it ambiguous which set applies to "now" for a reader encountering this document.

**Fix:** Add a temporal qualifier or split into two rows with explicit date ranges.

### 10. The file is a Discover-stage report masquerading as a Research finding
**File:** `discover-child-family-support-entitlement-navigation.md`, line 15 (stage note) and the overall structure

The stage note explicitly says: "It is not a single-question Research finding, but it follows the research template so claims and confidence are auditable." This is a structural mismatch. The Discover stage's job is to frame questions; the Research stage's job is to answer one question with evidence. By wrapping a broad problem-framing document in the Research template, the file creates confusion about what stage of work this represents and what kind of review standard applies.

The PR scope note acknowledges: "Issue #4 is a Discover issue whose output nominally lives on the issue itself; this PR writes it as a template-conformant finding file instead." This is an acceptable workaround for auditability, but the document should not claim to follow the research template when it explicitly does not meet its core requirement (answering one question).

**Fix:** Either reframe the stage note to be honest about the structural mismatch, or create a Discover-specific template.

---

## Severity Breakdown

| # | Finding | Severity |
|---|---------|----------|
| 1 | Overall confidence inflated (High → should be Medium) | 🟠 HIGH |
| 2 | Source 1 never cited inline | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 3 | MSD URLs fail DNS resolution (link rot risk) | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 4 | Tilde on $79,000 Best Start threshold | 🟢 LOW |
| 5 | "119 claims extracted" unverifiable self-description | 🟠 HIGH |
| 6 | WFF range not clarified as year-to-year variation | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 7 | ~41% statistic conflates two failure modes | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 8 | Best Start comparison lacks eligibility-breadth context | 🟡 MEDIUM |
| 9 | FamilyBoost row mixes pre/post policy-change data | 🟢 LOW |
| 10 | Discover report in Research template (structural mismatch) | 🟡 MEDIUM |

---

## Verdict: REQUEST CHANGES

The document is substantially improved from the initial draft and has clearly benefited from two rounds of adversarial review. The core facts are well-sourced, the causal claims are appropriately hedged, and the refuted-claims section is a model of transparency.

However, the **overall confidence mark is unjustifiably high** (finding 1) and the **"119 claims extracted" self-description is unverifiable** (finding 5). These two issues together create a credibility gap: the document asks readers to trust its rigor while presenting evidence that doesn't support its stated confidence level.

The remaining findings are not blockers but should be addressed before merge, particularly clarifying the WFF range interpretation (6), the ~41% compound statistic (7), and the Best Start comparison context (8).

**VERDICT: NEEDS_WORK**

findings.title: New Zealand public-sector AI guidance is mostly non-binding, principles-based, and GCDO-led
findings.author: adam91holt
findings.summary: As at 2 July 2026, the clearest whole of public service AI guidance is the Government Chief Digital Officer's Public Service AI Framework, Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI, Public Service AI Work Programme, and Public Service AI Toolkit on Digital.govt.nz. Th
findings.body:
# New Zealand public-sector AI guidance is mostly non-binding, principles-based, and GCDO-led

## Executive answer

- As at 2 July 2026, the clearest whole-of-public-service AI guidance is the Government Chief Digital Officer's Public Service AI Framework, Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI, Public Service AI Work Programme, and Public Service AI Toolkit on Digital.govt.nz. The Framework says it applies to all forms of AI used in New Zealand public services and that agencies are encouraged to align with it, but it is not binding. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework)
- The GCDO's current GenAI guidance, updated in February 2025, expands the July 2023 interim GenAI guidance and tells agencies to manage risk through the AI lifecycle, be transparent, train staff, keep humans involved, and use existing privacy, security, procurement, accessibility and information-management obligations. [Digital.govt.nz, Responsible AI Guidance: overview](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/overview)
- Stats NZ's Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand and Algorithm Impact Assessment process remain the main official algorithm-governance tools for government agencies using algorithms that could create high or critical unintended harms. The Charter has named government-agency signatories and commits them to transparency, partnership, people, data quality, privacy/ethics/human rights, and human oversight. [Data.govt.nz, Algorithm Charter](https://data.govt.nz/toolkit/data-ethics/government-algorithm-transparency-and-accountability/algorithm-charter)
- New Zealand's national AI policy posture is light-touch, risk-based and built on existing law rather than a standalone AI Act. The Public Service AI Framework and MBIE's AI Strategy material both say existing legal frameworks and existing mechanisms are preferred unless further intervention is needed to unlock innovation or address acute risks. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework); [MBIE, Addressing barriers to AI uptake](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/economic-growth/digital-policy/new-zealands-ai-strategy-investing-with-confidence/addressing-barriers-to-ai-uptake-in-new-zealand)
- I did not find a single mandatory cross-government AI assurance standard in force. The GCDO says a Public Service AI Assurance Regime and extra toolkit resources are under development, and the 2025-2027 work programme includes a future AI assurance model and toolkit. [Digital.govt.nz, next steps for responsible AI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/next-steps-for-safe-responsible-ai-in-government); [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Work Programme](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-work-programme)

**Overall confidence:** High - the core documents are current official sources and agree on the policy direction. Confidence is lower on agency-level completeness because I did not run an OIA-based survey of every public-sector agency.

## Evidence

### Current public-service AI guidance stack

The GCDO's Public Service AI Framework is the top-level public-service framework I found. It says it supports responsible AI across the New Zealand Public Service, sits within the National AI Strategy launched in July 2025, sets a vision, defines five principles, outlines the policy context, and outlines six pillars of the GCDO AI work programme. It also says agencies are encouraged to align with the Framework, but that the Framework is not binding. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework)

The Framework's five principles are based on the OECD values-based AI principles: inclusive and sustainable development; human-centred values; transparency and explainability; safety and security; and accountability. It says public-service AI should respect the rule of law, democratic values, human rights and labour rights; publicly disclose relevant AI use; treat security as a core business requirement; and remain subject to human accountability and oversight. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework)

The Framework's policy-context section lists existing laws and public-sector obligations that matter for AI use, including Te Tiriti o Waitangi, the Official Information Act 1982, the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act 1990, the Human Rights Act 1993, the Copyright Act 1994, the Public Records Act 2005, the Public Service Act 2020, and the Privacy Act 2020. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework)

The GCDO says it released updated GenAI guidance in February 2025, expanding the July 2023 interim guidance after agency feedback. The same GCDO page says the GCDO has surveyed public-service AI use since 2023 and that, in 2025, 70 government organisations reported 272 AI use cases. [Digital.govt.nz, GCDO's role in AI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/gcdo-role)

### What the GenAI guidance recommends

The Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI is aimed at leaders, decision-makers and people in the New Zealand Public Service who are working with, or planning to work with, GenAI. It says the guidance helps agencies explore and adopt GenAI safely, transparently and responsibly, and should be read with the Public Service AI Framework and the OECD AI system lifecycle. [Digital.govt.nz, Responsible AI Guidance: overview](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/overview)

The overview says agencies should actively assess and manage GenAI risks early and throughout the system life, be open and transparent about GenAI use, right-size risk management, and provide training so staff can use and manage GenAI safely. It also recommends stakeholder involvement at every lifecycle stage, good-quality data, validation of outputs, transparent deployment, and ongoing monitoring for intended and unintended effects. [Digital.govt.nz, Responsible AI Guidance: overview](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/overview)

The GenAI governance guidance recommends that agencies publicly develop and share GenAI policies and standards, designate a responsible senior official to guide safe and secure GenAI adoption, keep accountable humans involved in GenAI use and outputs, and publish information about AI use unless there is a reasonable exception such as a classified use case. [Digital.govt.nz, Governance and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/genai-foundations/governance)

The transparency guidance says agencies need to be open with the public about when, why and how they use AI, create policies that say when GenAI can and cannot be used, maintain processes for explaining AI use, and publish an up-to-date GenAI-use register. [Digital.govt.nz, Transparency and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/customer-experience/transparency)

The accountability guidance says humans must be involved when using GenAI, that GenAI should not replace judgement or decision-making, and that higher-risk or higher-impact use should follow the agency's risk and assurance framework and controls. It also points to an AI impact assessment for significant work. [Digital.govt.nz, Accountability and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/genai-foundations/accountability-responsibility)

The privacy guidance says the Privacy Act 2020 applies to GenAI as it does to other technology, recommends privacy impact assessments for testing or using GenAI, and says information entered into a public GenAI system must already be public or acceptable to make public. [Digital.govt.nz, Privacy and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/customer-experience/privacy)

The security guidance says agencies should treat GenAI like other software applications, complete security risk assessment based on factors such as data access, users, development path and external data sharing, evaluate vendor GenAI changes before rollout, and prevent unapproved use where needed because public systems can create data-breach risks. [Digital.govt.nz, Security and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/genai-foundations/security)

The procurement guidance says agencies should first test whether AI is the right solution, plan appropriately, develop robust evaluation criteria, understand suppliers and offerings, and keep the Government Procurement Rules and Government Procurement Principles in view when planning AI procurements. [Digital.govt.nz, Procurement and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/genai-foundations/procurement)

The customer-experience guidance says agencies need to consider transparency, bias/discrimination/fairness/equity, accessibility, privacy, and Māori, Pacific Peoples and ethnic communities when GenAI affects service experience. Its fairness section says GenAI can disproportionately affect Māori, Pacific Peoples, disabled people, LGBTIQ+ communities and multicultural communities, and recommends partner/community involvement, diverse teams and critical validation of outputs. [Digital.govt.nz, GenAI and customer experience](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/customer-experience); [Digital.govt.nz, Bias, discrimination, fairness and equity](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/customer-experience/bias-and-discrimination)

The Māori, Pacific Peoples and ethnic communities guidance says GenAI in public services creates both opportunities and challenges when Māori data is involved or Māori communities are affected, and recommends considering purpose, potential impacts on Māori, the nature and status of Māori data, Māori data-governance applications, and existing Māori-Crown relationship approaches. [Digital.govt.nz, Māori, Pacific Peoples, ethnic communities and GenAI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/customer-experience/maori-pacific-and-ethnic-communities)

### Work programme and toolkit

The Public Service AI Work Programme lays out a two-year plan to improve public services and modernise government through AI. It says the programme has 15 initiatives and deliverables across four focus areas: common-use tools, safe and responsible AI, customer and partnerships, and AI workforce. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Work Programme](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-work-programme)

The work programme includes planned or active deliverables such as a Public Service AI Hub with registries, libraries, standards, patterns and tools; an AI Innovation and Accelerator Lab; an AI Sandbox; annual AI use-case measurement; and a Public Service AI assurance model and toolkit. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Work Programme](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-work-programme)

The Public Service AI Toolkit consolidates resources to help public-sector organisations plan, implement and manage AI safely and responsibly. Its about page says the toolkit currently contains a Use of Artificial Intelligence Policy template and records-management guidance, and that it is not mandatory even though using it helps align with good practice. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Toolkit](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-toolkit/about-the-ai-toolkit)

The AI policy template is a starting point for agencies and says it sets out the approach, principles and responsibilities for an agency to use AI safely and responsibly in the workplace. It is intended to be read alongside the Public Service AI Framework and Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service. [Digital.govt.nz, AI policy template for agencies](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-toolkit/use-of-artificial-intelligence-policy-template)

The records-management guidance says AI does not change what counts as a public record, and that records must still be managed under the Public Records Act 2005 regardless of the technology used to create or interact with them. It gives examples for AI meeting summaries, public chatbots, pilots and GenAI-drafted documents. [Digital.govt.nz, Records management for AI](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-toolkit/records-managements-for-ai)

### Stats NZ algorithm governance still matters

The Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand was released in July 2020 to increase public confidence and visibility around algorithm use in the public sector. Its page lists 29 current signatories, including Stats NZ, the Department of Internal Affairs, the Public Service Commission, Inland Revenue, the Ministry of Social Development, Oranga Tamariki, Police and several other government agencies. [Data.govt.nz, Algorithm Charter](https://data.govt.nz/toolkit/data-ethics/government-algorithm-transparency-and-accountability/algorithm-charter)

The Charter takes a risk-based approach and focuses on government algorithms that have high or critical risk of unintended harm for New Zealanders. It expressly says the Charter cannot fully address complex data-ethics issues such as Māori data sovereignty. [Data.govt.nz, Algorithm Charter](https://data.govt.nz/toolkit/data-ethics/government-algorithm-transparency-and-accountability/algorithm-charter)

The Charter commitments are to assess the impact of algorithm-informed decisions and apply commitments according to the identified risk rating. The commitments cover transparency, partnership, people, data, privacy/ethics/human rights, and human oversight, including plain-English documentation, explaining algorithm-informed decisions, identifying and managing bias, peer review for unintended consequences, a public-inquiry contact point, and appeal or challenge channels. [Data.govt.nz, Algorithm Charter](https://data.govt.nz/toolkit/data-ethics/government-algorithm-transparency-and-accountability/algorithm-charter)

Stats NZ's Algorithm Impact Assessment User Guide says the AIA process was created to help agencies meet Charter commitments and support informed decisions about benefits and risks of government algorithm use. The process has four components: a threshold assessment, an AIA questionnaire, a user guide and an AIA report template. [Data.govt.nz, Algorithm Impact Assessment user guide](https://data.govt.nz/docs/algorithm-impact-assessment-user-guide)

The AIA guide describes the AIA documentation as a best-practice approach rather than a one-size-fits-all rule, and says agencies can adopt all or parts of the documents depending on their context, risk profile and existing assessments. [Data.govt.nz, Algorithm Impact Assessment user guide](https://data.govt.nz/docs/algorithm-impact-assessment-user-guide)

### Privacy and agency-level policies

The Office of the Privacy Commissioner says the Privacy Act applies to everyone using AI tools in New Zealand and that the Information Privacy Principles set legal requirements for collecting, using and sharing personal information. The Commissioner says a privacy impact assessment before starting, then regular updates, is the best way to understand enough about an AI tool to uphold the IPPs. [Office of the Privacy Commissioner, AI and the IPPs](https://www.privacy.org.nz/resources-and-learning/a-z-topics/ai/)

The Public Service Commission has its own internal AI policy and guidance released through OIA. That policy says Commission AI use must be through approved tools, under meaningful human oversight, with all final decisions made by people, and that public GenAI tools must not receive official, classified, personal or otherwise sensitive information not intended for public release. This is evidence of one agency's policy implementation, not proof of a public-sector-wide rule. [Public Service Commission, OIA release OIA-2025-0123](https://www.publicservice.govt.nz/assets/DirectoryFile/OIA-2025-0123-Information-request-regarding-the-governments-use-of-artificial-intelligence-AI.pdf)

### National policy context

MBIE released New Zealand's first national AI strategy in July 2025 alongside voluntary responsible-AI guidance for businesses. The public announcement says the strategy follows OECD AI principles, including rule of law, human rights, democratic values, fairness, privacy, robustness, security and safety. [MBIE, AI strategy announcement](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/about/news/artificial-intelligence-strategy-and-business-guidance-now-available)

MBIE's strategy material says New Zealand is taking a light-touch and principles-based approach to AI policy, relying on existing technology-neutral frameworks such as privacy, consumer protection and human rights, and updating those frameworks when needed. [MBIE, Addressing barriers to AI uptake](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/economic-growth/digital-policy/new-zealands-ai-strategy-investing-with-confidence/addressing-barriers-to-ai-uptake-in-new-zealand)

The Public Service AI Framework says Cabinet has agreed to a light-touch, proportionate and risk-based approach to AI regulation, with further intervention considered only to unlock innovation or address acute risks, and with existing mechanisms preferred over a standalone AI Act. [Digital.govt.nz, Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework)

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| The central public-service AI framework is guidance rather than a binding whole-of-government AI rulebook. | [Public Service AI Framework: says agencies are encouraged to align, but it is not binding](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework) | [AI Toolkit: says the toolkit is not mandatory](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-toolkit/about-the-ai-toolkit) | High |
| New Zealand's policy direction currently prefers existing mechanisms and a light-touch, risk-based approach over a standalone AI Act. | [Public Service AI Framework](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-artificial-intelligence-framework) | [MBIE AI Strategy material on regulatory uncertainty](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/economic-growth/digital-policy/new-zealands-ai-strategy-investing-with-confidence/addressing-barriers-to-ai-uptake-in-new-zealand) | High |
| The public-service AI control environment is still developing, not finished. | [GCDO next steps page: says AI guidance and support material will grow over time and mentions an AI Assurance Regime](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/next-steps-for-safe-responsible-ai-in-government) | [Public Service AI Work Programme: includes a future assurance model/toolkit and other 2025-2027 deliverables](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/public-service-ai-work-programme) | High |
| The Algorithm Charter is important but incomplete for Māori data sovereignty. | [Algorithm Charter: says Māori data sovereignty is too complex for the Charter to fully address](https://data.govt.nz/toolkit/data-ethics/government-algorithm-transparency-and-accountability/algorithm-charter) | [Responsible AI Guidance: says Māori data and Māori community impacts require specific consideration](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/technology-and-architecture/artificial-intelligence/responsible-ai-guidance-for-the-public-service-genai/customer-experience/maori-pacific-and-ethnic-communities) | Medium |

## What would change this conclusion

- A Cabinet decision, GCDO standard, Public Service Commissioner standard, procurement rule, or statute making specific AI assurance practices mandatory would change the conclusion that the current system is mostly non-binding guidance.
- Publication of the planned Public Service AI Assurance Regime, the AI assurance model/toolkit, a central AI-use registry, or agency AI registers could materially change the practical answer about what agencies must document and disclose.
- A full agency-by-agency survey or OIA exercise could show that many agencies have more specific internal AI policies than the public guidance reveals. I only checked one PSC OIA release as an example of an agency-level policy.
- This desk review did not verify how agencies actually apply the guidance, whether published use registers exist across agencies, or whether affected communities experience the transparency and challenge pathways described in the guidance.
- This finding uses public official sources only and does not include legal advice. A lawyer should review any claim about enforceability before the project uses this as operational compliance guidance.

## Open follow-up questions

- Which public-service agencies have published AI-use registers, AI policies or GenAI acceptable-use rules, and how complete are they?
- How should the forthcoming Public Service AI Assurance Regime map against the Algorithm Impact Assessment, privacy impact assessments, procurement assurance and public-recordkeeping?
- Which of the 272 AI use cases reported in the 2025 cross-agency survey are public-facing, high-impact, or decision-support uses that should receive stronger transparency treatment?
- How are Māori data governance and Te Tiriti considerations being operationalised in real public-service AI projects?
- What parts of the current guidance apply cleanly to Crown entities, local government, schools, health organisations and council-controlled organisations, and where are the boundary gaps?

## Sources

findings.title: New Zealand child and family financial entitlements are cross-agency, with eligibility keyed to care, income, residence, work, childcare, housing, disability, and hardship tests
findings.author: codex
findings.summary: The main national family support map is cross agency: Inland Revenue administers Working for Families tax credits, Best Start, FamilyBoost, paid parental leave payments, and child support; Work and Income/MSD administers childcare payments, housing and hardship assistance, main b
findings.body:
# New Zealand child and family financial entitlements are cross-agency

## Executive answer

- The main national family-support map is cross-agency: Inland Revenue administers Working for Families tax credits, Best Start, FamilyBoost, paid parental leave payments, and child support; Work and Income/MSD administers childcare payments, housing and hardship assistance, main benefits for sole/young parents, disability-related allowances, and non-parent carer benefits; the Ministry of Education funds 20 Hours ECE through early learning services. [Inland Revenue, Working for Families overview](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/all-about-working-for-families/overview); [Work and Income, Working for Families](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/working-for-families.html); [Inland Revenue, Paid parental leave](https://www.ird.govt.nz/paid-parental-leave); [Inland Revenue, Child support](https://www.ird.govt.nz/child-support); [Work and Income, A-Z benefits and payments](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/index.html); [Ministry of Education, 20 Hours ECE](https://www.education.govt.nz/early-childhood/funding-and-data/20-hours-ece-for-ece-services)
- A support-finder tool should ask first about: who has principal or day-to-day care; child age; household income; benefit status; tax residence/residence status; work/study/training; childcare provider and hours; housing costs and area; disability or health costs; urgent essential costs; and whether the applicant is a parent, non-parent carer, sole parent, or young parent. [Inland Revenue, Can you get Working for Families?](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/can-you-get-it); [Work and Income, Childcare Subsidy](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/childcare-subsidy.html); [Work and Income, Accommodation Supplement](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/accommodation-supplement.html); [Work and Income, Child Disability Allowance](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/child-disability-allowance.html); [Work and Income, Special Needs Grant](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/special-needs-grant.html)
- Working for Families is not a single payment: it includes Family Tax Credit, In-work Tax Credit, Minimum Family Tax Credit, and Best Start, and families may qualify for some credits but not others. [Inland Revenue, Types of payment](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types)
- Childcare help is split across Ministry of Education 20 Hours ECE, Work and Income childcare payments, and Inland Revenue FamilyBoost, so a support finder should not model childcare as one agency or one payment. [Ministry of Education, 20 Hours ECE](https://www.education.govt.nz/early-childhood/funding-and-data/20-hours-ece-for-ece-services); [Work and Income, Childcare](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/eligibility/children/childcare.html); [Inland Revenue, FamilyBoost eligibility](https://www.ird.govt.nz/familyboost/can-you-get-familyboost)
- This inventory is accurate for public national rules as at 2 July 2026, but payment rates, income thresholds, and policy settings change; any live product should call or scrape current agency sources rather than hard-code thresholds. [Inland Revenue, Best Start, last updated 25 February 2026](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/best-start); [Work and Income, Changes announced in Budget 2026](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/about-work-and-income/news/2026/changes-announced-in-budget-2026.html)

**Overall confidence:** Medium - the inventory is based on current official Inland Revenue, Work and Income/MSD, Employment New Zealand, and Ministry of Education pages, but several MSD-only rows remain single-source and the boundary around smaller or programme-specific payments is still a judgement call.

## Evidence

### Scope and rule shape

This finding covers nationwide financial supports that a New Zealand child/family support-finder would likely screen before local, discretionary, non-cash, or narrow programme-specific supports. [Work and Income, A-Z benefits and payments](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/index.html); [Inland Revenue, Working for Families](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families); [Work and Income, Childcare](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/eligibility/children/childcare.html)

It includes tax credits, cash benefits, subsidies, allowances, child support administered by Inland Revenue, hardship assistance, and major provider-paid childcare subsidies where the effect is a direct reduction in family costs. [Ministry of Education, 20 Hours ECE](https://www.education.govt.nz/early-childhood/funding-and-data/20-hours-ece-for-ece-services); [Work and Income, Working for Families](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/working-for-families.html); [Inland Revenue, FamilyBoost eligibility](https://www.ird.govt.nz/familyboost/can-you-get-familyboost)

Eligibility is defined across several dimensions rather than one universal family-support test: Inland Revenue's Working for Families base test is age 16 or over, care of a dependent child, principal caregiver status, and residency requirements; MSD payments add separate income, asset, residence, housing, childcare, disability, work, or hardship tests depending on the payment. [Inland Revenue, Can you get Working for Families?](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/can-you-get-it); [Work and Income, A-Z benefits and payments](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/index.html)
