### Eligibility logic a support finder should model first

A first-pass support finder should treat "who has care of the child" as a gateway variable because Working for Families uses "principal caregiver" and dependent-child tests, child support uses care percentages for formula assessments, and non-parent carer benefits turn on day-to-day care and whether the carer is a parent. [Inland Revenue, Principal caregiver](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/can-you-get-it/principal-caregiver); [Inland Revenue, Can you get child support?](https://www.ird.govt.nz/child-support/can-you-get-child-support); [Work and Income, Unsupported Child's Benefit](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/unsupported-childs-benefit.html)

The child's age is another gateway variable because Best Start applies during the first three years, FamilyBoost applies to children aged 5 and under, 20 Hours ECE applies to children aged 3 to 5, Childcare Subsidy is generally for pre-school children with specific cohort-entry and Child Disability Allowance exceptions, OSCAR is generally for school children under 14 or under 18 with Child Disability Allowance, Child Disability Allowance applies under 18, and Sole Parent Support ends when the youngest child turns 14. [Inland Revenue, Best Start](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/best-start); [Inland Revenue, FamilyBoost eligibility](https://www.ird.govt.nz/familyboost/can-you-get-familyboost); [Ministry of Education, 20 Hours ECE](https://www.education.govt.nz/early-childhood/funding-and-data/20-hours-ece-for-ece-services); [Work and Income, Childcare Subsidy](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/childcare-subsidy.html); [Work and Income, OSCAR Subsidy](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/oscar-subsidy.html); [Work and Income, Child Disability Allowance](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/child-disability-allowance.html); [Work and Income, Sole Parent Support](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/sole-parent-support.html)

Income needs to be represented with care because different schemes use different income concepts: Working for Families uses family income for the tax year, FamilyBoost uses quarterly household income, Disability Allowance uses weekly income limits, Recoverable Assistance uses annual income limits, and MSD extra help often assesses income and assets together. [Inland Revenue, Family tax credit](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/family-tax-credit); [Inland Revenue, FamilyBoost eligibility](https://www.ird.govt.nz/familyboost/can-you-get-familyboost); [Work and Income, Disability Allowance](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/disability-allowance.html); [Work and Income, Recoverable Assistance Payment](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/recoverable-assistance-payment-grant.html); [Work and Income, Temporary Additional Support](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/temporary-additional-support.html)

Benefit status changes which agency pays some support and which credits are available: MSD says benefit clients can get Family Tax Credit paid with other Work and Income payments while non-benefit clients are paid by Inland Revenue, and Inland Revenue says main-benefit families may qualify for Best Start and Family Tax Credit but not In-work Tax Credit or Minimum Family Tax Credit. [Work and Income, Working for Families](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/working-for-families.html); [Inland Revenue, Working for Families and MSD](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/all-about-working-for-families/msd)

Residency or tax-residence questions cannot be collapsed into one field because Working for Families uses its own residency requirements, FamilyBoost requires New Zealand tax residence, MSD benefits commonly require citizenship/permanent residence/residence-class status plus normal residence or ordinary residence, and paid parental leave is tied to primary-carer and work-history tests rather than the MSD residence formulations. [Inland Revenue, Working for Families residency requirements](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/can-you-get-it/residency-requirements); [Inland Revenue, FamilyBoost eligibility](https://www.ird.govt.nz/familyboost/can-you-get-familyboost); [Work and Income, Sole Parent Support](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/sole-parent-support.html); [Inland Revenue, Who can apply for paid parental leave](https://www.ird.govt.nz/paid-parental-leave/qualifying/who-can-apply)

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working for Families crosses the IRD/MSD boundary: Inland Revenue administers the tax-credit system, but MSD can pay Family Tax Credit and Best Start alongside benefits in some cases. | [Work and Income, Working for Families](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/working-for-families.html) | [Inland Revenue, Working for Families and MSD](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/all-about-working-for-families/msd) | High |
| Childcare support is not just Work and Income plus FamilyBoost; 20 Hours ECE is a Ministry of Education-funded childcare-cost entitlement that a support finder should screen alongside MSD childcare payments and Inland Revenue FamilyBoost. | [Ministry of Education, 20 Hours ECE](https://www.education.govt.nz/early-childhood/funding-and-data/20-hours-ece-for-ece-services) | [Work and Income, Childcare](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/eligibility/children/childcare.html); [Inland Revenue, FamilyBoost eligibility](https://www.ird.govt.nz/familyboost/can-you-get-familyboost) | High |
| Best Start first-year abatement depends on the child's birth date: children born on or after 1 April 2026 are income-tested in year 1, while children born before that date keep unabated year-1 Best Start. | [Inland Revenue, Best Start](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/best-start) | [Inland Revenue Tax Policy, Taxation (Budget Measures) Act 2025 commentary](https://www.taxpolicy.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/tp/publications/2025/ac-taxation-budget-measures-act-2025.pdf?modified=20251216030429) | High |
| Best Start and paid parental leave cannot be received at the same time. | [Inland Revenue, Best Start](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/best-start) | [Employment New Zealand, Parental leave payments](https://www.employment.govt.nz/pay-and-hours/pay-and-wages/leave-and-holiday-pay/parental-leave-payments) | High |
| Non-parent carers receiving Unsupported Child's Benefit cannot also receive Family Tax Credit or Minimum Family Tax Credit for the same child, but may qualify for In-work Tax Credit if working; Work and Income states the same Working for Families exclusion for Orphan's Benefit, while the Inland Revenue table names Unsupported Child Benefit but not Orphan's Benefit. | [Inland Revenue, Government payments that affect your Working for Families](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/government-payments) | [Work and Income, Unsupported Child's Benefit](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/unsupported-childs-benefit.html); [Work and Income, Orphan's Benefit](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/orphans-benefit.html) | Medium |
| Hardship support is not a single benefit: the relevant one-off payment can differ depending on whether the family is already on a benefit, with Special Needs Grant or Advance Payment of Benefit for beneficiaries and Special Needs Grant or Recoverable Assistance Payment for non-beneficiaries. This is supported by Work and Income pages, but I did not find an independent non-MSD source for the routing rule. | [Work and Income, Urgent or unexpected costs](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/eligibility/urgent-costs/index.html) | [Work and Income, Advance Payment of Benefit](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/advance-payment-of-benefit.html); [Work and Income, Recoverable Assistance Payment](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/recoverable-assistance-payment-grant.html) | Medium |

## What would change this conclusion

- A formal cross-agency product catalogue from Inland Revenue, MSD, Employment New Zealand, Oranga Tamariki, and the Ministry of Education could change the "main support" boundary used here, especially for smaller grants and non-cash supports. [Work and Income, A-Z benefits and payments](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/index.html); [Inland Revenue, Working for Families](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families); [Ministry of Education, 20 Hours ECE](https://www.education.govt.nz/early-childhood/funding-and-data/20-hours-ece-for-ece-services)
- Legislative or policy changes after 2 July 2026 could change thresholds, rates, qualifying ages, abatement rules, or agency ownership; Work and Income has already announced 1 April 2027 Temporary Additional Support changes, and Inland Revenue pages show 2026 Working for Families threshold/rate changes. [Work and Income, Changes announced in Budget 2026](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/about-work-and-income/news/2026/changes-announced-in-budget-2026.html); [Inland Revenue, Best Start](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/types/best-start)
- I could not verify a single official machine-readable eligibility ruleset covering Inland Revenue, MSD, and Ministry of Education supports; a production support-finder would need agency confirmation before presenting eligibility as more than a guided pre-check. [Work and Income, Check what you might get](https://check.msd.govt.nz/); [Inland Revenue, Working for Families eligibility tool](https://www.ird.govt.nz/working-for-families/can-you-get-it/eligibility-tool); [Work and Income, Childcare](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/eligibility/children/childcare.html)
- This finding uses public sources only. I did not verify where families misunderstand eligibility, abandon applications, or experience cultural/language barriers, so those should be treated as research questions for families, advocates, and frontline workers rather than as findings from this inventory.
- I did not include local council grants, NGO hardship funds, school-level support, Oranga Tamariki caregiver allowances, Home Help, International Custody Dispute Payment, or education-sector operational funding beyond 20 Hours ECE because those areas need separate scoped findings before they can be treated as part of the main entitlement inventory. [Work and Income, A-Z benefits and payments](https://www.workandincome.govt.nz/products/a-z-benefits/index.html); [Oranga Tamariki, Caregiver allowance](https://www.orangatamariki.govt.nz/caregiving/financial-help/caregiver-allowance/)

## Open follow-up questions

- Which of these supports have the highest non-take-up or application-abandonment risk for eligible families in New Zealand, and what official or peer-reviewed evidence quantifies that risk?
- Which eligibility variables are safe to automate in a public pre-check tool, and which should be framed as "ask the agency" because they involve discretion, evidence, or case-manager judgement?
- How do families currently move between MSD and Inland Revenue when their work, benefit, care, or relationship status changes, and where do overpayments or missed payments most often arise?
- What information should be gathered from family advocates and lived-experience participants before turning this inventory into a service design?

## Sources

findings.title: Most eligible NZ families get child/family entitlements, but a persistent ~10–25% take-up gap sits with families without a main benefit
findings.author: muhamdasim
findings.summary: Working for Families (WFF) take up is high overall but not complete. MSD estimates overall take up was 87% of eligible families in the 2020 tax year , and has been "relatively stable over time, ranging between 83 and 90 percent overall" since 2007. [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https:/
findings.body:
# Most eligible NZ families get child/family entitlements, but a persistent ~10–25% take-up gap sits with families without a main benefit

## Executive answer

- **Working for Families (WFF) take-up is high overall but not complete.** MSD estimates overall take-up was **87% of eligible families in the 2020 tax year**, and has been "relatively stable over time, ranging between 83 and 90 percent overall" since 2007. [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)
- **The WFF gap is concentrated among families without a main benefit, not beneficiaries.** Take-up is "close to 100 percent for eligible families with main benefit income" (because the payment is administered alongside the benefit — the study's own explanation) but "**between 75 and 85 percent** for eligible families without main benefit income" — i.e. up to a quarter of that group miss out. (This is the study's measured category. It overlaps heavily with working families but is not identical — it can include non-beneficiary families not in paid work — so this finding uses "without a main benefit", not "working families", as the operative group.) [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)
- **Best Start take-up is very high (~97%) — and shows the same benefit-status split.** A separate MSD administrative study estimates the Best Start tax credit was taken up for **96.9% of children in eligible families**, but with the same gradient as WFF: **99% for children in main-benefit families, 97% for paid-parental-leave families, and 94% for other eligible families**. So even for a near-universal payment, non-beneficiary families are the ones most likely to miss out — a child-specific corroboration of the pattern. [MSD via SIA Hub, Best Start take-up, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-take-up-of-the-best-start-tax-credit)
- **A broader benefits survey shows the same split for a third entitlement.** In MSD's Income Support Survey (working-age people 18–64 on low-to-middle incomes — *not* only families with children), among those who appeared potentially eligible for the Accommodation Supplement only **43.9% self-reported receiving** it, and those **not** on a main benefit were statistically significantly *less* likely to report receiving it than beneficiaries. Of those not receiving it, **32.3% were unaware** of the payment; of the aware non-recipients, **35.9% believed they were ineligible** (most because they thought they earned too much). Covers a wider population than families with children, so it is a supporting analogue. [MSD Income Support Survey, Findings Pack 7](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf)
- **System design *may* move the number (weak, single-source).** One MSD study suggests the 2018 Best Start tax credit (paid via the same WFF channel) lifted take-up of the main WFF credits for families with very young children by ~5 percentage points — but the source hedges this ("it appears that…") and it is *not* independently corroborated, so treat it as a lead, not established causality. [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)
- **Bottom line for the stream:** a real, measurable share of *eligible* families do not receive support they qualify for — so non-take-up (not just ineligibility) is part of the harm. This evidence does **not** on its own isolate the dominant cause, and does not rule out benefit adequacy, eligibility design, administrative churn, distrust, or application burden as larger factors. Whether the right remedy is a navigation/awareness tool or something else is a hypothesis to test next (see follow-ups), not a conclusion this finding can carry.

**Overall confidence:** Medium — two independent MSD administrative studies (WFF and Best Start) show the same benefit-status split for child/family entitlements, and a third (the Accommodation Supplement survey) shows it for a broader population. The two most recent WFF figures stop at the 2020 tax year. The Accommodation Supplement figure (43.9%) is *self-reported* receipt among people who *appeared* potentially eligible — not an administrative take-up rate — with an uncertain direction of bias (potential-eligibility misclassification could push apparent take-up down; self-report error could go either way), so read it as directional, not exact.

## Evidence

### Working for Families: 87% overall, but 75–85% for families without a main benefit

MSD's analysis of WFF eligibility and take-up over the 2007–2020 tax years, published on the Social Investment Agency's research Hub, estimates that **87% of eligible families took up the payments in the 2020 tax year**, and that the overall rate has been "relatively stable over time, ranging between 83 and 90 percent overall". [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)

The headline number hides the important split. Take-up is "**close to 100 percent for eligible families with main benefit income**" — for these families the family tax credit is administered alongside the benefit, so there is effectively nothing extra to claim. For "**eligible families without main benefit income**" — who must instead claim through Inland Revenue rather than having it administered automatically — take-up "ranged between **75 and 85 percent**". [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)

The same study notes "families with one or more parent of Asian ethnicity had low estimated take-up in recent years", flagging possible drivers (data-linkage error, claiming barriers, income not captured in administrative data) that it says warrant further investigation — i.e. MSD does not treat the ethnic gap as fully explained. [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)

### Best Start: ~97% take-up, but the same benefit-status gradient

A separate MSD administrative study (also on the SIA Hub, Dec 2022) estimates that **96.5% of children born July 2018–September 2019 had a family eligible for Best Start**, and that Best Start "was taken up for an estimated **96.9 percent of the children in eligible families**". Take-up is very high but not uniform, and the gradient matches WFF: **99% for children in eligible families supported by a main benefit, 97% for those on paid parental leave (no benefit), and 94% for other eligible families**. So the families most likely to miss out are, again, those *not* connected to the benefit system. [MSD via SIA Hub, Best Start take-up, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-take-up-of-the-best-start-tax-credit)

The same WFF study also offers a hedged, single-source observation that Best Start lifted take-up of the *other* WFF credits: it says "it appears that" the 2018 introduction of Best Start "increased take-up of the main Working for Families tax credits by families with very young children by around **five percentage points**", the suggested mechanism being that Best Start prompts a WFF registration around a birth. This is a plausible design-effect lead but is **not independently corroborated** and the source itself hedges the causation, so it should not carry a solution-direction conclusion on its own. [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020)

### Accommodation Supplement: a take-up gap concentrated the same way

MSD's Income Support Survey (Findings Pack 7; 1,852 respondents, fieldwork June–December 2022) surveyed working-age people (18–64) and identified those "potentially eligible" for the Accommodation Supplement — **52.9% of respondents** met the combined criteria (in eligible housing, meeting residency, below cash and income limits, paying enough toward accommodation). [MSD Income Support Survey, Findings Pack 7](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf)

Among those potentially eligible, only **43.9% self-reported that they were receiving** the Accommodation Supplement (56.1% said no, didn't know, or preferred not to say). Crucially, self-reported receipt tracked benefit status the same way WFF take-up does: respondents **receiving a main benefit were statistically significantly *more* likely** to say they were receiving it, while those **not** on a main benefit were significantly *less* likely — the same beneficiary/non-beneficiary split. This 43.9% is self-report among people who *appeared* eligible (with wide margins of error and possible mis-identification of eligibility), so the *level* is uncertain and its direction of bias is not established — but the beneficiary/non-beneficiary *direction* of the split is statistically significant. [MSD Income Support Survey, Findings Pack 7](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf)

The survey also quantifies *why* eligible people miss out, and it is not awareness alone — though the figures use different denominators and should not be read as equal thirds. Of the potentially-eligible people **not receiving** the payment, **32.3% said they were not aware** of it (across all potentially eligible, 81.0% had heard of it and 18.2% had not). Among the **aware non-recipients**, **35.9% thought they were not eligible** despite appearing eligible — and of those, **60.7% (± 15.6) said it was because they believed they earned above the income threshold**, even though their measured income was below it. The remainder were aware and possibly eligible but had not applied. Separately, understanding that a non-beneficiary *can* receive the payment was high and consistent across sub-groups (67–91%), with no statistically significant difference between beneficiaries and non-beneficiaries. [MSD Income Support Survey, Findings Pack 7](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf)

This echoes the WFF and Best Start patterns from a different measurement angle: benefit recipients were significantly more likely to receive the payment than non-recipients [MSD Income Support Survey, Findings Pack 7](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf). The mechanism differs by entitlement, though: the WFF study describes near-automatic *administration* of the family tax credit for main-benefit families [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020), whereas the Accommodation Supplement survey only shows main-benefit recipients were more likely to *self-report receiving* it — not that AS enrolment is automatic [MSD Income Support Survey, Findings Pack 7](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf). For AS specifically the driver is a mix of awareness *and* mistaken self-assessed ineligibility, not awareness alone.

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| For child/family entitlements, take-up is materially lower for eligible families *without* a main benefit than for those with one | [MSD WFF take-up study: ~100% for benefit families vs 75–85% for non-benefit families](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020) | [MSD Best Start take-up study: 99% for children in main-benefit families vs 94% for other eligible families](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-take-up-of-the-best-start-tax-credit) — a *separate, child-specific* MSD administrative study. (Further supporting analogue in a broader population: [Income Support Survey, Accommodation Supplement, p.15](https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf).) | Medium–High |
| Overall WFF take-up is ~87% and stable at 83–90% | [MSD via SIA Hub, 2022](https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020) | Not independently corroborated — single MSD study. Flagged. | Medium |

The benefit-status split is corroborated by **two independent MSD administrative studies of child/family entitlements** (WFF and Best Start), which is why it carries Medium–High confidence; the Accommodation Supplement survey adds a third, broader-population signal but is not child-specific. The *specific 87%* WFF figure rests on one study and should be treated as a good official estimate, not a hard fact.

## What would change this conclusion

- **A more recent WFF take-up estimate.** The figures stop at the 2020 tax year. Unverified post-2020 developments (income volatility, any subsequent WFF settings changes, and changes to how IRD reconciles entitlements at year end) could have moved take-up up or down. A refreshed MSD/IRD estimate would supersede this.
- **An administrative take-up *rate* for the Accommodation Supplement.** The 43.9% figure is self-reported receipt among survey respondents who *appeared* eligible, with wide margins of error and an uncertain direction of bias — not an administrative estimate. An MSD/IRD administrative take-up rate would supersede this and could be higher or lower; if it shows near-complete take-up, the AS gap would matter less than the survey implies.
- **Evidence that the "gap" is largely measurement error.** MSD itself flags that low estimated take-up (e.g. for Asian families) may partly reflect data-linkage error or income not captured in admin data. If most of the apparent gap is definitional, the real behavioural shortfall is smaller.
- **This needs a human with frontline/lived experience.** The survey already gives a quantified first pass at the mechanism (of non-recipients, 32.3% unaware; of aware non-recipients, 35.9% believe they're ineligible; the rest aware-but-not-applying), but *why* eligible people wrongly self-assess as ineligible — income confusion, application complexity, or distrust — determines what tool would help. Someone from Citizens Advice Bureau, a budgeting service, or MSD frontline should sense-check this before anything is designed.

## Open follow-up questions

- What is the *dollar* value of unclaimed WFF/Best Start/Accommodation Supplement per year (aggregate, not personal)? (Good Research issue.)
- Is there a comparable take-up estimate for childcare subsidies, the Best Start second/third-year (income-tested) portion, or dental/health entitlements for children?
- What specifically causes the non-beneficiary-family drop-off — never applied, applied and lapsed, or reassessment churn? (Needs IRD process data and frontline input.)

## Sources

1. Ministry of Social Development (hosted on the Social Investment Agency Hub), *Estimates of Working for Families eligibility and take-up rates 2007–2020*, published 01 Dec 2022. https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-working-for-families-eligibility-and-take-up-rates-2007-2020 (accessed 2 July 2026)
2. Ministry of Social Development (hosted on the Social Investment Agency Hub), *Estimates of take-up of the Best Start tax credit*, published 01 Dec 2022. https://thehub.sia.govt.nz/sitemap/estimates-of-take-up-of-the-best-start-tax-credit (accessed 2 July 2026)
3. Ministry of Social Development, *Income Support Survey — Findings Pack 7: Accommodation Supplement self-reported take-up and other findings* (New Zealand Income Support Survey; 1,852 respondents, fieldwork June–December 2022). https://www.msd.govt.nz/documents/about-msd-and-our-work/work-programmes/income-support-survey/findings-pack-7-accommodation-supplement-self-reported-take-up-and-other-findings.pdf (accessed 2 July 2026)
4. Inland Revenue, *Working for Families overview* (background context on entitlement/administration; not cited for a specific figure). https://www.ird.govt.nz/en/working-for-families/all-about-working-for-families/overview (accessed 2 July 2026)

findings.title: Existing NZ council reporting standards support high-level comparisons, not transaction-level spending comparability
findings.author: codex
findings.summary: New Zealand councils must publish audited annual reports, financial statements, and group of activities information, but the Local Government Act 2002 sets reporting obligations and prescribed statements rather than a public common chart of accounts for council spending [Local Go
findings.body:
# Existing NZ council reporting standards support high-level comparisons, not transaction-level spending comparability

## Executive answer

- New Zealand councils must publish audited annual reports, financial statements, and group-of-activities information, but the Local Government Act 2002 sets reporting obligations and prescribed statements rather than a public common chart of accounts for council spending [Local Government Act 2002, Schedule 10](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html).
- Accounting compliance makes audited financial statements comparable at statement level, because councils report under New Zealand GAAP and the public-benefit-entity standards framework, but those standards do not create a shared transaction taxonomy for "consultants", "parks maintenance", "library programmes", or individual projects [XRB submission on local-government reporting](https://www.xrb.govt.nz/dmsdocument/3374/) [XRB Accounting Standards Framework](https://www.xrb.govt.nz/standards/accounting-standards/accounting-standards-framework/).
- Published aggregate sources do support some cross-council comparisons: Stats NZ's Local Authority Census/Local Authority Financial Statistics publishes income, expenditure, operating income and expenditure by activity, and financial position at total and individual-council level; DIA/MCERT's council profiles compare selected rates, debt, operating, and capital-expenditure metrics within council groups [Stats NZ DataInfo+](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/9a6701e3-f1d3-4480-b879-5eea7716979b/101) [MCERT council performance](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/).
- My inference is that a civic spending-legibility tool should plan for a mapping layer for council-specific accounts, line items, procurement categories, programmes, and projects, because official sources note variation in council organisational structures, accounting systems, definitions, accounting practices, and activity categorisation [Stats NZ local authority data collection](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42) [MCERT council performance](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/) [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf).

**Overall confidence:** Medium — the aggregate-reporting part is High confidence, but the detailed-spending/mapping-layer part is a Medium-confidence inference from official caveats, the 2019 Productivity Commission accounting report, and a bounded negative search for a public mandated chart of accounts or transaction taxonomy.

## Evidence

### Accounting and audit compliance creates statement-level consistency

Local authorities must prepare planning and reporting documents under the Local Government Act 2002, including long-term plans, annual plans, and annual reports, and Schedule 10 specifies information that must be included in those documents [Local Government Act 2002, Schedule 10](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html). Schedule 10 requires annual reports to identify activities within each group of activities, compare budgeted and actual capital expenditure for each group of activities, include an audited statement of service provision, and include an audited funding impact statement for each group of activities [Local Government Act 2002, Schedule 10 clauses 23-26](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html).

Section 111 of the Local Government Act requires information in Part 6 or Schedule 10 plans and reports to be prepared in accordance with generally accepted accounting practice where GAAP has developed standards, except for funding impact statements; XRB described the same section as requiring a two-arm reporting regime of GAAP information and other local-government legislative information [Local Government Act 2002, section 111](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM172320.html) [XRB submission on local-government reporting](https://www.xrb.govt.nz/dmsdocument/3374/). New Zealand's Accounting Standards Framework sets the suites and tiers of accounting standards that apply to entities, including public benefit entities, and the XRB describes the framework as a multi-sector, multi-tier approach for financial statements rather than as a council-operational spending classification [XRB Accounting Standards Framework](https://www.xrb.govt.nz/standards/accounting-standards/accounting-standards-framework/).

**Per-claim confidence: High.** These are statutory and official accounting/audit sources; they support comparability of formal financial statements, not a shared operational account code.

### The Local Government Act creates activity-group reporting, but leaves councils room to define most activity structure

Schedule 10 of the Local Government Act requires long-term plans and annual reports to report by "groups of activities" [Local Government Act 2002, Schedule 10 clauses 2 and 23](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html). The current Schedule 10 identifies some activities as groups of activities and also says a local authority may treat other activities as a group of activities, subject to water-services exceptions introduced by later legislation [Local Government Act 2002, Schedule 10 clause 2](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html).

The Department of Internal Affairs' mandatory non-financial performance measures were designed to improve comparability for five service areas: stormwater drainage, sewerage and sewage disposal, flood protection and control works, water supply, and roads and footpaths [DIA local government policy](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Resource-material-Our-Policy-Advice-Areas-Local-Government-Policy). DIA says those measures require local authorities to use a standard set of performance measures for those activities, but they are non-financial service measures and do not prescribe service standards or spending categories [DIA local government policy](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Resource-material-Our-Policy-Advice-Areas-Local-Government-Policy).

The 2019 report "Local Government Funding and Finance - Accounting and Financial Management Issues", prepared for the Productivity Commission and now hosted by Treasury, identifies GAAP financial reporting, additional LGA/prudence disclosures, activity-group information, and financial prudence benchmarks as the four components of the local-government financial disclosure regime, and records submissions saying councils categorise activities differently in the funding impact statement, so comparisons cannot always be made across councils [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf). The same report says a proposed definition of "groups of activities" would leave the reporting entity responsible for determining the groups for which it reports, reinforcing that activity-group reporting is not a complete national operational taxonomy [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf).

**Per-claim confidence: High.** The activity-group obligation is statutory, and DIA's page is explicit about the purpose and scope of the mandatory measures.

### Prudence regulations benchmark financial health, not detailed spending categories

The Local Government (Financial Reporting and Prudence) Regulations 2014 prescribe financial prudence statements and benchmarks, including rates affordability, debt affordability, balanced budget, essential services, and debt servicing [Local Government (Financial Reporting and Prudence) Regulations 2014](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/secondary-legislation/pco-drafted/2014/76/en/latest/). The regulation's own template says the purpose is to disclose council financial performance against benchmarks so readers can assess whether the council is prudently managing revenues, expenses, assets, liabilities, and general financial dealings [Local Government (Financial Reporting and Prudence) Regulations 2014, Schedule 4](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/secondary-legislation/pco-drafted/2014/76/en/latest/).

These prudence benchmarks are useful for fiscal sustainability comparisons; my reading of the Schedule 4 template is that its fields contain benchmark disclosures rather than fields for suppliers, contracts, projects, or detailed spend categories [Local Government (Financial Reporting and Prudence) Regulations 2014, Schedule 4](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/secondary-legislation/pco-drafted/2014/76/en/latest/).

**Per-claim confidence: Medium.** The regulation is primary legal evidence for the benchmark set and its purpose; the absence of supplier/project fields is a direct reading of the template rather than a separately stated proposition in the regulation.

### Stats NZ and DIA/MCERT publish comparable aggregates, with explicit caveats

Stats NZ's Local Authority Census page describes an annual survey sent to city councils, district councils, and non-market council-controlled organisations, and says the resulting Local Authority Financial Statistics publish income and expenditure, operating income and expenditure by activity, and financial position at both total and individual-council level [Stats NZ DataInfo+](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/9a6701e3-f1d3-4480-b879-5eea7716979b/101). Stats NZ's separate local authority data collection page says it collects income, expenditure, and financial position data for 78 local authorities and three Auckland Council CCOs, mostly from audited financial accounts, annual reports, and follow-up enquiries to councils [Stats NZ local authority data collection](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42). I am treating those as related but distinct Stats NZ collection descriptions, not as identical coverage statements.

Stats NZ also warns that local authorities have varying organisational structures and accounting systems, so some council activities and data items are not always readily available and may be estimated by either the council or Stats NZ [Stats NZ local authority data collection](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42). Stats NZ's quarterly Local Authority Statistics metadata similarly says accounting procedures vary among local authorities, which can make fully comparable statistics challenging, although Stats NZ does not expect the differences to materially affect the aggregate statistics it produces [Stats NZ quarterly Local Authority Statistics](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/f0e135ea-1e3b-466d-a4dd-5eb83238605c).

MCERT's July 2025 council-performance profiles compare councils within council groups across demographics, rates revenue, debt, and operating and capital expenditure [MCERT council performance](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/). MCERT says officials sourced the performance information from annual reports, annual plans, long-term plans, Stats NZ, and the Local Government Funding Agency, and it explicitly warns that it cannot guarantee all councils apply the same definitions or accounting practices for metrics such as staffing levels or capital expenditure by activity group [MCERT council performance](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/).

**Per-claim confidence: High for aggregate availability; Medium for detailed comparability limits.** Stats NZ and MCERT directly describe both the available aggregate datasets and their caveats, but MCERT partly sources its profiles from Stats NZ, annual reports, plans, and the Local Government Funding Agency, so the Stats NZ/MCERT pair is not fully independent for underlying data even though their caveat wording is each organisation's own editorial statement.

### Sampled open-data portals and LGOIMA do not remove the mapping problem

Public open-data portals and LGOIMA are relevant alternatives to relying only on statutory PDFs and national aggregate datasets. A quick check of three large territorial authorities' public data surfaces found open-data infrastructure, but did not find a public, reusable, cross-council supplier-payment or transaction-spend dataset in the sampled portal searches: Auckland Council's ArcGIS open-data site is described as a place to explore and download council-owned geospatial datasets, and ArcGIS public search queries for Auckland Council "expenditure" and "supplier" returned zero items [Auckland Council Open Data](https://data-aucklandcouncil.opendata.arcgis.com/) [Auckland ArcGIS search: expenditure](https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3An4yPwebTjJCmXB6W%20expenditure&num=10) [Auckland ArcGIS search: supplier](https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3An4yPwebTjJCmXB6W%20supplier&num=10). Wellington City Council's data.govt.nz showcase is explicitly about geospatial data, and Christchurch City Council's ArcGIS Hub describes its portal as a spatial open-data portal; ArcGIS public search queries for Christchurch "expenditure" and "supplier" returned zero items [Wellington City Council geospatial open data](https://data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/wellington-city-council-geospatial-open-data) [Christchurch City Council Open Data Portal](https://opendata-christchurchcity.hub.arcgis.com/search?layout=grid) [Christchurch ArcGIS search: expenditure](https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3ArW4Z7Dz9Bz87fkds%20expenditure&num=10) [Christchurch ArcGIS search: supplier](https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3ArW4Z7Dz9Bz87fkds%20supplier&num=10).

This is only a sample and should not be read as a 78-council publication census; it is enough to keep "which councils publish detailed spend data?" as a live follow-up rather than an assumption against the mapping-layer conclusion. The sampled evidence weakens any absolute claim that no detailed council spending data is publicly available, but it does not show a national common transaction taxonomy or a consistent open-spend format across the sampled large councils [Wellington City Council geospatial open data](https://data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/wellington-city-council-geospatial-open-data) [Christchurch City Council Open Data Portal](https://opendata-christchurchcity.hub.arcgis.com/search?layout=grid) [Auckland Council Open Data](https://data-aucklandcouncil.opendata.arcgis.com/).

LGOIMA is a real access mechanism for detailed spending records held by councils: Ombudsman guidance says LGOIMA allows people to request official information held by local-government agencies, that the principle of availability applies unless there is good reason to withhold, and that city, district, and regional councils are subject to the Act [Ombudsman, LGOIMA guide for local government agencies](https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/sites/default/files/2024-07/The%20LGOIMA%20for%20local%20government%20agencies%20A%20guide%20to%20processing%20requests%20and%20conducting%20meetings.pdf). LGOIMA can therefore obtain or test specific council spend questions, but it is reactive access to information held by each council, not evidence of a proactive national open-data standard or shared chart of accounts [Ombudsman, LGOIMA guide for local government agencies](https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/sites/default/files/2024-07/The%20LGOIMA%20for%20local%20government%20agencies%20A%20guide%20to%20processing%20requests%20and%20conducting%20meetings.pdf).

**Per-claim confidence: Medium.** The portal checks are deliberately narrow, and LGOIMA establishes a route to request information rather than a ready-to-use comparative dataset.

### Transaction- and project-level comparability would require a mapping layer

I found no official source that publishes or mandates a common NZ local-government chart of accounts for public transaction-level spending. The evidence for a mapping layer is therefore inferential: Stats NZ says council organisational structures and accounting systems vary; MCERT says it cannot guarantee shared definitions or accounting practices across councils; and the Productivity Commission accounting report records activity-categorisation comparability concerns and says a more developed definition of groups of activities would still leave reporting entities responsible for determining their reported groups [Stats NZ local authority data collection](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42) [MCERT council performance](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/) [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf).

A civic tool can use existing standards for high-level views such as total operating expenditure, capital expenditure, debt, rates, prudence benchmarks, and some activity-level comparisons, but should treat semantic equivalence between two councils' ledger lines, procurement categories, programme names, or project budgets as a hypothesis to be mapped and verified council by council [Stats NZ DataInfo+](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/9a6701e3-f1d3-4480-b879-5eea7716979b/101) [MCERT council performance](https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/) [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf).

**Per-claim confidence: Medium.** The need for a mapping layer is my inference from official caveats, the Productivity Commission accounting report, sampled portal checks, and the absence of a discovered public mandated chart of accounts; it would become High if confirmed directly by DIA, Stats NZ, Taituarā, LGNZ, Audit New Zealand, or council finance practitioners.

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Existing public standards support aggregate comparisons, but not transaction- or project-level spending comparability. | [Stats NZ local authority data collection](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42) | [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf) | Medium |
| Activity-group reporting is mandatory, but it is not a complete national spending taxonomy. | [Local Government Act 2002, Schedule 10](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html) | [DIA local government policy](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Resource-material-Our-Policy-Advice-Areas-Local-Government-Policy) | High |
| A tool comparing detailed spending across councils should plan for a mapping layer across council-specific ledgers, definitions, and documents. This is an inference, not a directly stated official requirement. | [Stats NZ local authority data collection](https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42) | [Treasury-hosted Productivity Commission report, 2019](https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf) | Medium |

## What would change this conclusion

- The conclusion would change if DIA, MCERT, Stats NZ, Taituarā, LGNZ, or a statutory instrument published a current common chart of accounts, activity-code hierarchy, procurement-spend taxonomy, or data standard that councils are required to use below the aggregate reporting level.
- The conclusion would change for a narrower civic tool if a subset of councils using the same finance system or open-data portal exposed consistent transaction exports with stable field definitions and licences.
- I could not verify whether councils share any non-public finance-system implementation templates, model charts of accounts, or professional-practice guidance through Taituarā, LGNZ, Audit New Zealand, or finance-system vendors.
- I did not test every council's annual report, annual plan, procurement page, LGOIMA disclosure log, or open-data portal; this finding is about national reporting structures, official aggregate datasets, and a small portal sample, not a full council-by-council publication census.
- A human council-finance practitioner or local-government auditor should review any proposed mapping rules before a public tool labels detailed spend categories as comparable.

## Open follow-up questions

- Which councils publish transaction-level, supplier-level, contract-level, or project-level spend data in reusable formats, and under what licence?
- Which council spending questions are best answered by LGOIMA requests rather than proactive open data, and what request wording reliably produces reusable machine-readable exports?
- Can Stats NZ Local Authority Financial Statistics activity categories be used as a first-pass taxonomy for a civic spending tool, and which categories are too broad or inconsistently allocated?
- Are there common finance-system vendors or implementation templates across NZ councils that could make semi-automated account mapping feasible for clusters of councils?
- What council-spending questions do residents and local journalists most often ask that can be answered safely from aggregate data alone?

## Sources

1. Local Government Act 2002, section 111 and Schedule 10, New Zealand Legislation. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM172320.html> and <https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2002/84/en/latest/DLM3418900.html>
2. Local Government (Financial Reporting and Prudence) Regulations 2014, New Zealand Legislation. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.legislation.govt.nz/secondary-legislation/pco-drafted/2014/76/en/latest/>
3. External Reporting Board, "Accounting Standards Framework". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.xrb.govt.nz/standards/accounting-standards/accounting-standards-framework/>
4. External Reporting Board, submission on "Local Government Funding and Financing - Draft Report". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.xrb.govt.nz/dmsdocument/3374/>
5. Stats NZ DataInfo+, "Local Authority Census". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/9a6701e3-f1d3-4480-b879-5eea7716979b/101>
6. Stats NZ DataInfo+, "Local authority data collection". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/66c1d2c4-0ccc-4ff6-8fbd-1e972662c005/42>
7. Stats NZ DataInfo+, "Local Authority Statistics (quarterly)". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://datainfoplus.stats.govt.nz/Item/nz.govt.stats/f0e135ea-1e3b-466d-a4dd-5eb83238605c>
8. Department of Internal Affairs, "Local Government Policy", including Local Government Mandatory Performance Measures and Non-Financial Performance Measures Rules 2024. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.dia.govt.nz/Resource-material-Our-Policy-Advice-Areas-Local-Government-Policy>
9. Ministry of Cities, Environment, Regions and Transport, "Council performance". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.mcert.govt.nz/our-work/local-government/council-performance/>
10. Ian Ball/Public Sector Performance (NZ) Ltd, "Local Government Funding and Finance - Accounting and Financial Management Issues", report to the New Zealand Productivity Commission, hosted by The Treasury. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.treasury.govt.nz/sites/default/files/2024-05/pc-inq-lgff-public-sector-performance-lg-accounting-and-financial-management-issues.pdf>
11. Auckland Council Open Data and ArcGIS public search results. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://data-aucklandcouncil.opendata.arcgis.com/>; <https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3An4yPwebTjJCmXB6W%20expenditure&num=10>; <https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3An4yPwebTjJCmXB6W%20supplier&num=10>
12. Wellington City Council geospatial open data showcase, data.govt.nz. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/wellington-city-council-geospatial-open-data>
13. Christchurch City Council Open Data Portal and ArcGIS public search results. Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://opendata-christchurchcity.hub.arcgis.com/search?layout=grid>; <https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3ArW4Z7Dz9Bz87fkds%20expenditure&num=10>; <https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/search?f=json&q=orgid%3ArW4Z7Dz9Bz87fkds%20supplier&num=10>
14. Office of the Ombudsman, "The LGOIMA for local government agencies: A guide to processing requests and conducting meetings". Accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/sites/default/files/2024-07/The%20LGOIMA%20for%20local%20government%20agencies%20A%20guide%20to%20processing%20requests%20and%20conducting%20meetings.pdf>
