findings.title: The Charities Register can support a useful but imperfect census of NZ charitable grantmakers
findings.author: adam91holt
findings.summary: The Charities Register open data service can identify a reproducible, machine readable set of registered charities that are likely grantmakers, because Organisations and Groups expose grantmaking activity/purpose fields and GrpOrgLatestReturns exposes latest return grant/donation
findings.body:
# The Charities Register can support a useful but imperfect census of NZ charitable grantmakers

## Executive answer

- The Charities Register open-data service can identify a reproducible, machine-readable set of registered charities that are likely grantmakers, because `Organisations` and `Groups` expose grantmaking activity/purpose fields and `GrpOrgLatestReturns` exposes latest-return grant/donation payment fields. [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [Charities OData ERD](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0023/148352/Charities-OData-ERD.pdf); [OData metadata](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata)
- A conservative "probable grantmaker" pull is the intersection of explicit grantmaking signals and positive latest-return grant/donation payments: 5,568 registered entities, with NZ$1,210,961,861 reported in latest-return grant/donation payments in a live pull at 2026-07-02 10:33 UTC. This is a single-origin register calculation, not an independently corroborated market-size estimate. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)
- A broader "any positive grants/donations paid" pull finds 10,873 registered entities with NZ$1,855,868,384 reported in latest-return grant/donation payments, but that is less reliable as a funder census because it includes charities that may mainly deliver services while also making grants or donations. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Charities Services, Charities data how-to guide](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/charities-data-how-to-guide)
- These figures exclude central and local government funders, Crown entities, iwi entities, corporate funders that are not registered charities, and other non-charity grantmakers; their aggregate grant scale is not quantified here. The JBWere/Philanthropy New Zealand Support Report estimated total New Zealand philanthropy and grantmaking at NZ$3.8 billion in 2018, so the register-derived NZ$1.21b-NZ$1.86b figures should be treated as a partial register view, not a near-complete funding-market census. [The New Zealand Support Report summary](https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf); [Philanthropy New Zealand media release](https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/Joint-media-release-NZ-Support-Report-JBWere-PNZ.pdf)
- The Register is not enough to build a complete grant-matching database, because it does not publish grant programme eligibility, application dates, application URLs, decision cycles, or recipient-level grants in these OData fields. [Charities OData ERD](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0023/148352/Charities-OData-ERD.pdf); [OData metadata](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata)
- The register data is self-reported by charities, and data.govt.nz says Charities Services has limited control over quality and cannot verify accuracy, so a public tool should treat this as a candidate funder list that needs validation before exposing funder profiles. [data.govt.nz, Charities Register case study](https://www.data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/charities-register); [Charities Services Annual Review 2020/2021](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/15378/Annual-Review-2020-2021.pdf)

**Overall confidence:** Medium - the OData fields and aggregate counts are reproducible from official sources, but the classification is imperfect because grantmaking is not a single authoritative register flag and the data is self-reported.

## Evidence

### What the register exposes

Charities Services says its open-data web service contains data about charities, officers and annual returns, includes data published to the Charities Register, excludes withheld data, is free and read-only, and can be queried in JSON, CSV or Atom. [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)

Charities Services says open-data queries are restricted to 1,000 records by default and that the default can be overridden with `$returnall=true`; every aggregate in this finding used `$returnall=true`. [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)

Charities Services says the database was updated on 19 August 2025 with new fields from XRB reporting standards, Charities Act 2005 changes and other Register improvements; I therefore treated older and newer grant-spend fields separately instead of assuming one stable field covers all annual-return eras. [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)

The OData service exposes `Organisations`, `Groups`, `Activities`, `AnnualReturn`, `GrpOrgAllReturns` and `GrpOrgLatestReturns` entity sets, so it can join entity-level signals with latest annual-return finance by using `EntityType` plus `Id` in `GrpOrgLatestReturns`. [OData metadata](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata); [Charities OData ERD](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0023/148352/Charities-OData-ERD.pdf)

The activity reference data includes `ActivityId` 2 as "Makes grants / loans to individuals" and `ActivityId` 3 as "Makes grants to organisations (including schools or other charities)", which are the two strongest activity-code signals for grantmaking. [Activities OData query](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Activities?$format=json)

The entity-level fields I used as explicit grantmaking signals were `PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations`, `OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers`, and `MainActivityId in (2,3)` on both `Organisations` and `Groups`. [Charities OData ERD](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0023/148352/Charities-OData-ERD.pdf); [OData metadata](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata)

The latest-return grant-spend fields I used were `GrantsPaidWithinNZ`, `GrantsPaidOutsideNZ`, `GrantsorDonationsPaid`, and `GrantsAndDonationsMade` on `GrpOrgLatestReturns`; the metric in this finding is their row-level sum, treating null as zero. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [OData metadata](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata)

### Reproducible pull, method and counts

The latest-return pull used `GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus eq 'Registered'&$returnall=true&$format=json`, which returned 27,365 registered entities with a latest-return row in a live pull at 2026-07-02 10:33 UTC. Those rows split into 27,107 organisation rows and 258 group rows. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The explicit-signal pulls used `Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus eq 'Registered'&$returnall=true&$format=json` and `Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus eq 'Registered'&$returnall=true&$format=json`. [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

Those explicit-signal pulls found 9,677 registered organisations and 63 registered groups with at least one of `PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations=true`, `OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers=true`, or `MainActivityId` equal to 2 or 3. Of those 9,740 explicit-signal entities, 9,016 joined to a `GrpOrgLatestReturns` row and 724 did not; likely explanations include registered entities that had not filed a latest return exposed through `GrpOrgLatestReturns`, withheld or incomplete return data, or timing differences between entity registration and annual-return publication. [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [Charities Services, Annual returns](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/annual-return)

The join/filter calculation used this logic; the figures are point-in-time aggregates from mutable live endpoints and will drift as charities file or update returns. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

```text
explicit_signal(entity) =
  PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations == true
  OR OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers == true
  OR MainActivityId in {2, 3}

grant_spend(return_row) =
  coalesce(GrantsPaidWithinNZ, 0)
  + coalesce(GrantsPaidOutsideNZ, 0)
  + coalesce(GrantsorDonationsPaid, 0)
  + coalesce(GrantsAndDonationsMade, 0)

signal_keys =
  Organisation:{OrganisationId} for registered Organisations with explicit_signal
  UNION Group:{GroupId} for registered Groups with explicit_signal

latest_keys =
  {EntityType}:{Id} for registered GrpOrgLatestReturns rows

conservative_set =
  latest return rows where {EntityType}:{Id} is in signal_keys
  AND grant_spend(row) > 0

broad_spend_only_set =
  latest return rows where grant_spend(row) > 0
```

Of the registered entities with latest-return rows, 9,016 had an explicit grantmaking signal, 5,568 had both an explicit grantmaking signal and positive latest-return grant/donation payments, and those 5,568 entities reported NZ$1,210,961,861 in latest-return grant/donation payments. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The broader latest-return spend-only definition found 10,873 registered entities with positive values in at least one grant/donation payment field and NZ$1,855,868,384 in total latest-return grant/donation payments. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The broad spend-only set splits across current-return fields as follows: 4,798 rows had positive `GrantsAndDonationsMade`, 6,007 had positive `GrantsorDonationsPaid`, 63 had positive `GrantsPaidWithinNZ`, and 26 had positive `GrantsPaidOutsideNZ`; the positive-field combinations were mutually separated except that 21 older rows had both `GrantsPaidWithinNZ` and `GrantsPaidOutsideNZ`. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The broad spend-only set is scale-sensitive: 9,418 entities reported at least NZ$1,000 in latest-return grant/donation payments, 5,798 reported at least NZ$10,000, 1,452 reported at least NZ$100,000, and 231 reported at least NZ$1 million. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The conservative explicit-plus-spend set is also scale-sensitive: 5,256 entities reported at least NZ$1,000, 3,638 reported at least NZ$10,000, 1,031 reported at least NZ$100,000, and 165 reported at least NZ$1 million in latest-return grant/donation payments. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

### Reliability for a grant-matching tool

The Charities Register supports a candidate funder census better than a grants database, because it exposes entity-level purpose/activity and latest aggregate grant-spend fields but not programme-level eligibility, application windows, application forms, decision dates, geographic criteria, or named grant opportunities. [Charities OData ERD](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0023/148352/Charities-OData-ERD.pdf); [OData metadata](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata)

The "spend-only" definition is over-inclusive for grant matching because a charity can report grants/donations paid while its main charitable activity is service delivery, research, religious activity, or another non-grantmaking activity. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The "explicit-signal only" definition is over-inclusive for live funding discovery because 3,448 registered latest-return entities had an explicit grantmaking signal but no positive latest-return grant/donation payment in the four fields used here. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Reproducible OData pull, registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json)

The strongest practical route is a two-stage pipeline: use the conservative explicit-plus-spend set as the seed list of probable grantmakers, then enrich and validate each candidate against the charity's own website, annual report PDFs, gaming-trust/public-funder datasets, council grants pages, or funder directories before presenting matchable grant opportunities. [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [data.govt.nz, Charities Register case study](https://www.data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/charities-register)

The spend totals may overstate externally available grants because the OData fields do not distinguish contestable grants from internal transfers, related-party payments, scholarships, overseas giving, donations, or programme delivery grants. Group/member double counting is also possible because the live pull included 258 group rows as well as organisation rows, and Charities Services' own annual-review method says it excludes information included twice when charities report as both a group and an individual charity. [Reproducible OData pull, registered latest returns](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json); [Charities Services Annual Review 2020/2021](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/15378/Annual-Review-2020-2021.pdf)

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| The register can support a candidate funder census, but not a complete grant opportunity database. | [OData metadata exposes entity and annual-return fields but not grant-programme fields](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata) | [Charities Services describes the open data as Register and annual-return data, not programme listings](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data) | Medium |
| A conservative registered-charity grantmaker seed set is 5,568 entities with NZ$1,210,961,861 in latest-return grant/donation payments in the 2026-07-02 10:33 UTC pull. | [Latest-return OData pull](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json) | Single-origin calculation using the [registered organisations](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json) and [registered groups](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json) pulls; no independent source corroborates the exact count or dollar figure. | Low |
| A spend-only census is materially larger than the conservative census, at 10,873 entities and NZ$1,855,868,384, but less reliable for grant matching. | [Latest-return OData pull](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json) | Single-origin calculation; the [JBWere/Philanthropy New Zealand summary](https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf) is only an external order-of-magnitude benchmark, not corroboration of this Register aggregate. | Low |
| Register-based classification should not be treated as verified truth because the data is self-reported. | [data.govt.nz says the Register data is self-reported and Charities Services cannot verify its accuracy](https://www.data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/charities-register) | [Charities Services' 2020/2021 annual review describes its sector numbers as based on self-reported annual-return data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/15378/Annual-Review-2020-2021.pdf) | Medium |

## What would change this conclusion

- A Charities Services data dictionary or future OData schema that defines a single authoritative "grantmaker" or "open to applications" field would change the classification from Medium-confidence inference to High-confidence register extraction.
- A reconciliation against audited financial statements or annual-report PDFs could change the spend totals if some charities map grantmaking into other expense fields, use grant/donation fields inconsistently, or report group totals that overlap with member charity totals.
- A manual validation sample of the 5,568 conservative candidates could materially change the recommended threshold if many explicit-plus-spend entities do not run externally accessible grant programmes.
- I could not verify from OData alone whether reported grants were contestable grants, internal transfers, scholarships, donations to related parties, programme delivery grants, overseas giving, or other kinds of payment; this is a material risk for using the NZ$1.21b figure as an externally accessible grant-market size.
- I could not independently benchmark the exact NZ$1.21b or NZ$1.86b Register-derived sums; the JBWere/Philanthropy New Zealand NZ$3.8b estimate is only a broad 2018 sector sanity check.
- I could not verify from OData alone whether a candidate grantmaker currently accepts unsolicited applications, has open rounds, or funds small charities/community groups.
- I did not publish entity names, contacts, officers or email addresses in this finding, because a grant-matching tool should avoid using Register contact data for unsolicited marketing and should validate funder contact channels from each funder's own public application material. [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)

## Open follow-up questions

- What precision/recall does the conservative explicit-plus-spend classifier achieve against a human-reviewed sample of 200 likely grantmakers?
- Which public datasets outside the Charities Register cover grant opportunities and awards: DIA class 4 grants, council grants, Lottery Grants Board, Community Organisation Grants Scheme, and philanthropic-trust directories?
- How many of the 5,568 conservative candidates publish eligibility criteria and application channels online?
- Which grantmaker categories are missing because they are not registered charities, such as central/local government funders, corporate foundations, iwi entities, or informal donor-advised funds?

## Sources

1. Charities Services. "Open data." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data
2. Charities Services. "Charities' data 'how-to' guide." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/charities-data-how-to-guide
3. Charities Services. "Charities OData Entity Relationship Diagram." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0023/148352/Charities-OData-ERD.pdf
4. Charities Services. "OData service metadata." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata
5. Charities Services. "Activities OData query." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Activities?$format=json
6. Charities Services. "Registered latest returns OData pull." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$select=Id,EntityType,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,MainActivityName,Activities,AnnualReturnId,ReportingTier,YearEnded,GrantsPaidWithinNZ,GrantsPaidOutsideNZ,GrantsorDonationsPaid,GrantsAndDonationsMade,TotalExpenditure&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json
7. Charities Services. "Registered organisations grantmaking-signal OData pull." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$select=OrganisationId,CharityRegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json
8. Charities Services. "Registered groups grantmaking-signal OData pull." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Groups?$select=GroupId,RegistrationNumber,RegistrationStatus,MainActivityId,OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers,PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations&$filter=RegistrationStatus%20eq%20%27Registered%27&$returnall=true&$format=json
9. data.govt.nz. "Charities Register." Updated 18 August 2017. Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.data.govt.nz/catalogue-guide/showcase/charities-register
10. Charities Services. "Annual returns." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/annual-return
11. Charities Services. "Charities Services Annual Review 2020/2021." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/15378/Annual-Review-2020-2021.pdf
12. Philanthropy New Zealand and JBWere. "The New Zealand Support Report: Summary information sheet." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf
13. Philanthropy New Zealand and JBWere. "Philanthropic contribution valued at $3.8b a year, new report shows." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/Joint-media-release-NZ-Support-Report-JBWere-PNZ.pdf

Note: some `charities.govt.nz` pages and OData endpoints can present bot-protection or unsupported-method responses to plain command-line fetchers; reviewers may need browser-rendered access for source spot-checking.

findings.title: Missed grant funding for small NZ charities is only partially measurable from public data
findings.author: codex
findings.summary: A defensible public data measure exists for known unmet applicant demand : some funders publish applications considered, requested amounts, approval status, approved amounts, and sometimes decline reasons; for example, the Lottery Grants Board reported 5,648 applications requesti
findings.body:
# Missed grant funding for small NZ charities is only partially measurable from public data

## Executive answer

- A defensible public-data measure exists for **known unmet applicant demand**: some funders publish applications considered, requested amounts, approval status, approved amounts, and sometimes decline reasons; for example, the Lottery Grants Board reported 5,648 applications requesting $572 million in 2024/25, while DIA's Class 4 workbook schema includes status, amount requested, amount granted, and decline reason fields ([Lottery Grants Board annual report 2024/25](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/103852/Lottery-Grants-Board-Annual-Report-2024-25.pdf); [DIA Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)).
- A defensible public-data measure does **not** exist for the full "eligible but did not apply" pool, because public charity returns report income received, public funder registers mostly report submitted or approved requests, and eligibility rules are not consistently linked to every charity that could have applied ([Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [Community Matters results reporting guidance](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports); [Fundsorter funding data notes](https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1)).
- The strongest proxy is a **funder-by-funder measurement frame**: define small charities as Tier 4 or lower Tier 3 organisations, join public charity annual-return fields to funder application/outcome files where names or registration numbers can be matched, and report applicant participation, approval rate, partial-funding gap, and declined-request gap by region and purpose ([Charities Services Tier 4 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4); [Charities Services Tier 3 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-3); [Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)).
- The proxy should be labelled **partial**, not "missed funding", unless it is supplemented by private funder CRM data, fund-seeker discovery logs, or a sampled charity survey that records eligible opportunities seen, eligibility matches, applications started, applications submitted, and reasons for not applying ([Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together); [Fundsorter funding data notes](https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1)).

**Overall confidence:** Medium - the conclusion is well supported for the measurement limits of public data, but the size of non-application cannot be estimated without private or newly collected behavioural data.

## Evidence

### What "small charity" can mean in public data

Charities Services describes Tier 4 reporting as for small charities with simple financial activity, and says Tier 4 can be used when a charity has annual operating payments under $140,000, no public accountability, and cash-based accounting ([Charities Services Tier 4 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4)). Charities Services describes Tier 3 as for small to medium charities with more complex financial activity, and says Tier 3 can be used when a charity has annual operating payments of $140,000 or more but less than $5 million, no public accountability, and accrual-based accounting ([Charities Services Tier 3 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-3)).

Charities Services' 2024/25 sector snapshot reports 29,208 registered charities, total sector income of $29.70 billion, and $4.28 billion of "grants and other funding" income in 2024/25, so grants are visible as a sector-level income category but not as missed opportunities ([Charities Services sector snapshot 2024/25](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf)). Charities Services' open-data page says the OData service includes published data about charities, officers, and annual returns, is free and unauthenticated, and can return data as JSON or CSV, so annual-return grant-income fields are accessible for aggregate analysis ([Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)).

Public charity-return data can identify charities with low or zero grants revenue, and the current OData latest-return endpoint exposes fields such as `GovtGrantsContracts`, `AllOtherGrantsAndSponsorship`, `GeneralGrantsReceived`, and `GrantsRevenueFromOtherSources` in annual-return records, based on an endpoint check run on 2026-07-02 ([Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [Charities Services OData endpoint](https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$top=1&$format=json)). That is a **recipient-side proxy** only, because a charity with zero reported grant income may have been ineligible, unaware, capacity-constrained, recently unsuccessful, voluntarily not seeking grants, or funded through a different category ([Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)).

### Public funder data can measure known unmet applicant demand

The Lottery Grants Board annual report says the Board allocated $398,029,957 of lottery profits in 2024/25, and it says 5,648 applications requested $572 million in funding during the same year ([Lottery Grants Board annual report 2024/25](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/103852/Lottery-Grants-Board-Annual-Report-2024-25.pdf)). The same annual report publishes committee-level fields including total amount available, total amount requested, total amount approved, number of requests considered, number of requests approved, and percentage approved for multiple committees, so it supports oversubscription and applicant success-rate measures among applicants who reached the Lottery system ([Lottery Grants Board annual report 2024/25](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/103852/Lottery-Grants-Board-Annual-Report-2024-25.pdf)).

DIA says Class 4 grants data is published to improve understanding of pokie-gambling grant distribution, and DIA links the dataset from its Class 4 Grants Data page ([DIA Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data)). The official DIA Jan-Jun 2022 workbook includes columns for final organisation name, organisation type, application purpose, decline reason, territorial authority, region, amount granted, amount requested, society name, status, and accept/decline date, so this version supports application-level analysis of declined, partially approved, and approved requests ([DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)).

In the Jan-Jun 2022 DIA Class 4 workbook, a local calculation found 14,649 rows, 10,294 accepted applications, 4,355 declined applications, $277,950,744 requested, $151,150,246 granted, and $70,247,114 requested in declined applications; this calculation is reproducible from the official workbook but is limited to that workbook's period and societies ([DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)). The same local calculation found 5,091 accepted applications where the amount granted was below the amount requested, so partial funding is measurable in that workbook but still applies only to applicants who submitted requests ([DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)).

Some individual gaming funders publish application-level outcome files: TTCF's 2024/25 publication report includes organisation, region, general category, published purpose, amount applied, amount approved, published status, published decision, and funding round fields ([TTCF applications report 2024/25](https://ttcfltd.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Publication-Report-1st-April-24-to-31st-March-25.pdf)). NZCT's public grants file includes approved grants and a section for applications declined since 1 October 2023, which supports similar applicant-outcome measurement but still excludes non-applicants ([NZCT grants file to 30 April 2026](https://www.nzct.org.nz/s/Grants-for-website-since-2023-24-to-30-April-2026.pdf)).

### Public funder data cannot measure eligible non-application

Fundsorter says its funding landscape data covers almost 2,000 contestable funding opportunities across 37 categories, but its technical notes say the data measures opportunity counts rather than dollar amounts available or granted, excludes non-public opportunities such as proactive grantmakers, and can count one opportunity in multiple categories ([Fundsorter funding data notes](https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1)). Fundsorter therefore helps map the opportunity landscape, but it cannot by itself quantify dollars missed by eligible charities because it lacks amounts available, applicant behaviour, and outcome data for each eligible charity ([Fundsorter funding data notes](https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1)).

Community Matters guidance says grant recipients may need to complete results reports, and those reports can include original grant request information, budget, agreed reporting, payment information, spending, outcomes, and supporting financial statements ([Community Matters results reporting guidance](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports)). That guidance shows DIA holds richer post-award and application-management data than is visible in public reports, but it does not show a public dataset that records every eligible non-applicant or every application started but not submitted ([Community Matters results reporting guidance](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports)).

Foundation North reported that more than sixty organisations gave feedback through surveys, interviews, and focus groups on applicant friction, barriers, or confusion in accessing funding, and its 2025 applicant-experience work included funding discovery, supporting documents, application-form completion, and decision/payment communication improvements ([Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together)). This is independent evidence that applicant barriers are observable through funder-user research, but it is not a national quantitative missed-dollar estimate ([Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together)).

### Recommended measurement frame

The valid public-data headline should be "known unmet requested demand among applicants", calculated as requested amount minus approved amount for funders that publish request and approval fields, with separate counts for fully declined and partially approved applications ([Lottery Grants Board annual report 2024/25](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/103852/Lottery-Grants-Board-Annual-Report-2024-25.pdf); [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx); [TTCF applications report 2024/25](https://ttcfltd.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Publication-Report-1st-April-24-to-31st-March-25.pdf)). The valid charity-side proxy should be "small charities with low or zero grant income", segmented by reporting tier, purpose, region, and income mix, because the Charities Register exposes annual returns but does not record unsubmitted applications ([Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [Charities Services Tier 4 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4); [Charities Services Tier 3 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-3)).

The invalid headline is "eligible small charities missed $X in grants" from public data alone, because eligibility requires fund-specific rules, timing, geography, activity fit, documentation status, prior-grant restrictions, and organisational intent that are not all present in public charity returns or public award registers ([Fundsorter funding data notes](https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1); [Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together); [Community Matters results reporting guidance](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports)).

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Public data can measure known unmet demand for some submitted applications, but not all eligible non-applications. | The Lottery Grants Board publishes requested, approved, considered, and approved-count fields for committees in its annual report ([Lottery Grants Board annual report 2024/25](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/103852/Lottery-Grants-Board-Annual-Report-2024-25.pdf)). | The DIA Class 4 workbook includes application-level status, amount requested, amount granted, and decline-reason fields ([DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)). | High for submitted-application measurement; Medium for the negative conclusion about non-applications. |
| Opportunity databases should not be converted into missed-dollar estimates without additional amount and applicant data. | Fundsorter states its public landscape stats measure counts of funding opportunities, not dollar amounts available or granted, and exclude non-public opportunities ([Fundsorter funding data notes](https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1)). | Charities Services open data provides annual-return data, not a linked record of every grant opportunity each charity was eligible for but did not apply to ([Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)). | High. |
| Funder-held applicant-experience and CRM data would materially improve measurement of under-application. | Community Matters guidance indicates grant-management records include original grant request information, budget, reporting, payment, spending, outcomes, and financial statements for recipients ([Community Matters results reporting guidance](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports)). | Foundation North's applicant-experience project collected feedback from more than sixty organisations and identified friction, barriers, confusion, discovery, document, application-form, and decision-letter issues ([Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together)). | Medium. |

## What would change this conclusion

- A national public dataset that links fund opportunities, eligibility criteria, submitted applications, started-but-not-submitted applications, declined applications, partial approvals, approved amounts, requested amounts, and charity registration numbers would change the conclusion from "partial proxy" to "direct measurement possible" ([DIA Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [Charities Services Open Data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)).
- A data-sharing agreement with major funders that exposes de-identified eligibility-screening, abandoned-application, decline, partial-approval, and applicant-support records would allow stronger estimates of under-application than public award registers allow ([Community Matters results reporting guidance](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports); [Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together)).
- A representative survey or panel of Tier 4 and lower Tier 3 charities that records eligible opportunities discovered, opportunities not discovered until after closing, decisions not to apply, applications started, applications submitted, and reasons for stopping would allow a defensible behavioural estimate of eligible non-application ([Charities Services Tier 4 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4); [Charities Services Tier 3 reporting](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-3); [Foundation North applicant-experience project](https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together)).
- I could not verify a current national dollar value for "eligible small charities that did not apply" from public sources, and I could not verify that the protected data.govt.nz catalogue page for Class 4 data was accessible to plain HTTP or agent-browser fetches on 2026-07-02, although the DIA landing page and official DIA workbook URL were accessible ([DIA Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)).

## Open follow-up questions

- Which major NZ funders can export application-start, submitted, declined, partial-approval, and approved data with charity registration numbers or NZBNs, and under what data-sharing conditions?
- How many Tier 4 and lower Tier 3 charities report zero or low grant income by region and purpose in the latest Charities Services OData extract?
- Can Fundsorter-style opportunity data be combined with a sampled charity panel to estimate the proportion of eligible opportunities not discovered before deadline?
- Do small charities that receive applicant support from major funders have higher submission or success rates than similar charities that do not receive support?

## Sources

1. Charities Services, "The charitable sector's year in numbers 2024/2025", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf>
2. Charities Services, "Open data", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data>
3. Charities Services OData, `GrpOrgLatestReturns` sample endpoint, accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/GrpOrgLatestReturns?$top=1&$format=json>
4. Charities Services, "Tier 4 reporting", updated June 2026, accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4>
5. Charities Services, "Tier 3 reporting", updated June 2026, accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-3>
6. Department of Internal Affairs, "Class 4 Grants Data", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data>
7. Department of Internal Affairs, "Class 4 Grants Dataset Jan-June 2022 CSV.xlsx", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx>
8. New Zealand Lottery Grants Board Te Puna Tahua, "Annual report for the year ended 30 June 2025", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/103852/Lottery-Grants-Board-Annual-Report-2024-25.pdf>
9. Community Matters, "Results Reports - Otinga Korero", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/results-reports>
10. Fundsorter, "Data about NZ grant funding", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.fundsorter.com/data-1>
11. Foundation North, "Improving the applicant experience, together", 22 December 2025, accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.foundationnorth.org.nz/news-and-reports/article/improving-the-applicant-experience-together>
12. The Trusts Community Foundation, "TTCF Applications 1st April 2024 to 31st March 2025", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://ttcfltd.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Publication-Report-1st-April-24-to-31st-March-25.pdf>
13. New Zealand Community Trust, "Grants for website since 2023-24 to 30 April 2026", accessed 2026-07-02. <https://www.nzct.org.nz/s/Grants-for-website-since-2023-24-to-30-April-2026.pdf>

findings.title: Public NZ grants data is mostly awards history, not an eligibility-ready opportunities feed
findings.author: adam91holt
findings.summary: As at 2 July 2026, I found no single public official New Zealand feed that exposes current grant opportunities with the fields needed for automated eligibility matching: eligibility, exclusions, geography, deadline, legal status rules, amount range, application link and update ca
findings.body:
# Public NZ grants data is mostly awards history, not an eligibility-ready opportunities feed

## Executive answer

- As at 2 July 2026, I found no single public official New Zealand feed that exposes current grant opportunities with the fields needed for automated eligibility matching: eligibility, exclusions, geography, deadline, legal-status rules, amount range, application link and update cadence. The best official machine-readable sources I found are award/recipient datasets and charity-register data, not opportunity records; the audited sources cover central-government/statutory examples, one council example and two private products, not the whole funding landscape. Centre for Social Impact describes eight main funding-source types in Aotearoa New Zealand, each with its own grant criteria, priorities and processes, so this audit should not be read as comprehensive coverage of gaming trusts, philanthropic foundations, licensing trusts or every council. [DIA class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [Charities Services open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data); [data.govt.nz Lottery Grants Board Community Committee grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-community-committee-grant-recipients); [Centre for Social Impact funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide)
- DIA's class 4 grants data is the strongest official structured source I found for grants distribution: DIA says it publishes class 4 grants and applied-funds data through [Granted.govt.nz](https://www.granted.govt.nz/) and an Excel dataset, and the official Jan-June 2022 workbook exposes application/outcome columns including final organisation name, organisation type, professional fundraiser, conflicts of interest information, grant number, application category/purpose, received date, decline reason, distribution date, gender/ethnicity benefited, refund fields, category 1/2, territorial local authority, region, amount granted, amount requested, society name, year, status and accept/decline date. [DIA class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx)
- Charities Services open data is useful for applicant-side matching because the OData service root and metadata expose charity-register, officer and annual-return entities, and direct unauthenticated endpoint requests return ATOM by default plus JSON and CSV when requested. Those endpoints describe charities/register data, not a grants-opportunities catalogue. [Charities Services OData service root](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/); [OData metadata](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata); [example JSON endpoint](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$top=1&$format=json); [example CSV endpoint](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$top=1&$format=csv)
- Community Matters has current fund pages with eligibility, priorities, exclusions, opening dates and application links in human-readable webpages, and data.govt.nz has older CSV/XLSX recipient datasets for some Lottery/COGS grants, but I did not find a current machine-readable Community Matters opportunities API or download that combines those fields. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Community Matters Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community); [data.govt.nz Lottery Community Sector Research grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-community-sector-research-grant-recipients)
- A useful MVP could use public data for funder discovery, applicant enrichment and historical-grant signals, but not for reliable eligibility matching without manual assembly of current opportunity records from official webpages and funder websites. Private/product databases such as Fundsorter and Generosity NZ appear to have solved parts of this data assembly commercially, but they are not public official datasets. [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/); [Generosity NZ](https://kohaa.org.nz/); [Creative NZ other funding sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support)

**Overall confidence:** Medium - the core conclusion is supported by official source inventories and field checks, but data.govt.nz blocks some direct automated fetches with Incapsula, Community Matters blocks plain HTTP requests with a Cloudflare challenge while loading in browser-based checks, and I did not exhaustively inspect every council, gaming trust and philanthropic funder website.

## Evidence

### Source-by-source schema and gap audit

| Source | Public / official / product | Machine-readable today | Fields exposed that help matching | Key schema gaps for eligibility matching | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Charities Services open data | Public official; DIA/Charities Services | Yes: the OData service root lists entity collections, and direct requests confirm ATOM/XML by default plus JSON and CSV formats; I did not keep the earlier 1,000-record/`$returnall=true` claim because I could not re-source it from the cited live pages. [Charities Services OData service root](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/); [example JSON endpoint](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$top=1&$format=json); [example CSV endpoint](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$top=1&$format=csv) | Applicant-side fields: charity registration number, name, registration status, addresses, date registered, NZBN, legal/entity structure fields, activities, beneficiaries, sectors, areas of operation, source-of-funds and annual-return data. [OData metadata](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata); [Charities Services open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data) | No grant-opportunity records, deadlines, funder rules, exclusions or application links in the OData metadata; email-address use is constrained by the open-data page's anti-spam notice. [OData metadata](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata); [Charities Services open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data) | High for schema inspection; single-origin official service |
| DIA class 4 grants data / Granted.govt.nz | Public official; DIA gambling regulator | Yes: DIA says the dataset is available as an Excel file on data.govt.nz and visible on Granted.govt.nz; DIA also says its commitment is to make this data ongoing and annual, but I found this cadence only on DIA-origin pages. [DIA class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [Granted.govt.nz](https://www.granted.govt.nz/) | Award/application-history fields parsed from the official Jan-June 2022 workbook: `Final_Organisation_Name`, `Organisation_Type`, `Professional_Fundraiser`, `Conflicts_of_Interest_Information`, `Grant_number`, `Application_category_purpose`, `Grant_Application_Recieved_Date`, `Reason_for_Decline__If_Applicable_`, `Date_of_Grant_Distribution`, `Benefiting_Individuals_Gender`, `Benefiting_Individuals_Ethnicity`, refund fields, `Category_1`, `Category_2`, `Territorial_Local_Authority`, `Region`, `Amount_Granted`, `Amount_Requested`, `Society_Name`, `Year_of_Accept/Decline`, `Status` and `Date_of_Accept/Decline`. [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx) | Not an opportunities feed; the source page, workbook and draft guidance do not expose each society's current eligibility rules, local venue/funding-area rules, opening/closing dates, application forms or legal-status requirements in one structured schema. [DIA class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx); [DIA guidance](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-2025/%24file/Guidance-for-submitting-class-4-grants-data.pdf) | High for parsed workbook columns; Medium for ongoing cadence |
| Community Matters current fund pages: COGS, Lottery Community and related funds | Public official; DIA/Hapai Hapori | Partly: current pages are structured HTML when rendered in a browser, but I found no official current opportunities API or CSV/XLSX feed for fund rules; plain HTTP requests returned Cloudflare 403 challenges during review. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Community Matters Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community) | Browser-rendered official pages expose human-readable eligibility and rules. The COGS page includes 2026 application dates, outcomes, priority groups, the under-$2 million expenditure threshold, informal-group rules, fundholder rules, required profile/document fields, fundable costs and exclusions; Lottery Community includes closed status, regional/national committee routing, informal-group/fundholder rules, supporting documents, multi-year criteria, second-request priority rules and exclusions. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Community Matters Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community) | Need manual extraction into fields for opportunity name, deadline, status, geography/committee, legal entity, amount/term limits, exclusions, documents, application URL, reporting obligations and review cadence. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Community Matters Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community) | Medium; browser-rendered pages verified, no open data feed found |
| Community Matters / data.govt.nz older recipient datasets for Lottery/COGS | Public official metadata; DIA/data.govt.nz | Yes for some historical recipient datasets: data.govt.nz search/rendered metadata lists CSV/XLSX resources for Lottery Grants Board Community Committee, Community Facilities, Health Research and Community Sector Research grant recipients; several catalogue pages were blocked to automated fetches by Incapsula during this review. [data.govt.nz Community Committee grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-community-committee-grant-recipients); [data.govt.nz Community Facilities grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-community-facilities-grant-recipients); [data.govt.nz Health Research grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-health-research-grant-recipients); [data.govt.nz Community Sector Research grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-community-sector-research-grant-recipients) | Recipient-history fields vary by resource; visible metadata identifies recipient datasets by committee/fund and file format, and Community Matters pages link to annual "grants made" records for COGS and Lottery Community. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Community Matters Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community) | Historical recipients do not provide current opportunity eligibility; several data.govt.nz records appear old or fund-specific, and there is no single normalized schema across Community Matters funds in the public catalogue I could verify. [data.govt.nz Community Sector Research grant recipients](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/lottery-grants-board-community-sector-research-grant-recipients) | Medium |
| Wellington City Council funding open data | Public official council open data example | Yes, but stale: data.govt.nz and WCC ArcGIS expose ArcGIS Hub, Esri REST, GeoJSON and CSV resources for WCC funding open data, and the ArcGIS REST item for `FundingDataWCCbyfund.xlsx` reports `modified` 30 April 2018. [data.govt.nz WCC Funding Open Data](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/wcc-funding-open-data); [ArcGIS item](https://data-wcc.opendata.arcgis.com/documents/eef4e55911fe40e39a9ec0c0d2640479); [ArcGIS REST item metadata](https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/content/items/eef4e55911fe40e39a9ec0c0d2640479?f=json) | Application/history fields in the workbook include funding pool name and closing date, financial year, organisation, project/event title, umbrella organisation, location, venue, dates, target age/population, access fields, total project cost, other income, amount requested, granted total, decision, status, outcomes and project website. [ArcGIS item](https://data-wcc.opendata.arcgis.com/documents/eef4e55911fe40e39a9ec0c0d2640479); [data.govt.nz WCC Funding Open Data](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/wcc-funding-open-data) | Useful as one council's historical decisions only, weak for national or current opportunity matching: no cross-council coverage, no standard public schema for all councils, no full eligibility/exclusion rule set per funding pool in the dataset metadata I reviewed, and no evidence from the ArcGIS metadata that the workbook has been maintained since 2018. [ArcGIS REST item metadata](https://www.arcgis.com/sharing/rest/content/items/eef4e55911fe40e39a9ec0c0d2640479?f=json) | Medium |
| Creative New Zealand "other sources of funding and support" page | Public official arts-sector directory | No API or dataset found; it is a curated webpage and says it is not comprehensive. [Creative NZ other funding sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support) | Useful source discovery: it points to Generosity NZ, private trusts/foundations, government agencies and a large list of local-government funding pages. [Creative NZ other funding sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support) | Needs manual crawl/classification of each linked funder; does not expose normalized eligibility, deadlines, amount ranges or application links as structured records. [Creative NZ other funding sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support) | High |
| Generosity NZ / givUS | Private/subscription product | Product database, not public official open data; both the Koha-hosted Generosity NZ platform page and Creative NZ identify Generosity NZ as a funding-information search service, and both describe givUS as a tool/listing for communities, volunteer organisations, clubs or groups. [Generosity NZ platform](https://kohaa.org.nz/); [Creative NZ other funding sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support); [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/) | Likely high-value opportunity metadata for discovery; public pages describe funding-opportunity search but do not publish an open schema or bulk data export. [Generosity NZ platform](https://kohaa.org.nz/); [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/) | Cannot be treated as public official data; licensing, completeness, schema and update cadence would require product access or a partnership. [Generosity NZ platform](https://kohaa.org.nz/) | Medium |
| Fundsorter | Private/product database | Product database, not public official open data; Fundsorter's homepage showed 3,361 funding opportunities, 1,650 funders and 186,469 funding matches when checked on 2 July 2026. [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/) | Public marketing implies structured opportunity and matching data: the homepage describes grant matching and application drafting for organisations in Aotearoa, and the pricing page says a first scan and draft are included. [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/); [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing) | Not public official data; schema, source coverage, update process and licensing cannot be verified without product access. [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/) | Low |

### What public data can support in an MVP

Public official data can support three MVP functions. First, Charities Services can enrich applicant profiles with charity registration, status, activity, beneficiary, sector, area-of-operation, NZBN and annual-return information. [Charities Services open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data)

Second, DIA class 4 and public recipient datasets can provide historical evidence about who has been funded, by whom, for what categories, in what places and at what amounts. [DIA class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [DIA Class 4 Jan-Jun 2022 workbook](https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx); [data.govt.nz WCC Funding Open Data](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/wcc-funding-open-data)

Third, official webpages can seed an opportunities catalogue when a human or controlled scraper extracts fields from the page. COGS and Lottery Community pages already contain the relevant rules in text, including dates/status, eligible organisation types, priority groups, documents, fundable activities and exclusions. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Community Matters Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community)

Public data alone is not enough for a reliable eligibility-matching MVP unless the project creates and maintains a new opportunities schema. The minimum manual schema should include funder, fund name, status, opening date, closing date, decision date, application URL, geography, eligible legal statuses, charity-registration requirement, informal-group/fundholder rules, amount or term limits, eligible costs, exclusions, priority groups, required documents, reporting obligations, source URL, last checked date and update cadence. This conclusion follows from the observed gap between award/applicant datasets and current fund-rule webpages, and from independent sector evidence that funding-source criteria are distributed across many source types rather than a single public data feed. [Charities Services OData metadata](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata); [DIA class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); [Creative NZ other funding sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support); [Centre for Social Impact funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide)

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| The best public official machine-readable grants data I found is mainly historical award/application data, not current opportunity eligibility data. | [DIA says class 4 data covers grants data and applied funds and links a distribution dataset](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data) | [Wellington City Council's open-data example is historical application/decision data](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/wcc-funding-open-data), while [Creative NZ's official arts-sector funding page is a directory rather than a normalized opportunities dataset](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support) | Medium |
| Charities Services open data is important for applicant matching but is not a grants-opportunities source. | [The OData service root and metadata expose charity/register, officer and annual-return-style entities rather than grant-opportunity entities](http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata) | Single-origin official-service claim: I found no independent source that re-documents the exact Charities OData schema, so the confidence rests on direct schema inspection rather than two independent origins. | High for schema inspection; independence flagged |
| A public-data-only MVP would need manual opportunity assembly before it could do reliable eligibility matching. | [Community Matters COGS eligibility, dates and exclusions are on a human-readable fund page](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme) and [Lottery Community rules are another same-origin DIA page](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community) | [Centre for Social Impact describes eight funding-source types, each with its own criteria, priorities and processes](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide), and [Creative NZ says its funding-source directory is not comprehensive](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support) | Medium |
| DIA's class 4 data publication cadence is annual from the 2025-era source page. | [DIA says its commitment is to make this class 4 grants data ongoing and available annually](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data) | Single-sourced to DIA; I did not find an independent release-note source for the cadence. | Medium |
| Private products probably contain richer opportunity-matching data than public official datasets, but their data cannot be assumed reusable. | [Fundsorter describes a grant matching product and reports 3,361 opportunities / 1,650 funders / 186,469 matches on its homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/) | [Generosity NZ describes search tools including givUS for communities and volunteer organisations](https://kohaa.org.nz/) | Medium |

## What would change this conclusion

- A current official API or bulk download for Community Matters funding opportunities, with normalized fields for eligibility, exclusions, deadlines, geography, amount ranges and application links, would change the recommendation.
- A data.govt.nz catalogue/API route that reliably exposes current opportunity records across DIA, councils, gaming trusts and statutory funders would change the conclusion that manual assembly is required.
- Product access or a data-sharing agreement with Fundsorter, Generosity NZ, or another grants database could change the build path, but it would not change the conclusion about public official data unless reuse rights allowed public/open use.
- I could not verify every data.govt.nz resource directly because several catalogue/API fetches returned Incapsula incident pages during automated access; I used rendered search metadata, successful rendered pages and official source pages where possible. Community Matters pages also returned Cloudflare 403 challenges to plain HTTP requests during review, although browser-based checks loaded them.
- I did not audit every council or every class 4 society website, so there may be additional local machine-readable datasets beyond the Wellington City Council example.
- I could not access the data.govt.nz class 4 catalogue/API by plain HTTP because of Incapsula, but I did parse the official DIA-hosted Jan-June 2022 workbook linked from the class 4 data trail; a newer workbook or a data.govt.nz API response with different columns would change the exact schema row.
- A human funder, grants adviser or community-organisation user should review the proposed manual schema before build work, because eligibility language often contains judgement calls that are hard to encode from webpages alone.

## Open follow-up questions

- Which councils besides Wellington publish grants application/outcome data in machine-readable formats, and do any publish current opportunity rules as data?
- Can Community Matters records of grants be converted into a stable, normalized historical-awards dataset by fund and year without violating site terms or creating maintenance risk?
- Which private/product database, if any, would allow low-cost or public-benefit reuse of structured NZ grant opportunities?
- What exact eligibility schema gives the best tradeoff between usefulness for small charities and maintainability by volunteers?

## Sources

2. Charities Services OData. "$metadata." Accessed 2 July 2026. http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata
3. Charities Services OData. "Service root." Accessed 2 July 2026. http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/
4. Charities Services OData. "Organisations JSON example." Accessed 2 July 2026. http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$top=1&$format=json
5. Charities Services OData. "Organisations CSV example." Accessed 2 July 2026. http://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/Organisations?$top=1&$format=csv

8. Department of Internal Affairs. "Class 4 Grants Dataset Jan-June 2022 CSV.xlsx." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/diawebsite.nsf/Files/Gambling-Class-4-Grants-Data-Review/%24file/Class-4-Grants-Dataset-Jan-June-2022-CSV.xlsx
