findings.title: Small NZ charities find grants through a patchwork — a handful of commercial databases (free only via council library or council-sponsored access), funder-hosted link lists, and volunteer labour — and the process breaks down on cost, fragmented access, duplicated paperwork, and capacity
findings.author: mcinteerj
findings.summary: There is no official or universal channel. The discovery layer small charities are actually pointed to consists of commercial databases — givUS (Generosity New Zealand), Fundsorter, Strategic Grants' GEMS/GEM Local, and GrantsGuru — plus a handful of free partial directories and
findings.body:
# Small NZ charities find grants through a patchwork — a handful of commercial databases, funder link lists, and volunteer labour — and the process breaks down on cost, fragmented access, duplicated paperwork, and capacity

## Executive answer

- **There is no official or universal channel.** The discovery layer small charities are actually pointed to consists of commercial databases — givUS (Generosity New Zealand), Fundsorter, Strategic Grants' GEMS/GEM Local, and GrantsGuru — plus a handful of free partial directories and *curated link lists* hosted by funders, peak bodies, and government agencies. The sector's own peak body for funders (Philanthropy New Zealand) is explicit that it is "not a funding organisation" and refers fundseekers to exactly these tools [PNZ Fundseeker](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker).
- **The main free route is a library card.** givUS is subscription-only, but "most council libraries subscribe to givUS on behalf of ratepayers, which enables FREE public access" (vendor's claim, corroborated by library pages and practitioner guides) [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us). This makes free access to the incumbent funding database a function of which council you live under — and practitioners report the library route itself "can take some hunting to track it down" [NZCBA guide](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/).
- **After discovery, the workflow is manual.** The most detailed practitioner account found (a volunteer-written guide for community bands) describes the standard path: assemble a reusable document pack, search givUS at the library, build your own spreadsheet of funders/deadlines/limits, then tailor paperwork per funder — with six-month lead times and two competitive quotes per item [NZCBA guide](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/).
- **The breakdown points are structural, not informational gaps a pamphlet can fix:** (1) paywalls and opaque pricing; (2) council-dependent free access; (3) no open-licensed dataset of NZ grants (data.govt.nz searches return only distribution-side data — §1), so even the databases disagree on how many opportunities exist (from "over 1,200" to 3,361); (4) grant-writing time and skill that volunteer-run groups don't have — the reason Hui E! runs a volunteer grant-writer matching programme; and (5) capacity: 89% of NZ's ~115,770 non-profits have **no paid staff at all** [Stats NZ NPISA 2018](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf).
- **The outcome is highly concentrated:** "Nine per cent of charities receive 91 per cent of philanthropic and grant-maker giving, meaning 91 per cent of charities get 9 per cent" [PNZ/JBWere fact sheet](https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf). Discovery friction is not the only cause, but it lands hardest on the volunteer-run majority.

**Overall confidence:** Medium — the *channel inventory* is well-evidenced (vendor pages, peak-body and funder referral lists, government pages, all current). The *breakdown analysis* rests on a smaller demand-side evidence base: no NZ-wide survey directly measures how charities search for grants (the sector's longitudinal surveys measure funding adequacy, not discovery behaviour — itself a finding).

## Evidence

### 1. What charities are pointed to: the referral canon

The same short list of tools appears wherever a NZ community group asks "where do I find funding?":

**Philanthropy New Zealand (the funders' peak body)** maintains a "Fundseeker" page that refers grantseekers to: Fundsorter, Generosity NZ/givUS ("often available free through public libraries"), Strategic Grants (GEMS), the Fundraising Institute Australasia, and Hui E!'s Hoa Pūtea grant-writing support [PNZ Fundseeker](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker) (accessed 2 Jul 2026).

**Funders themselves host link lists.** The Tindall Foundation's "Other Funding" page is a representative map of the whole discovery landscape: 10 regional community trusts, 13 philanthropic family foundations, 12 government funds and ministries, 11 corporate foundations, 20 community foundations (counts scripted from page headings, 2 Jul 2026), and a closing "Funding Databases & Directories" section listing the DIA gaming-trusts directory (granted.govt.nz/societies), the Energy Trusts of NZ member list, Generosity NZ ("subscription-based, with free access through public libraries"), Fundsorter, the Local Community Trust Directory (localcommunity.org.nz), and Citizens Advice Bureau [Tindall Foundation](https://tindall.org.nz/other-funding-resources/) (accessed 2 Jul 2026). The Department of Conservation [DOC funding page](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/) and [Creative NZ](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support) publish equivalent lists for their sectors.

**Government's own front door is an application portal, not a search tool.** DIA's Community Matters is "a community funding hub for Lottery, Crown and Trust funding" where groups log in to *apply* for the funds DIA administers (Lottery, COGS) [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/); it does not search across the wider funder landscape. Nor does open data fill the gap: reproducible searches of the data.govt.nz catalogue (CKAN API, `package_search`, run 2 July 2026) return only grant *distribution* datasets — `grants` (264 results, all recipient/paid data such as COGS grant records and Class 4 grants paid), `grant eligibility` (7, none relevant), `grant funders` (1, a geology dataset) [data.govt.nz CKAN API](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/api/3/action/package_search?q=grants) — and Charities Services' open data covers charities' own filings, not funding opportunities [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data/). (The companion finding for issue #5, PR #11 — under review, not yet merged — documents this landscape in full.)

### 2. The commercial databases

**givUS (Generosity New Zealand)** — the incumbent, formerly FundView. Vendor describes it as covering "grants and schemes for communities, volunteer organisations, schools, groups, sport clubs and Iwi"; pricing is quote-based ("we generate personalised quotes"), and the vendor states "Most council libraries subscribe to givUS on behalf of ratepayers, which enables FREE public access" [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us) (accessed 2 Jul 2026). DOC's referral page describes it as "over 1,200 grants and schemes" [DOC](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/). Library examples of the free channel: Marlborough District Libraries walk users through accessing givUS with a library login [Marlborough Libraries](https://www.marlboroughlibraries.govt.nz/about-the-library/news?item=id:2f8r17q2v1cxbyg85tk7); Selwyn Libraries lists Generosity NZ among its e-resources [Selwyn Libraries](https://www.selwynlibraries.co.nz/booksandresources/eresources/generosity-nz-funding).

**Fundsorter** — the newer entrant (flagged by a community member on issue #2 as "about a year old"). Its homepage stat block states **3,361 funding opportunities / 1,650 funders** (deterministic extraction from page HTML, 2 Jul 2026; same figures verified for the issue #5 finding) [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/). Entry pricing for the smallest organisations is $16/month billed annually ($192/year) (verified for issue #5, 1 Jul 2026). PNZ describes it as an algorithm that "analyses over 50 factors across nearly 3,000 funding opportunities" and then "with one click, they draft your application" [PNZ Fundseeker](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker). Fundsorter's positioning confirms the incumbent workflow's failure mode from the supply side: "Your current database tells you what you ask it. Fundsorter surfaces what you didn't know to search for" [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/).

**Strategic Grants (GEMS / GEM Local)** — a trans-Tasman grants consultancy whose Grants Expertise Management System provides customised grants calendars, plus workshops and application-writing services [PNZ Fundseeker](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker); GEMS is marketed as "New Zealand's largest and most current grants database" (no counts published) [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/). Its small-organisation tier, GEM Local, is "designed specifically to help New Zealand community organisations and small non-profits find the right grants for them" [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/).

**GrantsGuru and council-sponsored free portals** — GrantsGuru ($228/yr, "database focused on council portals" per Fundsorter's pricing comparison [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing)) and GEM Local both run **council-sponsored instances free to local residents**: Rangitīkei District Council launched a GrantGuru-powered "Grant Finder" in March 2025 [Rangitīkei DC](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents), and Ruapehu District Council sponsors a GEM Local instance accessed with a free code [ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz](https://ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz/). This is a second postcode-dependent free channel alongside library givUS access: whether a small group gets a free grants database depends on what its council has bought. (Also in vendor comparisons: Go Gravy, a success-fee service that "finds and applies for NZ grants on your behalf" [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing) — verified only as described by a competitor.)

Free partial alternatives: localcommunity.org.nz, "an online directory listing a wide range of community funders, trusts, and grantmakers across Aotearoa New Zealand" [Tindall Foundation](https://tindall.org.nz/other-funding-resources/); [localcommunity.org.nz](https://www.localcommunity.org.nz/view/communities/) (URL live, 2 July 2026); and the trust-company portals — Perpetual Guardian's funding hub and Public Trust's grantseeker portal, each listing "current and upcoming funding rounds" only for the trusts that company administers [Tindall Foundation](https://tindall.org.nz/other-funding-resources/); [Public Trust grants search](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/?query=&type=organisation). Public Trust's portal is machine-readable in practice: its public search index returns 69 structured grant records including eligibility/exclusion text (queried directly, 2 July 2026; documented in PR #11).

### 3. The human-labour layer

Discovery tools only get a group to the paperwork. A visible ecosystem of human support exists precisely because the paperwork defeats volunteer-run groups:

- **Hui E! Hoa Pūtea Grant Writing Support** matches skilled volunteer grant writers with "small grassroots groups with a revenue of under $2 million dollars or [that] are fully voluntary run", supporting a group's *first* application of $5,000–$50,000. Its own programme review states the core problem plainly: "Grant writing is time consuming and requires a particular skillset that volunteer and small community organisations do not necessarily have" [Hui E! review](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/) ([review PDF](https://www.huie.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Hui-E-Hoa-Putea-Review-2022.pdf)).
- **Paid grant writers and consultants**: e.g. FundingNZ advertises application-writing across the major gaming trusts and Lottery [FundingNZ](https://www.fundingnz.co.nz/communitygrants); FundingHQ sells a fundraising-capability platform aimed at organisations "looking to raise funds $50,000 or more" (as described by Creative NZ) [Creative NZ](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support), [FundingHQ](https://funding-hq.com/).
- **Training**: Exult runs sector workshops on "Sustainable Funding" for community organisations [Exult](https://www.exult.co.nz/workshops/sustainable-funding-2-hour/).
- **Generalist navigation**: Citizens Advice Bureau appears on funder referral lists as a free information service covering funding access [Tindall Foundation](https://tindall.org.nz/other-funding-resources/).

### 4. The practitioner workflow, end to end

The clearest end-to-end account found is the NZ Community Brass Bands Association's volunteer-written funding guide [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/) (accessed 2 Jul 2026). Its described workflow:

1. Build a reusable document pack (bank verification, financials "preferably audited", charity registration number, incorporation certificate, committee contacts, minutes).
2. Plan **at least six months ahead** ("grant organisations with larger limits generally meet less often").
3. Gather **at least two competitive quotes per item** ("a requirement for most grants").
4. Search givUS — "Unfortunately GivUs and GivMe are both pay-to-access, but there is a way around it. Many councils have subscriptions so you can access it via your local library… though it can take some hunting to track it down."
5. Build your own spreadsheet of funders, closing dates, and grant-size limits.
6. Tailor each application to each funder's criteria — "the tedious part, simply from the perspective of organising slightly different sets of paperwork for each funding provider."

The guide's summary of what the database saves you — "many, many hours of googling and perusing funding criteria" — is the clearest available statement of what discovery costs without one.

### 5. Where it breaks down

**(a) Cost and opaque pricing.** Both national databases are paid products: Fundsorter from $192/year for the smallest organisations; givUS by "personalised quote" with no published price [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us). For fully-volunteer groups, any subscription competes with programme spending.

**(b) Free access is a postcode lottery.** The free route to givUS depends on the local council's library subscribing. The vendor says "most" do [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us); no public list of subscribing councils was found, and the practitioner guide notes the resource is hard to even locate in library catalogues [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/). The same pattern repeats with the newer council-sponsored GrantGuru/GEM Local portals (Rangitīkei, Ruapehu — section 2): free database access exists only where a council has bought it. Which councils' residents get free access, through either channel, is unverified — flagged below as a follow-up.

**(c) No ground truth on coverage or freshness.** Because there is no open-licensed dataset of NZ grants (data.govt.nz CKAN searches return only distribution-side data — §1; documented in full in PR #11), the commercial databases cannot be audited and their own counts diverge: DOC describes givUS as "over 1,200 grants and schemes" [DOC](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/); Fundsorter's analysis published via Hui E! covered "almost 2,000 funding opportunities" [Hui E!](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/); Fundsorter's live homepage now claims 3,361 [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/). Hui E!'s framing of that analysis concedes the baseline: "Up until now, very little public data has been available on what funding is available in New Zealand, and without good data, we can't make good decisions" [Hui E!](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/).

**(d) Time and skill burden on volunteers.** Hui E!'s programme review: grant writing "is time consuming and requires a particular skillset that volunteer and small community organisations do not necessarily have"; supported applicants said external writers freed "so many extra hours" [Hui E! review](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/). This sits inside a worsening macro picture: the 2024 State of the Sector survey (biennial, run by Community Networks Aotearoa/ComVoices) reports "a more competitive funding environment", demand up for 77% of organisations, and respondents saying "Funding is the hardest issue we are facing. Always begging for every cent" [SOTS 2024](https://www.communitynetworksaotearoa.org.nz/state-of-the-sector-survey-2024) ([report PDF](https://irp.cdn-website.com/96a27c73/files/uploaded/State_of_The_Sector_Report_2024_09.10.24_Master_Export.pdf)).
Notably, the survey instrument does not ask *how* organisations find funding: a deterministic search of the published 2024 questionnaire ([SSS24 Questions PDF](https://irp.cdn-website.com/96a27c73/files/uploaded/SSS24_Questions.pdf)) finds funding-*adequacy* questions (Q18–22, Q34) but zero questions on discovery methods, databases, or search tools. The sector's main longitudinal dataset measures funding adequacy, not discovery. No NZ survey of grant-discovery behaviour was found.

**(e) Most of the sector has nobody to do this work.** Stats NZ's Non-Profit Institutions Satellite Account (the most recent edition, 2018) counted 115,770 non-profit institutions and found **89% did not employ staff** — entirely volunteer-run ("in 2018, 89 percent of NPIs did not employ staff. This was 1 percentage point lower than in 2013 (90 percent)"; Table 6: 103,272 NPIs with zero paid employees vs 12,508 employing) [Stats NZ NPISA 2018, Employment numbers + Table 6](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf). (An earlier draft of this finding quoted 73% from the report's *Health activity group* section — corrected after adversarial review; the whole-sector figure is 89%.) Eligibility thresholds in the support ecosystem confirm "small" is the norm, not the exception: COGS requires under $2M annual operating expenditure [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme); Hoa Pūtea targets under $2M revenue or fully-voluntary groups [Hui E! review](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/).

**(f) The observable outcome is extreme concentration.** "Nine per cent of charities receive 91 per cent of philanthropic and grant-maker giving, meaning 91 per cent of charities get 9 per cent" — Philanthropy NZ's fact sheet summarising the JBWere NZ Support Report [PNZ/JBWere fact sheet](https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf). The report itself states "Fundraising is dominated by the largest organisations with the largest 9% of charities receiving 91% of all donations" — JBWere's live URLs have since rotted (404, 2 July 2026), so the report is cited via its Internet Archive snapshot [JBWere NZ Support Report, Feb 2020 (archived PDF)](https://web.archive.org/web/20230406140035/https://www.jbwere.co.nz/media/1qudxw3q/jbwere-nz-support-report-digital.pdf) (stat verified in the archived PDF text). Note this is **one underlying analysis**, not two independent sources — flagged in the claims table. FundingHQ's marketing makes the same point ("the vast majority of available funding goes to less than 10% of charities leaving most under-resourced and at risk" [FundingHQ](https://funding-hq.com/)) — though this likely derives from the same JBWere analysis, so it corroborates currency of the claim, not independence. Concentration has many causes (grant size, capability, relationships); discovery friction is one contributor, not proven to be the dominant one.

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| givUS, the main funding database, is free to most NZers only via council library subscriptions | [Generosity NZ (vendor)](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us) | [NZCBA practitioner guide](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/); examples: [Marlborough Libraries](https://www.marlboroughlibraries.govt.nz/about-the-library/news?item=id:2f8r17q2v1cxbyg85tk7) | High (that the channel exists); Low (that "most" councils subscribe — vendor claim, no public list found) |
| 9% of charities receive 91% of philanthropic and grant-maker giving | [PNZ/JBWere fact sheet](https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf) | **Single underlying analysis** (JBWere, 2020 data) — flagged per method. Primary report verified via [archived PDF](https://web.archive.org/web/20230406140035/https://www.jbwere.co.nz/media/1qudxw3q/jbwere-nz-support-report-digital.pdf) (JBWere's live URL now 404s); no independent replication found | Medium — verbatim in both documents, but one analysis and 2020-era data |
| 89% of NZ non-profits have no paid staff (115,770 NPIs; 103,272 with zero employees, 2018) | [Stats NZ NPISA 2018](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf) ("89 percent of NPIs did not employ staff", Table 6) | [Te Ara](https://teara.govt.nz/en/voluntary-welfare-organisations/page-8) ("90% of not-for-profits employ no paid staff", earlier Stats NZ data — consistent) | High — official statistics, corroborated in direction; note the satellite account has not been updated since 2018. An earlier draft said 73% (the Health activity-group figure) — corrected in review |
| Databases disagree on how many NZ funding opportunities exist (1,200 → 2,000 → 3,361) | [DOC](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/) + [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/) | [Hui E!/Fundsorter analysis](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/) | High that the counts diverge (all three verified 1–2 Jul 2026); the divergence itself is the point — no ground truth exists |
| No NZ survey measures how charities actually discover grants | [SOTS 2024 questionnaire](https://irp.cdn-website.com/96a27c73/files/uploaded/SSS24_Questions.pdf) (deterministic search: zero discovery-method questions) | [data.govt.nz CKAN searches](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/api/3/action/package_search?q=grants) (no discovery/eligibility datasets — queries in §1) | Medium — a negative claim; a survey may exist that this research did not find |

## What would change this conclusion

- **A demand-side survey.** The biggest gap in this finding is direct usage data: everything here shows what charities are *pointed to* and what practitioners *describe*, not measured behaviour (e.g. what % of small charities have used givUS or Fundsorter, or rely on word of mouth). A survey by Charities Services, Hui E!, or a funder would materially strengthen or overturn the breakdown analysis.
- **givUS subscriber and coverage data.** If Generosity NZ published its scheme count, update cadence, and the list of subscribing libraries/councils, points (b) and (c) could be verified or refuted.
- **A newer Non-Profit Institutions Satellite Account.** The 89%-volunteer-run figure is from 2018; the whole-sector trend 2013→2018 moved only one percentage point (90%→89%), so it is likely still broadly right — but a newer edition would confirm.
- **Evidence that concentration is driven by something other than discovery/capability** (e.g. deliberate funder strategy toward fewer, larger grants) would weaken the implicit link between discovery friction and the 9/91 outcome. The JBWere concentration figure also uses 2020-era data.
- **A human with sector lived experience** (a grants advisor, community trust staffer, or small-charity treasurer) reviewing this would catch informal channels this desk research can't see — especially word-of-mouth, funder newsletters, and relationships with local Community Advisors (DIA), which are under-represented in written sources.

## Open follow-up questions

- Which councils' libraries actually subscribe to givUS, and which councils sponsor a GrantGuru or GEM Local portal? (A census of ~67 territorial authorities' library e-resource and grants pages is mechanical and agent-friendly; would turn the vendor's "most" into a map of who has free access and who doesn't.)
- Could a machine-readable open dataset of NZ grant opportunities be assembled from public sources (funder websites, granted.govt.nz, localcommunity.org.nz), and what licence/maintenance model would keep it fresh? (This is Discover #2's third question; the issue #5 finding inventories the raw sources.)
- What does one grant application actually cost a volunteer-run group in hours, and how much of the paperwork is duplicated across funders? (No NZ "common application" initiative was found; measuring duplication would quantify the case for one.)
- What do DIA Community Advisors and community trust staff observe about how groups find them? (Interview-based; needs a human contributor.)

## Sources

All accessed 2 July 2026 unless noted.

1. Philanthropy New Zealand — Fundseeker: https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker
2. Generosity New Zealand — givUS: https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us
3. Fundsorter — homepage (stat block extracted from raw HTML): https://www.fundsorter.com/
4. Hui E! Community Aotearoa — "Funding in Aotearoa – what does the data tell us?" (Fundsorter analysis): https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/
5. NZ Community Brass Bands Association — funding guide: https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/
6. Tindall Foundation — Other Funding resources: https://tindall.org.nz/other-funding-resources/
7. Department of Conservation — Other funding organisations: https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/
8. Creative New Zealand — Other sources of funding and support: https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support
9. Marlborough District Libraries — givUS access: https://www.marlboroughlibraries.govt.nz/about-the-library/news?item=id:2f8r17q2v1cxbyg85tk7
10. Selwyn Libraries — Generosity NZ e-resource: https://www.selwynlibraries.co.nz/booksandresources/eresources/generosity-nz-funding
11. Hui E! — Review of the Hoa Pūtea Grant Writing Support kaupapa: https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/ (review PDF: https://www.huie.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Hui-E-Hoa-Putea-Review-2022.pdf)
12. Stats NZ — Non-profit institutions satellite account: 2018 (PDF, p.32 for staffing; p."NPI counts" for 115,770): https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf
13. Philanthropy NZ — JBWere NZ Support Report fact sheet (PDF): https://primrose-ranunculus-tm9g.squarespace.com/s/PNZ_JB-Were-Fact-Sheet.pdf
14. JBWere — The New Zealand Support Report, February 2020. JBWere's live URLs returned 404 on 2 July 2026; cited via Internet Archive: report PDF https://web.archive.org/web/20230406140035/https://www.jbwere.co.nz/media/1qudxw3q/jbwere-nz-support-report-digital.pdf (announcement page: https://web.archive.org/web/20250322231606/https://www.jbwere.co.nz/latest-insights/philanthropy/the-new-zealand-support-report-february-2020/)

Community Networks Aotearoa — 2024 State of the Sector Survey: https://www.communitynetworksaotearoa.org.nz/state-of-the-sector-survey-2024 (full report PDF: https://irp.cdn-website.com/96a27c73/files/uploaded/State_of_The_Sector_Report_2024_09.10.24_Master_Export.pdf; executive summary PDF: https://irp.cdn-website.com/96a27c73/files/uploaded/CNA_SSS_-_Executive_Summary.pdf)

findings.title: New Zealand has no official register of grant funders — the only published funder count is a vendor's 1,650 (scheme counts range ~2,000 to 3,400 by definition), and eligibility criteria live in commercial databases and funder web pages, machine-readable in places but nowhere open-licensed or national
findings.author: mcinteerj
findings.summary: I found no government maintained register or count of grant funders in New Zealand (searched the data.govt.nz catalogue, DIA, Charities Services and Community Matters — method below). The only funder count published anywhere is a commercial vendor's: Fundsorter's homepage states
findings.body:
# New Zealand has no official register of grant funders — the only published funder count is a vendor's 1,650 (scheme counts range ~2,000 to 3,400 by definition), and eligibility criteria live in commercial databases and funder web pages, machine-readable in places but nowhere open-licensed or national

## Executive answer

- **I found no government-maintained register or count of grant funders in New Zealand** (searched the data.govt.nz catalogue, DIA, Charities Services and Community Matters — method below). The only *funder* count published anywhere is a commercial vendor's: Fundsorter's homepage states **3,361 funding opportunities from 1,650 funders** [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/) (figures read from the live page, 2 July 2026). Scheme/opportunity counts vary by vendor and definition — Fundsorter's own data analysis describes "almost 2,000" *contestable* NZ opportunities as a census (reconciliation below), Strategic Grants' homepage claims "over 3K expertly curated grants" for GEMS [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/), and givUS is described as listing "over 1,200 grants and schemes" [DOC, Other funding organisations](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/). All are vendor figures, not audited.
- **Verified category counts** (each deterministically counted from primary sources, July 2026): **32** non-club Class 4 "pokie" societies — cross-checked across two separate DIA publications (same agency, so a consistency check rather than independent verification) [DIA society list](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites); [DIA quarterly licensing data, March 2026](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-gambling-venue-and-gaming-machine-numbers-quarterly-lists) — **12** statutory community trusts [CommunityNet Aotearoa](https://community.net.nz/resources/nz-navigator-trust/community-trusts), **18** community foundations [Community Foundations of Aotearoa NZ](https://communityfoundations.org.nz/), and the Lottery Grants Board (one funder distributing $311m in 2026/27 via 7 committees [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-grants-board-committee-allocations)). Larger categories — 78 councils, 18 ETNZ energy trusts, private/family trusts, corporate foundations, licensing trusts, iwi funders — are real but **not verified funder-by-funder as grantmakers**, so no overall "floor" count is claimed here.
- **Consolidated eligibility data exists in at least nine places I verified — five paid products and (at least) four free portals — but none is both open-licensed and national.** Paid: givUS (subscription; free public access via most council libraries) [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us), Fundsorter (from $16/month billed annually) [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing), GEMS and its small-organisation tier GEM Local by Strategic Grants [Strategic Grants, GEMS](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/); [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/), and GrantsGuru ($228/yr; also powers free council-sponsored "grant finder" portals) [Fundsorter pricing comparison](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing); [Rangitīkei District Council](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents). Free, partial-coverage: localcommunity.org.nz (region-searchable funder directory), Perpetual Guardian's Funding Hub, Public Trust's Grantseeker's Portal (each trust-company portal covering only the trusts it administers), and council-sponsored GrantGuru/GEM Local instances for local residents. Note: "not open-licensed" is not the same as "not machine-readable" — Public Trust's portal is backed by a publicly queryable search index (see Evidence). This inventory is what was verified, not a claim of completeness; Go Gravy (a success-fee application service, not a database) is also named in vendor comparisons [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing).
- **No open-licensed dataset of grant eligibility criteria was found** (Medium confidence — negative claim; reproducible search method below). Machine-readable eligibility *content* does exist in places — Public Trust's portal exposes 69 structured grant records including guideline/exclusion text via a public search index — but it is partial-coverage, undocumented, and carries no reuse licence. What IS open is *distribution-side* data: Class 4 grants paid [data.govt.nz](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-grants-data), COGS grant records [data.govt.nz search](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset?q=grants), and the Charities Register open data service — whose schema includes fields that identify grantmakers directly (`PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations`, `GrantsAndDonationsMade`; see Evidence) [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data/).
- **Implication for a grant-matching tool** (the decision this informs): eligibility data must be scraped/curated from funder websites or licensed from a commercial aggregator. The open data helps differently: the Charities Register can enumerate grantmaking charities and their scale, and distribution datasets show who actually funds what — useful for validating (or inferring) eligibility, not for reading it.

**Overall confidence:** Medium — category counts and the aggregator inventory are well-sourced and current (High); the total funder count rests on one vendor's figure (Low–Medium); the "no open eligibility dataset" conclusion is a negative claim backed by a documented but non-exhaustive search (Medium).

## Evidence

### No official register; what exists instead

I found no government-maintained register of grant funders: searches of the data.govt.nz catalogue (method below) return grant *distribution* datasets, not funder or eligibility registers, and none of DIA's gambling pages, Charities Services' register pages, or Community Matters' funding pages describe one. The funding landscape is described by the Centre for Social Impact as "eight main types of sources": community trusts, energy trusts, government and council funds, community foundations, gaming trusts, family and individual trusts and foundations, corporate foundations, and licensing trusts [Centre for Social Impact, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide). Total philanthropic and grant spend is estimated at $3.8 billion a year by JBWere's New Zealand Support Report, as cited by Philanthropy New Zealand [Philanthropy NZ, What we do](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/what-we-do).

The nearest thing to a funder count is Fundsorter's live homepage counter: **"3361 Funding opportunities · 1650 Funders · 186,469 Funding matches"** (read from page source, 2 July 2026) [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/). Fundsorter's coverage includes some international funders, so 1,650 slightly overstates the NZ-only count [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/).

**Reconciling Fundsorter's own divergent numbers.** The same vendor publishes a second, much lower figure: its data analysis (mirrored by Hui E!) "analysed almost 2,000 funding opportunities across 37 broad categories" and calls that "a census of all of the contestable funding opportunities in New Zealand" [Hui E!, Funding in Aotearoa](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/) (also at [fundsorter.com/data](https://www.fundsorter.com/data), which resisted automated scraping on 2 July 2026 but is quoted identically by the reviewer of this finding and by Hui E!). Philanthropy New Zealand describes the platform as analysing "nearly 3,000 funding opportunities" [PNZ, Fundseeker](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker). The plausible explanation is scope: ~2,000 *contestable NZ* opportunities versus 3,361 *total tracked* (including international funders and non-contestable schemes) — but the vendor does not state this, so the gap is flagged rather than resolved. **For a "how many" question the honest answer is a range: roughly 2,000 contestable NZ opportunities to ~3,400 opportunities tracked in total.**

Fundsorter's 1,650 is also the only *funder* count published, but not an uncontested "largest database" claim: Strategic Grants markets GEMS as "New Zealand's largest and most current grants database" [Strategic Grants, GEMS](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/) and its homepage advertises "Access over 3K expertly curated grants" and "the most comprehensive grants database in New Zealand" [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/) — a published *scheme* count comparable to Fundsorter's, though still no funder count. Neither vendor's claim can be adjudicated from public data; this finding therefore reports counts per vendor and avoids "largest database" language.

### Verified category counts (deterministic, July 2026)

| Category | Count | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Class 4 gaming societies (non-club, i.e. grant-distributing) | **32** | Two separate DIA publications agree — both DIA-derived and likely drawn from the same licensing records, so this is a cross-check within one authority (DIA is the licensing authority, so its records are also the authoritative universe): (1) DIA's [List of Society Websites](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites) — 32 unique society sites, script-counted; (2) DIA's [Quarterly List of Venues and GMs, March 2026 (XLSX)](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-gambling-venue-and-gaming-machine-numbers-quarterly-lists) — exactly 32 unique `Society Name` values on rows where `Club Type = "Non-Club"`, operating 805 venues (recounted 2 July 2026 with a full XML parse of the workbook's only sheet: 951 data rows = 951 unique venue numbers, of which 805 are `Non-Club`; the remaining 146 rows are 137 unique club societies — `Club Type` values `"Club - Chartered"` 82, `"Club - RSA"` 38, `"Club -Sports"` 16, `"Club - Racing"` 1 — which apply proceeds to their own purposes rather than making external grants). Note for reproduction: the club categories are spelled `"Club - Chartered"` etc., never bare `"Club"`, so an exact-match filter on `"Club"` finds zero rows. The two DIA lists differ by 2–3 names at the margins (e.g. TAB New Zealand appears in licensing data but not the website list), so "32" is stable but the membership churns slightly. |
| Statutory community trusts (1988 trustee-bank origin) | **12** | [CommunityNet Aotearoa](https://community.net.nz/resources/nz-navigator-trust/community-trusts); [Trust Waikato, Community Trusts history](https://trustwaikato.co.nz/about-us/our-history/community-trusts-history/) |
| Community foundations | **18** | [Community Foundations of Aotearoa NZ](https://communityfoundations.org.nz/) ("There are 18 Community Foundations across Aotearoa New Zealand") |
| Lottery Grants Board | **1 funder, 7 distribution committees** | [Community Matters, LGB Allocations](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-grants-board-committee-allocations) — $311,030,630 allocated for 2026/27 across 6 regional committees and 1 national committee. Counted as one funder: the committees distribute one board's funds. |

### Categories that exist but are not verified funder-by-funder

These are deliberately **not** rolled into a total, because membership of the category doesn't prove an entity makes grants:

- **Councils:** 78 exist (11 regional, 61 territorial, 6 unitary) [LGNZ](https://www.lgnz.co.nz/local-government-in-nz/councils-in-aotearoa/). Many run contestable community grant schemes, but I did not verify this per-council; 78 is a ceiling for this category, not a count of council grantmakers.
- **Energy trusts:** ETNZ lists 18 member trusts (script-counted from [ETNZ, Our Members](https://www.etnz.org.nz/our-members/), 2 July 2026), but ETNZ is a lines-company-ownership body — not all members make community grants, and non-member energy trusts exist.
- **Private and family trusts/foundations** (e.g. Tindall Foundation, JR McKenzie Trust, Todd Foundation, NEXT Foundation [Community Foundations NZ, A brief guide](https://communityfoundations.org.nz/latest-news/a-brief-guide-to-the-philanthropic-sector)): the largest un-counted long tail. Many are administered by trust companies — Public Trust runs a Grantseeker's Portal for "the grant-making trusts they administer" and Perpetual Guardian runs a Funding Hub for trusts it administers [CSI, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide). JBWere estimated this giving at ~$300m/year in 2018 [Philanthropy NZ, Trust and foundation giving](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/trust-and-foundation-giving). A Charities Register data pull could count these properly (see below).
- **Licensing trusts, corporate foundations, iwi/hapū funders:** named as categories by CSI [Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide); not enumerated here.

### Where eligibility data lives: the inventory verified (not claimed exhaustive)

**Paid products (5):**

- **givUS** (Generosity New Zealand): "New Zealand's primary source of information about funding for community organisations and contains over 1200 resource schemes" [Wheelhouse](https://wheelhouse.org.nz/funding-assistance/givus/); DOC describes it as "access to over 1,200 grants and schemes" [DOC](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/). Subscription-based; "most council libraries subscribe to givUS on behalf of ratepayers, which enables FREE public access" [Generosity NZ, givUS](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us) — CSI notes library access applies "outside Auckland" [CSI, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide). No public API or data licence found.
- **Fundsorter**: matching + application-drafting service; 3,361 opportunities / 1,650 funders (above). Pricing from $16/month billed annually ($192/yr) for organisations under $40k expenditure, rising to $100–125/month for organisations over $5m (read from the pricing page's plan data, 2 July 2026) [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing). No public API or data licence found.
- **GEMS** (Strategic Grants): "a paid subscription service … a sophisticated database and management system that helps organisations identify suitable grants, track applications, and manage reporting" [CSI, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide); [Strategic Grants, GEMS](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/). Marketed as "New Zealand's largest and most current grants database"; the homepage advertises "Access over 3K expertly curated grants" and "thousands of grant records" [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/) — a published scheme count, but no funder count.
- **GEM Local** (Strategic Grants): a lighter grants-calendar product "designed specifically to help New Zealand community organisations and small non-profits find the right grants for them" [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/). Councils and agencies sponsor branded instances their communities use free with an access code (e.g. Ruapehu District Council [ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz](https://ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz/)).
- **GrantsGuru**: described in Fundsorter's own pricing comparison as a "database focused on council portals. Large number of grants not tested for NZ eligibility", at $228/year [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing). Like GEM Local, it also runs council-sponsored free local portals: Rangitīkei District Council launched a GrantGuru-powered "Rangitīkei Grant Finder" for residents in March 2025, "featuring all government and private sector grants available" [Rangitīkei DC news](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents).

(Also named in vendor comparisons but not a database: **Go Gravy**, "a fundraising service that finds and applies for NZ grants on your behalf, with success-fee-based pricing" [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing) — verified only as described by a competitor.)

**Free, partial-coverage portals (4+):**

- **localcommunity.org.nz** — "lists a wide variety of different community funders and can be searched by region" [CSI, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide); [localcommunity.org.nz](https://www.localcommunity.org.nz/view/communities/) (URL live, 2 July 2026). Free web directory; not machine-readable.
- **Perpetual Guardian Funding Hub** — "lists current and upcoming funding rounds for philanthropic trusts administered by Perpetual Guardian" [CSI, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide). Covers only PG-administered trusts. (The hub URL returned 403 to automated checks on 2 July 2026 — likely bot protection; description relies on CSI.)
- **Public Trust Grantseeker's Portal** — "a search tool for the grant-making trusts they administer" [CSI, Funding guide](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide); [Public Trust grants search](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/?query=&type=organisation) (URL live, 2 July 2026). Covers only PT-administered trusts. **Machine-readable in practice** (verified 2 July 2026): the page config exposes a public Algolia search index (`prod_publictrust_grants`) queryable with the page's embedded search-only key; it returns **69 grant records** with structured fields (`title`, `uri`, `grant_type`, `sectors`, `grants_regions`, `applications_open_now`, `applications_open_date`) and a `sections` field on all 69 records containing structured guideline/exclusion text (e.g. "Applications requesting funding for overseas projects", "Applications not based in or supporting people within funding region"). Undocumented as an API and no reuse licence — machine-readable ≠ open-licensed.
- **Council-sponsored local grant finders** — free GrantGuru/GEM Local instances sponsored by individual councils for their residents (Rangitīkei, Ruapehu — above). Coverage is national-database data filtered to a locality, but access is limited to sponsored districts.

**Everything else:** individual funder websites and PDFs, one funder at a time — e.g. DIA notes pokie grant "application forms are available from societies" individually [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups), and government schemes are described page-by-page on Community Matters (e.g. [Lottery Community](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community)).

### Search methodology for the "no open eligibility dataset" claim

Reproducible searches run 2 July 2026 against the data.govt.nz CKAN API (`https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/api/3/action/package_search?q=<term>`):

| Query | Datasets returned | Relevant to eligibility? |
|---|---|---|
| `grants` | 264 | No — top results are all distribution/recipient data: council "Grant Applications", DIA "Record of Community Organisation Grants Scheme (COGS) grants", "Lottery Grants Board … grant recipients" series |
| `grant eligibility` | 7 | No — citizenship-by-grant processing status, satellite imagery footprints |
| `grant funders` | 1 | No — a geology dataset |
| `funding schemes` | 3 | No — legal aid projections, Companies Office, Lottery recipients |

Also checked: DIA's gambling data pages [Gambling statistics class 4 grants data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data), Charities Services [Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data/), Community Matters funding pages, and the public pages of the aggregators/portals above — none advertises an API, export, or open licence (Public Trust's Algolia index is publicly *queryable* but undocumented and unlicensed, which is why the negative claim is scoped to "open-licensed", not "machine-readable"). **Exclusions:** single-funder application pages and distribution-only datasets don't count as eligibility datasets. **Limits:** no OIA requests were made; vendors were not contacted; the aggregator inventory reflects what these searches surfaced, not a market census; an unlisted or academic dataset could exist unfound.

### What IS open and machine-readable (distribution-side)

- **Class 4 grants data**: "available on Granted.govt.nz which provides easy access to class 4 'pokie' grants distribution data right down to a local level. The dataset can be found as an excel file" [DIA](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data); CSV/XLSX at [data.govt.nz](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-grants-data) (live, 2 July 2026). Recipient, amount, purpose — not eligibility.
- **COGS grant records**: "Record of Community Organisation Grants Scheme (COGS) grants" published by DIA on data.govt.nz (surfaced by the `grants` search above).
- **Charities Register open data**: "intended to be used by software developers", with a published data dictionary and ERD, licensed CC BY 3.0 NZ [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data/); ~28,000 registered charities [DIA, Charities Act overview](https://www.dia.govt.nz/charitiesact-overview). **Grantmaker fields exist in the schema and are populated and filterable** — the OData metadata (`https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata`, retrieved 2 July 2026) includes `PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations`, `OrganisationGeneratesFundsGrantsDonationsToOthers`, `GrantsAndDonationsMade`, `GrantsPaidWithinNZ`, `GrantsPaidOutsideNZ` and `GrantsOrDonationsPaid_LastYear`, and live spot-queries on 2 July 2026 confirmed both `Organisations?$filter=PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations eq true` (returns flagged charities) and `GrpOrgLatestReturns?$filter=GrantsPaidWithinNZ gt 0` (returns dollar amounts) work. **A full census remains an untested inference:** these were spot-checks, not an enumeration — how *completely* the flags are filled across all filings, and whether they reliably separate systematic grantmakers from charities that occasionally donate, was not tested. Any census would also cover only funders that are registered charities (councils, government funds and some statutory bodies would be missed).

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| No open-licensed dataset of NZ grant eligibility criteria exists (machine-readable content exists but is partial, undocumented and unlicensed) | Documented data.govt.nz search (4 queries, method above, 2 July 2026) | None of the nine verified aggregators/portals advertises an API, export or open licence: [givUS](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us), [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [GEMS](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/), [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/), GrantsGuru + four free portals; Public Trust's queryable-but-unlicensed index verified directly (69 records, 2 July 2026) | Medium — negative claim; search documented but not exhaustive |
| The only published *funder* count is Fundsorter's 1,650; scheme counts range ~2,000 (Fundsorter's own "contestable census") to 3,361 (its live counter) to "over 3K" (GEMS) | [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/) (live counter) + [Hui E!/Fundsorter data analysis](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/) ("almost 2,000 … census of all of the contestable funding opportunities") | [Strategic Grants homepage](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/) ("Access over 3K expertly curated grants"; GEMS marketed as "New Zealand's largest") | Low–Medium — all vendor figures, definitions differ and the vendors don't reconcile them; reported as a range |
| 32 non-club Class 4 societies distribute pokie grants | [DIA List of Society Websites](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites) — 32, script-counted | [DIA Quarterly List of Venues and GMs, March 2026](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-gambling-venue-and-gaming-machine-numbers-quarterly-lists) — 32 unique Non-Club societies, script-counted from the XLSX | High — two separate DIA publications agree; note both trace to the same agency's licensing records (a consistency check, not independent sources — but DIA is the licensing authority, so its records define the category) |
| Class 4 grant distribution data is fully open and machine-readable | [DIA gambling statistics](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data) | [data.govt.nz catalogue entry](https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-grants-data) | High |
| Lottery Grants Board allocated $311m for 2026/27 | [Community Matters, LGB Allocations](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-grants-board-committee-allocations) | None found for this year's figure — single official primary source | Medium — official but single-sourced; flagged |
| Grantmaker fields in Charities Register open data are populated and filterable | [Charities Services, Open data](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data/) | OData `$metadata` inspection + live filtered spot-queries on `Organisations` and `GrpOrgLatestReturns` (both returned populated records, 2 July 2026) | High for schema + spot-queries; Medium for full-census feasibility (completeness and grantmaker-vs-donor precision untested) |

## What would change this conclusion

- **Discovery of an open eligibility dataset** — an academic compilation, a council or DIA release, or a Generosity NZ/PNZ data export — would overturn the central finding. The documented search covers data.govt.nz and the major sector sources, but not OIA requests, direct vendor contact, or academic repositories.
- **Vendor figures verified or corrected**: the 1,650-funder and 1,200+/3,361-scheme figures are vendors' own claims. Vendor confirmation of NZ-only counts, coverage overlap between givUS/Fundsorter/GEMS, and update cadence would materially firm up the landscape estimate.
- **A Charities Register census** (filter on `PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations` / `GrantsPaidWithinNZ`) could replace the vendor figure with an open, reproducible funder count for the charitable subset. Spot-queries confirm the fields are populated and filterable (2 July 2026); the census itself — total counts, field completeness, and whether the flags distinguish systematic grantmakers from occasional donors — has not been run.
- **Per-council and per-trust verification** could convert the "unverified categories" (78 councils, 18 energy trusts, licensing trusts, corporate foundations, iwi funders) into counted funders.
- **Could not verify**: whether givUS/Fundsorter/GEMS/GrantsGuru license data for reuse; whether Granted.govt.nz exposes an API (only file downloads confirmed); actual coverage of localcommunity.org.nz; Perpetual Guardian hub content (bot-blocked); Go Gravy beyond a competitor's description; how many councils sponsor GrantGuru/GEM Local instances.
- **No contact was made with any organisation** — desk research only.

## Open follow-up questions

- Run the Charities Register census: how many registered charities have `PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations` set or report non-zero `GrantsAndDonationsMade`, and how complete are those fields? (Directly buildable — open OData API.)
- Do Generosity NZ, Fundsorter or Strategic Grants license their scheme data, and would any partner with a public-good project?
- How many of the 78 councils run contestable community grant schemes, and in what format are criteria published?
- Is the open Class 4 + COGS distribution data rich enough to *infer* de facto eligibility (who funds organisations like yours, where), bypassing stated criteria?
- How many licensing trusts, corporate foundations, and iwi/hapū funders make grants, and where are their criteria published?

## Sources

1. Fundsorter. Homepage (live counters: 3,361 opportunities / 1,650 funders / 186,469 matches). Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.fundsorter.com/
2. Fundsorter. Pricing. Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing
3. Department of Conservation. "Other funding organisations." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/
4. Philanthropy New Zealand. "Fundseeker." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker
5. Department of Internal Affairs. "List of Society Websites." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites
6. data.govt.nz catalogue. "Class 4 Gambling Venue and Gaming Machine Numbers – Quarterly Lists" (March 2026 XLSX). Accessed 2 July 2026. https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-gambling-venue-and-gaming-machine-numbers-quarterly-lists
7. CommunityNet Aotearoa. "Community Trusts." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://community.net.nz/resources/nz-navigator-trust/community-trusts
8. Trust Waikato. "Community Trusts history." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://trustwaikato.co.nz/about-us/our-history/community-trusts-history/
9. Community Foundations of Aotearoa New Zealand. Homepage. Accessed 2 July 2026. https://communityfoundations.org.nz/
10. Energy Trusts of New Zealand. "Our Members." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.etnz.org.nz/our-members/
11. Local Government New Zealand. "Councils in Aotearoa New Zealand." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.lgnz.co.nz/local-government-in-nz/councils-in-aotearoa/
12. Community Matters (DIA). "Lottery Grants Board Allocations." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-grants-board-committee-allocations
13. Community Matters (DIA). "Lottery Community." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/lottery-community
14. Centre for Social Impact. "Funding guide." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide
15. Philanthropy New Zealand. "What we do." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/what-we-do
16. Philanthropy New Zealand. "Trust and foundation giving." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/trust-and-foundation-giving
17. Community Foundations NZ. "A brief guide to the philanthropic sector." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://communityfoundations.org.nz/latest-news/a-brief-guide-to-the-philanthropic-sector
18. Generosity New Zealand. "givUS." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us
19. Palmerston North City Council. "Generosity New Zealand database." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.pncc.govt.nz/council-city/community-funding/generosity-new-zealand-database/
20. The Wheelhouse. "givUS." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://wheelhouse.org.nz/funding-assistance/givus/
21. Strategic Grants. "GEMS." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/ (homepage with "over 3K expertly curated grants": https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/)
21a. Hui E! Community Aotearoa. "Funding in Aotearoa – what does the data tell us?" (mirror of Fundsorter's data analysis: "almost 2,000 funding opportunities … a census of all of the contestable funding opportunities in New Zealand"). Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/ (origin: https://www.fundsorter.com/data)
22. localcommunity.org.nz. "Communities." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.localcommunity.org.nz/view/communities/
23. Public Trust. "Grants search." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/?query=&type=organisation
24. Department of Internal Affairs. "Gambling statistics class 4 grants data." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data
25. data.govt.nz catalogue. "Class 4 grants data." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/dataset/class-4-grants-data
26. Granted.govt.nz. Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.granted.govt.nz/
27. Charities Services. "Open data." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/the-charities-register/open-data/
28. Charities Services OData service. `$metadata` schema. Retrieved 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/$metadata
29. Department of Internal Affairs. "Overview of the charitable sector and the Charities Act." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/charitiesact-overview
30. Department of Internal Affairs. "Funding for Community Groups." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups
31. data.govt.nz CKAN API. Search queries documented in Search methodology. Run 2 July 2026. https://catalogue.data.govt.nz/api/3/action/package_search?q=grants
32. Strategic Grants. "GEM Local." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/ (council-sponsored instance example: https://ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz/)
33. Rangitīkei District Council. "Grant Guru launches for Rangitīkei District residents" (March 2025). Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents
34. Public Trust Algolia grants index `prod_publictrust_grants` (application ID and search-only key embedded in the public grants page config). Queried 2 July 2026: 69 records; fields as listed in Evidence.
35. Charities Services OData service. Spot-queries: `Organisations?$filter=PurposeToGiveGrantsAndDonations eq true` and `GrpOrgLatestReturns?$filter=GrantsPaidWithinNZ gt 0`. Run 2 July 2026. https://www.odata.charities.govt.nz/
