findings.title: New Zealand grant discovery is a fragmented source-location problem, not a small directory cleanup
findings.author: adam91holt
findings.summary: I did not find a single authoritative public register that counts every New Zealand grant funder and every contestable grant opportunity; the most defensible answer separates official lower bounds from vendor reported scale signals. A related count focused finding already covers
findings.body:
# New Zealand grant discovery is a fragmented source-location problem, not a small directory cleanup

## Executive answer

- I did not find a single authoritative public register that counts every New Zealand grant funder and every contestable grant opportunity; the most defensible answer separates official lower bounds from vendor-reported scale signals. A related count-focused finding already covers the wider "how many funders/opportunities" question; this finding adds source-location mapping by source class. [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/), [DIA Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data), [Creative New Zealand, other sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support), [related count finding](./nz-grant-funder-count-and-eligibility-data.md)
- Official public sources already put eligibility and application rules across at least three separate architectures: DIA-administered Lottery, Crown and Trust funding on Community Matters; class 4 gaming-machine societies and Granted.govt.nz; and local council/funder pages linked from Creative New Zealand and council sites. [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/), [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups), [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme)
- A cautious lower-bound count from official pages is dozens of source-owning funders before philanthropic trusts are counted: Community Matters exposes at least 14 named funds or grouped fund families in public navigation and fund pages as at 2 July 2026, DIA lists 32 gaming-machine societies' websites, and Creative NZ's Creative Communities Scheme pushes applications and closing dates to local council websites. [Community Matters COGS page](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme), [Community Matters, Oranga Marae](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/oranga-marae), [DIA, List of Society Websites](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites), [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme)
- The best public commercial benchmarks I found are Fundsorter-derived, not audited public counts: a Hui E! mirror of Fundsorter's funding analysis says Fundsorter analysed almost 2,000 contestable grant opportunities across 37 broad categories; Fundsorter's product page says it has analysed nearly 2,000 grant opportunities across 37 categories; Philanthropy New Zealand describes Fundsorter as analysing nearly 3,000 funding opportunities; and Fundsorter's live homepage displays 3,361 funding opportunities and 1,650 funders. Fundsorter does not publicly reconcile those scopes, so I treat them as Low-confidence funder/opportunity-count benchmarks and Medium-confidence evidence that the scale is in the low thousands rather than dozens. [Hui E!, Funding in Aotearoa](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [Fundsorter product page](https://www.fundsorter.com/product), [Philanthropy New Zealand, Fundseekers](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker), [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/)
- The eligibility data a small charity needs is not located in one field or one site: eligibility, deadlines, regions, purposes, exclusions, forms and reporting rules live on different fund pages, society websites, council websites, trustee portals and subscription databases. [Community Matters COGS page](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme), [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups), [Public Trust, grants](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/), [Perpetual Guardian, grants open and upcoming](https://www.perpetualguardian.co.nz/philanthropy/grant-seekers/grants-open-upcoming/)

**Overall confidence:** Medium - confidence is High that source locations are fragmented, Medium that public commercial signals put opportunity scale in the low thousands, and Low on any deduplicated national funder/opportunity count because the only published funder count I found is vendor-reported and no complete public register was found.

## Evidence

### Official source classes show fragmentation

Community Matters describes itself as "a community funding hub for Lottery, Crown and Trust funding". As a point-in-time lower bound on 2 July 2026, I counted at least 14 named funds or grouped fund families visible across public navigation and fund pages: COGS, Community and Volunteering Capability Fund, Disarmament Education UN Implementation Fund, Ethnic Communities Development Fund, Lottery Fund grouped as a family rather than individual Lottery sub-funds, Racing Safety Development Fund, Safer Communities Fund, Oranga Marae, Norman Kirk Memorial Trust, Viet Nam Veterans and their Families Trust, Winston Churchill Memorial Trust, Chinese Poll Tax Heritage Trust, Pacific Development and Conservation Trust, and Peace and Disarmament Education Trust. This is a navigation/source-location count, not a complete count of current open rounds or Lottery sub-committees. [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/), [Community Matters, Oranga Marae](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/oranga-marae)

Community Matters centralises some deadline information for funds it administers, but the underlying eligibility rules are still fund-specific: the COGS page gives a 2026 application window, 37 Local Distribution Committees with local priorities, an annual operating-expenditure eligibility threshold, eligible informal groups under $10,000, required documents, funded costs, exclusions and reporting links. [Community Matters, COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme)

DIA's class 4 material is a separate source class from Community Matters: DIA says class 4 grants data and applied funds are collected from class 4 societies, made available annually, and exposed through Granted.govt.nz and an Excel dataset. [DIA, Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data)

DIA's applicant guidance for gaming-machine grants sends applicants to individual society websites, says application forms are usually available from societies, CABs or venues, and says applicants must apply directly to the society that operates venues in their area and has an authorised purpose matching the organisation's goals. [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups)

DIA's list of society websites, last updated September 2025, names 32 gaming-machine societies that operate and distribute grant funding around Aotearoa New Zealand. [DIA, List of Society Websites](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites)

Class 4 eligibility is partly statutory and partly society-specific: DIA says licensed class 4 corporate societies must apply net proceeds to authorised purposes, including charitable purposes, non-commercial purposes with community benefits, and promoting, controlling and conducting race meetings; DIA also says each society's website outlines its authorised purpose and venue locations. [DIA, Gambling in Pubs and Clubs](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Gambling-in-Pubs-and-Clubs-%28Class-4%29), [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups)

Creative New Zealand explicitly marks its cross-sector funding page as "not a comprehensive list", and that page points users to Generosity NZ, foundations, societies, trusts, government agencies, local government, awards, fellowships, scholarships, residencies and capability-building sources. [Creative NZ, other sources](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support)

Creative NZ's own Creative Communities Scheme is a concrete example of eligibility data being split between a national programme page and councils: Creative NZ says the scheme is distributed by city and district councils, supports more than 1,800 projects each year, has local council closing dates that vary, and tells applicants to search their council website for the application form, guide and closing dates. [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme)

Local-government source fragmentation is structurally large because New Zealand has 67 territorial authorities, and Creative NZ's council-distributed CCS is only one nationally framed grant programme whose forms and closing dates sit on local council websites. Separate council grant pages also show councils hosting their own local grant rounds across community, arts, events, environment, heritage, sport and wellbeing purposes, but I did not count those local opportunities systematically. [Local Government Commission, About local government in New Zealand](https://www.lgc.govt.nz/about-us/about-local-government-in-new-zealand/), [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme), [Auckland Council, Hibiscus and Bays grants](https://ourauckland.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/news/2025/02/grants-open-soon-for-the-hibiscus-and-bays-community/), [Wellington City Council, Council funds](https://wellington.govt.nz/community-support-and-resources/community-support/funding/council-funds)

Philanthropic and trustee-administered grants add another layer: Community Foundations NZ says there are 18 Community Foundations across Aotearoa New Zealand, Public Trust's grant search says each trust has specific application criteria and processes, and Perpetual Guardian tells grantseekers to register for its Funding Hub and check 2026 application schedules for open and upcoming grants. [Community Foundations NZ](https://communityfoundations.org.nz/), [Public Trust, grants](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/), [Perpetual Guardian, grants open and upcoming](https://www.perpetualguardian.co.nz/philanthropy/grant-seekers/grants-open-upcoming/)

### Opportunity-count benchmarks

The prior count-focused finding for issue #5 found no government-maintained register of grant funders and recorded Fundsorter's live homepage counter as 3,361 funding opportunities and 1,650 funders on 2 July 2026, while noting that Fundsorter's coverage includes some international funders and therefore is not a NZ-only, deduplicated funder count. That finding is the better reference for the funder-count question; this document uses the same benchmark only to explain where count signals live. [related count finding](./nz-grant-funder-count-and-eligibility-data.md), [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/)

A live Hui E! mirror of Fundsorter's funding analysis says Fundsorter analysed almost 2,000 contestable grant funding opportunities available to New Zealand groups across 37 broad categories; its technical notes say the count measures the number of grant opportunities, not dollars available or granted, excludes non-public funding opportunities such as proactive grantmakers, includes a minority of international funding available to New Zealand groups, and may count opportunities in multiple categories where more than one priority applies. Fundsorter's current product page separately says it has analysed nearly 2,000 grant opportunities across 37 categories. [Hui E!, Funding in Aotearoa](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [Fundsorter product page](https://www.fundsorter.com/product)

Philanthropy New Zealand, the peak body for philanthropists, grantmakers, investors and other funders, points fundseekers to Fundsorter and says Fundsorter's algorithm analyses over 50 factors across nearly 3,000 funding opportunities. [Philanthropy New Zealand, Fundseekers](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker)

Fundsorter's live homepage displays broader platform counters of 3,361 funding opportunities and 1,650 funders. The page does not state whether those counters are limited to New Zealand-only contestable opportunities, and Fundsorter does not publicly reconcile the homepage counter with the "almost 2,000" contestable-opportunity analysis. [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/)

Generosity NZ is another coverage benchmark, but it is not a public count of funders or opportunities: it describes itself as the largest digital search facility for funding information in Aotearoa, says givUS lists resource schemes for communities, volunteer organisations and clubs, and says its online search tools provide access to $3.9 billion worth of opportunities. [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/)

Because the Hui E! mirror, Fundsorter product page, Fundsorter homepage and Philanthropy NZ figures all point back to Fundsorter rather than independent enumerations, I treat "roughly 2,000 contestable NZ opportunities to 3,361 total tracked opportunities" and "1,650 funders" as Low-confidence commercial count benchmarks rather than a verified census. They still support a Medium-confidence scale conclusion that publicly advertised opportunity coverage is in the low thousands, which is directionally consistent with Strategic Grants' separate homepage claim of "over 3K" curated grants. [Hui E!, Funding in Aotearoa](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [Fundsorter product page](https://www.fundsorter.com/product), [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [Philanthropy New Zealand, Fundseekers](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker), [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/)

### Where the data actually lives

| Source class | Approximate count signal | Where eligibility and rules live | Deadline / region / purpose location | Confidence |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| DIA-administered Community Matters funds | At least 14 named funds or grouped fund families visible across public navigation and fund pages as at 2 July 2026. [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/), [Community Matters, Oranga Marae](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/oranga-marae) | Individual fund pages and the DIA Grant Management System. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme) | Community Matters fund pages, funding-date pages, fund-specific records of grants and regional committee pages. [Community Matters COGS](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme) | High for source location; Medium for count because this groups Lottery sub-funds and fund availability changes. [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/) |
| Class 4 gaming-machine grants | 32 society websites listed by DIA as at September 2025. [DIA, List of Society Websites](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites) | Society websites, society authorised-purpose statements, DIA rules, and Granted.govt.nz data. [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups), [DIA, Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data) | Society websites for application rules and venue geography; Granted.govt.nz/DIA dataset for past grants. [DIA, Funding for Community Groups](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups), [DIA, Class 4 Grants Data](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data) | High for source location; Medium for society count because it comes from one DIA list. [DIA, List of Society Websites](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites) |
| Creative Communities Scheme and council-administered local grants | 67 territorial authorities nationally; CCS applications go to local councils and closing dates vary. [Local Government Commission](https://www.lgc.govt.nz/about-us/about-local-government-in-new-zealand/), [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme) | Local council websites, application guides and forms. [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme), [Wellington City Council, Council funds](https://wellington.govt.nz/community-support-and-resources/community-support/funding/council-funds) | Council pages for closing dates; Creative NZ for national CCS eligibility frame. [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme), [Auckland Council, Hibiscus and Bays grants](https://ourauckland.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/news/2025/02/grants-open-soon-for-the-hibiscus-and-bays-community/) | High for source location; Low for total local grant-opportunity count because this finding did not crawl all council grant pages. [Local Government Commission](https://www.lgc.govt.nz/about-us/about-local-government-in-new-zealand/), [Creative NZ, Creative Communities Scheme](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme) |
| Philanthropic trusts, community foundations and trustee-administered funds | 18 Community Foundations; Public Trust and Perpetual Guardian host trust-specific grant searches and schedules. [Community Foundations NZ](https://communityfoundations.org.nz/), [Public Trust, grants](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/), [Perpetual Guardian, grants open and upcoming](https://www.perpetualguardian.co.nz/philanthropy/grant-seekers/grants-open-upcoming/) | Individual foundation/trustee portals and trust pages. [Public Trust, grants](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/), [Perpetual Guardian, grants open and upcoming](https://www.perpetualguardian.co.nz/philanthropy/grant-seekers/grants-open-upcoming/) | Foundation or trustee portals, often by region, sector, trust deed or application round. [Public Trust, grants](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/), [Perpetual Guardian, grants open and upcoming](https://www.perpetualguardian.co.nz/philanthropy/grant-seekers/grants-open-upcoming/) | Medium for source class; Low for deduplicated count because trust-company and foundation coverage is partial from public pages. [Community Foundations NZ](https://communityfoundations.org.nz/), [Public Trust, grants](https://www.publictrust.co.nz/grants/) |
| Commercial/subscription discovery tools | A Hui E! mirror of Fundsorter's analysis says almost 2,000 contestable opportunities; Fundsorter's product page says nearly 2,000 opportunities across 37 categories; Fundsorter's homepage displays 3,361 funding opportunities / 1,650 funders; PNZ describes Fundsorter as nearly 3,000 opportunities; Generosity NZ says $3.9 billion worth of opportunities; Strategic Grants says users can access over 3K curated grants. [Hui E!, Funding in Aotearoa](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [Fundsorter product page](https://www.fundsorter.com/product), [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [Philanthropy New Zealand, Fundseekers](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker), [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/), [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/) | Proprietary databases and search tools, with public pages exposing only summary coverage. [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/), [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/) | Inside subscription or product interfaces, not fully auditable from public pages. [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/), [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/) | Medium for broad low-thousands scale signal; Low as independently verifiable funder/opportunity census because exact Fundsorter counts are vendor-reported and unreconciled. [related count finding](./nz-grant-funder-count-and-eligibility-data.md), [Fundsorter homepage](https://www.fundsorter.com/) |

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| New Zealand grant discovery is fragmented across official fund hubs, class 4 society websites, council pages and private databases rather than a single complete public register. | [The related count finding documents searches that found no government-maintained grant-funder register](./nz-grant-funder-count-and-eligibility-data.md) | [Creative NZ says its cross-sector list is not comprehensive and links to multiple source types](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support); [DIA says class 4 applicants need to use society websites and apply directly to the relevant society](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups) | High for fragmentation; Medium for the negative no-register claim because it rests on documented desk search rather than an official statement saying no register exists |
| Published commercial benchmarks put grant-opportunity coverage in the low thousands, not merely dozens, but do not provide an audited census. | Fundsorter-derived public signals: [Hui E! mirrors Fundsorter's analysis of almost 2,000 contestable grant opportunities](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [Fundsorter's homepage displays 3,361 funding opportunities / 1,650 funders](https://www.fundsorter.com/), and [PNZ describes Fundsorter as nearly 3,000 opportunities](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker); these are not independent enumerations. | Independent vendor scale signal: [Strategic Grants says users can access over 3K curated grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/). | Medium for broad low-thousands scale across vendors; Low for exact counts, because the public Fundsorter numbers are vendor-reported and the scopes are not publicly reconciled |
| Class 4 grant discovery requires looking beyond DIA to individual society websites. | [DIA says applicants use society websites and must apply directly to the relevant society](https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups) | [Four Winds Foundation's own grants page publishes eligibility, round timing, local-venue preference, documentation requirements and a portal link](https://www.fourwindsfoundation.co.nz/grants/who-can-apply/) | High for source-location pattern; Medium for the 32-society count because the count comes from one DIA list |
| Council-administered grants create many separate source locations even when the programme has a national frame. | [Creative NZ says CCS is distributed through city and district councils and closing dates vary](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme) | [The Local Government Commission says New Zealand has 67 territorial authorities](https://www.lgc.govt.nz/about-us/about-local-government-in-new-zealand/) | High for CCS source-location pattern; Medium for broader council-grant fragmentation until all council grant pages are crawled |

## What would change this conclusion

- A public, current, deduplicated register of all New Zealand grant funders and contestable opportunities, with source URLs for eligibility, deadlines, regions, purposes and application rules, would change the conclusion from "fragmented source-location problem" to "directory quality and maintenance problem".
- Direct access to Generosity NZ/givUS, Fundsorter, GEM Local, Public Trust, Perpetual Guardian and major foundation datasets would improve or overturn the funder/opportunity-count range, because public product pages expose coverage claims but not auditable records.
- A systematic crawl of all 67 territorial-authority websites, all 32 DIA-listed class 4 society websites, Community Matters fund pages, trustee portals and major philanthropic funders could produce a stronger deduplicated funder count; I did not run that crawl in this finding.
- The class 4 grants dataset on data.govt.nz was blocked by Incapsula during command-line access, although DIA's own page and search index confirm the dataset and Granted.govt.nz exist; a browser-verified download of the Excel file would allow stronger counts of annual applications, approvals, recipient geography and society participation.
- Some eligibility constraints may be embedded in PDFs, online application portals, trust deeds, annual schedules or account-only workflows; those remain unverifiable from public landing pages alone.

## Open follow-up questions

- Which fields would a machine-readable NZ grant-opportunity schema need to represent Community Matters, class 4, council, trustee and philanthropic funders without losing important eligibility nuance?
- How many council-administered grants are currently open across all 67 territorial authorities, and how often do their dates and rules change?
- How many of the DIA-listed class 4 society websites expose application rules in structured HTML versus PDFs, portals or scanned documents?
- What overlap exists between Fundsorter, Generosity NZ/givUS, GEM Local, Community Matters, Public Trust, Perpetual Guardian and council lists?

## Sources

1. Department of Internal Affairs. "Community Matters." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/
2. Department of Internal Affairs. "Community Organisation Grants Scheme (COGS)." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme
3. Department of Internal Affairs. "Class 4 Grants Data." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Gambling-statistics-class-4-grants-data
4. Department of Internal Affairs. "Funding For Community Groups." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Funding-For-Community-Groups
5. Department of Internal Affairs. "List of Society Websites." Last updated September 2025. Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-List-of-Society-Websites
6. Department of Internal Affairs. "Gambling in Pubs and Clubs (Class 4)." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.dia.govt.nz/Services-Casino-and-Non-Casino-Gaming-Gambling-in-Pubs-and-Clubs-%28Class-4%29
7. Creative New Zealand. "Other sources of funding and support." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support
8. Creative New Zealand. "Creative Communities Scheme (CCS)." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/creative-communities-scheme
9. Community Foundations NZ. "Community Foundations NZ." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://communityfoundations.org.nz/

13. Fundsorter. "How Fundsorter Works | AI Grant Matching & Application Drafting." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.fundsorter.com/product

17. Strategic Grants. Homepage. Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/

findings.title: Small NZ charities find grants through a patchwork of paid databases, council-mediated free access, funder lists, and volunteer labour; the strongest documented failures are matching, access, paperwork, and capacity
findings.author: adam91holt
findings.summary: Small New Zealand charities and community groups are pointed to a mixed discovery system: paid or subscription databases such as givUS, Fundsorter, GEMS/GEM Local and GrantGuru; free access mediated by libraries or council sponsored portals; public funder and peak body link lists
findings.body:
# Small NZ charities find grants through a patchwork of paid databases, council-mediated free access, funder lists, and volunteer labour; the strongest documented failures are matching, access, paperwork, and capacity

## Executive answer

- Small New Zealand charities and community groups are pointed to a mixed discovery system: paid or subscription databases such as givUS, Fundsorter, GEMS/GEM Local and GrantGuru; free access mediated by libraries or council-sponsored portals; public funder and peak-body link lists; and human support from grant writers, funder advisers, libraries and sector organisations [Philanthropy NZ](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker), [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us), [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/), [Rangitikei District Council](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents).
- The best documented end-to-end seeker workflow is still manual: gather governance and finance documents, plan months ahead, obtain quotes, search/filter databases, build a spreadsheet of funders and deadlines, then tailor different paperwork for different funders [NZ Community Brass Bands Association](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/).
- The most defensible failure points are not simply "there is no information"; they are that eligibility matching is split across non-open commercial tools, free access depends on local subscriptions, application paperwork varies by funder, and small/volunteer-led groups lack time and grant-writing skill [Hui E!](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/), [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/), [Stats NZ](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf).
- The affected population is large enough to matter: Charities Services reports 29,208 registered charities and 122,608 charity officers in its 2024/2025 sector snapshot, while Stats NZ's most recent non-profit satellite account report found that 89% of non-profit institutions did not employ staff in 2018 [Charities Services snapshot PDF](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf), [Charities Services research page](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/research-into-charities), [Stats NZ report PDF](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf).
- The claim that small groups miss eligible grants because discovery is hard remains plausible, but not proven by public desk research alone; confirming it needs user interviews, library/database access mapping, and application-log or funder-intake evidence [Community Networks Aotearoa](https://www.communitynetworksaotearoa.org.nz/state-of-the-sector-survey-2024), [Hui E! / Fundsorter](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/).

**Overall confidence:** Medium - channel inventory and friction points are supported by current public sources, but direct demand-side evidence from small charities' lived workflow is thin and mostly comes from practitioner guidance, support programmes, vendor positioning and sector surveys rather than a representative grant-seeker study.

## Evidence

### Scope and affected population

For this finding, "small charity" means the groups closest to Charities Services Tier 4: charities with annual operating payments under $140,000, no public accountability and cash-based accounting [Charities Services](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/tier-4), [Charities Services, choosing the right tier](https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/which-tier-will-i-use). The broader affected population includes registered charities and unregistered community groups, because givUS explicitly targets communities, volunteer organisations, schools, groups, sports clubs and iwi, while COGS is open to voluntary and not-for-profit organisations working in local communities [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us), [Community Matters](https://www.communitymatters.govt.nz/community-organisations-grants-scheme).

Charities Services' 2024/2025 snapshot reports 29,208 registered charities and 122,608 charity officers, so the governance and administration burden of grant seeking falls across a large distributed sector [Charities Services snapshot PDF](https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf), [Charities Services research page](https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/research-into-charities). Stats NZ's most recent non-profit satellite account report found that 89% of non-profit institutions did not employ staff in 2018; Volunteering NZ summarises the same Stats NZ release and notes that these figures exclude informal volunteering [Stats NZ report PDF](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf), [Volunteering NZ](https://www.volunteeringnz.org.nz/news-events/news-post/formal-volunteering-valued-at-4-billion).

### What groups currently use or are pointed to

Philanthropy New Zealand tells fundseekers that it is not a funding organisation and directs them to several discovery and support channels: Fundsorter, Generosity NZ/givUS, Strategic Grants/GEMS, Fundraising Institute Australasia, and Hui E!'s Hoa Putea grant-writing support [Philanthropy NZ](https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker). Centre for Social Impact's funding guide similarly lists Fundsorter, Generosity NZ, GEMS/Strategic Grants, Public Trust, Perpetual Guardian and localcommunity.org.nz as places to look for funding information [Centre for Social Impact](https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide).

givUS is a paid Generosity NZ product for communities, volunteer organisations, schools, groups, sports clubs and iwi; Generosity NZ says it generates personalised quotes and that most council libraries subscribe on behalf of ratepayers, enabling free public access [Generosity NZ](https://generosity.org.nz/giv-us). Generosity NZ also publishes "where to access" pages listing partner access points, including council and library entries; this supports a mediated-access model, but not a quantified measure of national coverage or service quality [Generosity NZ, where to find access](https://generosity.org.nz/where-to-find-access), [Generosity NZ, where to access](https://generosity.org.nz/where-to-access-).

Fundsorter positions itself for NZ charities and community groups that need matching and application drafting: its homepage says it helps users find grants and draft applications, its pricing page says plans start from $16/month, and its product copy targets both the experienced grants manager who wants broader coverage and the community leader who does not know where to start [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [Fundsorter pricing](https://www.fundsorter.com/pricing). Because Fundsorter is a vendor, I treat its claim that it surfaces opportunities users did not know to search for as corroborating product evidence of the search problem, not as independent proof that small groups miss grants [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/).

Strategic Grants' GEM Local is designed for small New Zealand charitable and community organisations, provides a customised grants calendar matched by activity, geography and legal status, tracks applications and report deadlines, and says its research team updates NZ grants daily [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/). Council-sponsored portals create another free-but-local route: Ruapehu District Council provides eligible local community groups with free access to GEM Local, and Rangitikei District Council launched a free GrantGuru-powered grant finder for local residents, groups and businesses [Ruapehu GEM Local](https://ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz/), [Rangitikei District Council](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents).

Funder and sector link lists remain part of the workflow. The Tindall Foundation maintains an "other funding resources" page that points users to community trusts, government funds, corporate foundations, community foundations and funding databases, while DOC and Creative NZ each publish other-funder lists for their sectors [Tindall Foundation](https://tindall.org.nz/other-funding-resources/), [Department of Conservation](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/), [Creative New Zealand](https://creativenz.govt.nz/funding-and-support/all-opportunities/other-sources-of-funding-and-support).

### Documented workflow evidence

The clearest public seeker-side workflow I found is the NZ Community Brass Bands Association guide, which is a practitioner guide rather than a representative survey [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/). It advises groups to gather bank verification, financial statements, charity and incorporation documents, committee details and minutes; to obtain at least two competitive quotes because many funders require them; to use givUS via a local library where possible; to record funders, website links, deadlines and grant limits in a spreadsheet; and to tailor applications to different funders' criteria and paperwork [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/).

That guide also identifies high-friction moments in plain workflow terms: finding the right place to apply is daunting because criteria vary, givUS may require hunting through a library catalogue, database filtering saves many hours of manual searching, and multiple applications require slightly different paperwork for each provider [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/). This is direct evidence of the process small groups may follow, but it is one sector-specific guide, so it should be treated as a strong lead rather than a population-level measure [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/).

Hui E!'s Hoa Putea Grant Writing Support kaupapa is indirect but important workflow evidence because it exists to solve the application-capability gap for small grassroots or fully voluntary groups; Hui E! says the programme supports first applications of $5,000-$50,000 for groups under $2 million revenue or fully voluntary groups, and its review says grant writing is time consuming and requires skills that volunteer and small community organisations do not necessarily have [Hui E!](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/).

### Where the workflow breaks down

**1. Matching appears fragmented and non-open, but the seeker impact is an inference to test.** Fundsorter says it has 3,361 opportunities and 1,650 funders on its homepage, its separate data analysis describes almost 2,000 contestable opportunities, DOC says givUS has over 1,200 grants and schemes, and GEMS markets itself as a comprehensive grants database without publishing comparable counts [Fundsorter](https://www.fundsorter.com/), [Hui E! / Fundsorter](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [Department of Conservation](https://www.doc.govt.nz/get-involved/funding/other-funding-organisations/), [Strategic Grants](https://www.strategicgrants.co.nz/gems/). These product counts are vendor or directory claims and are not directly comparable; they support a hypothesis that grantseekers face coverage and freshness uncertainty, but public desk research did not prove how often that uncertainty causes missed grants [Hui E! / Fundsorter](https://www.huie.org.nz/funding-in-aotearoa-what-does-the-data-tell-us/), [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/).

**2. Free access depends on local intermediaries.** givUS is free to many users because council libraries subscribe, and Generosity NZ's access pages point users to partner councils and libraries rather than to an open national dataset [Generosity NZ](https://www.generosity.org.nz/giv-us), [Generosity NZ, where to find access](https://generosity.org.nz/where-to-find-access). Council-sponsored GrantGuru or GEM Local instances show the same pattern: free database access exists for Rangitikei or Ruapehu users because those councils bought or partnered for it, not because the data is universally open [Rangitikei District Council](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents), [Ruapehu GEM Local](https://ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz/).

**3. Application work remains manual after discovery.** NZCBA's guide tells groups to build their own spreadsheet, print or upload repeated documents, monitor expected response dates, and handle follow-through requirements after funds are granted [NZCBA](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/). GEM Local's features - application tracking, report-deadline tracking, deadline alerts, tip sheets, videos and writing samples - are also evidence that discovery alone does not complete the grant workflow [GEM Local](https://www.gemlocal.co.nz/).

**4. Volunteer capacity is a binding constraint.** Hui E! explicitly frames grant writing as time-consuming skilled work that small and volunteer organisations may lack, and Stats NZ found that most non-profit institutions had no paid staff in 2018 [Hui E!](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/), [Stats NZ report PDF](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf). Community Networks Aotearoa's 2024 State of the Sector report states that 77% of surveyed organisations had experienced increased demand for services or activities; NZ Navigator Trust's summary repeats the same report figure, so this is single-origin survey evidence rather than independent corroboration [Community Networks Aotearoa report PDF](https://irp.cdn-website.com/96a27c73/files/uploaded/State_of_The_Sector_Report_2024_-_Community_Networks_Aotearoa.pdf), [NZ Navigator Trust](https://nzntrust.org.nz/news/state-of-the-sector-2024-report).

**5. The missing evidence is behavioural, not just documentary.** Public sources show the tools and support ecosystem, but I did not find a representative NZ survey asking small charities which grant-discovery channels they use, how many eligible grants they miss, or where they abandon the process [Community Networks Aotearoa](https://www.communitynetworksaotearoa.org.nz/state-of-the-sector-survey-2024), [Community Research](https://communityresearch.org.nz/research/state-of-volunteering-in-aotearoa-new-zealand-report-2024/). That gap means the strongest conclusion is "these are the highest-friction steps to test", not "these steps have been quantified nationally" [docs/METHOD.md](../../../docs/METHOD.md).

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Most non-profit institutions had no paid staff in 2018, making volunteer capacity central to grant-discovery hypotheses. | [Stats NZ NPISA 2018 report PDF](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf) | [Volunteering NZ summary of the same Stats NZ release](https://www.volunteeringnz.org.nz/news-events/news-post/formal-volunteering-valued-at-4-billion) | High for the 2018 figure from one official statistical source; Medium for current conditions because the second source is derivative and NPISA has not been updated since 2018. |
| Free access to major grant databases is mediated by local institutions rather than universal open access. | [Generosity NZ givUS](https://www.generosity.org.nz/giv-us) and [where to find access](https://generosity.org.nz/where-to-find-access) | [Ruapehu GEM Local](https://ruapehudc.gemlocal.co.nz/) and [Rangitikei GrantGuru](https://www.rangitikei.govt.nz/news/2025/grant-guru-launches-for-rangitikei-district-residents) | High that this model exists; Medium-Low on the share and practical quality of access because no coverage audit was found. |
| Matching is a high-friction step, not just application writing. | [Fundsorter product positioning](https://www.fundsorter.com/) | [NZCBA practitioner guide](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/) | Medium; one source is a vendor and one is a sector-specific practitioner guide. |
| Small groups may miss eligible grants because discovery is hard. | [NZCBA describes hours of manual searching saved by givUS](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/) | [Fundsorter says it surfaces opportunities users did not know to search for](https://www.fundsorter.com/) | Low; plausible and supported by workflow evidence, but not directly measured by public NZ seeker data found in this pass. |
| Grant-writing skill and time are a real barrier for small or voluntary groups. | [Hui E! Hoa Putea review page](https://www.huie.org.nz/review-of-the-hoa-putea-grant-writing-support-kaupapa/) | [NZCBA guide describes document, quote, spreadsheet and tailoring workload](https://nzcba.org/resources/funding/) | Medium-High; direct programme and practitioner evidence, but not nationally quantified. |

## What would change this conclusion

- A representative survey of small NZ charities and community groups asking which tools they use, how they heard about them, what they pay, and where they abandon applications would move the workflow findings from Medium to High confidence.
- A council-by-council access audit of givUS, GEM Local and GrantGuru would test whether local mediation is a major barrier or a minor edge case.
- Funder application logs showing many incomplete or abandoned applications at eligibility, document-gathering or submission stages would identify the highest-friction step more directly than vendor and practitioner descriptions.
- Interviews with Tier 4 charity officers, library staff, DIA Community Advisors, community trust staff and Hui E! grant writers are needed to confirm whether groups miss grants because they never discover them, misread eligibility, run out of time, or decide the likely grant is not worth the effort.
- I found Generosity NZ partner access pages, but could not verify an open coverage audit showing which councils provide givUS, GEM Local or GrantGuru access, whether access is remote or in-library only, and whether non-ratepayers can use it. I also could not verify a public NZ dataset that measures grant-discovery behaviour by small charities.

## Open follow-up questions

- Which councils and libraries provide free givUS access, what kind of access do they provide, and which councils sponsor GEM Local or GrantGuru portals?
- What is the median time cost of one grant application for a volunteer-led group, split by discovery, eligibility checking, document gathering, writing, submission and reporting?
- Which funder eligibility fields are hardest for small groups to interpret: geography, legal status, donee status, charitable purpose, project type, contribution limits, or quote/reporting rules?
- Do small groups under-apply because they do not discover grants, because they discover them too late, or because the application/reporting burden exceeds the expected value?

## Sources

All sources accessed 2 July 2026.

1. Charities Services, "The charitable sector's year in numbers 2024/2025": https://www.charities.govt.nz/__data/assets/pdf_file/0017/102617/Charities-Sector-Snapshot-2024_2025.pdf

3. Charities Services, "Choosing the right tier": https://www.charities.govt.nz/reporting-standards/which-tier-will-i-use
4. Charities Services, "Research into charities": https://www.charities.govt.nz/charities-in-new-zealand/research-into-charities
5. Stats NZ, "Non-profit institutions satellite account: 2018": https://www.stats.govt.nz/reports/non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/
6. Stats NZ, "Non-profit institutions satellite account: 2018" PDF: https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Reports/Non-profit-institutions-satellite-account-2018/non-profit-institution-satellite-account-2018.pdf
7. Volunteering NZ, "Formal volunteering valued at $4 billion": https://www.volunteeringnz.org.nz/news-events/news-post/formal-volunteering-valued-at-4-billion
8. Philanthropy New Zealand, "Fundseeker": https://www.philanthropy.org.nz/fundseeker
9. Centre for Social Impact, "Funding guide": https://www.centreforsocialimpact.org.nz/knowledge-base/funding-guide

findings.title: NZ small-credit transparency should be framed as a total-cost help front door, not a flat fee comparison
findings.author: Codex
findings.summary: The core problem is real enough to research as a stream: small consumer credit in New Zealand spans personal loans, overdrafts, credit cards, BNPL, and high cost credit, and the law treats those products differently enough that a plain fee table would risk misleading people [Cons
findings.body:
# NZ small-credit transparency should be framed as a total-cost help front door, not a flat fee comparison

> **Scope note:** this is the stream-framing output for Discover root [#60](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/60) — a Discover-stage framing memo that maps the landscape and fans out the stream's research children, following the same pattern as the merged stream #61 framing. It is not a leaf research finding; the evidence bearing on each fanned-out question lives in the children (#67–#71).

## Executive answer

- The core problem is real enough to research as a stream: small consumer credit in New Zealand spans personal loans, overdrafts, credit cards, BNPL, and high-cost credit, and the law treats those products differently enough that a plain fee table would risk misleading people [Consumer Protection](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do), [MBIE](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later), [FMA changes to credit laws](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/).
- The right framing is "total cost of credit for realistic scenarios, plus eligibility and risk notes, plus a route to human help", because the CCCFA covers lender disclosure, responsible lending, reasonable fees, and high-cost-credit limits, while MoneyTalks is a public financial-mentoring front door operated by FinCap and funded by MSD through its Building Financial Capability work [Consumer Protection](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do), [FMA changes to credit laws](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/), [MSD](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html), [FinCap about MoneyTalks](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks/about).
- The regulatory landscape changed on 1 July 2026, the day before this stream was framed: the FMA took over regulatory responsibility for the CCCFA from the Commerce Commission, becoming the single conduct regulator for financial markets including consumer credit [FMA CCCFA](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/), [Commerce Commission transfer notice](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/transfer-of-responsibility-for-regulation-of-consumer-credit/). Any regulator-facing output from this stream now has one primary desk: the FMA.
- Regulator-facing context (not an endorsement of this tool type): MBIE records the Commerce Commission's 2024 personal-banking market study as finding that New Zealand's four largest banks do not face strong competition in personal banking services and as making 14 recommendations to promote competition [MBIE banking study](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/competition-regulation-and-policy/market-studies/competition-study-into-personal-banking-services). That study is about competition between large banks, not about consumer credit comparison tools — it establishes that competition and consumer information are active regulatory concerns, but no regulator has specifically called for the artifact this stream contemplates. CoFI fair conduct programmes are obligations on financial institutions, cited here only as context for how conduct regulation frames consumer treatment [FMA CoFI report](https://www.fma.govt.nz/assets/Reports/Fair-conduct-programme-insights-report.pdf).
- The stream should fan out before ideation: I opened five research children on total-cost scenarios, disclosure comparability, regulatory/advice boundaries, vulnerable-borrower impacts, and data/referral requirements [#67](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/67), [#68](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/68), [#69](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/69), [#70](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/70), [#71](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/71).

**Overall confidence:** Medium — the regulatory ownership and public-source landscape are clear from official sources, but this stream-framing memo does not yet calculate product-level total costs, does not validate user needs with borrowers, financial mentors, lenders, or regulators, and rests on an unvalidated demand assumption (see "What would change this conclusion").

## Evidence

### Problem statement and affected users

The discover issue frames the problem as the difficulty of seeing the all-in cost of small borrowing across personal loans, overdrafts, credit cards, BNPL, and high-cost or short-term loans, with particular risk for lower-income households and people under financial pressure [issue #60](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/60). Stats NZ reported that 9.1 percent of people lived in households in material hardship in the year ended June 2025, and its companion child-poverty release for the same period reported 14.3 percent of children living in households in material hardship, which supports prioritising clarity for people with little room to absorb extra borrowing costs [Stats NZ household income](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/household-income-and-housing-cost-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/), [Stats NZ child poverty](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/). Both figures come from the same Stats NZ survey programme, so this is a single-origin official statistic rather than two independently verified numbers; that is flagged in the load-bearing claims table below. MoneyTalks is funded through Building Financial Capability to provide free advice to individuals and whanau who want to improve financial wellbeing, and MSD says trained financial mentors can connect people with local BFC providers [MSD](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html); FinCap's own MoneyTalks page confirms the service is "operated by FinCap and funded by the Ministry of Social Development (MSD)" [FinCap about MoneyTalks](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks/about).

### Product scope

Consumer Protection says CCCFA responsible-lending rules apply to providers of mortgages, loans, agreed overdrafts, BNPL accounts, buying on credit, truck shops, consumer leases, and home buy-backs, and that responsible lending covers advertising, loan agreements, affordability assessments, and limits on interest and fees [Consumer Protection](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do). RBNZ publishes lending and monetary statistics that include bank and non-bank sector lending, credit-card balances and average interest rates, credit-card spending, and credit conditions, so aggregate official data exists for some parts of the small-credit landscape but not for every product-level fee or eligibility rule [RBNZ](https://www.rbnz.govt.nz/statistics/series/lending-and-monetary).

BNPL needs separate treatment from ordinary interest-bearing loans because MBIE says BNPL lets consumers access goods or services now and pay later in interest-free instalments, and because BNPL providers were not automatically required to comply with CCCFA rules before the 2024 changes when they did not charge interest, credit fees, or take a security interest [MBIE](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later). The FMA's credit-law timeline confirms that BNPL loans were declared consumer credit contracts from 2 September 2024, making BNPL lenders subject to creditor obligations under the CCCFA subject to some exemptions [FMA changes to credit laws](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/).

High-cost credit also needs separate treatment because the CCCFA itself caps the recoverable rate of charge under a high-cost consumer credit contract at 0.8 percent per day (s 45H), limits total recoverable costs of borrowing to the amount of the first advance (s 45E), prohibits compound interest (s 45I), and presumes default fees above the prescribed amount — $30 if no regulations set another figure — to be unreasonable unless the lender proves otherwise (s 45J) [CCCFA s 45H rate cap](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/#LMS352922), [CCCFA s 45E total cost cap](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/#LMS347730), [CCCFA s 45J default fees](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/#LMS347744). The FMA's credit-law timeline summarises the same high-cost rules: a 0.8 percent per day rate cap, a total cost cap of 100 percent of the first advance, a compound-interest ban, and default fees exceeding $30 considered unreasonable unless the lender can prove otherwise [FMA changes to credit laws](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/).

### Current information map

The public information landscape appears to have at least four layers that need separate provenance: regulator and consumer-protection guidance, statutory rules, lender-owned product pages or disclosure documents, and third-party calculators or comparators. Consumer Protection summarises lender duties for borrowers, NZ Legislation publishes the CCCFA text, and RBNZ publishes aggregate credit statistics [Consumer Protection](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do), [NZ Legislation](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/), [RBNZ](https://www.rbnz.govt.nz/statistics/series/lending-and-monetary). Lender-owned examples show that rates, fees, eligibility, and terms can sit in different places: ASB's personal-loan page gives an example total interest cost and says assessment considers regular income, account operation, and credit history, while ANZ's personal-loan rates page states a fixed-rate range, default-rate treatment, and rate-change caveats [ASB](https://www.asb.co.nz/personal-loans), [ANZ](https://www.anz.co.nz/rates-fees-agreements/personal-loans/).

Public education and comparison material already exists: Sorted's personal-loans guide tells users to add up total costs and notes that many personal loans have establishment or documentation fees [Sorted](https://sorted.org.nz/guides/tackling-debt/personal-loans/), and commercial comparison sites (MoneyHub's personal-loans guide is one example, treated here as a lead rather than a formal source because it is an affiliate-revenue commercial site) list rates and fees across lenders. Whether this existing material is *sufficient* for the target cohort is a hypothesis inherited from the parent issue's scoping steer, not something this memo has evidence for: the strongest counter-hypothesis to this stream is that Sorted plus commercial comparators already serve the need, and the gap is distribution or trust rather than a missing artifact. Documenting specifically where (and whether) existing tools fail money-tight borrowers is assigned to [#70](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/70), and demand-side evidence on whether this cohort would use any comparison tool before borrowing is assigned to [#71](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/71) and [#69](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/69).

### Regulatory desks and product guardrails

The primary regulatory desk changed on 1 July 2026. From that date the FMA holds regulatory responsibility for the CCCFA — disclosure, responsible lending, fees, and high-cost-credit rules — becoming the single conduct regulator for financial markets, including consumer credit; the transfer was enacted under the Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Amendment Act 2026, and lender certification has been replaced by market-services licensing under the Financial Markets Conduct Act [FMA CCCFA](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/), [Commerce Commission transfer notice](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/transfer-of-responsibility-for-regulation-of-consumer-credit/). The Commerce Commission's role in consumer credit is now historical: it administered the CCCFA up to 30 June 2026 (including the credit-law changes summarised above) and ran the 2024 personal-banking market study, and its website now directs credit queries to the FMA [Commerce Commission transfer notice](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/transfer-of-responsibility-for-regulation-of-consumer-credit/). FMA was already the conduct desk for CoFI: its fair conduct programme report says CoFI requires financial institutions to be licensed by FMA and to establish, implement, maintain, and comply with an effective fair conduct programme designed to ensure compliance with the fair conduct principle [FMA CoFI report](https://www.fma.govt.nz/assets/Reports/Fair-conduct-programme-insights-report.pdf). MBIE remains the policy desk because it publishes consumer-credit policy material including the BNPL reforms and the personal-banking market-study government response page [MBIE BNPL](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later), [MBIE banking study](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/competition-regulation-and-policy/market-studies/competition-study-into-personal-banking-services). This consolidation materially simplifies the stream's regulator-facing framing — CCCFA, CoFI, and the financial-advice regime now sit with one regulator — and child issue [#68](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/68) must work from the post-transfer allocation.

The proposed AI "reality-check" layer must stay on the information-and-education side until a qualified human confirms the advice boundary. The Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 provides that a person must not act as a provider of a financial advice service without holding, or being authorised under, a market services licence covering that service (s 388(ba)) [FMC Act s 388](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2013/69/en/latest/#DLM4091564). FMA guidance says the same for its licensing regime: a person providing financial advice to retail clients must hold a FAP licence or be an authorised body under someone else's licence, financial advisers must be registered, and nominated representatives have less discretion and can provide advice only under provider processes and controls [FMA FAP](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/financial-advice-provider/), [FMA adviser](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/financial-adviser/).

### Recommended research fan-out

I opened the following research children under stream #60:

| Issue | Research question | Why this is chunky enough |
|---|---|---|
| [#67](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/67) | Total cost scenarios for small consumer credit in NZ | Requires scenario design, product selection, and current rate/fee calculations from public sources. |
| [#70](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/70) | Where NZ small-credit costs, fees, and criteria are disclosed today | Requires source mapping, freshness checks, and comparability analysis across product types — including whether existing tools (Sorted, commercial comparators) already cover the gap. |
| [#68](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/68) | CCCFA, CoFI, and financial-advice boundaries for a credit transparency tool | Requires legal/regulatory source reading and explicit advice-boundary caveats, under the post-1-July-2026 single-regulator allocation. |
| [#71](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/71) | Which credit fees and criteria most affect vulnerable NZ borrowers | Requires hardship evidence, fee-impact evidence, demand-side evidence on borrowing behaviour under pressure, and careful confidence marking. |
| [#69](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/69) | Data and partner requirements for an NZ credit help front door | Requires product requirements, provenance/update rules, referral guardrails, and NFP co-design questions. |

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regulatory responsibility for the CCCFA transferred from the Commerce Commission to the FMA on 1 July 2026, making the FMA the single conduct regulator including for consumer credit. | [FMA CCCFA](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/) | [Commerce Commission transfer notice](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/transfer-of-responsibility-for-regulation-of-consumer-credit/) | High |
| BNPL should not be flattened into ordinary personal-loan comparison because its fee/interest structure and CCCFA treatment changed only from 2 September 2024. | [MBIE](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later) | [FMA changes to credit laws](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/) | High |
| High-cost consumer credit needs separate scenario treatment because NZ law has specific caps and restrictions for that product class (0.8%/day rate cap, total cost cap, compound-interest ban, $30 default-fee presumption). | [CCCFA ss 45E–45J](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/#LMS352922) | [FMA changes to credit laws](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/) | High |
| A reality-check feature that becomes personalised product advice may cross into regulated financial-advice territory. | [FMC Act s 388](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2013/69/en/latest/#DLM4091564) | [FMA FAP](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/financial-advice-provider/) | Medium |
| Human-help referral is central, not ornamental, because MoneyTalks is publicly funded (MSD, Building Financial Capability) to provide free financial mentoring access and connect people to local BFC providers. | [MSD](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html) | [FinCap about MoneyTalks](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks/about) | High |
| 9.1 percent of people in NZ lived in households in material hardship in the year ended June 2025 — the figure used to justify prioritising financially vulnerable users. | [Stats NZ household income](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/household-income-and-housing-cost-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/) | Single origin — flagged. Stats NZ is the sole official producer of this statistic; secondary citations (e.g. the companion [child-poverty release](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/), 14.3% of children) trace to the same survey programme, so no genuinely independent second source exists. | Medium — official but single-origin; high confidence only that Stats NZ published the figure |

## What would change this conclusion

- This framing would weaken if child research finds that product-level public disclosures are too unstable, too licence-restricted, or too non-standardised to support defensible total-cost scenarios.
- This framing would change if the FMA, MBIE, a qualified NZ financial-services lawyer, or a licensed FAP concludes that the intended reality-check layer cannot be safely delivered as general information and education.
- This framing would change if financial mentors or hardship-service providers say a comparison front door would distract from better first-line interventions, or if they do not want referral/warm-handoff integration.
- **Unvalidated demand assumption:** this memo cites no behavioural evidence that money-tight borrowers would consult a transparency tool *before* borrowing rather than after; people in acute hardship often borrow out of necessity, not after comparison-shopping. If [#71](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/71) or NFP co-design under [#69](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/69) finds the cohort would not use a pre-borrowing front door, the framing fails regardless of how good the total-cost data is.
- **Existing-tools counter-hypothesis:** if [#70](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/70) finds that Sorted, commercial comparators (MoneyHub, Canstar, interest.co.nz), or lender disclosure already adequately serve the target cohort, the case for a new artifact weakens to a distribution/trust problem rather than an information-gap problem.
- I could not verify current product-level costs across enough lenders to recommend any named comparison set; that belongs in [#67](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/67) and [#70](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/70).
- I did not contact regulators, lenders, borrowers, MoneyTalks, FinCap, or other NFPs; human validation is required before the stream moves from research into ideation.

## Open follow-up questions

- [#67](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/67) — What consumer-credit products do money-tight New Zealand borrowers actually use for small borrowing needs, and what is the realistic total cost of credit for a small set of borrower scenarios across genuinely comparable product types?
- [#70](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/70) — Where is cost, fee, term, and eligibility information for small personal/consumer credit disclosed today in New Zealand, how current is it, and how comparable is it across product types?
- [#68](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/68) — What do the CCCFA, CoFI fair-conduct regime, and financial-advice rules require or constrain for a small-credit transparency and education tool in New Zealand?
- [#71](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/71) — Which fees, charges, product structures, and eligibility criteria in small consumer credit appear to fall hardest on vulnerable New Zealand borrowers, and what is the evidence on scale and impact?
- [#69](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/69) — What would a credible NZ small-credit transparency, education, and human-help front door need to show, what public/licensed data would it require, and how could referral or warm-handoff to community financial mentoring work?

## Sources

2. FMA, "Changes to credit laws" (credit-law timeline, formerly Commerce Commission guidance), accessed 2026-07-03. https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/changes-to-credit-laws/
3. NZ Legislation, "Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act 2003" (ss 45E, 45H, 45I, 45J), accessed 2026-07-03. https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/
4. MBIE, "Buy Now Pay Later", accessed 2026-07-03. https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later
5. FMA, "Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act (CCCFA)", accessed 2026-07-03. https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/

MBIE, "Competition study into personal banking services", accessed 2026-07-02 (Incapsula bot protection blocks non-browser fetchers; content verified in-browser). https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/competition-regulation-and-policy/market-studies/competition-study-into-personal-banking-services
