findings.title: High-cost credit history, arrears fees, BNPL defaults, and eligibility gaps are key credit-cost risks for vulnerable NZ borrowers
findings.author: Codex
findings.summary: This answers issue 71 as a ranked scan across the fee, product structure, and eligibility categories named in the issue , not as five separate product findings. That is a deliberately broad interpretation of the issue's one question; it should be used to decide what a transparenc
findings.body:
# High-cost credit history, arrears fees, BNPL defaults, and eligibility gaps are key credit-cost risks for vulnerable NZ borrowers

## Executive answer

This answers issue #71 as a **ranked scan across the fee, product-structure, and eligibility categories named in the issue**, not as five separate product findings. That is a deliberately broad interpretation of the issue's one question; it should be used to decide what a transparency artifact must cover, while product-specific follow-up work should handle deeper questions about BNPL, car finance, add-ons, and debt collection [issue #71](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/71).

- **High-cost consumer credit and repeat borrowing** are the clearest historically severe and statutorily targeted harm pattern, because NZ law singles out loans at 50%+ annual interest or equivalent default-rate exposure, caps total high-cost-loan interest and fees, and MBIE says those rules were introduced to reduce problem debt from excessive cost and repeat borrowing by vulnerable consumers [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions), [MBIE, consumer-credit RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29099-regulatory-impact-statement-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-law), [NZ Legislation, CCCFA](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/). This is not a High-confidence claim that 50%+ loans are the largest current harm: MBIE says the rules eliminated the high-cost lending market and shifted some concern toward 30-50% loans, BNPL, other credit, and hardship assistance [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions).
- **Default, dishonour, late-payment, and debt-collection fees** matter most when a borrower is already in arrears, because they increase the amount owed at the point of least cash-flow resilience; this is strongly supported for default and debt-collection fees, but dishonour-fee-specific public NZ evidence found in this pass is thinner [Consumer Protection, unfair fees](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/unfair-fees), [Commerce Commission, Real Finance](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/news-and-media/news-and-events/2022/real-finance-to-pay-1-36m-to-borrowers-for-unreasonable-fees/), [MBIE, 2018 credit-regulation discussion paper background](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/assets/3f19e04173/background-and-technical-detail-for-discussion-paper.pdf).
- **BNPL product structure** is a priority risk even when no interest is charged: MBIE says BNPL can cause or worsen hardship for some consumers, missed instalments can trigger default fees, and the evidence base is still limited because causal attribution is difficult [MBIE, Buy Now Pay Later](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later), [MBIE, BNPL RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/27270-regulatory-impact-statement-applying-the-credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act-in-a-proportionate-way-to-buy-now-pay-later-lenders-proactiverelease-pdf).
- **Eligibility and affordability criteria cut both ways**: weak screening can let unaffordable debt accumulate, but strict affordability or low-cost-loan criteria can exclude the same people from safer credit and push them toward BNPL, high-cost lenders, friends/family debt, or going without essentials [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do), [FinCap, 2024 consumer-credit submission](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29553-fincap-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-consultation-submission-pdf), [MSD, microfinance](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/microfinance-debt-solutions.html).
- Vulnerability is not theoretical: Stats NZ estimated that 9.1% of people were in material hardship in the year ended June 2025, and its 2025 child-poverty data estimated child material-hardship rates of 14.3% overall, 25.1% for Maori children, 31.0% for Pacific children, and 26.9% for disabled children [Stats NZ, household income and housing costs 2025](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/household-income-and-housing-cost-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/), [Stats NZ child-poverty 2025 CSV](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Child-poverty-statistics/Child-poverty-statistics-Year-ended-June-2025/Download-data/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025.zip).

**Overall confidence:** Medium - the direction of harm is well supported by official law/policy material, regulator enforcement, and financial-mentor evidence, but public NZ data does not cleanly quantify the marginal impact of each fee type by borrower vulnerability group.

## Evidence

### Vulnerability baseline: many households have no buffer

Stats NZ reported that 9.1% of people in New Zealand were living in households experiencing material hardship in the 12 months ended June 2025, which means a credit-cost transparency tool should assume a material share of borrowers have little room for repayment shocks [Stats NZ, household income and housing costs 2025](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/household-income-and-housing-cost-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/). Stats NZ's 2025 child-poverty CSV estimated 169,300 children in material hardship, or 14.3% of all children; the same dataset estimated materially higher hardship rates for Maori children at 25.1%, Pacific children at 31.0%, disabled children at 26.9%, and children in disabled households at 27.5% [Stats NZ child-poverty 2025 CSV](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Child-poverty-statistics/Child-poverty-statistics-Year-ended-June-2025/Download-data/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025.zip).

These hardship figures do not prove that credit fees cause poverty, but they are directly relevant to impact: a missed-payment fee, default interest charge, collection cost, or unaffordable instalment has a larger practical effect when the household already lacks essentials or cannot absorb an unexpected expense [Stats NZ, child poverty release](https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/).

### 1. High-cost credit and repeat borrowing have the strongest historical/statutory evidence, but not the strongest current-prevalence evidence

MBIE says the CCCFA defines a high-cost consumer credit contract as one with an average annual interest rate of 50% or more, or where the combined annual interest rate and default rate are likely to be 50% or more [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions). The same MBIE page summarises the high-cost provisions as covering the first-advance total cost cap, a 0.8% maximum daily rate of charge, a rebuttable presumption that default fees over $30 are unreasonable, a compound-interest ban, and restrictions on high-cost loans to some repeat borrowers [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions). The CCCFA text also sets out the first-advance cost cap, repeat-borrower restrictions, and 0.8% rate cap [NZ Legislation, CCCFA](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/).

MBIE's 2024 consumer-credit RIS says the 2020 high-cost-credit rules were introduced to reduce problem debt by addressing excessive cost and repeat borrowing by vulnerable consumers, and MBIE's high-cost review page says the impacts included elimination of the high-cost lending market, a doubling of lenders offering loans in the 30-50% interest range, and no longer having debt spirals and repeated borrowing from high-cost loans [MBIE, consumer-credit RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29099-regulatory-impact-statement-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-law), [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions). This means the rules may already have reduced the specific high-cost-loan harm they targeted, while leaving a live question about borrowers shifted into lower-rate high-fee products or other forms of debt [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions).

Regulator enforcement still supports keeping the high-cost pattern in scope. In 2024, the Commerce Commission said Eagle M.A.N Group had been ordered to pay a $200,000 penalty for high-cost loans breaching multiple CCCFA requirements; the Commission said Eagle MAN charged up to 182.5% annual interest on top of credit and default fees, issued loans whose interest and fees exceeded the first advance, and gave repeat high-cost loans to borrowers [Commerce Commission, Eagle MAN](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/news-and-media/news-and-events/2024/commerce-commission-secures-200k-penalty-against-lender-charging-up-to-182-5-interest/). Buddle Findlay's summary of the High Court decision also says the penalty followed multiple CCCFA breaches, including up to 182.5% annual interest plus credit/default fees, and that the case involved high-cost-loan repeat-borrowing rules [Buddle Findlay via Lexology, Eagle MAN](https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=ba2074fb-1b73-4f22-859a-215ea2ca90bd). This is a specific enforcement case, not a market-wide prevalence estimate, but it demonstrates the harm pattern the rules are designed to prevent [Commerce Commission, Eagle MAN](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/news-and-media/news-and-events/2024/commerce-commission-secures-200k-penalty-against-lender-charging-up-to-182-5-interest/), [Buddle Findlay via Lexology, Eagle MAN](https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=ba2074fb-1b73-4f22-859a-215ea2ca90bd).

**Confidence: Medium** that high-cost credit and repeat borrowing should remain a core harm category in the stream. Public evidence supports the mechanism, statutory response, and enforcement examples, but the current-source counter-evidence means this finding should not rank 50%+ high-cost lending above BNPL, 30-50% lending, arrears fees, debt collection, or vehicle-finance/add-on harms without more current comparative data.

### 2. Default, dishonour, late-payment, and debt-collection fees compound arrears

Consumer Protection summarises responsible-lending duties as requiring lenders to limit interest and fees for high-cost loans, charge reasonable credit and default fees, assess affordability and suitability, and treat borrowers fairly when they cannot pay [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do). The same guidance lists unfair or oppressive examples that include charging high fees for profit, giving too little time to pay missed payments, and letting fees and interest stack up by taking too long to act on overdue payments [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do).

Consumer Protection's unfair-fees guidance says borrowers can be charged default interest when repayments are missed or an account falls below zero or an agreed overdraft limit, and it lists late-payment default fees, demand-notice fees, repossession-warrant fees, debt-collection fees, and repossession fees as possible arrears-linked costs [Consumer Protection, unfair fees](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/unfair-fees). MBIE's 2018 consumer-credit background paper named "payment dishonour fees" alongside repayment fees, default fees, and other credit fees that borrowers may not fully account for when entering a credit contract, but I did not find current public NZ data isolating dishonour-fee frequency, amounts, or impacts on vulnerable borrowers [MBIE, 2018 credit-regulation discussion paper background](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/assets/3f19e04173/background-and-technical-detail-for-discussion-paper.pdf).

Commerce Commission enforcement shows that unreasonable credit/default fees can affect large borrower groups even outside the high-cost-loan niche. Real Finance agreed in 2022 to compensate borrowers $1.36 million after acknowledging unreasonable fees on loans [Commerce Commission, Real Finance](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/news-and-media/news-and-events/2022/real-finance-to-pay-1-36m-to-borrowers-for-unreasonable-fees/). Co-operative Bank admitted CCCFA breaches in 2025 and remediated 48,249 customers $7.225 million for charging higher fees than it should have [Commerce Commission, Co-operative Bank](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/news-and-media/news-and-events/2025/co-operative-bank-admits-charging-unreasonable-fees/), [Simpson Grierson, Co-operative Bank](https://www.simpsongrierson.com/insights-news/legal-updates/co-operative-bank-admits-breaches-of-cccfa-for-unreasonable-fees/). The 2026 High Court judgment later imposed a $2.482 million penalty and records that the conduct during the pecuniary-penalty period affected about 28,261 customers who were overcharged $1.3 million to $2 million, so the earlier $7.225 million/48,249-customer remediation figure should be read as the broader remediation programme rather than the narrower penalty-period harm assessed by the Court [Commerce Commission v The Co-operative Bank judgment, 31 March 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/assets/Documents/timeline/Commerce-Commission-v-The-Co-operative-Bank-Ltd-Judgement-31-March-2026.pdf), [Simpson Grierson, Co-operative Bank](https://www.simpsongrierson.com/insights-news/legal-updates/co-operative-bank-admits-breaches-of-cccfa-for-unreasonable-fees/). The Co-operative Bank case involved home loans, personal loans, overdrafts, and credit-card cash advances, so it supports the scale risk of unreasonable fees but should not be read as evidence that all remediated borrowers were vulnerable borrowers [Commerce Commission v The Co-operative Bank judgment, 31 March 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/assets/Documents/timeline/Commerce-Commission-v-The-Co-operative-Bank-Ltd-Judgement-31-March-2026.pdf).

FinCap's 2025 Voices report says missed repayments are concerning because further debt-collection fees can be added, prolonging and increasing debt harm for whanau supported by financial mentors [FinCap, Voices 2025](https://fincap.org.nz/images/fincap-voices-report-2025.pdf?_cchid=d38904d07c9a450c991dfc33fc4c7994). FinCap's client base is not representative of all borrowers, but it is directly relevant to vulnerable borrowers because financial mentors work with people already facing financial stress [FinCap, Voices 2025](https://fincap.org.nz/images/fincap-voices-report-2025.pdf?_cchid=d38904d07c9a450c991dfc33fc4c7994).

**Confidence: Medium** that arrears-linked fees should be a high-priority feature in any transparency or education artifact. The mechanism is direct and enforcement is strong, but public evidence does not consistently separate default fees, dishonour fees, default interest, debt-collection fees, and repossession costs by borrower vulnerability.

### 3. BNPL is lower-cost when paid on time, but missed instalments and multiple accounts are a priority hardship risk

MBIE describes BNPL as a credit alternative that lets consumers access goods or services now and pay later through interest-free instalments, and says new BNPL consumer protections took effect on 2 September 2024 to protect consumers from potential financial harms while preserving access to low-cost credit [MBIE, Buy Now Pay Later](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later). MBIE's BNPL RIS states the policy problem as BNPL causing or worsening financial hardship for some consumers, while also saying no substantial quantitative data clearly demonstrates causation because the sector is newer and hardship can arise from other debts, existing poverty, or overspending [MBIE, BNPL RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/27270-regulatory-impact-statement-applying-the-credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act-in-a-proportionate-way-to-buy-now-pay-later-lenders-proactiverelease-pdf).

MBIE identifies several BNPL harm pathways: hardship may worsen when a consumer defaults and pays late fees, when BNPL payments cause default on another obligation, when consumers use BNPL for essentials because they are already struggling, or when consumers make BNPL payments but go without essentials or miss other debt repayments [MBIE, BNPL RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/27270-regulatory-impact-statement-applying-the-credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act-in-a-proportionate-way-to-buy-now-pay-later-lenders-proactiverelease-pdf). MBIE's 2024 addendum says financial mentors raised concerns about accumulated BNPL default fees, and says default fees can worsen hardship by increasing the amount owed even though attributing hardship to those fees is difficult [MBIE, BNPL default-fee RIS addendum](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29100-regulatory-impact-statement-addendum-buy-now-pay-later-regulations).

Financial-mentor evidence is consistent with MBIE's concern. FinCap told MBIE in 2024 that unaffordable BNPL loans were among the most common consumer-credit issues financial mentors had raised since December 2021, and that mentors saw debt spirals where people took multiple loans and kept paying them while going without essentials to keep facilities open [FinCap, 2024 consumer-credit submission](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29553-fincap-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-consultation-submission-pdf). Christians Against Poverty submitted to MBIE in 2021 that the share of its clients with BNPL debt rose from 2% in 2019 to 17% in 2021, and that those clients had two BNPL debts on average; this is one debt-help organisation's own client data and may not represent the broader borrowing population, so it is supporting evidence for vulnerable-client risk rather than market-wide prevalence evidence [Christians Against Poverty, BNPL submission](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/18981-christians-against-poverty-buy-now-pay-later-submission-pdf).

**Confidence: Medium** that BNPL missed-payment fees, multiple concurrent BNPL debts, and instalment timing should be prioritised. The evidence is strong enough to flag risk for vulnerable borrowers, but MBIE is explicit that public quantitative evidence is limited, causation is hard to isolate, and post-September-2024 effects are not yet clear in the public sources reviewed here.

### 4. Add-on products and suitability checks matter, but the public evidence found here is thinner

Consumer Protection says lender suitability checks can include whether a borrower needs extra products such as insurance, and it gives credit-related insurance examples such as payment protection or mechanical-breakdown insurance added to car finance [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do). This supports including add-ons in a total-cost-of-credit artifact, because an add-on can change the amount financed and the repayment burden even when the headline interest rate is unchanged [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do).

I did not find enough current public NZ evidence in this pass to rank add-on insurance above high-cost credit, BNPL default structures, or arrears/default fees for vulnerable borrowers. Add-ons should still be captured as a disclosure and suitability issue, especially for vehicle finance and consumer leases, but this finding treats them as **Low confidence** on scale and impact until a product-specific evidence pass is done [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do).

### 5. Eligibility criteria can protect borrowers or exclude them from safer credit

Affordability criteria have a protective purpose: Consumer Protection says lenders must be satisfied borrowers are likely to make required repayments without substantial hardship, and must make reasonable inquiries such as income and expenses checks [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do). That matters for vulnerable borrowers because unaffordable lending can turn a short-term cash-flow problem into repeat borrowing, arrears, and collection costs [Consumer Protection, lender duties](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do).

The exclusion side is also real. MSD says Good Shepherd's Good Loans are no-interest, fee-free loans for people on low incomes to pay for essential expenses and avoid or manage high-cost debt, but the same MSD page says those loans cannot be used for overdue bills, rent arrears, day-to-day living expenses, cash, or urgent needs because applications take time [MSD, microfinance](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/microfinance-debt-solutions.html). Good Shepherd's eligibility page similarly says no-interest loans cannot be used for day-to-day living expenses or urgent needs; any operational capacity messages on that page are live service details and should be rechecked before being used for design decisions [Good Shepherd NZ, Good Loans](https://goodshepherd.org.nz/get-support/our-services/loans/).

FinCap's 2024 submission makes the tradeoff concrete: it says people supported by financial mentors who can no longer access loans from CCCFA-bound responsible lenders can end up in substantial hardship to make repayments on further BNPL, and it reports whanau borrowing from BNPL lenders, Work and Income, friends, family, and other CCCFA lenders to relieve financial pressure [FinCap, 2024 consumer-credit submission](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29553-fincap-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-consultation-submission-pdf), [FinCap, affordability-code submission](https://fincap.org.nz/images/240610-SUB-FinCap-RLC-exposure-draft.pdf).

**Confidence: Medium** that eligibility design should be treated as a harm vector, not just an access rule. It is harder to rank than direct fees because the same criterion can prevent unaffordable lending in one case and exclude someone from lower-cost credit in another.

### Current rules may already be reducing some harm

The strongest counter-evidence is for high-cost credit: MBIE's high-cost review page says the provisions eliminated the high-cost lending market and ended debt spirals and repeated borrowing from high-cost loans, while also identifying indirect shifts into BNPL, other credit types, and more government hardship assistance [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions). MBIE's consumer-credit RIS also says the high-cost provisions led to elimination of lending at 50%+ interest rates, but the review requirement specifically asked whether the threshold should be reduced to 30-50%, which keeps open the question of whether harm has shifted just below the threshold [MBIE, consumer-credit RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29099-regulatory-impact-statement-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-law), [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions).

For BNPL and affordability checks, the public evidence is less settled. MBIE says BNPL protections took effect on 2 September 2024, so a post-reform borrower-outcome dataset would be needed before saying whether those protections materially reduced arrears, default-fee accumulation, or payment juggling [MBIE, Buy Now Pay Later](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/consumer-protection/buy-now-pay-later). MBIE's 2024-25 financial-services reform page says prescriptive affordability requirements in the Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Regulations 2004 were revoked from 31 July 2024, which is evidence that some earlier affordability settings were considered too prescriptive or burdensome, not proof that affordability checks should be weak [MBIE, 2024-25 financial services reforms](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms).

**Confidence: Medium** that current high-cost-credit rules have reduced the original high-cost-loan harm. **Confidence: Low** that public evidence can yet show whether BNPL regulation and revised affordability settings are working as intended for vulnerable borrowers.

## Surprising or load-bearing claims

| Claim | Source 1 | Source 2 | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-cost credit and repeat borrowing are historically severe and statutorily targeted harms, but current public evidence does not prove that 50%+ high-cost loans are the largest current harm category. | [MBIE says high-cost-credit rules were introduced to reduce problem debt from excessive cost and repeat borrowing by vulnerable consumers](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29099-regulatory-impact-statement-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-law) | [MBIE's high-cost review page says impacts included elimination of high-cost lending and no longer having debt spirals from high-cost loans, while listing shifts into 30-50% loans, BNPL, other credit, and hardship assistance](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions) | Medium |
| BNPL can worsen hardship through missed instalments, late fees, and payment juggling, but public evidence does not prove clean causation or post-2024 reform effects. | [MBIE BNPL RIS says BNPL can cause or worsen hardship but substantial quantitative causal evidence is lacking](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/27270-regulatory-impact-statement-applying-the-credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act-in-a-proportionate-way-to-buy-now-pay-later-lenders-proactiverelease-pdf) | [FinCap reports financial-mentor observations of multiple BNPL loans and going without essentials to keep facilities open](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29553-fincap-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-consultation-submission-pdf) | Medium |
| Default and debt-collection fees matter because they add cost after repayment failure, but public data does not fully separate default, dishonour, collection, repossession, and default-interest costs by vulnerability group. | [Consumer Protection says borrowers may face default interest, late-payment default fees, demand-notice fees, debt-collection fees, and repossession fees](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/unfair-fees) | [FinCap Voices says further debt-collection fees can prolong and increase debt harm after missed repayments](https://fincap.org.nz/images/fincap-voices-report-2025.pdf?_cchid=d38904d07c9a450c991dfc33fc4c7994) | Medium |
| Low-cost credit eligibility is itself a risk point because safer products may not cover urgent, bill, rent, or living-cost needs. | [MSD says Good Loans are no-interest/fee-free but cannot be used for overdue bills, rent arrears, day-to-day living expenses, cash, or urgent needs](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/microfinance-debt-solutions.html) | [Good Shepherd says Good Loans cannot cover day-to-day essentials or urgent needs](https://goodshepherd.org.nz/get-support/our-services/loans/) | Medium |

## Source access and verification notes

- Commerce Commission's Eagle MAN and Co-operative Bank news pages returned bot-protection errors to plain curl in review. I kept the official URLs but added accessible corroboration for the load-bearing figures: Buddle Findlay/Lexology for Eagle MAN, Simpson Grierson for the Co-operative Bank remediation figure, and the Commerce Commission-hosted 2026 High Court judgment PDF for the later Co-operative Bank penalty-period figures [Buddle Findlay via Lexology, Eagle MAN](https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=ba2074fb-1b73-4f22-859a-215ea2ca90bd), [Simpson Grierson, Co-operative Bank](https://www.simpsongrierson.com/insights-news/legal-updates/co-operative-bank-admits-breaches-of-cccfa-for-unreasonable-fees/), [Commerce Commission v The Co-operative Bank judgment, 31 March 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/assets/Documents/timeline/Commerce-Commission-v-The-Co-operative-Bank-Ltd-Judgement-31-March-2026.pdf).
- The old Commerce Commission "changes to credit laws" URL now returns 404 in `agent-browser read` after consumer-credit regulatory responsibility transferred to the FMA on 1 July 2026, so this version cites MBIE's high-cost-credit review page, the CCCFA text, and the FMA consumer-credit provider page instead [FMA, consumer credit providers](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/consumer-credit/), [MBIE, high-cost credit review](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/financial-markets-conduct-regulation/2024-financial-services-reforms/fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-legislation-high-cost-credit-contracts-provisions), [NZ Legislation, CCCFA](https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2003/52/en/latest/).
- Several MBIE DMS PDF links and MBIE HTML pages returned Incapsula/challenge responses to plain curl, `agent-browser read`, and the repo's CloakBrowser helper in this environment on 2 July 2026, although public search indexes still expose the official page/document text. I retained them because they are official public documents, supplemented key claims with MBIE HTML pages where possible, and treated unsupported MBIE-only points as uncertainty rather than market-wide findings; this access note is a limitation, not an evidentiary substitute for the cited documents [MBIE, consumer-credit RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29099-regulatory-impact-statement-fit-for-purpose-consumer-credit-law), [MBIE, BNPL RIS](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/27270-regulatory-impact-statement-applying-the-credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act-in-a-proportionate-way-to-buy-now-pay-later-lenders-proactiverelease-pdf), [MBIE, BNPL default-fee RIS addendum](https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/29100-regulatory-impact-statement-addendum-buy-now-pay-later-regulations).
- Stats NZ's child-poverty figures are in a ZIP/CSV download, so reviewers checking the exact 169,300 and 14.3% figures need to download and parse the file rather than relying on a plain HTML page [Stats NZ child-poverty 2025 CSV](https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Child-poverty-statistics/Child-poverty-statistics-Year-ended-June-2025/Download-data/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025.zip).

## What would change this conclusion

- A current, representative NZ dataset linking borrower income, ethnicity, disability, product type, arrears status, default fees, collection fees, and repeat borrowing would materially change the ranking and could move the overall confidence to High.
- The conclusion on high-cost credit would change if post-reform evidence showed borrowers formerly using high-cost credit are now materially worse off because of exclusion, lower-rate high-fee substitutes, illegal lending, or unmet urgent needs.
- The conclusion on BNPL would change if post-September-2024 CCCFA/credit-reporting data showed that BNPL arrears, repeat use, or default-fee accumulation had materially fallen, or if it showed stronger causal harm than MBIE could establish in 2023-2024.
- The conclusion on add-on insurance would change if a product-specific review of vehicle finance, mobile trading, consumer leases, and personal loans found large public evidence of unsuitable add-ons or add-on-driven arrears.
- The conclusion on dishonour fees would change if current public lender, regulator, Banking Ombudsman, or financial-mentor data separated dishonour fees from other default, overdraft, debt-collection, and repossession costs for vulnerable borrowers.
- I could not verify a current public, regulator-grade count of NZ borrowers affected by each fee type; the evidence is strongest on mechanisms, rules, and selected enforcement/remediation cases.
- I did not contact borrowers, lenders, financial mentors, MoneyTalks, FinCap, Good Shepherd, Commerce Commission, FMA, or MBIE; lived-experience and frontline validation should happen before this stream designs a borrower-facing tool.

## Open follow-up questions

- For realistic borrower scenarios in sibling issue #67, how much total cost comes from interest, establishment fees, account fees, default fees, collection costs, and add-ons across comparable product types?
- Which current public disclosures in sibling issue #70 expose default-fee caps, hardship options, repeat-borrowing restrictions, BNPL late-fee policies, and low-cost-credit eligibility criteria clearly enough for a non-expert borrower?
- After BNPL became subject to the CCCFA on 2 September 2024, what public evidence exists on arrears, complaints, credit-reporting impacts, hardship applications, and default-fee revenue?
- How do financial mentors rank high-cost credit, BNPL, vehicle finance, rent arrears, Work and Income debt, utility arrears, and informal family/friend debt in actual client triage?
- Should a separate research issue examine whether high-cost-credit restrictions have shifted vulnerable borrowers into 30-50% APR products, BNPL, government hardship assistance, or unmet need?

## Sources

2. Stats NZ. "Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.stats.govt.nz/information-releases/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025/
3. Stats NZ. "Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025 - CSV." Accessed 2 July 2026. https://www.stats.govt.nz/assets/Uploads/Child-poverty-statistics/Child-poverty-statistics-Year-ended-June-2025/Download-data/child-poverty-statistics-year-ended-june-2025.zip

findings.title: An NZ small-credit help front door needs scenario comparison, provenance, and consented handoff to MoneyTalks-style financial mentoring
findings.author: codex
findings.summary: A credible NZ small credit front door should show scenario based total cost outputs, because NZ consumer credit disclosures cover interest, fees, dispute resolution details, cancellation rights, hardship rights, and continuing disclosure, and Consumer Protection tells borrowers t
findings.body:
# An NZ small-credit help front door needs scenario comparison, provenance, and consented handoff to MoneyTalks-style financial mentoring

## Executive answer

- A credible NZ small-credit front door should show scenario-based total-cost outputs, because NZ consumer-credit disclosures cover interest, fees, dispute-resolution details, cancellation rights, hardship rights, and continuing disclosure, and Consumer Protection tells borrowers to check total repayment including admin fees, debt collection fees, and interest before signing. It should avoid ranked or personalised "best product for you" recommendations at minimum viable stage because the consumer-credit-specific advice boundary remains a legal-risk question: FMA says advice to retail clients must be provided by, or under, a Financial Advice Provider licence, while this research did not find a current NZ source proving that every interactive consumer-credit ranking is regulated financial advice. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [Consumer Protection loans and lenders, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/loans-and-lenders); [FMA Financial Advice Provider, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/financial-advice-provider/); [Financial Services Council financial advice law guide, accessed 2 July 2026](https://blog.fsc.org.nz/hubfs/FSC%20Corporate/Documents/Website%20Docs/FSC%20Financial%20Advice%20Law%20Guide.pdf)
- Minimum viable data fields are: product type, provider legal/trading identity, FSPR number if available, FMA consumer-credit provider licence or deemed-licence status where applicable, dispute-resolution scheme where applicable, amount/term/repayment frequency constraints, advertised rate range, all credit/default/service fees, missed-payment treatment, eligibility caveats, security/guarantor requirements, hardship/dispute-resolution routes, source URL/document date, scrape/check date, and confidence in comparability. [Consumer Protection, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/cccfa-transfer-faq/); [FSPR DRS help, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fsp-register.companiesoffice.govt.nz/help-centre/dispute-resolution-schemes/choosing-a-scheme/)
- The front door should use public lender/regulator sources for public education, but any reliable automated provider register or bulk identity matching will probably need Companies Office API/data-service access and explicit licence/terms review, because Companies Office says its APIs are designed for automated retrieval and customers who regularly use register data. [Companies Office APIs, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.companiesoffice.govt.nz/data-services/ways-to-get-our-data/using-our-data-through-apis/)
- Human help should be presented as a first-class outcome: MSD says MoneyTalks is funded through Building Financial Capability, provides free advice through trained financial mentors, connects people with local BFC providers, and acts as a front door and triage to BFC services; FinCap's MoneyTalks page separately says MoneyTalks connects people with financial mentors and services to help with financial hardship. [MSD MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html); [FinCap MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks)
- A warm handoff should not be shipped until an NFP partner has co-designed it and a privacy review has approved the consent flow, because NZ organisations that collect personal information must follow the Privacy Act, and DPUP guidance says consent establishes what a person is agreeing to when personal information is needed to provide a service. [Office of the Privacy Commissioner, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.privacy.org.nz/responsibilities/your-obligations/); [Digital.govt.nz DPUP consent guidance, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/privacy-security-and-risk/privacy/data-protection-and-use-policy-dpup/use-dpup-in-your-work/resources-for-working-with-dpup/how-to-approach-consent)

**Overall confidence:** Medium - the minimum data, disclosure, privacy, and MoneyTalks pathway requirements are well supported by official/public sources, but the exact referral operating model, capacity, product set, and legal advice boundary require human partner validation.

## Evidence

### What the front door needs to show

The first screen should ask for a small number of scenario inputs: purpose category, amount needed, when the money is needed, expected repayment timeframe, whether the user already has a credit card or overdraft, whether the purchase is merchant-linked, and whether the user is currently behind on payments; these inputs map to product differences that NZ sources treat separately, including loans, agreed overdrafts, BNPL accounts, credit cards, and hardship/payment-problem pathways. [Consumer Protection borrowing duties, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/what-lenders-must-do); [Consumer Protection BNPL, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/ways-to-buy-and-pay/buy-now-pay-later); [Commerce Commission consumer credit contracts, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/credit-providers/consumer-credit-contracts/)

The output should separate "publicly comparable cost" from "not comparable or eligibility-dependent" because Consumer Protection says lenders must disclose standard terms and borrowing costs publicly to help borrowers compare, while Commerce Commission affordability guidance says lenders still need reasonable inquiries to be satisfied that payments are likely affordable without substantial hardship. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [Commerce Commission affordability, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/credit-providers/assessing-for-affordability/)

Minimum scenario output should include principal, term, repayment frequency, total repayment if paid on time, total interest and fees if paid on time, a missed-payment/default-fee warning where the source discloses it, a "not a quote" caveat, and an "eligibility may change this" caveat; the need to include total repayment, interest, fees, default penalties, and contract changes follows from Consumer Protection's CCCFA and loans guidance. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [Consumer Protection loans and lenders, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/loans-and-lenders)

The front door should include debt-stress exits before product comparison, because Consumer Protection directs people with BNPL payment problems to talk to the provider, notes hardship application rights, and says a free financial mentor can help prepare or talk to the lender for them. BNPL also needs current regulatory treatment, not old "unregulated instalment" assumptions: Commerce Commission says BNPL lenders must comply with CCCFA lender obligations from 2 September 2024 unless an exemption applies. [Consumer Protection BNPL, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/ways-to-buy-and-pay/buy-now-pay-later); [Commerce Commission BNPL, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/credit-providers/buy-now-pay-later/)

The tool should avoid a "best product for you" answer unless a qualified legal review and, if needed, a licensed financial-advice pathway are deliberately created. The evidence supports this as a risk-control requirement rather than a settled legal conclusion for all consumer-credit rankings: FMA says advice to retail clients must be provided by, or under, a Financial Advice Provider licence, and the Financial Services Council's legal guide says regulated financial advice to retail clients must be given by, or under, an FMA-licensed FAP, but this research did not find direct current FMA or legal guidance saying a scenario-based consumer-credit ranking is always regulated financial advice rather than advertising, comparison, responsible-lending, or consumer-credit licensing territory. [FMA Financial Advice Provider, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/financial-advice-provider/); [Financial Services Council financial advice law guide, accessed 2 July 2026](https://blog.fsc.org.nz/hubfs/FSC%20Corporate/Documents/Website%20Docs/FSC%20Financial%20Advice%20Law%20Guide.pdf)

Existing NZ comparison tools reduce duplication risk but do not remove the need for a support-first front door. Money Compare lists personal-loan filters, rates, amount ranges, terms, payment frequency, and establishment fees across dozens of plans; MoneyHub, Canstar, Finance.co.nz, and Glimp publish personal-loan comparisons, lender summaries, or rate-comparison tables. These are useful market context, but they are comparison surfaces rather than MoneyTalks-style triage, warm handoff, consent, and provenance governance models. [Money Compare personal loans, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.moneycompare.co.nz/compare/borrowings.personal-loans); [MoneyHub personal loans, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.moneyhub.co.nz/personal-loans.html); [Canstar personal loans, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.canstar.co.nz/personal-loans/); [Finance.co.nz personal loans, accessed 2 July 2026](https://finance.co.nz/personal-loans/); [Glimp personal loans, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.glimp.co.nz/personal-loans)

### Minimum viable data model

| Field group | Minimum fields | Why it is needed | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Provider identity | Legal name, trading name, source URL, provider homepage, FSPR number if available, FMA consumer-credit provider licence or deemed-licence status where applicable, DRS membership if available | As at 2 July 2026, FMA says the earlier Commerce Commission certification regime is being replaced by FMA licensing for consumer lenders, and consumer lenders certified at transfer are deemed to hold an FMA licence for the consumer-credit service; FSPs serving retail clients must generally record DRS membership on the FSPR. [FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/cccfa-transfer-faq/); [FSPR DRS help, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fsp-register.companiesoffice.govt.nz/help-centre/dispute-resolution-schemes/choosing-a-scheme/) | Medium |
| Product shape | Product type, secured/unsecured flag, revolving/fixed-term flag, purchase-linked/cash-access flag, amount range, term range, repayment frequency options | Consumer-credit products are not interchangeable, and Commerce Commission identifies mortgages, credit cards, arranged overdrafts, personal or cash loans as consumer credit contract examples. [Commerce Commission consumer credit contracts, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/credit-providers/consumer-credit-contracts/) | High |
| Cost inputs | Annual interest rate or range, default interest, establishment fees, regular account/administration fees, payment-processing fees, early repayment fees, missed-payment/default fees, debt-collection or enforcement-cost caveats | Consumer Protection says lenders must disclose annual interest rate, all fees, cancellation information, and DRS details before agreement, and says interest, default interest, credit fees, and early repayment fees have specific CCCFA treatment. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act) | High |
| Scenario maths | Calculation method, total repayment, total interest, total fees, included/excluded fees, source rate date, scrape/check date, "not a lender quote" flag | Consumer Protection says borrowers should check total repayment including admin fees, debt collection fees, and all interest, and says a lender's standard contract terms and costs of borrowing must be public for each loan type. [Consumer Protection loans and lenders, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/loans-and-lenders); [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act) | High |
| Eligibility and risk | Age/residency/income caveats where public, credit-check caveat, affordability-assessment caveat, security/guarantor caveat, "approval not guaranteed" caveat | Lenders must make reasonable inquiries before entering or materially changing an agreement to be satisfied that payments are likely affordable without substantial hardship; Consumer Protection also tells borrowers to check eligibility, security, and default consequences before applying. [Commerce Commission affordability, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/business/credit-providers/assessing-for-affordability/); [Consumer Protection loans and lenders, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/loans-and-lenders) | High |
| Help pathways | MoneyTalks contact options, local financial mentoring finder/contact route if partner-approved, hardship/dispute/complaint links, emergency deflection language | MSD says MoneyTalks connects people to BFC providers in their community and acts as a front door and triage to BFC services, while FinCap says MoneyTalks connects people with financial mentors and services to help with financial hardship. [MSD MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html); [FinCap MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks) | High |
| Provenance and freshness | Source owner, URL, document type, observed value, observed date, page/document last-updated date if visible, next-check date, human-review status, confidence | Lender-owned pages and disclosure documents can change, and the project needs an audit trail to defend comparisons against adversarial review; Companies Office says APIs support automated retrieval for people using register data regularly. [Companies Office APIs, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.companiesoffice.govt.nz/data-services/ways-to-get-our-data/using-our-data-through-apis/) | Medium |

### Public and licensed data requirements

Public regulator/consumer-protection sources are sufficient for general education pages covering disclosure duties, high-cost-credit constraints, hardship applications, payment problems, and complaint routes. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [Consumer Protection payment problems, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/borrowing-money/payment-problems); [Consumer Protection complaints page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/help-product-service/managing-money/how-to-complain-about-your-financial-service-provider)

Current regulator context needs an explicit date stamp: FMA says regulatory responsibility for the CCCFA transferred from the Commerce Commission to FMA on 1 July 2026, and FMA's transfer FAQ says the earlier Commerce Commission certification regime is ending and being replaced by FMA licensing for consumer lenders. A live product should therefore treat older Commerce Commission guidance and certification pages as transition or historical source material while checking current FMA/MBIE/Consumer Protection pages for governance changes. [FMA CCCFA, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/); [FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/cccfa-transfer-faq/)

Lender-owned product pages and disclosure statements are necessary for current rates, fees, amount ranges, term ranges, and eligibility caveats, because Consumer Protection says lenders must make standard terms and borrowing costs publicly available through their website or clearly displayed notices. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act)

The Commerce Commission certified-lender register can help with transition provenance for entities certified before 1 July 2026, but it is not the current validation model on its own: FMA says certified consumer lenders at transfer are deemed to hold an FMA market-services licence for acting as a creditor under a consumer credit contract, and the FSPR will be updated automatically. It also should not be treated as a product catalogue because the Commerce Commission register page describes certification status rather than product-level rates, fees, and criteria. [FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/cccfa-transfer-faq/); [Commerce Commission certified lenders register, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/consumers/dealing-with-typical-situations/borrowing-money-and-buying-on-credit/register-of-certified-lenders-and-mobile-traders/)

The FSPR should be used to verify provider identity and dispute-resolution information where applicable, because FSPR guidance says providers of financial services to retail clients must generally belong to an approved dispute-resolution scheme and record membership details on the register. [FSPR DRS help, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fsp-register.companiesoffice.govt.nz/help-centre/dispute-resolution-schemes/choosing-a-scheme/)

Bulk or automated FSPR/NZBN/Companies Office matching should go through the official API or data-request route rather than scraping as an assumed right, because Companies Office says APIs are designed for automated retrieval and are used by customers who regularly use register data or develop products using that data. [Companies Office APIs, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.companiesoffice.govt.nz/data-services/ways-to-get-our-data/using-our-data-through-apis/)

### Provenance and update requirements

Each numeric value should store a source URL, source owner, observed date, and extraction method, because a comparison cannot be defended if a lender changes rates or fees after the tool records them; this is an engineering requirement inferred from the changeable nature of lender disclosures, and it needs validation against actual lender update patterns. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act)

The statutory requirement is change-based rather than calendar-based: lender disclosures need to stay clear and not misleading, and Consumer Protection describes continuing-disclosure duties for changes such as interest rates, credit fees, repayment changes, and some hardship/guarantor information. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act)

A pilot could start with an explicit, unvalidated internal freshness class: regulator/static guidance checked quarterly, lender rates and fees checked at least monthly before publication, and high-risk/default-fee fields checked before each campaign or public release. This cadence is a product-governance assumption derived from the statutory need for clear, non-misleading, change-based disclosure, not an NZ statutory interval; I found no official NZ rule prescribing a monthly update frequency for a third-party comparison tool, so the cadence should be reviewed after the first data-quality cycle. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act)

Every scenario output should display its last checked date and should degrade to "needs recheck" when a source is stale, because Consumer Protection says lender disclosures must be clear, likely to be noticed, and not misleading or deceptive about important things. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act)

Named provider inclusion should require two checks: the product page/disclosure source supports the cost fields, and a current register/licensing/dispute-resolution source supports the provider identity where applicable; this is a governance recommendation based on the separate roles of lender disclosures, FMA consumer-credit licensing or deemed-licensing status after 1 July 2026, transition certification records, and the FSPR DRS record. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/cccfa-transfer-faq/); [Commerce Commission certified lenders register, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.comcom.govt.nz/consumers/dealing-with-typical-situations/borrowing-money-and-buying-on-credit/register-of-certified-lenders-and-mobile-traders/); [FSPR DRS help, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fsp-register.companiesoffice.govt.nz/help-centre/dispute-resolution-schemes/choosing-a-scheme/)

### Referral and warm-handoff guardrails

MoneyTalks is the only referral destination I would name at minimum viable stage without further consent work, because MSD says MoneyTalks is funded through BFC, provides free advice, uses trained financial mentors, connects people to local BFC providers, and acts as the front door and triage to BFC services. [MSD MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html)

FinCap can be named as the contracted MoneyTalks provider and as public context for the financial mentoring sector, because MSD says FinCap is contracted to provide MoneyTalks and FinCap says MoneyTalks connects people with financial mentors and services to help with financial hardship. [MSD MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html); [FinCap MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://fincap.org.nz/moneytalks)

Local financial mentoring services should not be listed as operational referral partners until the service owner, MoneyTalks/FinCap, or a human steward confirms the appropriate public listing and referral terms, because this research did not contact any organisation and the issue instruction forbids listing an organisation as a referral partner unless public material or later consent supports it. [Issue #69, accessed 2 July 2026](https://github.com/thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project/issues/69)

The safest first release is a "self-directed referral" button that opens MoneyTalks contact channels and clearly says no personal data has been sent; MSD publishes phone, text, email, online chat, and availability details for MoneyTalks. [MSD MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html)

A true warm handoff should require explicit consent, minimal data transfer, a copy-to-user option, and a partner-side intake agreement, because NZ organisations that collect personal information must comply with the Privacy Act and DPUP says consent establishes expectations about what a person agrees to when personal information is needed for service delivery. [Office of the Privacy Commissioner, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.privacy.org.nz/responsibilities/your-obligations/); [Digital.govt.nz DPUP consent guidance, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.digital.govt.nz/standards-and-guidance/privacy-security-and-risk/privacy/data-protection-and-use-policy-dpup/use-dpup-in-your-work/resources-for-working-with-dpup/how-to-approach-consent)

Warm-handoff data should be limited to the user's preferred name, contact method, best contact time, broad issue category, whether contact is urgent, and optional free text; it should not include lender account numbers, bank statements, IDs, or detailed debt lists unless a partner explicitly asks for them through an approved intake process, because the Privacy Act covers any information that can identify a person and agencies must handle personal information under the Act. [Office of the Privacy Commissioner, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.privacy.org.nz/responsibilities/your-obligations/)

### Partner validation needs

Before ideation, a human steward should validate with MoneyTalks/FinCap whether the front door should direct everyone to MoneyTalks first, whether a postcode-based local finder is appropriate, what language avoids shame or urgency traps, what hours/capacity constraints matter, and what information mentors actually want before first contact; MSD describes MoneyTalks as the BFC front door, but public pages do not define an API, intake payload, service-level agreement, or co-branding permission. [MSD MoneyTalks, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.msd.govt.nz/what-we-can-do/providers/building-financial-capability/moneytalks/index.html)

Before naming any lender product in a public release, a human or reviewer should check whether the source terms permit comparison, whether the value is current, whether the comparison risks implying advice, and whether provider identity is verified against current FMA/FSPR/register sources where applicable. [Consumer Protection CCCFA page, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.consumerprotection.govt.nz/general-help/consumer-laws/credit-contracts-and-consumer-finance-act); [FMA CCCFA transfer FAQ, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/legislation/cccfa/cccfa-transfer-faq/); [Companies Office APIs, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.companiesoffice.govt.nz/data-services/ways-to-get-our-data/using-our-data-through-apis/)

Before launching personalised ranking, a qualified NZ financial-services lawyer or FMA-facing adviser should review the advice boundary, because FMA says advice to retail clients must be provided by, or under, a FAP licence, but this research did not verify whether a consumer-credit ranking tool would necessarily fall inside that regime. [FMA Financial Advice Provider, accessed 2 July 2026](https://www.fma.govt.nz/business/services/financial-advice-provider/)

## What would change this conclusion

- A written response from MoneyTalks/FinCap or MSD saying third-party warm referrals are unwanted, capacity-constrained, or need a different path would change the referral design; I did not contact MoneyTalks, FinCap, MSD, or local providers.
- Legal advice or FMA guidance saying the proposed scenario outputs or rankings are outside regulated financial advice, advertising, fair-dealing, and consumer-credit licensing risk would allow a less conservative ranking model; I did not obtain legal advice.
- Lender or Companies Office terms that restrict automated reuse of register/product data would change the data architecture; I verified public API/data-service pages but did not obtain API access or licence terms.
- Evidence from financial mentors or borrowers that comparison-before-support causes harm would move the human-help route ahead of any cost output; I found public support for financial mentoring but no lived-experience testing for this specific front door.
- I could not verify an official MoneyTalks API, formal referral SLA, current intake payload, local-service capacity, or permission to co-brand a handoff.
- I could not verify a complete public, machine-readable dataset of current NZ small-credit product rates, fees, and eligibility criteria; existing comparison pages show some rates and fees, but lender-owned pages remain necessary unless a licensed dataset is obtained.
- I could not verify the Commerce Commission disclosure-guidelines PDF or lender-responsibility-principles page with `agent-browser`; on 2 July 2026 both returned 404 through that browser fetcher after plain HTTP fetches had returned 403 in review. Both sources were removed from load-bearing support and replaced with accessible Consumer Protection, Commerce Commission affordability/BNPL/register pages, FinCap, FMA, FSC, and comparison-tool sources.

## Open follow-up questions

- What referral pathway, language, and intake fields would MoneyTalks/FinCap approve for a third-party small-credit front door?
- Which lender product pages or disclosure documents can be ingested under stable, lawful terms, and which require manual review?
- Where exactly is the boundary between scenario education, advertising/comparison, regulated financial advice, and consumer-credit licensing obligations for an interactive NZ consumer-credit tool?
- What evidence do financial mentors have about the debt situations where a comparison table helps versus where it delays a better hardship, dispute, or mentoring pathway?
